AI.tldr: Proposes a taxonomy and expert survey to standardize AI sycophancy definitions and measurements, highlighting varied interpretations and the need for a shared vocabulary.
AI.motivation: Inconsistent definitions of AI sycophancy hinder comparison, mitigation, and governance; a unified framework is needed to unify research and practice.
AI.method: (1) Systematic review of 70 papers to construct a taxonomy of sycophantic behaviors (targets beliefs vs. traits/emotions; explicit vs. implicit). (2) Survey of 106 AI sycophancy and related-field experts to gauge agreement on what counts as sycophancy.
AI.result: The taxonomy reveals two dimensions (target: beliefs vs. personal traits/emotions; form: explicit language vs. implicit behaviors). Mapping shows emphasis on overt, belief-focused sycophancy; expert consensus on the problem is high (94.3%), but agreement on which specific behaviors qualify is low.
AI.conclusion: AI sycophancy comprises a broad family of behaviors with diverse measurement, intervention, and governance needs; the taxonomy provides a shared vocabulary to standardize study and management across actors.

title: SceneGraphGrounder: Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding via Structured Scene Graph Matching
summary: Zero-shot 3D visual grounding requires localizing objects in unstructured environments from free-form natural language. Recent vision-language model (VLM) approaches achieve promising results but rely on view-dependent reasoning or implicit representations, limiting spatial consistency and interpretability for compositional queries. We propose SceneGraphGrounder, a framework that reformulates 3D grounding as structured graph matching over a reconstructed 3D scene graph. To enable this formulation, we introduce a visual marker prompting strategy that enables a VLM to infer object-object relationships from 2D views, which are subsequently lifted into a persistent 3D scene graph encoding both spatial and semantic relations. Given a query, we construct a query graph and perform constrained alignment with the scene graph, ensuring multi-view consistency and interpretable reasoning. Experiments on the ScanRefer benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance among zero-shot approaches, using only RGB-D inputs. We further validate our framework through real-world deployment on a mobile robot, demonstrating robust spatial reasoning in long-horizon physical environments. We will make our code publicly available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot 3D grounding via structured 3D scene graphs: SceneGraphGrounder uses visual-marker prompts to build a persistent graph from multi-view RGB-D and performs constrained graph matching for interpretable, multi-view-consistent grounding, validated on ScanRefer and real-world robotics.
AI.motivation: Limitations of current VLM-based 3D grounding: reliance on view-dependent reasoning and implicit representations leads to spatial inconsistencies and poor interpretability for compositional queries. A persistent, interpretable 3D scene graph is desirable.
AI.method: 1) Build a 3D scene graph from multi-view RGB-D via a visual-marker prompting strategy that elicits object-object relations from 2D views; 2) Lift relations into a persistent 3D scene graph encoding spatial and semantic relations; 3) Given a natural language query, construct a query graph and perform constrained alignment with the scene graph to achieve multi-view-consistent grounding; 4) Evaluate on ScanRefer; 5) Deploy on mobile robot.
AI.result: Competitive zero-shot performance on ScanRefer using only RGB-D; successful real-world robotic deployment demonstrating robust spatial reasoning in long-horizon environments; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, interpretable zero-shot 3D grounding is achievable via structured graph matching over a persistent 3D scene graph, with the visual-marker prompting enabling cross-view relational understanding and multi-view consistency.

title: Tabular foundation models for robust calibration of near-infrared chemical sensing data
summary: Near-infrared spectroscopy is increasingly used as a rapid, non-destructive chemical sensing technology for the analysis of food, pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples. However, the practical deployment of NIR sensors still depends on calibration models able to handle high-dimensional, collinear spectra, limited sample sizes, preprocessing dependence, spectral outliers, and extrapolation beyond the calibration domain. Here, we evaluate whether tabular foundation models can provide a new calibration strategy for NIR chemical sensing. We benchmark TabPFN on 66 NIR datasets covering 54 regression and 12 classification tasks, and compare direct inference on raw spectra with preprocessing-optimized inference against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, Catboost, and one-dimensional convolutional neural networks. The study uses a unified validation framework in which preprocessing and model selection are performed exclusively on calibration data before external test evaluation. In regression, preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves the best overall average rank and significantly outperforms PLS, CatBoost, TabPFN on raw spectra, and CNN-1D, while remaining statistically comparable to Ridge. In classification, TabPFN applied directly to raw spectra provides the best average rank, with performance close to the optimized variant. Robustness analyses show that TabPFN provides strong average predictive performance but that its advantage decreases on spectral outliers and extrapolated samples, where classical chemometric models remain competitive. These results suggest that tabular foundation models can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR chemical sensing, especially in small- to medium-sized calibration settings, while highlighting the need for spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies.

AI.tldr: TabPFN-based tabular models can complement chemometrics for NIR calibration: preprocessing-optimized TabPFN excels in regression, raw-spectra TabPFN excels in classification, with robustness to outliers/extrapolation generally strong but diminishing in those regimes.
AI.motivation: NIR spectroscopy faces high-dimensional, collinear spectra, small sample sizes, dependence on preprocessing, spectral outliers, and extrapolation beyond calibration domains. The paper investigates whether tabular foundation models offer a new calibration strategy for NIR chemical sensing.
AI.method: Benchmark TabPFN on 66 NIR datasets (54 regression, 12 classification), comparing direct inference on raw spectra vs preprocessing-optimized inference against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, CatBoost, and CNN-1D. A unified validation framework performs preprocessing and model selection only on calibration data before external test evaluation.
AI.result: Regression: preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves best overall average rank and significantly outperforms PLS, CatBoost, TabPFN on raw spectra, and CNN-1D, while being statistically comparable to Ridge. Classification: TabPFN on raw spectra yields the best average rank, with performance close to the optimized variant. TabPFN shows strong average predictive performance overall, but its advantages diminish on spectral outliers and extrapolated samples, where classical chemometric models remain competitive.
AI.conclusion: TabPFN can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR sensing, especially in small- to medium-sized calibration settings; however, spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies are needed to robustly handle outliers and extrapolation.

title: TacO: Benchmarking Tactile Sensors for Object Manipulation
summary: Vision-based learning from demonstrations has achieved remarkable success in enabling robots to perform manipulation tasks and high-level semantic reasoning, yet it remains insufficient for complex, contact-rich manipulation. While there is broad agreement that tactile sensing improves manipulation, there is no empirical guidance on which tactile sensors are best suited for which manipulation tasks. In this paper, we provide a systematic, task-driven evaluation of tactile sensors for robot manipulation and propose a framework for selecting and evaluating sensors based on manipulation policy performance. Separate manipulation policies are trained for tactile sensors of four distinct modalities: visual, acoustic, magnetic, and resistive, across three tasks: pick-and-place with unknown mass, object reorientation, and plug insertion. For each task, an analysis of how sensor properties such as spatial resolution, shear sensing, and tactile representation, and the inherent material friction affect task performances is done. Rather than tactile sensing being universally beneficial in the same way, our results show that the usefulness of tactile information depends strongly on sensor modality, material properties, and the specific manipulation tasks. All of the tactile sensors, code, data, and hardware setup will be publicly available on the project website.

AI.tldr: A systematic, task-driven evaluation of four tactile sensor modalities (visual, acoustic, magnetic, resistive) across three manipulation tasks, highlighting that tactile usefulness is modality- and task-dependent; the work proposes a framework to select sensors based on policy performance and provides public data/code.
AI.motivation: Empirically determine which tactile sensor modalities are best suited for different manipulation tasks, addressing the lack of guidance on sensor-task compatibility.
AI.method: Train separate manipulation policies for each of four tactile modalities across three tasks: (1) pick-and-place with unknown mass, (2) object reorientation, (3) plug insertion. Analyze how sensor properties (spatial resolution, shear sensing, tactile representation) and material friction influence task performance. Develop a framework for selecting/evaluating sensors based on policy performance; release code, data, and hardware setup.
AI.result: Findings indicate that the usefulness of tactile information is not universal and depends strongly on sensor modality, material properties, and the specific manipulation task.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a task-driven framework for sensor selection and a comparative evaluation across modalities, guiding practitioners in choosing tactile sensors; all materials will be publicly available on the project site.

title: Trace2Skill: Verifier-Guided Skill Evolution for Long-Context EDA Agents
summary: Complex Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) challenge hardware LLM agents because solving them requires localizing verifier-relevant RTL, testbenches, include paths, and build dependencies inside large repository snapshots, making precise edits, and recovering from sparse hidden-verifier failures. We present Trace2Skill, a test-time scaling framework that improves a hardware agent without RTL-specialized model fine-tuning. Rather than training a new model or only sampling more candidate solutions, Trace2Skill treats the agent's natural-language skill as an evolvable policy. It mines repeated rollout traces for success and failure modes, converts them into dense diagnostics and oracle lessons, and uses an oracle, mutator, and selector loop to produce task-specific skills that guide later search, editing, validation, and recovery. Because final pass/fail labels are often too coarse for hard failures, Trace2Skill also supports bounded runtime dense verifier feedback that returns sanitized functional observations while keeping hidden harnesses and reference solutions inaccessible to the agent. This feedback helps guide skill evolution and agent execution by connecting skill text, verifier evidence, and downstream behavior. Across hard CVDP tasks that defeat the seed CVDP agent, including tasks that also defeat frontier coding agents, Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves task pass rates and produces breakthrough passes on previously unsolved tasks, without requiring high-quality fine-tuning data, specialized RTL model training, or model weight updates. The same framework provides a general test-time scaling strategy that can extend beyond digital design to other verifiable EDA tasks.

AI.tldr: Trace2Skill is a test-time scaling framework that evolves an LLM-based hardware agent’s NL skill using rollout traces and dense verifier feedback, without RTL fine-tuning, to improve performance on hard CVDP tasks and even solve previously unsolved cases.
AI.motivation: CVDP tasks demand locating verifier-relevant RTL, testbenches, include paths, and build dependencies within large repositories. Existing approaches rely on fine-tuning or brute-force sampling, which is inefficient and brittle in the presence of sparse verifier feedback.
AI.method: Compute and mine repeated rollout traces of agent behavior to identify success/failure modes. Convert traces into dense diagnostics and oracle lessons. Apply an oracle-mutator-selector loop to evolve task-specific NL skills that guide search, edits, validation, and recovery. Use bounded-runtime dense verifier feedback to obtain sanitized functional observations, preserving hidden harnesses. No RTL-intensive fine-tuning or model weight updates are performed; the policy evolves at test time.
AI.result: Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves pass rates on hard CVDP tasks, achieving breakthrough passes on tasks previously unsolved and outperforming seed and frontier coding agents without fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a general, non-finetuning, test-time scaling strategy for verifiable EDA tasks, extendable beyond digital design to other verifiable domains.

title: MM-Conv: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Context-Aware Grounding in 3D Dialogue
comment: Extended version of the paper published at LREC 2026 (Palma de Mallorca, Spain), with expanded VLM baselines and inter-annotator agreement analysis
summary: Grounding language in the physical world requires AI systems to interpret references that emerge dynamically during conversation. While current vision-language models (VLMs) excel at static image tasks, they struggle to resolve ambiguous expressions in spontaneous, multi-turn dialogue. We address this gap by introducing (1) a benchmark for referential communication in dynamic 3D environments, built from 6.7 hours of egocentric VR interaction with synchronized speech, motion, gaze, and 3D scene geometry, and (2) a two-stage grounding pipeline that explicitly resolves conversational ambiguity before visual localization. The benchmark includes over 4,200 manually verified referring expressions spanning full, partitive, and pronominal types. Our contextual rewriting approach improves grounding performance by 11-22 percentage points on average, with a pure detector (GroundingDINO) reaching 56.7% on pronominals after rewriting, nearly double the best end-to-end baseline. Results demonstrate that decoupling linguistic reasoning from visual perception is more effective than end-to-end approaches for conversational grounding.

AI.tldr: A dynamic 3D referential-grounding benchmark and a two-stage grounding pipeline with contextual rewriting; decoupling linguistic reasoning from perception yields significant gains (11-22 pp) and 56.7% pronoun grounding with GroundingDINO, approaching double the best end-to-end baseline.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models excel on static images but struggle with referential ambiguity and dynamic, multi-turn dialogue in 3D environments. A benchmark and methods that separate language reasoning from visual perception are needed to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Constructed a benchmark from 6.7 hours of egocentric VR data with synchronized speech, motion, gaze, and 3D scene geometry. Proposed a two-stage grounding pipeline that explicitly resolves conversational ambiguity before visual localization via a contextual rewriting module. Evaluated on over 4,200 manually verified referring expressions across full, partitive, and pronominal types. Used GroundingDINO as the detector after rewriting.
AI.result: Contextual rewriting yields 11–22 percentage point gains in grounding accuracy on average. GroundingDINO achieves 56.7% accuracy on pronominal expressions after rewriting, nearly twice the best end-to-end baseline.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling linguistic reasoning from visual perception is more effective for conversational grounding; the benchmark provides a valuable resource for dynamic 3D referential grounding and the two-stage approach offers strong improvements, especially for pronouns.

title: Industrial Dual-Arm Box Handling via Online Inertial Estimation and Convex Wrench Optimization
comment: 14 pages, submitted to Robotics and Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (RCIM) Journal
summary: Industrial robotic object handling often involves boxes and packages whose mass and center of mass are not known in advance. These uncertainties affect the force--moment balance required for stable lifting, and improper regulation of contact wrenches can lead to slip, object drop, orientation deviation, or excessive squeezing. This paper presents a friction-aware dual-arm box-handling framework for objects with unknown inertial properties. The proposed approach estimates the object mass and center of mass online from measured contact wrenches, and computes friction-feasible contact forces and torsional moments through a second-order cone program (SOCP) under ellipsoidal friction-limit-surface constraints. An offline trajectory refinement stage is also included to reduce undesired object--environment contact when geometric constraints are present. By enforcing friction feasibility as a hard constraint and minimizing contact effort within the feasible region, the framework achieves stable lifting without treating slip avoidance and excessive squeezing as separately tuned objectives. Experiments on a real dual-arm robotic system under different center-of-mass configurations demonstrate that the method lifts objects with unknown inertial properties while maintaining stable frictional contact.

AI.tldr: A friction-aware dual-arm framework estimates object mass and CoM online from contact wrenches, computes friction-feasible contact forces/torques via an SOCP under ellipsoidal friction limits, and includes offline trajectory refinement; validated on real hardware for unknown-inertia box handling.
AI.motivation: Industrial box handling with unknown mass distribution causes unstable lifting due to uncertain force–moment balance; there is a need to enforce friction feasibility and avoid slip, drop, and excessive squeezing without separate tuning.
AI.method: Online estimation of object mass and center of mass from measured contact wrenches. Compute friction-feasible contact forces and torsional moments by solving a second-order cone program (SOCP) constrained by ellipsoidal friction-limit surfaces. Include an offline trajectory refinement stage to reduce undesired object–environment contact when geometry imposes constraints. Enforce friction feasibility as a hard constraint and minimize contact effort within the feasible region.
AI.result: Experiments on a real dual-arm robotic system across different center-of-mass configurations show successful lifting of objects with unknown inertial properties while maintaining stable frictional contact.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing friction feasibility as a hard constraint within an SOCP-based planner, together with online inertia estimation and trajectory refinement, yields stable lifting for unknown inertial properties without requiring separate tuning of slip-avoidance or squeezing objectives.

title: Implicit Safety Alignment from Crowd Preferences
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) can reveal implicit objectives such as safety considerations that go beyond task completion. In this work, we focus on the common safety criteria embedded in crowd preference datasets, where different users may express distinct preferences or objectives, yet follow similar safety principles. Our aim is to discover shared safety criteria from crowd preferences and then transfer them to downstream RL tasks to regularize agent behavior and enforce safety. We first show that direct reward combination-optimizing a preference-learned reward model together with downstream task rewards-has inherent limitations. Motivated by this, we propose Safe Crowd Preference-based RL, a hierarchical framework that extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences and composes them via a high-level policy to safely solve downstream tasks. Experiments across safe RL environments and a preliminary LLM-style task with diverse user goals and shared safety constraints demonstrate that our approach substantially lowers safety costs without access to explicit safety rewards, while achieving task performance comparable to oracle methods trained with ground-truth safety signals.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Safe Crowd Preference-based RL extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences to regularize behavior, reducing safety costs without explicit safety rewards while matching oracle-safe performance.
AI.motivation: RLHF can reveal implicit safety objectives; crowd preferences reflect shared safety principles even with diverse goals, but direct reward shaping can be limited and brittle.
AI.method: Introduce Safe Crowd Preference-based RL: a hierarchical framework that learns safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences and composes them via a high-level policy to solve downstream tasks safely. This approach decouples safety from task rewards rather than directly combining them.
AI.result: Empirical results across safe RL environments and a preliminary LLM-style task show reduced safety costs and task performance comparable to oracle methods trained with ground-truth safety signals, without needing explicit safety rewards.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that safety criteria embedded in crowd preferences can be extracted and transferred to downstream RL tasks, enabling safer, competitive behavior without explicit safety rewards.

title: Seizure-Semiology-Suite (S3): A Clinically Multimodal Dataset, Benchmark, and Models for Seizure Semiology Understanding
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 as a Spotlight presentation
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in general video understanding, their capacity to interpret involuntary, and spatio-temporally evolving pathologic motor behaviors such as seizure semiology remains largely untested. To address this gap, we introduce Seizure-Semiology-Suite, a clinically grounded dataset and benchmark for fine-grained, structured seizure semiology understanding. The dataset includes 438 seizure videos annotated with over 35,000 dense labels covering 20 ILAE-defined semiological features. Building on this dataset, we propose a seven-task hierarchical benchmark that systematically evaluates MLLMs from low-level visual perception to temporal sequencing, narrative report generation, and seizure diagnosis. To enable clinically meaningful evaluation of generated reports, we further introduce the Report Quality Index for Seizure Semiology (Seizure-RQI). Extensive baselines across 11 open-weight MLLMs reveal systematic weaknesses in laterality reasoning, temporal localization, symptom sequencing, and clinically faithful reporting. We show that seizure-specific fine-tuning substantially improves performance across tasks, and that a two-stage neuro-symbolic framework achieves an F1 score of 0.96 on epileptic versus non-epileptic seizure classification. Seizure-Semiology-Suite establishes a rigorous benchmark for evaluating multimodal models in safety-critical medical video understanding and guides the development of clinically reliable, domain-adaptive multimodal intelligence.

AI.tldr: A new dataset and benchmark for multimodal seizure semiology understanding, Seizure-Semiology-Suite, with 438 videos, 35k dense labels over 20 ILAE features, a 7-task hierarchy, and Seizure-RQI for report quality; baselines on 11 MLLMs reveal systematic weaknesses; fine-tuning and a two-stage neuro-symbolic approach yield strong epileptic vs non-epileptic classification (F1=0.96).
AI.motivation: Address the lack of clinically grounded evaluation of multimodal models on involuntary, spatio-temporally evolving pathologic motor behaviors (seizure semiology) and establish a safety-critical benchmark for medical video understanding.
AI.method: Construct a clinically grounded video dataset (Seizure-Semiology-Suite) with 438 seizures annotated with 35k dense labels across 20 ILAE-defined semiological features; define a seven-task hierarchical benchmark spanning perception, temporal localization, sequencing, report generation, and diagnosis; introduce Seizure-RQI for report quality evaluation; evaluate 11 open-weight MLLMs; perform seizure-specific fine-tuning; develop a two-stage neuro-symbolic framework for epileptic vs non-epileptic seizure classification.
AI.result: Identified systematic weaknesses in laterality reasoning, temporal localization, sequencing, and clinically faithful reporting across baseline MLLMs; seizure-specific fine-tuning improves performance; two-stage neuro-symbolic framework achieves F1=0.96 on epileptic vs non-epileptic classification.
AI.conclusion: Seizure-Semiology-Suite provides a rigorous, clinically grounded benchmark for evaluating multimodal models in safety-critical medical video understanding and guides development of domain-adaptive, clinically faithful multimodal AI.

title: Expectation Consistency Loss: Rethink Confidence Calibration under Covariate Shift
summary: Confidence calibration for classification models is vital in safety-critical decision-making scenarios and has received extensive attention. General confidence calibration methods assume training and test data are independent and identically distributed, limiting their effectiveness under covariate shifts. Previous calibration methods under covariate shift struggle with class-wise or canonical calibrations and often rely on unstable importance weighting when density ratios are large or unbounded. Given the above limitations, this paper rethinks confidence calibration under covariate shifts. First, we derive a necessary and sufficient condition for confidence calibration under covariate shifts, named Expectation consistency condition, which reveals covariate shifts do not necessarily lead to uncalibrated confidence and provides a weaker condition for confidence calibration than global covariate distribution alignment. Then, utilizing Expectation consistency condition, this paper proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation loss to calibrate confidence of the target domain, named Expectation consistency loss (ECL), which is compatible with canonical calibration, class-wise calibration, and top-label calibration. Third, we prove that computing ECL loss has the same sample complexity as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and provide a theoretically grounded mini-batch trainable scheme for ECL loss. Finally, we validate the effectiveness of our method on both simulated and real-world covariate shift datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces an Expectation Consistency framework for confidence calibration under covariate shift, with an unsupervised domain adaptation loss (ECL) that supports canonical, class-wise, and top-label calibration; proves ECL has same sample complexity as ECE and validates on simulated and real data.
AI.motivation: Calibration under covariate shift is misaligned with standard i.i.d. assumptions; existing methods rely on unstable importance weighting and dense density ratio estimation, and may fail for class-wise or canonical calibrations. A weaker, verifiable condition and an unsupervised adaptation loss can enable reliable calibration without labeled target-domain data.
AI.method: Derives a necessary and sufficient Expectation Consistency Condition for calibration under covariate shifts; proposes ECL loss for unsupervised domain adaptation to calibrate target-domain confidence; ensures compatibility with canonical, class-wise, and top-label calibration; provides a mini-batch trainable scheme and analyzes sample complexity.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness of the proposed approach on both simulated and real-world covariate-shift datasets, achieving calibrated confidence in the presence of covariate shift.
AI.conclusion: The Expectation Consistency framework enables robust confidence calibration under covariate shift; ECL offers a theoretically grounded, trainable, and calibration-compatible loss with practical efficacy and matching sample complexity to ECE.

title: CoRMA: Contrastive RMA for Contact-Rich Meta-Adaptation
summary: We present CoRMA(Contrastive Robotic Motor Adaptation), a context-based meta-adaptation framework that modifies RMA for force-dominant assembly. CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D simulator-only semantic contact context describing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. A deployable causal Transformer adapter infers this context online from force, proprioceptive, and action histories using semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, oracle context is removed and replaced by the inferred context, enabling within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates. We evaluate CoRMA on PegInsert, GearMesh, and NutThread in Isaac Lab / Isaac Sim~5.0 and on a real Marvin arm. Compared with FORGE baselines that achieve high simulation success but degrade substantially on hardware, CoRMA retains higher verified real success under controlled target-pose noise. These results support semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family, while broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain future work.

AI.tldr: CoRMA introduces a compact 6D semantic contact context and a deployable Transformer-based adapter to enable within-episode, demonstration-free adaptation for force-dominant assembly tasks, improving real-world success compared to FORGE baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the sim-to-real gap in force-dominant assembly by replacing brittle simulator-parameter adaptation with a reusable, context-driven adaptation interface that can be inferred online from sensor histories.
AI.method: Define a 6D simulator-only semantic contact context capturing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. Use a deployable causal Transformer adapter to infer this context online from force, proprioception, and action histories via semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, replace the oracle context with the inferred context to enable within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates. Evaluate on PegInsert, GearMesh, NutThread in Isaac Lab/Sim 5.0 and a real Marvin arm, comparing to FORGE baselines.
AI.result: CoRMA maintains higher verified real success under controlled target-pose noise, reducing simulation-to-real degradation seen with FORGE baselines. Supports semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family; Real2Sim calibration and broad unseen-task generalization remain as future work.
AI.conclusion: Semantic contact inference via a compact 6D context and online Transformer-based estimation enables robust, demonstration-free adaptation for related assembly tasks, offering improved real-world performance with clear avenues for broader generalization and calibration in future work.

title: Toward AI VIS Co-Scientists: A General and End-to-End Agent Harness for Solving Complex Data Visualization Tasks
summary: The ability to inspect, interpret, and communicate complex data is crucial for virtually any scientific endeavor, but often requires significant expertise outside the core domain ranging from data management and analysis to visualization design and implementation. We present an end-to-end agentic harness that, based on only the data and a high level description of the tasks, independently designs custom visual analysis applications (VIS apps). This represents an important step towards a general AI co-scientist envisioned by many as an autonomous system that can autonomously execute long horizon tasks based on high-level directions. Our proposed VIS co-scientist is an essential component of this broader AI co-scientist vision: a harness that can autonomously analyze data and design visualization solutions using a collection of agents and specialized skills that coordinate exploratory analysis, plan, configure the environment, implement, validate the interface, and most importantly evaluate the overall task completion. Each stage produces document and instruction artifacts that guide downstream work and enable iterative refinement. We validate this approach on IEEE SciVis Contests spanning multiple science and engineering fields. These contests serve as ideal proving grounds because they encode real-world complexity: ambiguous requirements, diverse data modalities, design trade-offs, and task-driven validation. Given only the data and target tasks, our system autonomously produces functional single-page VIS Apps with verified linked-view behavior, highly customized to domain experts' specified tasks and needs.

AI.tldr: An autonomous end-to-end harness that designs and validates single-page visualization apps (VIS apps) from data and high-level tasks using a multi-agent system; demonstrated on IEEE SciVis Contests.
AI.motivation: To reduce dependence on domain-specific visualization expertise and enable end-to-end data-to-visualization pipelines, moving toward an autonomous AI co-scientist capable of long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: A coordinated ensemble of agents for exploratory analysis, planning, environment configuration, implementation, interface validation, and task evaluation. Each stage emits documents/instructions to guide downstream work, enabling iterative refinement and producing functional VIS apps with linked-view behavior.
AI.result: Validated on IEEE SciVis Contests across multiple science and engineering domains; autonomously produces domain-tailored, functional single-page VIS apps with verified linked-view behavior.
AI.conclusion: The VIS co-scientist harness is a foundational step toward a general AI co-scientist, enabling autonomous data analysis and visualization design with iterative refinement in real-world, complex scenarios.

title: CrossVLA: Cross-Paradigm Post-Training and Inference Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Workshop draft, 14 pages, 4 figures. Code, ckpts, data: https://github.com/lz-googlefycy/vla-lab
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have rapidly converged on a small set of architectural patterns: discrete-token autoregression (e.g. OpenVLA) and continuous-action flow-matching (e.g. pi-0.5). Yet preference alignment via Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) -- the de-facto post-training step in language models -- has been studied almost exclusively on autoregressive VLAs. We present CrossVLA, an empirical study of cross-paradigm VLA post-training. Three contributions: (i) a surrogate flow-matching log-probability estimator that lets DPO operate on continuous-action backbones without probability-flow ODE integration; (ii) a head-to-head comparison of LoRA and DoRA as the parameter-efficient layer for VLA DPO, finding DoRA improves over OpenVLA SFT by a mean +10.4 pp across LIBERO 4-suite (600 trials, 3 seeds) -- per-suite +20.0 Object, +11.0 Long-horizon, +8.0 Goal, +2.7 Spatial -- with zero seed variance on Object (38/50 on each of 3 seeds); (iii) an inference-time anatomy showing the denoise loop dominates 78.6% of sample_actions latency and prefix-K/V caching a la VLA-Cache caps at a 21% acceleration ceiling -- both chunk-level and token-level cache strategies degrade success rate to 0-80% in our benchmarks. We further pretrain a multi-view + temporal projection head on 6000 LIBERO frames, achieving 99.5% k-NN recall@1 for same-task retrieval (36x over random), available as a downstream initialisation. All code, ckpts, training logs, and reproduction scripts are open at https://github.com/lz-googlefycy/vla-lab.

AI.tldr: CrossVLA studies post-training for cross-paradigm Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, introducing a surrogate log-prob estimator for continuous-action backbones, comparing LoRA and DoRA layers for DPO, analyzing inference-time bottlenecks and caching, and pretraining a retrieval head; results show DoRA beats OpenVLA SFT by ~10.4 percentage points on LIBERO, with notable latency and caching trade-offs, plus strong retrieval initialization.
AI.motivation: VLA models fall into discrete-token autoregression or continuous-action flow-matching. DPO has been explored mainly for autoregressive models; it is unknown how cross-paradigm post-training performs and what practical bottlenecks and gains exist when applying DPO beyond autoregressive settings.
AI.method: 1) Develop a surrogate flow-matching log-probability estimator enabling DPO on continuous-action backbones without probability-flow ODE integration. 2) Conduct a head-to-head comparison of LoRA vs DoRA as parameter-efficient layers for VLA DPO across the LIBERO 4-suite (600 trials, 3 seeds). 3) Analyze inference-time anatomy to quantify denoise loop latency and the impact of VLA-Cache prefix-K/V caching on acceleration (and its limits). 4) Pretrain a multi-view + temporal projection head on 6000 LIBERO frames for downstream initialization.
AI.result: DoRA improves over OpenVLA SFT by a mean +10.4 percentage points across LIBERO 4-suite (600 trials, 3 seeds); breakdown: +20.0 on Object, +11.0 on Long-horizon, +8.0 on Goal, +2.7 on Spatial; Object shows zero seed variance (38/50 across seeds). Inference: denoise loop accounts for 78.6% of sample_actions latency; VLA-Cache yields at most 21% acceleration and can degrade success rate to 0–80% depending on chunk‑level and token‑level caching. A pretraining head on 6000 LIBERO frames achieves 99.5% k-NN recall@1 for same-task retrieval (36x over random).
AI.conclusion: CrossVLA provides practical insights into cross-paradigm VLA DPO. DoRA is a more effective, parameter-efficient choice than LoRA/OpenVLA SFT for DPO on continuous-action backbones. Inference bottlenecks are dominated by the denoise loop, and caching offers limited acceleration with potential performance degradation. The multi-view temporal head offers strong downstream initialization for retrieval tasks. All code and checkpoints are open for reproduction.

title: Beyond Pixels: Learning Invariant Rewards for Real-World Robotics From a Few Demonstrations
summary: Designing reward functions that generalize beyond controlled laboratory settings remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning for robotics. In open-world manipulation problems, a single task can appear in numerous variants through different object instances, positions, and camera viewpoints. Recent vision-based reward models tend to memorize specific pixel distributions and fail to generalize beyond their training conditions. To address this, we propose a framework that learns invariant symbolic reward functions from as few as five demonstrations. The insight is to shift from visual feature-fitting to the discovery of behavioral invariants: task-level properties that remain constant across diverse visual instantiations. The framework has two coupled components: a structural reward formulation that encodes task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance, and a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills these invariants from demonstrations without online interaction. Experiments on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking abilities compared to baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world out-of-distribution experiments further show that the same learned reward generalizes zero-shot to position, viewpoint, and object variations, enabling a single reward representation to be reused across diverse task variants in practice.

AI.tldr: Proposes a framework to learn invariant symbolic reward functions for open-world robotics tasks from few demonstrations, generalizing across object instances, positions, and viewpoints by focusing on behavioral invariants rather than visual features.
AI.motivation: Vision-based reward models often memorize pixel distributions and fail to generalize to new object instances, positions, or viewpoints; there is a need for reward representations that capture task-level invariants applicable across diverse visual instantiations in open-world manipulation.
AI.method: Two coupled components: (1) a structural reward formulation that encodes task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance; (2) a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills these invariants from demonstrations without online interaction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks shows stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking versus baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world out-of-distribution experiments demonstrate zero-shot generalization of the learned reward to variations in position, viewpoint, and objects, enabling reuse of a single reward across diverse task variants.
AI.conclusion: Invariant, symbolic reward representations enable cross-variant generalization in open-world robotics and facilitate rapid policy learning by reusing a single reward across task variants.

title: FLUID: From Ephemeral IDs to Multimodal Semantic Codes for Industrial-Scale Livestreaming Recommendation
summary: Modern recommender systems rely heavily on ID-based collaborative filtering: each item is represented by a unique ID embedding that accumulates collaborative signals from user interactions. Livestreaming recommendation, however, faces a unique challenge in this paradigm: a live room typically broadcasts for only tens of minutes, so its item ID remains poorly learned in a persistent cold-start state and ID-centric ranking models fail to generalize. We present FLUID, the first framework to fully retire the candidate-side item ID from a production-scale livestreaming ranker. FLUID couples a cross-domain multimodal encoder, jointly trained on short videos and livestreams to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID), with a late-fusion, ID-free design that injects slice-level and room-level LUCID as independent tokens, stabilized by a staged warmup under online incremental training. Deployed on our industrial livestreaming recommenders with a cross-platform combined user base of over one billion globally, FLUID delivers significant online gains of +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, and +0.05% Active Hours.

AI.tldr: Introduces FLUID, an ID-free livestream recommender that uses a cross-domain multimodal encoder to create discrete codes (LUCID) and late-fusion tokens, enabling better cold-start handling and improving online metrics.
AI.motivation: ID-based collaborative filtering struggles in livestreaming due to short session lengths causing poor item-ID learning and cold-start problems; need production-scale ID-free ranking.
AI.method: Train a cross-domain multimodal encoder on short videos and livestreams to produce hierarchical discrete codes (LUCID); integrate as independent tokens via late fusion with staged warmup; online incremental training; deployment in industry-scale recommender.
AI.result: Online gains: +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, +0.05% Active Hours; scalable across billion-user platform.
AI.conclusion: ID-free design with LUCID codes and late-fusion tokens provides robust cold-start generalization and performance gains in production livestreaming recommender systems; demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness at scale.

title: Learning Emergent Modular Representations in Multi-modality Medical Vision Foundation Models
summary: Multi-modality medical vision (MV) foundation models (FM) are fundamentally challenged by pronounced Non-IID feature statistics across heterogeneous imaging modalities. Monolithic self-supervised optimization on such data induces conflicting gradients, driving representations to collapse toward modality-dominant shortcuts. This work reframes this failure as an imbalance between specialization and coordination in emergent modularity, and proposes Director-Experts (DEX), a modular network that explicitly regulates these dynamics in stacked modules. Each DEX module comprises a pool of experts, dynamically adapted by our image-wise activation strategy, autonomously specializing in modality-dominant statistics, together with a director, updated via our group exponential moving average, which distills multi-expert knowledge into a shared space for semantic integration across modalities, thus driving the emergence of modular representations. We curate a new benchmark, Medical Vision Universe, over 4 million images across 10 modalities, which provides a FM-level pre-training with the broadest coverage of distinct imaging modalities to our DEX. Extensive evaluations on 26 downstream tasks demonstrate improved optimization behavior and transferability, indicating DEX as a principled step toward general-purpose multi-modality medical AI. Our code and dataset will be opened at https://github.com/YutingHe-list/DEX.

AI.tldr: DEX: a modular Director-Experts network to handle non-IID multi-modality medical data, using image-wise expert activation and a group EMA director; introduces Medical Vision Universe benchmark; shows improved optimization and transfer across 26 tasks; code forthcoming.
AI.motivation: Non-IID feature statistics across heterogeneous imaging modalities cause conflicting gradients and modality-dominant shortcuts when using monolithic self-supervised learning; need a balance between specialization and coordination via modular design.
AI.method: Stacked DEX modules, each with a pool of experts and an image-wise activation mechanism that dynamically selects experts. Experts specialize in modality-dominant statistics. A director, updated via group exponential moving average, distills multi-expert knowledge into a shared semantic space, enabling cross-modality integration and emergent modular representations.
AI.result: Introduction of Medical Vision Universe benchmark: 4 million images across 10 modalities for FM-level pre-training. Evaluations on 26 downstream tasks show improved optimization behavior and transferability.
AI.conclusion: DEX demonstrates modular emergence that improves cross-modality transfer and is a principled step toward general-purpose multi-modality medical AI; code and dataset will be released.

title: Beyond Single Slot: Joint Optimization for Multi-Slot Guaranteed Display Advertising
summary: Guaranteed display advertising is crucial for platform monetization, yet existing methods often operate under a single-slot assumption, limiting their ability to optimize allocation across multi-slot page views. In this paper, we propose a novel joint optimization framework for multi-slot GD allocation, addressing key challenges such as slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration. Our approach formulates the allocation as an offline bipartite matching problem with a contract roulette mechanism for slot exclusivity and Page View constraints for impression control, and incorporates a scalable allocation optimization algorithm for efficient large-scale deployment. Extensive online tests on the Meituan advertising platform demonstrate that our method significantly improves merchant ROI, platform revenue efficiency, and contract fulfillment robustness. Specifically, online A/B tests show a 28.99% increase in Average Revenue Per User under 70% traffic, and DID analysis further indicates improved contract stability, demonstrating the strong applicability and effectiveness of our framework in real-world advertising deployments.

AI.tldr: Jointly optimize multi-slot guaranteed display (GD) allocation using a bipartite matching formulation with a contract roulette for slot exclusivity and Page View (PV) constraints, plus a scalable optimization algorithm. Demonstrates online gains in merchant ROI and platform revenue, with improved contract stability on Meituan's ad platform.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of single-slot GD allocation by enabling cross-slot optimization, mitigating slot redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration to improve overall monetization and contract fulfillment.
AI.method: Model the problem as an offline bipartite matching with a contract roulette mechanism to enforce slot exclusivity, and PV constraints to control impressions. Propose a scalable allocation optimization algorithm suitable for large-scale deployment and real-time or near-real-time operation.
AI.result: Online experiments (A/B on Meituan) show a 28.99% lift in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) at 70% traffic. Difference-in-differences (DID) analysis indicates improved contract stability and robustness in fulfillment, alongside higher merchant ROI and platform revenue efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a practical, scalable solution for multi-slot GD allocation, with empirically validated performance and robust contract fulfillment in real-world deployment.

title: Action with Visual Primitives
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist robotic manipulation. A common design in current architectures maps language instructions and visual observations to actions in a single forward pass. While conceptually simple, this formulation entangles instruction comprehension, spatial scene understanding, and motor control within a single learning objective. As a result, the action expert must implicitly relearn cognitive and perceptual capabilities already present in the pretrained VLM, which can limit both learning efficiency and generalization. We introduce AVP (Action with Visual Primitives), an end-to-end architecture that implements this visual-primitive-centric interface: the VLM infers the next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert, with supervision derived from end-effector kinematics. Real-robot experiments on general pick-and-place tasks show that AVP improves the success rate by 27.61% over pi_0.5 and outperforms other recent methods, with consistent gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

AI.tldr: AVP proposes a visual-primitives interface where a VLM outputs tokens that condition a flow-based action expert, improving data efficiency and generalization in real-robot pick-and-place tasks, achieving a 27.61% higher success rate over a baseline (pi_0.5).
AI.motivation: Current VLA architectures entangle perception, instruction, and motor control, forcing the action module to relearn perception/cognition embedded in the VLM. This hampers learning efficiency and generalization to new object configurations and domains.
AI.method: Introduce AVP: an end-to-end visual-primitive-centric architecture. The VLM outputs next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens, which condition a flow-matching action expert. Supervision comes from end-effector kinematics; evaluated on real robots for general pick-and-place tasks.
AI.result: Real-robot experiments show a 27.61% improvement over pi_0.5 baseline; AVP outperforms other recent methods with gains in data efficiency, spatial compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling perception via visual primitives and a dedicated flow-based action module yields more efficient learning and better generalization for VLA-based robotics, enabling more robust real-world performance.

title: Planning in the LLM Era: Building for Reliability and Efficiency
summary: Growing attention to intelligent agents has put a spotlight on one of their central capabilities: planning. Early attempts to leverage large language models (LLMs) for planning relied on single-shot plan generation, followed by hybrid approaches that coupled LLMs with limited external search. These methods, unsound and incomplete by their very nature, often require substantial resources without yielding better solutions on unseen problems. As the limitations of LLMs become clearer, recent work has shifted toward using them at solution construction time -- generating symbolic solvers for a family of problems that can be verified and then used efficiently at inference time. This trend reflects the growing need for agents that are both reliable and resource-efficient. It also offers a path towards generating maintainable planners with minimal dependence on language models at inference time. In this paper, we argue that this shift reflects a broader realignment of the planning field in the LLM era. We examine three major categories of planner-generation methods, discuss their current limitations, and outline research steps towards a more reliable and efficient LLM-based generation of planners.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys how planner generation is evolving in the LLM era—from single-shot plans to symbolic solver generation—and outlines a research agenda across three categories to build reliable, efficient planners.
AI.motivation: To address LLM limitations (unsoundness, incompleteness, high resource use) and to achieve reliable, maintainable planners with low inference-time dependency on LLMs.
AI.method: Conceptual survey: (i) categorize three families of planner-generation methods; (ii) critique their limitations; (iii) propose future research steps toward more reliable and efficient generation of planners.
AI.result: Framework/analysis and roadmap rather than empirical results; provides a taxonomy, identified limitations, and suggested steps for future work.
AI.conclusion: The LLM era is realigning planning; success depends on generating verifiable, efficient planners with minimal LLM reliance at inference, enabling maintainable, scalable agents.

title: Two-Stage Multimodal Framework for Emotion Mimicry Intensity Prediction
comment: 10th Affective & Behavior Analysis in-the-wild, CVPR Workshop 2026
summary: We present our submission to the Hume-ABAW10 Emotional Mimicry Intensity (EMI) Challenge, which aims to predict six continuous emotion intensity dimensions: Admiration, Amusement, Determination, Empathic Pain, Excitement, and Joy, from in-the-wild multimodal video clips. We propose a staged multimodal framework that combines textual, acoustic, and visual representations, with an optional motion branch. Our approach first trains modality-specific encoders independently and then fuses their learned representations through a lightweight regressor with modality dropout and controlled encoder adaptation. Across our submitted systems, the best validation performance is obtained by the text--audio--vision--motion fusion model under the expanded 4:1 split, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.4722. Although the motion branch yields only very slight gains, its behavior can be interesting to study. Our team was placed third in the EMI challenge, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.57 for the test set. Overall, we provide a practical and reproducible baseline for EMI prediction.

AI.tldr: A staged multimodal framework for EMI prediction from in-the-wild video, combining text, audio, visual (and optionally motion) representations; best validation fusion (text+audio+vision+motion) on a 4:1 split yields avg Pearson 0.4722; test set de-liver 0.57; third place; provides a practical, reproducible EMI baseline.
AI.motivation: Address the EMI challenge by predicting six continuous emotion-intensity dimensions from unconstrained multimodal video, leveraging complementary signals across text, audio, and visual modalities and assessing the utility of a motion branch.
AI.method: Train modality-specific encoders independently, then fuse learned representations with a lightweight regressor using modality dropout and controlled encoder adaptation; evaluate variants including text–audio–vision–motion fusion; use an expanded 4:1 split for validation; optional motion branch.
AI.result: Best validation performance: average Pearson correlation 0.4722 for the text–audio–vision–motion fusion under the expanded 4:1 split; motion branch provides only minor gains; team placed third in EMI with test-set Pearson correlation 0.57; provides a practical, reproducible EMI baseline.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal fusion with staged training yields competitive EMI predictions; text dominates performance, motion offers limited gains but can reveal interesting behavior; establishes a solid baseline for EMI prediction in unconstrained settings.

title: From Parameters to Data: A Task-Parameter-Guided Fine-Tuning Pipeline for Efficient LLM Alignment
summary: Adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to specialized domains typically incurs high data and computational overhead. While prior efficiency efforts have largely treated data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning as isolated processes, our empirical analysis suggests they may be intrinsically coupled. We posit the Strong Map Hypothesis: a sparse subset of attention heads plays a dominant role in task-specific adaptation, acting as keys that unlock specific data patterns. Building on this observation, we propose From Parameters to Data (P2D), a unified framework that leverages these task-sensitive attention heads as a dual compass for both sample mining and structural pruning. To rigorously quantify the total pipeline cost, we introduce the Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) metric for both selection latency and training time. Mechanistically, P2D identifies critical heads via a lightweight proxy and uses them as a functional filter to curate high-affinity data, establishing a synergistic pipeline. Empirically, by updating merely 10% of attention heads on 10% of the data, P2D achieves an 8.3 pp performance gain over strong baselines and delivers a 7.0x end-to-end time speedup. These results validate that precise parameter-data synchronization eliminates redundancy, offering a new paradigm for efficient alignment.

AI.tldr: P2D: A unified approach that uses task-sensitive attention heads as a dual compass for data selection and structural pruning to efficiently adapt LLMs; introduces Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) and achieves significant speedups with minimal parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency of domain adaptation by coupling data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning; sparse heads may govern task-specific learning.
AI.method: Identify critical heads with a lightweight proxy; use these heads to filter high-affinity data; update only 10% of heads on 10% of data; compute AER for total pipeline cost; demonstrate end-to-end speedups.
AI.result: 8.3 percentage-point gain over strong baselines; 7.0x end-to-end training/inference speedup; data-efficiency improvements with minimal parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: Illustrates strong parameter-data synchronization as a path to efficient alignment and a new paradigm for domain adaptation of LLMs.

title: Learning A Unified Risk Map for Autonomous Driving in Partially Observable Environments
comment: Published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
summary: Occlusion-aware prediction remains a critical challenge in autonomous driving due to the inherent uncertainty of unobserved regions. Existing approaches either overestimate risk based on reachable states or struggle to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion uncertainty. To address these limitations, we propose a unified risk map modeling and learning framework for partially observable environments. Our method integrates traffic flow risk and collision risk through spatiotemporal modeling, enabling fine-grained assessment of occlusion-induced hazards. To address the scarcity of scenarios involving occluded interactions, we introduce a diffusion-based scenario generation framework that produces realistic yet adversarial scenarios. We integrate the modeling and learning of a unified risk map into a framework that supports risk-aware planning under partial observability. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset show that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baseline, improving minimum time-to-collision by 0.78 times and average time-to-collision by 1.67 times. The proposed framework offers a comprehensive and practical solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable environments.

AI.tldr: A unified risk map framework for partially observable driving, combining traffic-flow and collision risk via spatiotemporal modeling, plus a diffusion-based generator for realistic occluded scenarios; shows large gains in risk-aware planning on Waymo data.
AI.motivation: Occlusion-induced uncertainty leads to misestimated risk and unreliable trajectories. Existing methods either overestimate risk from reachable states or underperform under high occlusion; a cohesive risk map and robust scenario generation are needed.
AI.method: Develop a unified risk map that fuses traffic-flow risk and collision risk with spatiotemporal modeling. Introduce a diffusion-based framework to generate realistic yet adversarial occluded scenarios. Integrate these into a planning framework prioritizing risk under partial observability.
AI.result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset, the approach outperforms state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baselines, achieving a 0.78x improvement in minimum time-to-collision and a 1.67x improvement in average time-to-collision.
AI.conclusion: The proposed risk-map plus diffusion-based scenario generation offers a practical, comprehensive solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable driving environments.

title: AI-Enabled Serious Games: Integrating Intelligence and Adaptivity in Training Systems
comment: Book chapter, 1 figure. To appear in "Advances in Global Applied Artificial Intelligence," G. A. Tsihrintzis, M. Virvou, N. G. Bourbakis, and L. C. Jain (Eds.), Springer, Learning and Analytics in Intelligent Systems book series, 2026
summary: Serious games are widely used for learning and training across domains such as healthcare, defense, and education. Persistent challenges remain, however, including static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty implementing meaningful real-time instructional adaptation. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) introduce novel capabilities such as dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling that may help address some of these limitations. At the same time, integrating AI into serious games raises important questions related to validity, transparency, system control, and learner trust. This chapter examines how contemporary AI approaches may support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games. It distinguishes between instructional intelligence, defined as a system's capacity to infer learner knowledge and reason about pedagogically appropriate responses, and adaptivity, defined as the ability to modify instructional actions during interaction. A historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems is presented, tracing developments from early computer-assisted instruction through intelligent tutoring systems (ITS), dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA), authoring platforms, learning analytics, and recent AI-enabled architectures. Building on this perspective, the chapter discusses how large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL), and agent-based architectures may contribute to more integrated forms of intelligence and adaptivity in serious games. It also highlights practical and research challenges associated with AI-enabled systems, including explainability, validation, computational cost, and the limited empirical evidence regarding long-term learning outcomes in AI-enabled serious games.

AI.tldr: A conceptual, literature-based examination of how AI—LLMs, RL, and agent-based systems—can support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games, including historical context and a framework for integration plus discussion of challenges.
AI.motivation: Serious games face persistent challenges such as static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty with real-time adaptation. AI offers dynamic variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling, but raises concerns about validity, transparency, system control, and learner trust.
AI.method: Historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems; distinguishes instructional intelligence from adaptivity; analyzes potential roles for LLMs, reinforcement learning, and agent-based architectures; discusses practical and research challenges and outlines future directions.
AI.result: Provides a framework for integrating AI into serious games, highlighting potential benefits for real-time instructional adaptation, and identifying key challenges (explainability, validation, computational cost, and limited long-term learning evidence) to guide future research.
AI.conclusion: AI-enabled serious games can enhance instruction and adaptation but require rigorous attention to validation, explainability, cost, and empirical evidence on long-term learning outcomes; an integrated approach linking ITS, DDA, learning analytics, and AI architectures is needed.

title: Thermo-VL: Extending Vision-Language Models to Thermal Infrared Perception
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often fail under low illumination because their visual grounding is learned predominantly from RGB imagery, whereas thermal infrared preserves complementary scene structure when visible cues degrade. We present Thermo-VL, a wavelength-aware VLM that augments a frozen Molmo-7B backbone with a trainable thermal encoder and a text-guided dual-attention fusion module. Given aligned RGB tokens, thermal tokens, and prompt embeddings, the fusion module conditions thermal features on both language and RGB context, then injects a gated residual into the frozen RGB stream so thermal evidence can be incorporated without disrupting Molmo's pretrained RGB-language interface. We train the model with the standard language-modeling objective together with auxiliary alignment and regularization losses that improve cross-modal grounding and reduce over-reliance on RGB. We also introduce a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal instruction-tuning dataset and Thermo-VL-Bench, a manually screened RGB-thermal VQA benchmark for low-light and cross-spectrum reasoning. Experiments show strong gains on challenging thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks, highlighting the value of prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://thusharakart.github.io/Thermo-VL

AI.tldr: Thermo-VL is a wavelength-aware vision-language model that fuses RGB with a trainable thermal encoder via a text-guided dual-attention module, enhancing low-light and cross-spectrum reasoning by augmenting a frozen Molmo-7B backbone; it introduces a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal dataset and a VQA benchmark, with code released publicly.
AI.motivation: RGB-dominated VLMs under low illumination struggle to ground concepts when RGB cues degrade; thermal infrared provides complementary structure, but effective fusion requires conditioning thermal features on language and RGB context without disrupting pretrained RGB-language alignment.
AI.method: Freeze Molmo-7B as the RGB backbone; add a trainable thermal encoder; implement a text-guided dual-attention fusion module that conditions thermal features on language and RGB context; inject a gated residual from the fused thermal stream into the RGB path; train with standard LM objective plus auxiliary alignment and regularization losses; create a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal instruction-tuning dataset and Thermo-VL-Bench; release dataset and code.
AI.result: Experiments show strong gains on thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion and improved cross-modal grounding with reduced RGB over-reliance.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion is valuable for low-light and cross-spectrum reasoning; the dataset and code enable further research and deployment in multispectral VLMs.

title: AutoMCU: Feasibility-First MCU Neural Network Customization via LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Deploying neural networks on microcontroller units (MCUs) is critical for edge intelligence but remains challenging due to tight memory, storage, and computation constraints. Existing approaches, such as model compression and hardware-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS), often depend on proxy metrics, incur high search cost, and do not fully bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment. This paper presents AutoMCU, a feasibility-first large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system for automated neural network customization under MCU constraints. Given natural-language task requirements and hardware specifications, AutoMCU iteratively generates structured architecture candidates, filters infeasible designs through vendor toolchain feedback before training, evaluates feasible models under a controlled protocol, and verifies deployability through backend-grounded deployment analysis. AutoMCU includes two key mechanisms: 1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation for early elimination of undeployable candidates under RAM and Flash constraints, and 2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for stable coordination of proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment stages. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under strict MCU constraints show that AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to about 1--2 hours, compared with hundreds of GPU hours for representative MCU-oriented HW-NAS baselines. Comparisons with ColabNAS and the LLM-based NAS method GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 further demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of AutoMCU. Real-device deployments on multiple STM32 microcontrollers validate its practical applicability to MCU-scale edge intelligence.

AI.tldr: AutoMCU is an LLM-based multi-agent system that customizes neural networks for MCU constraints by combining hardware-in-the-loop generation, vendor-toolchain feasibility checks, controlled evaluation, and deployment verification, achieving fast customization with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: MCUs have severe memory, storage, and compute limits; existing model compression and HW-NAS approaches rely on proxies or incur high search costs and do not ensure deployability, creating a gap between design and real deployment.
AI.method: AutoMCU uses a two-layer approach: (1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation to prune infeasible designs under RAM/Flash constraints; (2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling coordinating proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment. It iteratively generates architecture candidates using an LLM, filters via vendor toolchains before training, evaluates under a controlled protocol, and performs backend-grounded deployment analysis.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10/100 with strict MCU constraints, AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to ~1–2 hours, versus hundreds of GPU hours for HW-NAS baselines. It outperforms or matches ColabNAS and GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201, and real-device STM32 deployments validate practicality for MCU-scale edge intelligence.
AI.conclusion: AutoMCU demonstrates that feasibility-first, hardware-informed, LLM-driven multi-agent workflows can bridge architecture design and deployment for MCU-constrained neural networks, delivering rapid customization and verifiable deployability.

title: Imagine2Real: Towards Zero-shot Humanoid-Object Interaction via Video Generative Priors
summary: Whole-body Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data. While video generative priors offer a promising alternative, existing methods suffer from \textit{Representation Misalignment} due to their reliance on geometric priors (e.g., explicit CAD models), and \textit{Retargeting Complexity} arising from intensive morphing and morphological mismatch. We propose Imagine2Real, a zero-shot HOI framework for flexible, geometry-free interaction. To resolve misalignment, we formulate robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories. To overcome retargeting complexity, our Keypoints Tracker tracks only sparse critical points (base, hands, and object), entirely bypassing the error-amplifying retargeting process. To maintain natural gaits despite these sparse signals, we utilize the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker's search domain. Using a progressive training strategy, Imagine2Real learns robust behaviors with simple tracking rewards, enabling zero-shot physical deployment within a motion capture(mocap) system.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot, geometry-free HOI framework (Imagine2Real) using unified 4D trajectories and sparse keypoints guided by a Behavior Foundation Model to enable mocap-ready humanoid-object interactions without CAD priors.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data for whole-body HOI, issues with Representation Misalignment when relying on geometric priors, and Retargeting Complexity due to morphing/mismatch; need scalable, geometry-free interaction.
AI.method: Represent robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories; Keypoints Tracker focuses on sparse critical points (base, hands, object); use latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker’s search domain; progressive training with simple tracking rewards to achieve robust behaviors; zero-shot physical deployment in a mocap system.
AI.result: Abstract does not provide empirical results; claims robust zero-shot behavior and deployment within mocap, but no quantitative evaluation is shown.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a geometry-free, scalable HOI framework that mitigates misalignment and retargeting through unified 4D trajectories and sparse keypoints, guided by a BFM, with potential for zero-shot mocap deployment.

title: Format-Constraint Coupling in Knowledge Graph Construction from Statistical Tables
comment: 8 pages main body, 18 pages appendices. Submitted to EMNLP 2026 via ACL Rolling Review (ARR). Corresponding author: Yuxiang Feng (yxfeng@scut.edu.cn). Code and data available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/sge_lightrag-BE19
summary: An extraction schema should not reduce knowledge graph fidelity. On statistical CSV, however, it can. We study country-by-year time-series matrices, a common layout on open-data portals. In this setting, serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively. Their joint effect exceeds the sum of independent effects by up to +1.180 (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets). Bootstrap 95% CIs are strictly positive on 4/6 datasets, with strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices. More critically, a schema applied to a mismatched format can trigger catastrophic mismatch. Fact coverage falls below the unconstrained baseline on 4/6 datasets through entity inflation or extraction refusal. We call this observed pattern format-constraint coupling. Probing and token ablation support a surface-form anchoring explanation centred on column-name references. Controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families show the same direction within the measured scope; one LLM family shows only partial activation. The observation also has a diagnostic consequence. Three standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta <= 1pp), whereas direct graph access exposes gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001). To support fidelity-aware evaluation, we release CSVFidelity-Bench. It contains 15 datasets, 11 Type-II matrices, 4 Type-III tables, and 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains.

AI.tldr: A study shows that extracting data from statistical CSV formats into knowledge graphs can degrade fidelity when schema and format constraints interact. The joint effect is super-additive, causing coverage gaps, especially on wide or mismatched formats; surface-form anchoring explains this. The work proposes CSVFidelity-Bench for fidelity-aware evaluation and demonstrates diagnostic value of direct graph access over standard retrieval modes.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how serialization format constraints and data-schema constraints jointly affect knowledge-graph fidelity when deriving facts from time-series country-by-year data in CSVs; to identify when conventional extraction yields unreliable results and to provide an evaluation benchmark.
AI.method: 2x2 factorial experiments across 6 datasets with bootstrap 95% CIs; probing and token ablation to test surface-form anchoring; controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families; evaluation comparing standard retrieval modes vs direct graph access; release of CSVFidelity-Bench with 15 datasets (11 Type-II, 4 Type-III, 1,892 gold facts across 6 domains).
AI.result: Format-schema coupling yields super-additive degradation in knowledge fidelity (up to +1.180 effect in a 2x2 design); bootstrap CIs positive on most datasets; schema on mismatched format triggers catastrophic mismatch with fact coverage dropping below baseline (4/6 datasets); anchoring on column-name references; some LLM families show partial activation; standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality, while direct graph access exposes substantial gaps up to 47.6 percentage points (p<0.0001); CSVFidelity-Bench provided for fidelity-aware evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Fidelity-aware evaluation is essential; format and schema interact nonlinearly, necessitating evaluation benchmarks and diagnostic methods that go beyond standard retrieval; direct graph access and targeted probes can reveal hidden gaps; the CSVFidelity-Bench dataset enables systematic assessment across domains.

title: Universal CT Representations from Anatomy to Disease Phenotype through Agglomerative Pretraining
summary: Computed tomography (CT) is a central to three-dimensional medical imaging, yet CT-based artificial intelligence remains fragmented across task-specific models for segmentation, classification, registration, and report analysis. Here we present FlexiCT, a family of CT foundation models trained by agglomerative continual pretraining on 266,227 CT volumes from 56 publicly available datasets, forming a large-scale public resource for CT representation learning. FlexiCT uses agglomerative pretraining across three stages: two-dimensional axial pretraining, three-dimensional anatomical pretraining and report-guided semantic alignment. This training strategy supports slice-level, volume-level and vision-language analysis. Across five downstream task families (segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding and clinical retrieval), FlexiCT matches or exceeds prior task-specific approaches on multiple benchmarks.
