Its embeddings further organize CT scans along gradients associated with various tumor stages, suggesting that CT foundation models can capture imaging features relevant to disease phenotype characterization. Code is available at https://github.com/ricklisz/FlexiCT

AI.tldr: FlexiCT is a CT foundation-model family trained via agglomerative continual pretraining on 266k CT volumes from 56 public datasets, enabling 2D axial, 3D anatomical, and report-guided semantic alignment for slice-, volume-, and vision-language analysis. It matches or exceeds prior task-specific methods across segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding, and clinical retrieval, with embeddings organizing scans along tumor-stage gradients.
AI.motivation: CT AI is currently fragmented across task-specific models; a universal CT foundation model could unify representations and improve cross-task performance and data efficiency.
AI.method: Agglomerative pretraining in three stages: (1) 2D axial pretraining, (2) 3D anatomical pretraining, (3) report-guided semantic alignment; trained on 266,227 CT volumes from 56 publicly available datasets; supports slice-, volume-, and vision-language analysis.
AI.result: Across five downstream task families—segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding, clinical retrieval—FlexiCT matches or surpasses prior task-specific approaches; CT embeddings organize scans by tumor-stage gradients, suggesting capture of disease phenotype features.
AI.conclusion: CT foundation models like FlexiCT can generalize across tasks; public release of code fosters reproducibility and further development.

title: Objective-Induced Bias and Search Dynamics in Multiobjective Unsupervised Feature Selection
summary: Unsupervised feature selection is commonly formulated as a multiobjective optimisation problem that jointly optimises subset quality and subset size. Yet the behaviour of this formulation depends critically on the choice of evaluation objective, the direction of subset-size regularisation, and the initialisation strategy. We study these factors in a controlled setting using a synthetic dataset with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant feature types. Six formulations are compared by combining three evaluation objectives: accuracy, silhouette score, and PCA reconstruction loss with subset-size minimisation or maximisation. The results show that formulation strongly affects both search dynamics and the quality of the resulting Pareto front. Silhouette-based formulations exhibit a strong bias toward trivial low-cardinality solutions and remain weak proxies for predictive performance. In contrast, the proposed PCA loss objective produces compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained by directly optimising supervised accuracy. Overall, the study shows that objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised feature selection with multiobjective optimisation is highly sensitive to the evaluation objective and regularisation direction; PCA reconstruction loss yields compact, accurate subsets, outperforming silhouette-based objectives that bias toward tiny subsets.
AI.motivation: To dissect how objective choice, subset-size regularisation, and initialization affect search dynamics and Pareto fronts in unsupervised feature selection using synthetic data with known feature types (informative, redundant, irrelevant).
AI.method: Six formulations formed by combining three evaluation objectives (prediction accuracy, silhouette score, PCA reconstruction loss) with subset-size minimisation or maximisation. The methods are evaluated on a synthetic dataset to analyze search dynamics and Pareto-front quality across objectives.
AI.result: Objective design substantially shapes search dynamics and Pareto-front quality. Silhouette-based formulations bias toward trivial, low-cardinality solutions and are weak proxies for predictive performance. The PCA reconstruction loss objective yields compact feature subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained via supervised accuracy optimization.
AI.conclusion: Design of the objective is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection; PCA reconstruction loss is a robust objective for achieving compact and predictive subsets, whereas silhouette-based objectives may mislead selection toward overly small subsets.

title: Spatial Memory for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action
summary: We introduce SOMA, the Spatial Memory framework for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Most existing VLAs implicitly assume that task-relevant objects are always visible, leading to brittle and reactive behaviors when targets fall outside the camera's field of view. SOMA addresses this limitation by equipping VLAs with a persistent spatial memory constructed from multi-view observations acquired via a movable head camera, enabling reasoning beyond the current visual frustum. The framework consists of three components: Spatial Memory Construction, which aggregates angular-wise observations into a unified spatial-semantic representation through scanning; Dynamic Memory Refinement, which maintains global consistency over time; and Contextual Memory Retrieval, which activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation. We evaluate SOMA on five challenging real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks, including multi-step and dual-arm scenarios where target objects are initially invisible. Experimental results show that SOMA not only improves task success rates, but also induces qualitatively different manipulation behaviors, with faster target localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability. Additional experiments on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv further validate the effectiveness of SOMA's memory design under conventional fully observable settings. Code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: SOMA introduces a persistent spatial memory for vision-language-action models to operate beyond the current field of view by constructing a multi-view spatial-semantic map from a movable head camera, with three components (memory construction, dynamic refinement, retrieval), achieving faster target localization and higher success in out-of-view manipulation tasks, and generalizes to fully observable settings.
AI.motivation: VLAs are brittle under partial observability because they assume target objects are always visible; there is a need for long-term, spatially grounded memory to reason about occluded or out-of-view targets to improve robustness and efficiency in manipulation.
AI.method: Spatial Memory Construction aggregates angular-wise observations into a unified spatial-semantic map via scanning; Dynamic Memory Refinement maintains global temporal consistency; Contextual Memory Retrieval activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation. The memory is built from multi-view observations using a movable head camera, enabling planning beyond the current frustum.
AI.result: SOMA improves task success rates on five real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks, including multi-step and dual-arm scenarios with initially invisible targets. It yields qualitatively different manipulation behaviors: faster localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability. Additional experiments on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv validate the memory design under fully observable settings.
AI.conclusion: Memory-based spatial reasoning in VLA models effectively addresses out-of-vision manipulation. The approach demonstrates robust performance gains in challenging conditions and generalizes to standard fully observable scenarios; code release is anticipated.

title: Echo: Learning from Experience Data via User-Driven Refinement
summary:
Static "human data" faces inherent limitations: it is expensive to scale and bounded by the knowledge of its creators. Continuous learning from "experience data" - interactions between agents and their environments - promises to transcend these barriers. Today, the widespread deployment of AI agents grants us low-cost access to massive streams of such real-world experience. However, raw interaction logs are inherently noisy, filled with trial-and-error and low information density, rendering them inefficient for direct model training.
  We introduce Echo, a generalized framework designed to operationalize the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge, effectively "echoing" environmental feedback back into the training loop for model optimization. In today's agent ecosystem, user refinement serves as a primary source of such feedback: driven by responsibility for the outcome, users rigorously transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions. These user-driven refinement sequences inherently distill agents' crude attempts into high-quality training signals. Echo systematically harvests these signals to continuously align the agent with real-world needs. Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment confirms that Echo effectively harnesses this pipeline, breaking the static performance ceiling by increasing the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.

AI.tldr: Echo harvests and echo-lizes user-refinement signals from agent-environment interactions to convert raw experience into actionable training signals, boosting production performance (code completion) beyond static data limits.
AI.motivation: Static labeled data are costly and bounded; continuous, scalable learning from real-world interactions can surpass these limits by leveraging human-in-the-loop refinements to derive high-quality supervision.
AI.method: Proposed Echo framework that captures, organizes, and feeds back environmental feedback and user refinements into the training loop; demonstrates in production code completion with large-scale validation; increases acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.
AI.result: Validation in production code completion shows significant improvement in acceptance rate (25.7% -> 35.7%), demonstrating that echoing experiential feedback can improve learning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Echo enables continuous learning from experience data by distilling noisy, trial-and-error interactions through user refinements, surpassing static data ceilings and aligning models with real-world needs.

title: Guided Trajectory Optimization with Sparse Scaling for Test-Time Diffusion
summary: The efficient Test-Time Scaling (TTS) paradigm offers a promising perspective for enhancing the generation performance of diffusion models. However, current solutions are limited to a static, pre-defined noise pool and suffer from inflexible noise exploration across the denoising trajectory. To bridge this gap, we propose RTS, a novel Reward-guided Trajectory Scaling method to fully unlock the generative potential of diffusion models. Unlike existing methods, RTS facilitates the synthesis of refined, high-fidelity images via two core innovations: 1) a reward-guided noise optimization strategy to actively direct the search towards promising regions; and 2) a sparse test-time scaling framework together with a PCA-driven curvature analysis scheme to prioritize key intermediate steps in the entire denoising space, effectively compressing the search space. Experiments show our approach outperforms baselines by 15.6% across GenEval Score, and a 60.4% enhancement in ImageReward score, setting a new SOTA while providing a practical guideline for more effective test-time scaling across diffusion-specific architectures.

AI.tldr: RTS introduces Reward-guided Trajectory Scaling for diffusion models, combining reward-based noise optimization with PCA-driven sparse scaling to explore key denoising steps efficiently; achieves SOTA gains on GenEval and ImageReward metrics.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of existing Test-Time Scaling that rely on static noise pools and inflexible trajectory exploration, aiming to unlock higher-fidelity generation with efficient search.
AI.method: Two-fold approach: (1) reward-guided noise optimization to steer search toward promising regions in the denoising process; (2) sparse test-time scaling plus PCA-based curvature analysis to identify and prioritize key intermediate steps, compressing the search space.
AI.result: Empirical results show RTS outperforms baselines by 15.6% on GenEval Score and 60.4% on ImageReward score, establishing new state-of-the-art and providing practical guidelines for diffusion-specific architectures.
AI.conclusion: RTS offers a practical, efficient TTS framework that enhances image fidelity by actively guiding trajectory exploration and focusing computation on the most impactful denoising steps.

title: How can reasoning capability empower the AI copilot robot in endoscopic surgery
summary: Reasoning capability has significantly advanced complex logical inference and robotic decision-making in general domains. However, its potential in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) copilot robot-particularly implemented based on the Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model-remains unexplored in endoscopic surgery. Effective reasoning should enable AI copilot robots to integrate multimodal cues, interpret surgical intent, and infer hidden tissue dynamics, thereby alleviating intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden on surgeons. Properly implemented, reasoning-driven autonomy can transform AI copilot robots from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators, enhancing precision, safety, and sustainability in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Advocates reasoning-driven autonomy for AI copilot robots in endoscopic surgery using Vision-Language-Action models to fuse multimodal cues, infer tissue dynamics and surgical intent, thereby reducing intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden on surgeons, and transforming copilots into cognitive collaborators to improve precision, safety, and sustainability.
AI.motivation: Current AI copilot approaches in endoscopy lack robust reasoning capabilities to interpret multimodal signals and latent tissue dynamics, leading to intraoperative uncertainty and higher cognitive load. A reasoning-enabled, cognitively collaborative copilot could enhance decision support, precision, and safety in clinical practice.
AI.method: Proposes a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that integrates reasoning into the AI copilot, enabling interpretation of multimodal cues, inference of surgical intent and tissue dynamics, and autonomous, yet surgeon-aligned, decision making.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with no empirical results reported; outlines the design principle and potential benefits of reasoning-driven autonomy in AI copilots for endoscopic surgery.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-enabled autonomy can transform AI copilots from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators, improving precision, safety, and sustainability in endoscopic robotic surgery; further development and empirical validation are required.

title: Multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution
summary: Stereo image super-resolution aims to generate high-resolution images by leveraging complementary information from binocular systems. Although previous studies have achieved impressive results, the potential of intra-view and cross-view information has not been fully exploited. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution. Specifically, we design a Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module that utilizes multi-scale large separable kernel attention and simple channel attention to improve intra-view feature extraction. Additionally, we propose a Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module, utilizing an optimal transport algorithm to achieve more accurate matching along the epipolar line. Extensive experimental and ablation studies show that our method achieves competitive results that outperform most SOTA methods.

AI.tldr: A multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution that jointly exploits intra-view and cross-view information via two attention modules; achieves competitive performance and surpasses most SOTA methods.
AI.motivation: To address the under-exploitation of intra-view and cross-view information in stereo SR and to enhance feature extraction and cross-view matching.
AI.method: Introduce Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module incorporating multi-scale large separable kernel attention and simple channel attention for intra-view features; introduce Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module that uses an optimal transport algorithm to align correspondences along epipolar lines for cross-view matching.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and ablations show the proposed method is competitive and outperforms most state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modules effectively leverage multi-scale intra-view cues and epipolar cross-view information, yielding improved stereo SR performance and offering a versatile framework for future cross-view attention designs.

title: Leveraging Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Enhanced Modality Balance in Multimodal Conversational Emotion Recognition
comment: Accepted at Neural Computing and Applications (Springer), 2026
summary: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) is a crucial task for understanding human interactions, where multimodal approaches integrating language, facial expressions, and vocal tone have achieved significant progress. However, modality misalignment and imbalanced learning remain major challenges, limiting the effective utilization of multimodal information. To address this issue, we propose a plug-and-play framework based on Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) for MERC. We introduce a dual-level Difficulty Measurer that captures both utterance-level and conversation-level challenges. The utterance-level score models fine-grained modality-specific difficulty, while the conversation-level score captures broader dialogue structures, including emotional dependencies and modality coherence. Based on these scores, the Learning Scheduler dynamically guides training from easier to more difficult instances. By integrating SPCL into existing MERC architectures, our method alleviates modality imbalance and improves model robustness. Extensive experiments on the IEMOCAP and MELD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements across different architectures and modality settings. On IEMOCAP, SPCL improves weighted F1-score by approximately +1.2% to +6.6% over baseline models, while on MELD, gains reach up to +10.4%. These results highlight the effectiveness and generalizability of SPCL as a lightweight plug-and-play module for multimodal emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: A lightweight SPCL-based plug-and-play framework for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) that uses dual-level difficulty measurements to guide curriculum learning, mitigating modality misalignment and imbalance with consistent gains on IEMOCAP and MELD across architectures.
AI.motivation: Modality misalignment and imbalanced learning hinder effective use of multimodal signals in MERC; a robust, adaptable training strategy is needed to exploit cross-modal information.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-level Difficulty Measurer (utterance-level for fine-grained modality difficulty and conversation-level for dialogue structure and modality coherence). A Learning Scheduler powered by Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) guides training from easy to hard instances and is designed as a plug-and-play module compatible with existing MERC architectures.
AI.result: Empirical results on IEMOCAP and MELD show consistent improvements. On IEMOCAP, weighted F1-score gains range from ~1.2% to ~6.6% over baselines; on MELD, gains reach up to ~10.4%, across various architectures and modality configurations.
AI.conclusion: SPCL is an effective, lightweight, and generalizable plug-in for MERC that reduces modality imbalance and improves robustness across datasets and architecture settings.

title: Real-Time Auto-Optimization in Unknown Environments via Structure-Exploiting Dual Control for Exploration and Exploitation
summary: This paper develops a fast numerical dual control for exploration and exploitation (DCEE) method to address auto-optimization problems in unknown environments. In auto-optimization problems, the optimal operating condition is unknown a priori and may vary with the environment. As in classical dual control techniques, computational burden remains a major concern in DCEE for active learning. Existing DCEE methods provide a principled exploration-exploitation objective, but mainly realized through standard optimization packages or explicit gradient-type update laws, where the numerical structure of the DCEE has not been fully exploited. This paper shows that the reward function in DCEE has an inherent convex-over-nonlinear structure, where the exploitation and exploration terms form a unified nonlinear residual map equipped with a convex outer loss. Benefiting from this structure, a structure-exploiting numerical method is developed by linearizing only the nonlinear residual map while preserving the convex outer loss. Thus, each subproblem is transformed into a structured convex form that can be solved reliably. The resulting generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation is positive semidefinite and depends only on first-order derivatives, thereby supporting fast online computation. The proposed method is evaluated on a vehicle cruising auto-optimization problem and compared with existing methods. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop experimental results show that the proposed method improves control performance and achieves a speedup of approximately one order of magnitude, with a microsecond-level maximum computation time of only 83 μs on a typical vehicle embedded CPU.

AI.tldr: A structure-exploiting dual control for exploration/exploitation (DCEE) in unknown environments; linearizes the nonlinear residual while keeping a convex outer loss, enabling fast, reliable online optimization via a Gauss-Newton-like Hessian.
AI.motivation: Auto-optimization in unknown environments faces high computational burden and real-time constraints; existing DCEE methods rely on generic optimization tools and may not exploit problem structure efficiently.
AI.method: Identify a convex outer loss and a nonlinear residual; linearize the nonlinear residual while preserving the convex outer loss, converting subproblems into structured convex forms. Use a generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation (positive semidefinite, uses only first-order derivatives) to enable fast online computation.
AI.result: Evaluation on a vehicle cruising auto-optimization task with simulations and hardware-in-the-loop tests. The method achieves about an order of magnitude speedup and maximum online computation time of 83 microseconds on a typical vehicle embedded CPU, with improved control performance.
AI.conclusion: Structure-exploiting transformation yields reliable, fast DCEE suitable for real-time embedded control in unknown environments, leveraging convex outer loss and first-order information; the approach shows practical viability and potential for broader applicability.

title: The Log is the Agent: Event-Sourced Reactive Graphs for Auditable, Forkable Agentic Systems
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure. Open-source Apache-2.0 implementation with reproducible quickstart demo, deterministic replay, fork-and-diff, and lineage tracing
summary: Most agent frameworks are built around the language model: a conversation loop comes first, then tools, then rules, and finally a logging layer bolted on for observability, with state persisted as retrievable "memory." We describe ActiveGraph, a runtime that inverts this arrangement. The append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of that log; and behaviors--ordinary functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or logic attached to typed edges--react to changes in the graph and emit new events. No component instructs another; coordination happens entirely through the shared graph. This single design decision yields three properties that retrieval-and-summarization memory systems do not provide: deterministic replay of any run from its log, cheap forking that branches a run at any event without re-executing the shared prefix, and end-to-end lineage from a high-level goal down to the individual model call that produced each artifact. We present the architecture, a determinism contract that makes replay sound, and a worked diligence example whose full causal structure is reconstructable from the log alone. We discuss--without claiming to demonstrate--why this substrate is unusually well suited to self-improving agents, and how it extends the BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research.

AI.tldr: ActiveGraph introduces an append-only event-log runtime where a deterministic graph projection drives behaviors that react to graph changes, enabling replay, forking, and end-to-end lineage from goals to individual model calls.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional retrieval-and-summarization memory systems by providing deterministic provenance, reproducibility, and composable, observable behavior through graph-based coordination; also to advance self-improving agent architectures and connect to the BabyAGI lineage.
AI.method: Architectural design where the append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of the log; behaviors (functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or edge-attached logic) react to graph changes and emit new events; components coordinate solely through the shared graph; includes a determinism contract, a worked-diligence example, and discussion of applicability to self-improvement and prior graph-memory work.
AI.result: Realizes three advantages over memory systems: (1) deterministic replay of any run from the log, (2) cheap forking by branching at any event without re-executing shared prefixes, (3) end-to-end lineage from high-level goals to individual model calls; provides architectural exposition and a concrete example; discusses implications for self-improving agents and BabyAGI lineage.
AI.conclusion: Argues that the graph-centric substrate yields strong traceability and reproducibility benefits for agent systems and is a promising foundation for future work in self-improvement research, while not claiming definitive empirical proof but encouraging further exploration.

title: MAVEN: A Multi-stage Agentic Annotation Pipeline for Video Reasoning Tasks
summary: Training Vision Language Models (VLMs) for video event reasoning requires high-quality structured annotations capturing not only what happened, but when, where, why, and with what consequence, at a scale manual labelling cannot support. We present MAVEN (Multi-stage Agentic Video Event aNnotation), a multi-stage agentic pipeline that turns raw videos into multi-task training data with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning traces, organized around a designated Event of Focus. At its core, MAVEN synthesizes a Multi-Scale Spatio-Temporal Event Description (MSTED) from three complementary caption levels; this explicit intermediate serves as the sole input to downstream Q&A generation across multiple task formats. Crucially, MAVEN supports agent-driven domain adaptation: given a new video dataset and target question examples, the agent redesigns all prompts top-down without manual re-engineering. A hierarchical refinement loop further classifies annotation errors against a taxonomy, traces root causes to the originating pipeline stage, and applies targeted edits that rewrite prompts or modify the pipeline structure itself, iteratively improving data quality. We apply MAVEN to label over 5,300 traffic videos and fine-tune Cosmos-Reason2-8B on the resulting data. On a private CCTV evaluation set, fine-tuning surpasses both Gemini 2.5 Pro and 3.1 Flash, including a $+38.8$-point gain in MCQ accuracy over zero-shot. On AccidentBench, CCTV-only training lifts Cosmos-Reason2 by $+10.7$ MCQ points and matches Gemini 2.5 Pro despite seeing no dashcam videos; adding agent-adapted dashcam annotations narrows the gap to Gemini 3.1 Flash, and RL post-training pushes overall performance past both Gemini baselines. Qualitative results on warehouse surveillance and public safety videos further show the agentic workflow readily adapts the pipeline to new domains.

AI.tldr: A multi-stage, agentic pipeline MAVEN converts raw videos into multi-task training data with chain-of-thought reasoning traces around a designated Event of Focus; enables domain-adaptive annotation and data-quality refinement; achieves strong cross-domain performance on surveillance datasets.
AI.motivation: To scale high-quality, structured video event annotations for VLMs and enable domain adaptation via prompting, avoiding manual re-labelling.
AI.method: MAVEN: Multi-Stage Agentic Video Event Annotation; MSTED generation from three caption levels; input to downstream Q&A tasks; agent-driven prompt redesign for new datasets; hierarchical refinement loop diagnosing errors by taxonomy and editing prompts/pipeline; trained on 5,300 traffic videos; fine-tuned Cosmos-Reason2-8B; RL post-training.
AI.result: Surpasses Gemini baselines on private CCTV with +38.8 MCQ gain in zero-shot context after fine-tuning; on AccidentBench CCTV-only training, Cosmos-Reason2 improves by +10.7 MCQ and matches Gemini 2.5 Pro; adding dashcam data narrows gap to Gemini 3.1 Flash; RL post-training surpasses Gemini baselines; qualitative results show adaptability across warehouses and public-safety videos.
AI.conclusion: MAVEN enables scalable, domain-adaptive video event annotation with CoT reasoning, improving VLM fine-tuning performance and cross-domain transfer; the agentic loop provides systematic data-quality improvements and fast adaptation to new domains.

title: Calibration, Uncertainty Communication, and Deployment Readiness in CKD Risk Prediction: A Framework Evaluation Study
comment: 27 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables. Supplementary materials (S1-S4) included as ancillary file
summary:
Machine learning models for chronic kidney disease (CKD) risk prediction often post strong discrimination scores on internal test sets. Calibration and uncertainty quantification get far less attention, leaving clinicians without reliable information about whether the probability outputs are accurate.
  We trained five classifiers on the UCI CKD dataset (400 patients, 62.5% CKD prevalence): logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM with Platt scaling, and Gaussian naive Bayes. We evaluated each across calibration quality, conformal prediction coverage, and an eight-criterion deployment readiness framework. A distributional stress-test applied the best-calibrated variant of each model to the open-access MIMIC-IV demo cohort (97 patients, 23.7% CKD) to assess behaviour under prevalence shift and feature missingness. We measured calibration before and after Platt scaling and isotonic regression using Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score, and quantified uncertainty through split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage.
  All five models reached AUROC 1.00 on the UCI test set. Isotonic recalibration reduced internal ECE to 0.000-0.022. On MIMIC-IV, AUROC fell to 0.48-0.58, ECE rose to 0.68-0.76, and conformal coverage dropped from 0.80-0.98 to 0.21-0.25 against a 90% target. No model scored above 4 out of 16 on the deployment readiness checklist.
  Near-perfect internal performance did not transfer. Calibration stability and conformal coverage should be evaluated on external data before any clinical prediction model moves toward deployment.

AI.tldr: Internal AUROC 1.00 on UCI CKD data but poor external calibration and uncertainty coverage on MIMIC-IV; highlights need for external validation and robust uncertainty quantification before deployment.
AI.motivation: Calibration and uncertainty quantification in CKD risk prediction are often neglected; clinicians require trustworthy probability estimates and reliable uncertainty measures.
AI.method: Trained five classifiers on the UCI CKD dataset; evaluated calibration (ECE, Brier), applied Platt scaling and isotonic regression; used split conformal prediction for 90% coverage; performed a distributional stress test on the external MIMIC-IV cohort; assessed using an eight-criterion deployment readiness framework.
AI.result: AUROC 1.00 on UCI; isotonic recalibration reduced internal ECE to 0.000–0.022; on MIMIC-IV AUROC 0.48–0.58; ECE 0.68–0.76; conformal coverage 0.21–0.25 vs 0.80–0.98; deployment readiness score ≤4/16.
AI.conclusion: Calibration stability and conformal coverage must be validated on external data before clinical deployment; internal performance does not guarantee external reliability.

title: Terminal Constraint Model Predictive Control for Image-Based Visual Servoing of UAVs with Kalman Filter-Based Moment Loss Compensation
summary: Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) provides an efficient vision-guided control paradigm for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) by directly regulating image-space errors. However, conventional IBVS controllers are vulnerable to two critical issues: loss of closed-loop stability near the target due to input and state constraints, and control failure caused by intermittent loss of moment-based visual features under aggressive motion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a terminal-constraint model predictive control (TC-MPC) framework for IBVS, integrated with a Kalman filter (KF)-based state-prediction mechanism. The TC-MPC explicitly incorporates terminal-state constraints and a terminal cost into the IBVS error dynamics, ensuring recursive feasibility, improved convergence behavior, and closed-loop stability under control and state constraints. In parallel, the Kalman filter predicts the temporal evolution of image moments during short-term visual degradation, enabling the controller to preserve control continuity when moment measurements are partially unavailable. The proposed approach is validated through real-time UAV visual servoing experiments.

AI.tldr: A terminal-constraint MPC for IBVS with a Kalman-filter-based moment predictor improves stability under input/state constraints and robustness to intermittent visual feature loss; validated on real-time UAV experiments.
AI.motivation: Address (i) loss of recursive feasibility and stability in IBVS under control/state constraints, and (ii) control failure due to intermittent loss of moment-based features during aggressive UAV motion.
AI.method: Integrates a terminal-constraint model predictive control (TC-MPC) for IBVS with a Kalman filter that predicts the temporal evolution of image moments during short-term visual degradation. The MPC includes terminal-state constraints and a terminal cost to guarantee recursive feasibility and closed-loop stability; KF-based state prediction maintains control continuity when image moments are partially unavailable.
AI.result: Real-time UAV visual servoing experiments demonstrate improved recursive feasibility, faster convergence, and sustained control when moment measurements degrade, validating enhanced robustness and stability of the IBVS under constraints and feature loss.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TC-MPC with KF-based moment prediction provides a robust IBVS framework capable of handling input/state constraints and intermittent visual feature loss, with demonstrated real-time applicability on UAVs; potential extensions could explore computational efficiency, sensor noise, and broader VB control contexts.

title: Active Evidence-Seeking and Diagnostic Reasoning in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support
summary: Large language models perform well on static medical examinations, yet clinical diagnosis often requires iterative evidence gathering under uncertainty. Building on prior interactive evaluation efforts, we introduce an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator and a controlled, reproducible benchmark for active diagnostic inquiry. Across 468 cases and 15 models in our protocol, we observe that multi-turn evidence seeking reduces diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and lowers supporting-evidence quality by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation; error analyses associate these drops with premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning. Together, these results suggest that static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive evidence-seeking settings, motivating complementary interactive assessment for safer clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: Interactive SP-based benchmarking reveals multi-turn inquiry hurts diagnostic accuracy and evidence quality vs full-context evaluation; static benchmarks may overestimate AI performance in dynamic clinical diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between static, full-context evaluation and real-world interactive diagnostic reasoning by creating a standardized, OSCE-inspired benchmarking framework.
AI.method: OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator with 468 cases and 15 models, comparing multi-turn active evidence seeking against full-context evaluation; metrics include diagnostic accuracy and quality of supporting evidence; subsequent error analysis.
AI.result: In multi-turn scenarios, diagnostic accuracy declines by 12.75% and supporting-evidence quality declines by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation; errors linked to premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning.
AI.conclusion: Static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive, uncertain diagnostic settings; complementary interactive assessment is needed for safer clinical decision support.

title: SDGBiasBench: Benchmarking and Mitigating Vision--Language Models' Biases in Sustainable Development Goals
summary: Assessing progress toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) requires multi-step reasoning over visual cues, contextual knowledge, and development indicators, where incomplete evidence use and imperfect evidence integration can introduce hidden prediction biases. Real-world SDG monitoring further spans both qualitative judgments and quantitative estimation. However, existing benchmarks typically evaluate these aspects in isolation, obscuring systematic biases that emerge when models substitute priors for evidence. To address this gap, we propose SDGBiasBench, a large-scale benchmark suite for SDG-oriented vision-language reasoning. Spanning 500k expert-involved multiple-choice questions and 50k regression tasks, the benchmark enables comprehensive assessment of both decision-level and estimation-level bias in Vision--Language Models (VLMs). Evaluations on SDGBiasBench reveal an intrinsic SDG bias in current VLMs, where predictions are frequently driven by SDG specific priors rather than reliable multi-modal cues. To mitigate such bias, we propose CADE (Contrastive Adaptive Debias Ensemble), a training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages modality-specific answer priors. CADE yields significant gains on the proposed benchmark, improving multiple-choice accuracy by up to 25% and reducing regression MAE by up to 12 points across multiple VLMs. We hope our work can foster the development of more fair and reliable AI systems for sustainable development.

AI.tldr: SDGBiasBench introduces a large-scale SDG-focused VLM benchmark to quantify decision- and estimation-level biases from priors, and CADE—an input-free debiasing method using modality-specific priors—to mitigate this bias, yielding notable gains.
AI.motivation: To address hidden prediction biases that arise when SDG monitoring models substitute priors for evidence, and to unify evaluation across qualitative (judgments) and quantitative (estimation) aspects within vision-language models.
AI.method: SDGBiasBench comprises 500k expert-involved multiple-choice questions and 50k regression tasks to assess both decision-level and estimation-level bias in SDG-oriented VLMs. CADE (Contrastive Adaptive Debias Ensemble) is a training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages modality-specific answer priors to reduce bias in predictions.
AI.result: Experiments reveal an intrinsic SDG bias in current VLMs, where predictions are driven by SDG priors rather than multi-modal cues. CADE yields significant gains: up to 25% improvement in multiple-choice accuracy and up to 12-point reductions in regression MAE across multiple VLMs.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables systematic bias analysis in SDG-oriented VLMs, and the CADE method provides a practical, training-free tool to improve fairness and reliability of AI systems for sustainable development.

title: Equilibrium Propagation and Hamiltonian Inference in the Diffusive Fitzhugh-Nagumo Model
summary: In this work, we extend the Equilibrium Propagation framework to skew-gradient systems and show an equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks. We focus on networks of diffusively coupled Fitzhugh-Nagumo neurons as a prototypical example. We show that since stationary solutions of the Fitzhugh-Nagumo model are described by self-adjoint operators, the methods of equilibrium propagation for performing credit assignment can be applied. Furthermore, for Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks with the topology of a deep residual network, we show that the steady state solutions admit a (spatial) Hamiltonian, and thus the methods of Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation can be applied. We end by deriving an explicit layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence relation governing inference for stationary solutions of both deep Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks and deep Energy-Based Models.

AI.tldr: Extends Equilibrium Propagation to skew-gradient systems and proves an equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks, using diffusively coupled Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks as a key example. For deep residual topologies, stationary solutions admit a Hamiltonian; yields a layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence for inference in stationary states of deep FHN networks and deep EBMs.
AI.motivation: Bridge and unify learning in energy-based and Hamiltonian frameworks, enabling credit assignment and inference in dynamical, skew-gradient systems, with Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks as a concrete prototype.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of stationary solutions in Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks; demonstration that stationary equations are described by self-adjoint operators; extension to deep residual topology to obtain a spatial Hamiltonian; derivation of a layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence governing inference in stationary states; linking Equilibrium Propagation with Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation.
AI.result: Establishes an equivalence between deep EBMs and Hamiltonian neural networks within skew-gradient systems; shows applicability of EP and HEBP to Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks; derives an explicit layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence for inference in stationary solutions of both deep FHN networks and deep EBMs.
AI.conclusion: Unifies two gradient-based learning paradigms (EP and HEBP) under a common Hamiltonian/energy-based framework, providing practical recurrence relations for inference in stationary states of deep dynamical networks, with Fitzhugh-Nagumo as a canonical example.

title: Steins;Gate Drive: Semantic Safety Arbitration over Structured Futures for Latency-Decoupled LLM Planning
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 5 tables, submitted to IEEE transaction of intelligent vehicles
summary: Cloud-hosted LLM driver agents provide useful semantic judgments, but their inference latency exceeds stepwise vehicle-control windows. Learned world models predict futures, but they usually keep future generation and action selection inside large coupled loops. We present SteinsGateDrive, a latency-decoupled planner-runtime architecture in which the worldline metaphor from the eponymous story names one plausible consequence of an intervention: the LLM selects counterfactual driving futures before the final control instant, and a runtime reuses the selected forecast only while safety contracts remain valid. The generator builds three world-line roles: alpha nominal ego-conditioned futures, beta interaction counterfactuals around nearby vehicles, and gamma hazard-stress futures such as braking, cut-ins, or blocked corridors. The selected branch becomes a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority. On a within-subject, matched-seed normal-highway protocol with 10 seeds and 20 steps, GPT-5.4 mini reduces effective lag from +3.07 s at 1-second horizon to -0.01 s at 4-second horizon while preserving the measured no-collision safety boundary. The architecture's safety contribution comes from the atom-predicate runtime check, not from the drift score, which functions as a refresh-frequency knob.

AI.tldr: Latency-decoupled planner using worldline forecasts (alpha, beta, gamma) to preselect counterfactual driving futures, enabling a GPT-based driver to reduce perceived lag while preserving safety; evaluated on highway task with significant lag reduction and maintained no-collision boundary.
AI.motivation: Address the latency bottleneck of cloud-hosted LLM drivers and the tight coupling between future generation and action; enable timely, safe driving decisions via decoupled planning.
AI.method: SteinsGateDrive constructs three world-line roles (alpha nominal ego-conditioned futures, beta counterfactuals around nearby vehicles, gamma hazard-stress futures like braking or blocked corridors). The selected branch yields a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority. Safety is enforced via an atom-predicate runtime check; drift score acts as a refresh-frequency knob. Evaluation is within-subject on a normal-highway protocol with 10 seeds and 20 steps, comparing lag and safety boundaries using GPT-5.4 mini.
AI.result: Effective lag reduced from +3.07 s at 1-second horizon to -0.01 s at 4-second horizon; safety boundary (no collisions) preserved while expanding horizon. The approach decouples planning from action, using a structured forecast with explicit validity and abort criteria.
AI.conclusion: Latency decoupling in planning via worldline-based forecasts can achieve timely, safe autonomous driving decisions. Safety relies on atom-predicate checks rather than drift scores, with drift score serving as a rate-control knob for forecast refresh.

title: Enhancing Visual Token Representations for Video Large Language Models via Training-Free Spatial-Temporal Pooling and Gridding
summary: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced video understanding tasks, yet challenges remain in efficiently compressing visual tokens while preserving spatiotemporal interactions. Existing methods, such as LLaVA family, utilize simplistic pooling or interpolation techniques that overlook the intricate dynamics of visual tokens. To bridge this gap, we propose ST-GridPool, a novel training-free visual token enhancement method designed specifically for Video LLMs. Our approach integrates Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG), which captures multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions through hierarchical temporal gridding, and Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP), which preserves high-information visual regions by leveraging the correlation between token norms and semantic richness. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that ST-GridPool consistently enhances performance of Video LLMs without requiring costly retraining. Our method offers an efficient and plug-and-play solution for improving visual token representations. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-GridPool.

AI.tldr: ST-GridPool provides a training-free visual token enhancement for video LLMs, using Pyramid Temporal Gridding and Norm-based Spatial Pooling to preserve spatiotemporal information without retraining, yielding consistent performance gains on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome lossy visual token compression in video LLMs that results from naive pooling/interpolation, which fails to capture complex spatiotemporal dynamics, while enabling a practical plug-and-play improvement without retraining.
AI.method: ST-GridPool combines two components: (1) Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG) to capture multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions via hierarchical temporal partitioning, and (2) Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP) to retain high-information regions by exploiting the correlation between token norms and semantic richness; it is training-free and plug-and-play.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on multiple benchmarks show consistent performance improvements for Video LLMs without retraining; the approach is efficient and the authors provide code for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: ST-GridPool offers a practical, plug-and-play solution for enhancing visual token representations in Video LLMs, delivering efficiency and performance gains without costly retraining.

title: Visual-Advantage On-Policy Distillation for Vision-Language Models
summary: On-policy knowledge distillation has proven effective for language models, yet its application to vision-language models (VLMs) remains underexplored. We observe that standard on-policy distillation can improve a student's output quality while failing to strengthen its reliance on visual input: on vision-critical tokens, the student's predictions remain largely unchanged whether or not fine-grained visual detail is present, even though the teacher's predictions depend heavily on it.To make this difference observable, we introduce visual advantage (VA), the token-level log-probability difference when the teacher scores a student-generated rollout with versus without access to fine-grained visual detail. VA is concentrated in a small minority of tokens, and these high-VA tokens are the ones that actually carry the visual supervision signal. This motivates a distillation objective that treats them differently from language scaffolding, so their contribution is not diluted by the abundant surrounding language tokens.We propose Visual-Advantage On-Policy Distillation (VA-OPD), which uses VA at two granularities: rollout-level reweighting by trajectory-averaged VA, and token-level KL averaged within high-VA and low-VA groups separately. We train on two math datasets (Geometry3K and ViRL39K) and evaluate on eight benchmarks covering both mathematical reasoning and visual understanding, across three teacher sizes (4B, 8B, and 32B) on the Qwen3-VL family. VA-OPD improves over standard on-policy distillation on every benchmark, with the gain growing monotonically along both the teacher-size and data-scale axes, suggesting that these factors compound consistently.

AI.tldr: Introduces Visual-Advantage On-Policy Distillation (VA-OPD) that leverages a token-level visual-advantage signal to reweight on-policy distillation, improving VLM performance over standard on-policy distillation.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation improves language generation but often fails to enhance reliance on visual input; teacher predictions depend heavily on fine-grained visual details, which standard distillation neglects.
AI.method: Define visual advantage (VA) as the log-probability difference when the teacher scores a student rollout with vs without access to fine-grained visual detail. Use VA at two granularities: (1) rollout-level reweighting using trajectory-averaged VA, and (2) token-level KL divergence computed separately for high-VA and low-VA token groups. Train on Geometry3K and ViRL39K, evaluate on eight benchmarks across math reasoning and visual understanding, with teacher sizes 4B, 8B, 32B on Qwen3-VL.
AI.result: VA-OPD consistently improves over standard on-policy distillation on all eight benchmarks; gains grow with teacher size and data scale, suggesting compounding benefits.
AI.conclusion: Visually grounded supervision can be more effectively transferred via VA-OPD, providing a scalable method to improve VLM distillation by explicitly leveraging visual signals.

title: ConTact: Contact-First Antibody CDR Design via Explicit Interface Reasoning
summary: Computational antibody CDR design methods condition on antigen structure to generate binding loops, yet existing architectures conflate two fundamentally distinct sub-problems: identifying which CDR positions will contact the antigen, and selecting amino acids at those positions. This conflation forces models to learn contact reasoning implicitly through uniform message passing, diluting antigen signal across all positions equally. We introduce ConTact, a contact-then-act architecture that explicitly decomposes CDR design into three cascaded stages: learning surface complementarity fingerprints, predicting CDR-antigen contacts, and injecting contact-gated antigen features into the sequence head. A distance-biased cross-attention module encodes geometric priors favoring spatial neighbors, while a contact-weighted cross-entropy loss concentrates gradient signal on binding-critical positions. On CHIMERA-Bench dataset, ConTact achieves the best structural quality (7% RMSD improvement over the next-best baseline), best epitope awareness (10% F1 score over GNN baselines), and competitive sequence recovery (AAR 0.38) among several CDR-H3 design baselines.

AI.tldr: ConTact proposes a contact-then-act design for CDR-H3 antibody design that explicitly separates contact identification from sequence optimization, using a three-stage cascade with geometry-aware attention and a contact-focused loss, yielding superior structure quality and epitope awareness on CHIMERA-Bench with competitive sequence recovery.
AI.motivation: Current computational CDR design models conflate identifying CDR-antigen contact positions with selecting amino acids, diluting antigen-specific signals through uniform message passing. A decoupled, physics-informed approach could improve binding specificity and structural fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce ConTact with three cascaded stages: (1) learn surface complementarity fingerprints, (2) predict CDR-antigen contacts, (3) inject contact-gated antigen features into the sequence head. Use a distance-biased cross-attention module to encode geometric priors favoring spatial neighbors and a contact-weighted cross-entropy loss to focus gradients on binding-critical positions.
AI.result: On CHIMERA-Bench, ConTact achieves best structural quality (7% RMSD improvement over the next-best baseline), best epitope awareness (10% F1 score over GNN baselines), and competitive sequence recovery (AAR 0.38) among several CDR-H3 design baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly decoupling contact inference from sequence design with geometry-informed signaling and a contact-focused loss enhances CDR-H3 design performance in structure, epitope targeting, and signal efficiency.

title: Quantifying Full-Body Immersion
comment: This manuscript is under consideration for possible publication in the Nature. Copyright may be transferred to Nature if the manuscript is accepted for publication, without further notice
summary: Humanity is at the forefront of yet another digital revolution, where the lines between real and virtual worlds are dissolving, reshaping how we perceive and interact with our surroundings. In this context, we introduce a transformative paradigm for immersive virtual experiences centered around whole-body kinetic interactions. Our approach redefines immersion through three distinct levels: audio-visual immersion, capturing sensory realism; physical immersion, delivering haptic feedback; and full-body immersion (FBI), where dynamic bodily interaction integrates seamlessly with virtual environments. At the core of this innovation lies a scalable, distributable platform based on modular robotic surface units inspired by the adaptive designs of nature. These units enable the rendering of immersive environments at any scale, from intimate personal experiences to expansive multi-user settings, dynamically adapting to interactions in real-time. The modular system distributes force, shape, and motion feedback throughout entire spaces, replicating the physical characteristics of the environment and enabling new depth of engagement through FBI. By combining scalability, adaptability, and dynamic physical engagement, this framework bridges the gap between real and virtual worlds. It offers an unprecedented level of immersion where users can engage their entire bodies in symbiotic interactions with the virtual space. This work not only advances immersive technology but also redefines how humans and virtual environments coexist, setting a foundation for a new era of human-environment synthesis.

AI.tldr: A scalable modular robotic platform enables whole-body immersive experiences with distributed haptic and environmental feedback, supporting scalable, multi-user FBI in virtual environments.
AI.motivation: Rising digital convergence demands deeper immersion that engages the entire body and spaces, overcoming limitations of localized haptics and fixed setups to enable scalable, real-time interaction across intimate to large environments.
AI.method: Conceptual and architectural design of modular robotic surface units inspired by adaptive natural systems; a platform architecture for distributing force, shape, and motion feedback; real-time interaction and scalability across spaces; orchestration for multi-user FBI.
AI.result: Outlines a cohesive framework and hardware-software stack for scalable immersive environments; demonstrates potential for full-body immersion and distributed feedback across spaces; emphasizes scalability and adaptability, but does not present empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundation for human–environment synthesis, redefining coexistence with virtual spaces and enabling expansive whole-body immersion in immersive technologies.

title: A Camera-Cooperative ISAC Framework for Multimodal Non-Cooperative UAVs Sensing
summary: The detection of non-cooperative unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) presents significant challenges for Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems due to the inherent limitations of single-modal perception and the competition for shared communication and sensing resources. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework that employs multimodal sensing to enable efficient UAV beam steering and tracking. The proposed framework employs cameras for coarse-grained airspace monitoring and utilizes ISAC for fine-grained, high-precision sensing, forming a complementary perception loop that enhances both sensing accuracy and resource efficiency. Within this framework, two key modules are developed: (1) a Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) model that aligns visual and echo-domain features through cross-attention mechanisms, and (2) a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) model that integrates historical multimodal data with current observations for robust state estimation. Extensive evaluations conducted on the DeepSense 6G dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an average reduction of 71% in beam steering overhead and 1.69-11.15% in tracking overhead while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy. The CC-ISAC framework effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance while freeing substantial system resources for additional communication tasks, thereby representing a practical advancement in ISAC system design.

AI.tldr: Introduces Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) for UAV detection using multimodal sensing; reduces beam steering and tracking overhead while preserving angular accuracy; validated on DeepSense 6G with V2EDA and MMFE modules.
AI.motivation: Single-modal sensing in ISAC faces perception limits and causes resource contention between sensing and communication. There is a need for efficient, accurate, and resource-aware UAV detection and tracking.
AI.method: Proposes CC-ISAC framework combining camera-based coarse monitoring with ISAC-based fine sensing. Develops Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) using cross-attention to align visual and echo features, and a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) to fuse historical and current multimodal data for robust state estimation.
AI.result: On the DeepSense 6G dataset, the framework achieves an average 71% reduction in beam steering overhead and 1.69–11.15% reduction in tracking overhead, while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: CC-ISAC effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance and freeing resources for additional communication tasks, representing a practical advancement in ISAC system design.

title: EvoVid: Temporal-Centric Self-Evolution for Video Large Language Models
summary: Recent Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in video reasoning through reinforcement learning (RL). However, existing RL pipelines rely heavily on human-annotated tasks and solutions, making them costly to scale and fundamentally constrained by human expertise. Self-evolving frameworks have recently emerged as a promising alternative through autonomous Questioner-Solver self-play. Unfortunately, these approaches are primarily designed for static modalities such as text and images, fundamentally failing to capture the temporal dynamics that are central to video reasoning. In this work, we propose $\textbf{EvoVid}$, a temporal-centric self-evolving framework that enables Video-LLMs to improve directly from raw, unannotated videos. Specifically, we introduce two complementary temporal-centric rewards: a temporal-aware Questioner reward that encourages temporally dependent question generation through temporal perturbation sensitivity, and a temporal-grounded Solver reward that provides automatic temporal supervision via inherent video segment localization. Extensive experiments across four base models and six benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over both base models and existing self-evolving baselines, achieving competitive performance with supervised methods. These results highlight temporal-centric self-evolution as an effective and scalable paradigm for video understanding and reasoning.

AI.tldr: EvoVid introduces a temporal-centric self-evolving framework for Video-LLMs that learns from raw, unannotated videos using two rewards—the temporal-aware Questioner reward and the temporal-grounded Solver reward—achieving consistent gains across multiple models and benchmarks and competing with supervised methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the heavy reliance on human-annotated tasks in RL-based video reasoning and to capture temporal dynamics essential for video understanding, enabling scalable learning from unlabeled video data.
AI.method: Propose EvoVid with two temporal-centric rewards: (1) temporal-aware Questioner reward that promotes temporally dependent question generation via temporal perturbation sensitivity; (2) temporal-grounded Solver reward that provides automatic temporal supervision through inherent video segment localization. Evaluate across four base models and six benchmarks, comparing against strong baselines and supervised approaches.
AI.result: EvoVid yields consistent improvements over base models and existing self-evolving baselines, and its performance is competitive with supervised methods across the evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Temporal-centric self-evolution is an effective and scalable paradigm for video understanding and reasoning, enabling Video-LLMs to learn effectively from unannotated videos by leveraging temporal cues and supervision.

title: When Are Teacher Tokens Reliable? Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning
comment: Pre-print. Code is available at https://github.com/SaFo-Lab/PW-OPSD
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a student on its own rollouts using a privileged teacher, but its standard objective weights all generated tokens equally, implicitly treating the privileged teacher target as equally reliable at every student-visited prefix. Existing entropy-based OPD methods relax this uniformity by modulating token-level supervision with teacher entropy, but high teacher entropy in reasoning has an ambiguous reliability meaning: it can reflect either non-viable uncertainty or benign solution diversity. To identify this phenomenon, we introduce a branch-viability diagnostic. Specifically, we record next-token alternatives from the privileged-answer teacher prompt, force each alternative after the student prompt plus its on-policy spine prefix, and test whether the resulting student-template continuation recovers the correct answer. On Qwen3-4B, we find that an oriented within-sequence position score is the strongest tested predictor of teacher-token reliability, reaching an area-under-ROC-curve (AUROC) of 0.83; local uncertainty scores are at most 0.57. Motivated by this trajectory-level structure, we propose Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation (PW-OPSD), which applies an increasing position weight while keeping the same student rollout, privileged teacher pass, and clipped forward-KL target as OPSD. In our comprehensive evaluations with different random seeds, the diagnostic-derived PW-OPSD improves AIME 2024 and AIME 2025 Avg@12 by +1.0 and +1.1 points, and a generalization evaluation on two larger-scale models from different families, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Think, also demonstrates consistent aggregate Avg@12 improvements. These results show that teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured and can be utilized without additional teacher computation.

AI.tldr: PW-OPSD: a trajectory-aware token-weighting for on-policy self-distillation that reveals and exploits that teacher-token reliability is trajectory-structured, improving Avg@12 on AIME tasks and generalizing across models.
AI.motivation: Address ambiguity of teacher entropy as a proxy for reliability; identify how reliability varies along the reasoning trajectory; provide a diagnostic to quantify per-token reliability and leverage it.
AI.method: Develop branch-viability diagnostic recording next-token alternatives and test recoverability; derive an oriented within-sequence position score; design PW-OPSD that applies increasing position weights to the standard OPSD with the same rollout, teacher pass, and forward-KL target; evaluate on Qwen3-4B and larger models DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Think across random seeds.
AI.result: PW-OPSD yields +1.0 Avg@12 on AIME 2024 and +1.1 on AIME 2025; cross-model generalization shows consistent Avg@12 gains; AUROC 0.83 for the position-score predictor; local uncertainty scores up to 0.57; implies reliable trajectory-structured teacher-targets without extra teacher computation.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured; position-weighted supervision can exploit it without extra cost, improving generalization and performance across seeds and architectures.

title: Knowledge Graph Re-engineering Along the Ontological Continuum (extended version)
summary: Knowledge graphs have become the primary vehicle for data integration and are critical to the success of modern AI, but the diversity of KG modelling practices, from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies, makes integration and reuse expensive and brittle. This challenge is particularly acute in neuro-symbolic AI, where bridging neural and symbolic components depends on the ability to reengineer KGs to fit new requirements; GenAI now offers unprecedented automation capability, but without a principled understanding of the KG space, such automation remains conceptually ungrounded. We introduce the ontological continuum as that missing conceptualisation, a theoretical construct a theoretical construct whose characterisation framework is defined by two orthogonal distinctions: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances; together these define a vocabulary to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs across the full range of modelling practices. The methodological stance is empirical: rather than prescribing how KGs should be modelled, the continuum aims to define a theory of the existent, derived from observation of real-world KG engineering practices and whose structure can be made formally explicit, for example, through Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). We ground the vision through a case study on provenance knowledge, showing how a single concern manifests differently across the continuum. We articulate five open research challenges and invite the community to develop the ontological continuum as a shared research agenda.

AI.tldr: Proposes ontological continuum to unify KG modelling practices, enabling principled integration and re-use; empirically grounded and FCA-formalizable; illustrated by a provenance knowledge case study; outlines five open challenges and a community-driven agenda.
AI.motivation: KGs vary widely in modelling practices, from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatized ontologies, making integration, reengineering, and reuse expensive. In neuro-symbolic AI and GenAI-driven automation, a principled, common conceptual space is needed to adapt KGs across requirements and tools.
AI.method: Introduce the ontological continuum with two orthogonal distinctions: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances. Adopt an empirical stance to describe the existing KG engineering landscape rather than prescribe norms. Formalize the continuum concept using Formal Concept Analysis (FCA) and illustrate with a provenance knowledge case study. Identify five open research challenges and invite community collaboration to develop the continuum as a shared research agenda.
AI.result: A conceptual framework—the ontological continuum—for describing and transforming KGs across diverse modelling practices. A formalizable, FCA-ready articulation anchored by empirical observation; demonstrated via a provenance knowledge case study. Five open challenges provide a roadmap for future work and community engagement.
AI.conclusion: The ontological continuum offers a unifying theory of KG modelling practice, enabling principled comparison, navigation, and transformation across diverse approaches. It sets a research agenda for the community to validate, refine, and extend the framework, particularly in neuro-symbolic AI and GenAI-enabled KG automation.

title: MLLMs Know When Before Speaking: Revealing and Recovering Temporal Grounding via Attention Cues
summary: Video temporal grounding (VTG), which localizes the start and end times of a queried event in an untrimmed video, is a key test of whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) understand not only what happens but also when it happens. Although modern MLLMs describe video content fluently, their timestamp predictions remain unreliable, while existing remedies either require costly post-training on temporal annotations or rely on coarse training-free heuristics. In this work, we probe the cross-modal attention of MLLMs and uncover a perception-generation gap. Our key finding is that MLLMs often know the target interval during prefill, but lose this signal when generating the final answer. In the prefill stage, a sparse set of attention heads, which we call \emph{Temporal Grounding Heads} (TG-Heads), concentrates query-to-video attention on the ground-truth interval. During autoregressive decoding, however, the answer tokens shift attention away from this interval toward visually salient but query-irrelevant segments. This observation motivates an inference-time read-then-regenerate framework. We first convert TG-Head prefill attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal and extract the high-attention interval it highlights. We then re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval, using video cropping or attention masking to suppress distractors. Without parameter updates and architectural changes, our framework consistently improves MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B on three VTG benchmarks, with gains of up to +3.5 mIoU. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/mllmsknowwhen.github.io/.

AI.tldr: MLLMs exhibit a perception-generation gap for video temporal grounding (VTG): they can locate the interval in prefilling but lose this signal during autoregressive generation. The paper proposes an inference-time read-then-regenerate framework that leverages TG-Heads to extract a high-attention ground-truth interval and grounds the answer within that interval by cropping/masking, yielding consistent VTG gains without training.
AI.motivation: Address unreliable timestamp predictions in multimodal LLMs for VTG and the observed drop from prefill to generation, aiming to achieve accurate grounding without costly supervision or retraining.
AI.method: Identify Temporal Grounding Heads (TG-Heads) which concentrate attention on the ground-truth interval during prefill. Convert this prefill attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal to extract a high-attention interval. Re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval via video cropping or attention masking, in a read-then-regenerate, parameter-free inference process.
AI.result: Achieves consistent VTG improvements on MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B across three benchmarks, with gains up to 3.5 mIoU. Demonstrates that an inference-time read-then-regenerate approach can recover the correct interval without model updates.
AI.conclusion: The approach reveals a practical solution to the perception-generation gap in VTG by exploiting prefill attention to ground the final answer within the correct interval, offering performance gains without architectural or parameter changes.

title: AgForce Enables Antigen-conditioned Generative Antibody Design
summary: Antibody design methods condition on antigen structure to generate complementarity-determining regions (CDR), yet a systematic evaluation of baseline methods reveals that they largely ignore the antigen input. We identify three failure modes that explain this behavior. Antigen blindness arises because models derive predictions from antibody framework context rather than antigen information, producing nearly identical CDRs regardless of the target. Vocabulary collapse reduces predicted amino acids to three to five per position, far below the ground truth distribution in native sequences. Moreover, any model trained with standard per-position cross-entropy converges to the positional marginal distribution, making it provably unable to produce antigen-specific sequence predictions. We propose a novel encoder-decoder architecture called AgForce, that uses a graph neural network (GNN) as the encoder and specialized decoders for sequence-structure co-design. Specifically, we apply framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross attention that prevent the antibody shortcut path. In the decoder, a Mixture Density Network (MDN) sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL) replaces the cross-entropy objective with a multi-component distribution whose optimal solution differs from the positional marginal. An antigen cycle consistency head routes gradients through the sequence decoder, forcing predicted distributions to encode antigen identity. AgForce achieves the best binding quality and sequence recovery simultaneously on the CHIMERA-Bench dataset, improving amino acid recovery by 8% over the strongest sequence baseline while surpassing the baselines across all interface metrics, and nearly doubling the effective vocabulary of GNN methods. The source code is available at: https://github.com/mansoor181/ag-force.git

AI.tldr: Introduces AgForce, an antigen-aware antibody design model using a GNN encoder, specialized decoders, and an MDN-based sequence head with aMCL and cycle-consistency to overcome antigen blindness and vocabulary collapse; achieves superior binding and sequence recovery on CHIMERA-Bench.
AI.motivation: To overcome three failure modes that plague antigen-conditioned antibody design: (i) antigen blindness where models rely on antibody context rather than antigen input, (ii) vocabulary collapse reducing amino-acid diversity, and (iii) standard cross-entropy converging to positional marginals, hindering antigen-specific sequence generation.
AI.method: Proposes AgForce: a graph neural network (GNN) encoder with framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross attention to block shortcut paths. Decoder includes a Mixture Density Network (MDN) sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL). An antigen cycle-consistency head routes gradients through the sequence decoder to enforce antigen identity in predictions."
AI.result: On CHIMERA-Bench, AgForce yields the best binding quality and sequence recovery, improving amino acid recovery by 8% over the strongest sequence baseline and surpassing all baselines across interface metrics, while nearly doubling the effective vocabulary size for GNN-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The architecture successfully addresses the identified failure modes and delivers superior antigen-aware antibody design performance; code is released at the provided GitHub repository for reproducibility.

title: Decoupling Ego-Motion from Target Dynamics via Dual-Interval Motion Cues for UAV Detection
summary: Object detection from Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is challenged by severe ego-motion, camera jitter, and large scale variations. While modern detectors perform well on static images, their direct application to UAV video often fails, particularly for small objects in dynamic scenes. Existing motion-based methods either rely on computationally expensive optical flow or use single-interval differencing, which is sensitive to jitter and limited in capturing diverse motion patterns. We propose a vision-only motion-guided detection framework that decouples target motion from camera-induced disturbances. A homography-based Global Motion Compensation (GMC) first aligns adjacent frames. We then introduce a Dual-Interval Motion Extraction strategy that captures both short-term and long-term motion cues. To integrate these cues, a lightweight Motion-Guided Attention (MGA) module enhances feature representations within a Feature Pyramid Network. Experiments on the VisDrone-VID dataset demonstrate consistent improvements over a strong YOLOv8 baseline under severe ego-motion. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of the dual-interval design and the proposed motion-guided attention mechanism.

AI.tldr: A vision-only, motion-guided detection framework for UAV videos that decouples target motion from camera disturbances using (1) homography-based Global Motion Compensation, (2) Dual-Interval Motion Extraction for short- and long-term cues, and (3) a lightweight Motion-Guided Attention module within a Feature Pyramid Network, yielding improved detections over YOLOv8 on VisDrone-VID under severe ego-motion.
