AI.motivation: UAV object detection is severely affected by ego-motion, jitter, and large object scale variation. Existing motion methods are either computationally heavy (optical flow) or rely on brittle single-interval differencing, limiting performance in dynamic scenes.
AI.method: Apply homography-based Global Motion Compensation to align adjacent frames, extract motion using Dual-Interval Motion Extraction to capture both short-term and long-term cues, and fuse these cues via a lightweight Motion-Guided Attention module integrated into a Feature Pyramid Network for improved representations. Evaluation on VisDrone-VID against a strong YOLOv8 baseline with ablations.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over the strong YOLOv8 baseline under severe ego-motion; ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the dual-interval design and the motion-guided attention mechanism.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, vision-only motion-guided framework effectively decouples scene and camera motion, improves small-object detection under UAV ego-motion, and demonstrates the value of combining GMC, dual-interval motion cues, and MGA within a unified detection pipeline.

title: MPDocBench-Parse: Benchmarking Practical Multi-page Document Parsing
summary: Document parsing converts visually rich documents into machine-readable structured representations, forming a crucial foundation for information systems. Although many benchmarks have been proposed for document parsing, they remain inadequate for realistic scenarios. Existing benchmarks either focus on specific tasks or assess only single-page, text-centric settings, making them insufficient for practical multi-page parsing. Moreover, they lack fine-grained evaluation of semantic continuity, hierarchical structure recovery, and visual content preservation. To address these gaps, we propose MPDocBench-Parse, a benchmark for multi-page document parsing in real-world applications. It contains 433 manually annotated documents with 3,246 pages, covering 15 document types in English and Chinese, with diverse layout styles, and supports document-level end-to-end evaluation. We further design a comprehensive protocol for content fidelity and logical structure, covering text, table, and formula recognition, truncated text and table merging, figure extraction, reading order, and heading hierarchy recovery. Experiments show that, while existing models perform well on basic text extraction, they still suffer clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery. MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation for advancing document parsing toward more realistic scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduces MPDocBench-Parse, a multi-page document parsing benchmark with 433 annotated documents (3,246 pages) across 15 document types in English and Chinese, enabling end-to-end document-level evaluation for text, tables, formulas, figures, reading order, and heading hierarchy; reveals gaps in current models' handling of semantic continuity, visual content, and hierarchical structure.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks are limited to single-page, text-centric tasks and do not evaluate semantic continuity, visual content preservation, or hierarchical structure in realistic multi-page parsing; a realistic, end-to-end benchmark is needed for real-world applications.
AI.method: Create MPDocBench-Parse dataset with manual annotations: 433 documents, 3,246 pages, 15 types, English/Chinese, diverse layouts; design an evaluation protocol for content fidelity and logical structure covering text, tables, formulas recognition; handle truncated text and table merging; figure extraction; reading order; heading hierarchy; and document-level end-to-end evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show that existing models perform reasonably on basic text extraction but exhibit clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery; MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation to advance document parsing toward more realistic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MPDocBench-Parse advances the field by offering a comprehensive, multi-page, multilingual benchmark with a robust evaluation protocol, enabling better assessment and development of document parsers for realistic, real-world scenarios.

title: Bounding-Box Trajectories Matter for Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Video anomaly detection is critical for public safety and security, yet remains highly challenging despite extensive research due to large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and scene dynamics. Among existing approaches, human pose-based methods have emerged as a major line of research, showing strong performance since many anomalies in public datasets involve humans and pose representations are robust to appearance changes while providing compact motion descriptions. However, these methods often overlook bounding-box trajectories, although such information is inherently available in pose-based pipelines. In this paper, we explicitly leverage these trajectories as a primary anomaly cue. We present TrajVAD, a framework that models multi-class bounding-box trajectories using normalizing flows to learn normal kinematic patterns. Its trajectory-only variant (TrajVAD-T) eliminates pose estimation and surpasses all compared pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech in AP (87.7%), while achieving the best results on MSAD. An extended version (TrajVAD-P) incorporates pose information and further improves performance to 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech, highlighting bounding-box trajectories as an effective yet underexplored modality for video anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Trajectory-based anomaly detection for videos using normalizing flows over multi-class bounding-box trajectories; TrajVAD-T (trajectory-only) outperforms pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech (AP 87.7%) and MSAD; TrajVAD-P (with pose) further improves ShanghaiTech metrics (AUROC 88.6%, AP 90.9%).
AI.motivation: Public video anomaly detection is challenging due to appearance, viewpoint, and scene dynamics. Pose-based methods, while effective, often ignore bounding-box trajectories which are readily available and informative for motion patterns.
AI.method: Model normal kinematic patterns of bounding-box trajectories with normalizing flows to learn normal behavior. TrajVAD-T uses trajectories only; TrajVAD-P augments with pose information.
AI.result: TrajVAD-T surpasses pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech in AP (87.7%) and achieves best results on MSAD. TrajVAD-P achieves 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech.
AI.conclusion: Bounding-box trajectories constitute an effective and underexplored modality for video anomaly detection. Combining trajectories with pose information yields state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.

title: $\textit{BlockFormer}$ : Transformer-based inference from interaction maps
summary: Inference from interaction maps, such as centromere identification from genome-wide chromosome conformation capture techniques -- notably Hi-C -- can be formulated as a generic inverse problem: infer a set of parameters given a map summarizing pairwise interactions between entities through blocks of variable numbers and sizes. In this work, we introduce a data-driven approach that leverages shared structure between these maps, such as global alignment between localized patterns, while handling the variability in number and size of entities arising in real-world data. Our approach relies on a transformer architecture capable of handling such variability and a custom simulator to generate abundant, yet computationally cheap synthetic data for training. Applied to the problem of centromere localization, the method accurately recovers their genomic positions across a wide range of species of various genome sizes.

AI.tldr: A data-driven transformer-based approach solves centromere localization from Hi-C–type interaction maps by exploiting shared structure and global alignment, using synthetic data for training to handle variable entity counts and sizes across species.
AI.motivation: Inference from interaction maps is an ill-posed inverse problem with variable block structures; a robust method is needed to locate centromeres across diverse genomes despite heterogeneity in block numbers and sizes.
AI.method: A transformer architecture designed to handle variable numbers and sizes of entities, trained on abundant synthetic data generated by a custom simulator that captures essential interaction patterns and global alignments across blocks.
AI.result: The approach accurately recovers centromere genomic positions across a wide range of species with different genome sizes, demonstrating strong generalization and robustness to variability.
AI.conclusion: A data-driven, transformer-based framework can effectively localize centromeres from Hi-C-like maps by leveraging shared global patterns and a cheap synthetic-data generator, offering a scalable solution for cross-species genome analysis.

title: SE3Kit: A Lightweight Python Library for Specialized Geometric Primitives in Robotics
summary: The Python robotics ecosystem faces a challenge: while many libraries exist for rigid body transformations, few are both lightweight and mathematically strict. This paper introduces SE3Kit, a lightweight Python library efficient operations on the Special Euclidean Group SE(3) and the Special Orthogonal Group SO(3). Unlike established frameworks that require heavy dependencies (e.g., SpatialMath, PyPose) or general tools that lack robotics-specific features (e.g., SciPy), SE3Kit targets the gap between these extremes. It is designed for embedded deployment, rapid prototyping, and education while providing rigorous mathematical implementation. It provides a pure-Python, NumPy-only implementation of Lie Group operations, without the overhead of deep learning or other visualization software.

AI.tldr: SE3Kit provides a lightweight, pure-Python NumPy implementation of Lie group operations for SE(3) and SO(3), bridging the gap between heavy robotics frameworks and general-purpose math libraries.
AI.motivation: The Python robotics ecosystem lacks a small, rigorous, dependency-light library for rigid-body transformations, suitable for embedded deployment, rapid prototyping, and education.
AI.method: Develop a pure-Python, NumPy-only library that implements Lie Group operations on SE(3) and SO(3) without heavy dependencies or DL/visualization tooling, prioritizing mathematical correctness and lightweight deployment.
AI.result: Introduction of SE3Kit as a lightweight, mathematically strict library providing SE(3)/SO(3) operations with minimal dependencies and no deep learning or visualization software.
AI.conclusion: SE3Kit fills the gap between bulky robotics frameworks and general-purpose math tools, enabling accessible, rigorous Lie group computations for resource-constrained scenarios.

title: ExComm: Exploration-Stage Communication for Error-Resilient Agentic Test-Time Scaling
summary: A common failure mode in long-horizon agentic test-time scaling is error propagation, where factual errors or invalid deductions introduced at intermediate steps persist in the agent's belief state and contaminate later reasoning. Existing test-time scaling methods provide limited control over this process, as they often rely on agents to detect their own mistakes, select among flawed trajectories, or refine solutions only after errors have already shaped the reasoning path. We propose ExComm, a communication protocol for exploration-stage agentic test-time scaling. ExComm is motivated by the empirical observation that the majority of intermediate errors in parallel agentic reasoning produce detectable cross-agent factual conflicts. Leveraging the iterative structure of agentic workflows, ExComm periodically audits agent belief states to detect such conflicts, resolves them through a dedicated tool-based verification loop, and returns concise, targeted feedback to the involved agents. Corrections are incorporated through soft belief updates, which append verified feedback rather than overwriting existing beliefs. Furthermore, to prevent collapsing trajectory diversity due to communication, ExComm further introduces a trajectory diversification module that redirects redundant trajectories toward orthogonal strategies. Experiments on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and GAIA with Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B show that ExComm consistently outperforms strong test-time scaling baselines, achieving average performance gains of 5.7% and 5.0% over the best-performing baselines, respectively. Further analyses demonstrate improved error recovery, favorable scaling behavior, stronger diversity than adapted communication baselines, and the best performance-cost trade-off among the evaluated methods.

AI.tldr: ExComm is a communication-based protocol to mitigate error propagation in parallel agentic test-time reasoning by periodic belief audits, cross-agent conflict resolution via a tool-based verification loop, soft belief updates, and trajectory diversification, achieving ~5% gains on AIME/GAIA benchmarks over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the pervasive issue of error propagation in long-horizon, parallel agentic reasoning during test-time scaling by enabling explicit cross-agent auditing and targeted corrections rather than relying on agents to self-detect and correct errors after they have polluted the reasoning path.
AI.method: Iterative belief audits that detect cross-agent factual conflicts, a dedicated tool-based verification loop to resolve conflicts, soft updates that append verified feedback to beliefs, and a trajectory diversification module that redirects redundant trajectories toward orthogonal strategies; evaluated on AIME 2024/2025 and GAIA using Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B.
AI.result: ExComm consistently outperforms strong test-time scaling baselines, with average performance gains of 5.7% and 5.0% over the best-performing baselines in respective settings; improvements in error recovery, scaling behavior, and diversity; favorable cost-performance trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Explicit cross-agent communication, verification, and diversity mechanisms can substantially improve robustness and efficiency of long-horizon agentic test-time scaling, with potential applicability beyond the evaluated benchmarks; future work could explore audit frequency, toolset expansion, and dynamics of soft belief updating.

title: Dual-Integrated Low-Latency Single-Lens Infrared Computational Imaging for Object Detection
comment: 15 pages, 11 figures; supplementary material: 3 pages, 2 figures
summary: Computational imaging enables compact infrared systems, but deep-learning pipelines that combine image reconstruction and object detection often introduce substantial inference latency. Most existing acceleration strategies compress the reconstruction network while overlooking physical priors from the optical path, leaving a trade-off between accuracy and speed. We present Physics-aware Dual-Integrated Network (PDI-Net), a low-latency framework that integrates infrared reconstruction with object detection and further embeds optical priors into the learning process. PDI-Net uses a supervised U-Net during training, while a semi-U-Net encoder shares features directly with a YOLO-based detector during inference, avoiding full image reconstruction. To bridge the gap between fidelity-oriented reconstruction features and detection-oriented semantics, we introduce a physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge), which uses field-dependent point spread function priors to adaptively modulate multiscale convolutional branches. A physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline is also developed for training and validation. The method is deployed on a single-lens infrared camera, reducing system weight by about 50% compared with traditional multi-lens designs. On the M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions, PDI-Net reduces inference time by 84.06% compared with the Rec+Det with pruning strategy while improving mAP@0.5:0.95 by 5.07%. These results demonstrate compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms.

AI.tldr: PDI-Net integrates physics-aware priors into a dual-network framework for fast infrared imaging and detection, achieving large latency reductions and accuracy gains on a single-lens system.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency in deep-learning pipelines for infrared imaging while preserving accuracy by leveraging optical priors, enabling a compact single-lens system.
AI.method: Train a supervised U-Net for reconstruction but deploy a semi-U-Net encoder that shares features with a YOLO-based detector during inference. Introduce a physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge) using field-dependent PSF priors to modulate multiscale branches. Use a physics-informed optical degradation simulation for training and validation.
AI.result: Single-lens IR camera with ~50% weight reduction; on M3FD under low-SNR, inference time reduced by 84.06% vs Rec+Det with pruning; mAP@0.5:0.95 improved by 5.07%.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time detection on resource-constrained platforms by fusing reconstruction priors with detection semantics.

title: Alike Parts: A Feature-Informed Approach to Local and Global Prototype Explanations
comment: Accepted for publication in International Journal of Applied Mathematics and Computer Science (IJAMCS)
summary: Prototype-based explanations offer an intuitive, example-based approach to support the interpretability of machine learning black box classifiers but often lack feature-level granularity. We introduce a framework that integrates feature importance at two levels to address this gap. First, for local explanations, we propose \textit{alike parts}: a method that uses feature importance scores to highlight the most relevant, shared feature subsets between a classified instance and its nearest prototype, guiding user attention. Second, we augment the global prototype selection objective function with a feature importance term to actively promote diversity in the feature attributions of the selected prototypes. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that this augmented selection process maintains or, in some cases, increases the prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, suggesting that feature diversity does not compromise model fidelity.

AI.tldr: A two-level enhancement of prototype-based explanations: locally via alike parts that highlight shared feature subsets between instances and prototypes, and globally by adding a feature-importance term to prototype selection to diversify attributions; experiments on six datasets show maintained or improved surrogate fidelity with diverse feature attributions.
AI.motivation: Prototype-based explanations often lack feature-level granularity; integrating feature importance at local and global levels can improve interpretability by highlighting relevant features and encouraging diverse attributions without sacrificing fidelity.
AI.method: 1) Local explanations: introduce 'alike parts' that use feature importance scores to identify and highlight the most relevant shared feature subsets between a classified instance and its nearest prototype. 2) Global explanations: augment the prototype selection objective with a feature importance term to actively promote diversity in the feature attributions of the selected prototypes.
AI.result: Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that the augmented prototype selection maintains or increases the prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, indicating that feature diversity can be achieved without compromising fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The framework integrates feature importance at local and global levels to improve interpretability of prototype-based explanations, providing more granular and diverse attributions while preserving model fidelity.

title: Symmetries Here and There, Combined Everywhere: Cross-space Symmetry Compositions in Robotics
summary: Robots exhibit a rich variety of symmetries arising from their mechanical structure and the properties of their tasks. Although many robotics problems exhibit several symmetries simultaneously, existing approaches typically treat them in isolation, failing to exploit their combined potential. This paper introduces cross-space symmetry compositions, a framework for learning robot policies that are jointly equivariant to multiple symmetries across configuration and task spaces. Leveraging the differential-geometric structure of the forward kinematics map, we both descend symmetries from configuration to task space and lift symmetries from task to configuration space, enabling their composition within a unified representation space. We validate our framework on simulated and real-world experiments on a dual-arm robot, demonstrating that jointly leveraging multiple symmetries yields improved generalization.

AI.tldr: Cross-space symmetry compositions for learning robot policies equivariant to multiple symmetries across configuration and task spaces; uses differential-geometric forward kinematics to descend/ascend symmetries and compose them in a unified representation; validated on a dual-arm robot with improved generalization.
AI.motivation: Robotics tasks exhibit multiple, simultaneously present symmetries. Treating them in isolation underutilizes their combined potential and hinders generalization; a framework is needed to jointly exploit symmetries across configuration and task spaces.
AI.method: Introduce a cross-space symmetry composition framework that (i) descends symmetries from configuration space to task space and (ii) lifts symmetries from task space to configuration space using the differential-geometric structure of the forward kinematics map, enabling their unified, joint equivariance in representation space for policy learning.
AI.result: Demonstrated improved generalization in both simulated and real-world experiments on a dual-arm robot, showing benefits of jointly leveraging multiple symmetries over single-symmetry baselines.
AI.conclusion: Jointly leveraging multiple symmetries via cross-space composition yields stronger generalization and a unified representation space, with potential to extend to additional symmetry types and tasks.

title: ArborKV: Structure-Aware KV Cache Management for Scaling Tree-based LLM Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in LLM reasoning has increasingly shifted from single-pass generation to explicit search over intermediate reasoning states. Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) organizes inference to tree-structured search with branching and backtracking, but it substantially amplifies the Key--Value (KV) cache: retaining KV states for a frontier of partial trajectories quickly becomes a memory bottleneck that limits throughput and constrains search depth and width under fixed hardware budgets. We address this challenge by observing that KV reuse in ToT-style inference is governed by search dynamics: near-term decoding depends primarily on the active branch and its ancestors, whereas inactive subtrees have low short-term reuse probability yet must remain recoverable for backtracking. Motivated by this, we propose ArborKV, a structure-aware eviction framework that couples a lightweight value estimator with a tree-aware allocation policy, and performs purely token-extractive eviction with lazy rehydration to support revisits. Experiments on ToT-style reasoning benchmarks show that ArborKV achieves up to ~4x peak KV-memory reduction while preserving near-full-retention accuracy, enabling larger search configurations under fixed device budgets that would otherwise run out of memory.

AI.tldr: ArborKV reduces KV-memory in Tree-of-Thoughts reasoning by about 4x with negligible accuracy loss, via a structure-aware eviction strategy and lazy rehydration.
AI.motivation: ToT-style inference incurs large KV caches as partial trajectories are stored for backtracking; memory bottlenecks limit search depth/width under fixed hardware budgets.
AI.method: Introduce ArborKV: a structure-aware eviction framework that uses a lightweight value estimator and tree-aware allocation; performs token-extractive eviction and lazy rehydration to enable revisits.
AI.result: On ToT-style reasoning benchmarks, achieves up to ~4x peak KV-memory reduction while preserving near-full-retention accuracy; enables larger search configurations within fixed device budgets.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware eviction with lazy rehydration effectively mitigates memory bottlenecks in ToT-style inference without substantial performance loss, broadening feasible search configurations.

title: Foresee-to-Ground: From Predictive Temporal Perception to Evidence-Driven Reasoning for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Current Video-LLM approaches for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) typically rely on direct timestamp generation from an unstructured visual-token stream, often leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries. To address this, we propose Foresee-to-Ground (F2G), a framework that reformulates VTG as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem. F2G integrates Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning: it learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments, and exposes these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units that bind boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses. By decoupling event identification from precise boundary measurement, F2G stabilizes grounding and makes predictions verifiable. Extensive experiments demonstrate that F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

AI.tldr: Reframes Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) as an Identify-then-Measure problem via Foresee-to-Ground (F2G). It combines Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning to build a video-wide pool of candidate segments and presents them to LLMs as citable evidence, decoupling event identification from boundary measurement. This yields more stable grounding, improved accuracy, and robust transfer across backbones while preserving general video understanding.
AI.motivation: Current VTG methods typically generate direct timestamps from unstructured visual tokens, which leads to brittle boundaries and inconsistent results. A verifiable, evidence-based grounding framework is needed to stabilize predictions and facilitate cross-model transfer.
AI.method: F2G learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to form a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments. These segments are exposed to the LLM as citable evidence units that link boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses, implementing a two-stage Identify-then-Measure paradigm. The approach decouples identifying an event from precise boundary localization, enabling verification and improved grounding stability. The framework integrates Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning to support this process.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent gains in grounding accuracy across diverse VTG benchmarks, robust transfer when paired with different Video-LLM backbones, and preservation of general video understanding capabilities.
AI.conclusion: F2G stabilizes VTG grounding by decoupling event identification from boundary measurement and by making predictions verifiable through a citable evidence mechanism. The method demonstrates strong, cross-benchmark improvements and adaptability to various backbones, indicating practical viability for robust video understanding tasks.

title: Dropout Universality: Scaling Laws and Optimal Scheduling at the Edge-of-Chaos
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 36 pages, 11 figures
summary: We develop a mean-field theory of dropout as a perturbation of critical signal propagation at the edge of chaos. Dropout shifts the perfect-alignment fixed point, making the depth scale for information propagation finite even at critical initialization. We derive critical and crossover scaling laws for correlation decay and establish that smooth activations and kinked, ReLU-like activations constitute distinct universality classes, with different critical exponents and a universal two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength. The distinction traces to the analytic structure of the correlation map: smooth activations admit a Taylor expansion near perfect alignment, while kinked activations develop a branch point with universal non-analyticity. As a corollary, the framework yields saturated dropout profiles under fixed budget; a rank-flow tie-breaker then selects front-loaded schedules, substantially reducing held-out test loss at no extra computational cost, with accuracy gains as a consistent secondary effect. We test the predictions in MLPs and Vision Transformers and discuss CNN/ResNet extensions.

AI.tldr: Mean-field dropout near the edge of chaos acts as a perturbation of the critical signal-propagation fixed point, generating finite depth-scale for information propagation and revealing two universality classes (smooth vs kinked activations) with universal two-parameter scaling; yields practical dropout schedules (rank-flow tie-breaker) that reduce test loss with no extra compute; validated in MLPs and Vision Transformers, with discussion for CNN/ResNet.
AI.motivation: To understand how dropout interacts with signal propagation at initialization and during training, derive universal scaling laws, and connect theory to practical dropout scheduling across architectures.
AI.method: Mean-field analysis of the correlation map near perfect alignment, introducing dropout as a perturbation. Distinguishes smooth activations (Taylor-expandable) from kinked activations (branch-point non-analyticity). Derives critical and crossover scaling laws for correlation decay, and a universal two-parameter collapse in detuning and dropout strength. Proposes saturated dropout profiles and a rank-flow tie-breaker for front-loaded schedules. Empirically tests predictions in MLPs and Vision Transformers; discusses extensions to CNN/ResNet.
AI.result: Derived scaling laws for correlation decay; established two universality classes with distinct exponents; demonstrated universal two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength; proposed and validated practical dropout schedules (front-loaded under fixed budget) via rank-flow; observed reduced held-out loss with accuracy gains in tested models; framework applicable to MLPs and ViTs with discussion of CNN/ResNet extensions.
AI.conclusion: Dropout is a finite-depth perturbation of critical signal propagation at initialization, giving universal, activation-structure–dependent scaling behavior. Smooth activations and kinked/ReLU-like activations define distinct universality classes. The theory yields practical, schedulable dropout profiles with performance gains and broad applicability across architectures, motivating extensions to CNNs and ResNets.

title: Scout-Assisted Planning for Heterogeneous Robot Teams under Partially Known Environments
summary: Autonomous robot teams navigating partially known environments face costly backtracking when ground robots encounter blocked roads that are only revealed upon physical traversal. We address this with Scout-Assisted Planning, a heterogeneous planning framework in which scouting Unmanned Aerial Vehicles proactively gather environmental information to improve Unmanned Ground Vehicle navigation. To focus scouting on the most consequential edges, we propose Information Gain-based Action Pruning, which scores candidate scouting actions by their expected impact on ground robot behavior. Since exact Information Gain-based Action Pruning computation is prohibitively expensive, we develop a Graph Neural Network based model that predicts information gain values directly from graph structure and belief state, reducing planning time to real-time levels without sacrificing solution quality. Experiments across three environment types show that SAP with Information Gain Action Pruning reduces ground robot travel cost by 31.9--37.7% over the Canadian Traveler Problem baseline, and outperforms proximity-based scouting guidance by an additional 8--14%, confirming that principled information-gain-guided scouting is both more effective and computationally feasible for real-world deployment

AI.tldr: Scout-Assisted Planning (SAP) uses UAV scouts to guide ground robot navigation via Information Gain-based Action Pruning; a Graph Neural Network predicts information gain from graph structure and belief state, enabling real-time planning.
AI.motivation: Autonomous robot teams incur costly backtracking in partially known environments because blocked edges are only revealed through traversal; proactive, information-rich scouting can substantially reduce travel costs.
AI.method: Introduce SAP, a heterogeneous planning framework where scouting UAVs proactively gather environmental information. Use Information Gain-based Action Pruning to score candidate scouting actions by their expected impact on ground robot behavior. Since exact information gain computation is expensive, train a Graph Neural Network to predict information gain directly from the graph and belief state, enabling real-time planning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across three environment types shows SAP with Information Gain Action Pruning reduces ground robot travel cost by 31.9–37.7% versus the Canadian Traveler Problem baseline and outperforms proximity-based scouting guidance by an additional 8–14%.
AI.conclusion: Principled information-gain-guided scouting is both more effective and computationally feasible for real-world deployment.

title: Perception or Prejudice: Can MLLMs Go Beyond First Impressions of Personality?
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed in human-facing roles where personality perception is critical, yet existing benchmarks evaluate this capability solely on numerical Big Five score prediction, leaving open whether models truly perceive personality through behavioral understanding or merely prejudge through superficial pattern matching. We address this gap with three contributions. (i) A new task: we formalize Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR), which requires MLLMs to anchor each Big Five rating in observable evidence through a chain of rating, reasoning, and grounding. (ii) A new dataset: we release MM-OCEAN (1,104 videos, 5,320 MCQs), produced by a multi-agent pipeline with human verification, with timestamped behavioral observations, evidence-grounded trait analyses, and seven categories of cue-grounding MCQs. (iii) Benchmark and analysis: we design a three-tier evaluation (rating, reasoning, grounding) plus four sample-level failure-mode metrics: Prejudice Rate (PR), Confabulation Rate (CR), Integration-failure Rate (IR), and Holistic-grounding Rate (HR), and benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed, 14 open). The analysis uncovers a striking Prejudice Gap: across the field, 51% of correct ratings are not grounded in retrieved cues, and the Holistic-Grounding Rate spans only 0-33.5%. These findings expose a disconnect between getting the right score and reasoning for the right reason, charting a roadmap for grounded social cognition in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Formalizes Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR) for Multimodal LLMs, introducing MM-OCEAN dataset with 1,104 videos and 5,320 MCQs, plus a three-tier evaluation and four failure-mode metrics. Finds a large prejudice gap: many correct ratings are not grounded and holistic grounding is low, highlighting the need for grounded social reasoning in MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for personality perception in MLLMs rely on predicting numerical Big Five scores, which may reflect pattern matching rather than grounded understanding of behavior. There is a need to require models to justify ratings with observable evidence.
AI.method: Define GPR task requiring rating, reasoning, and grounding anchored to observable cues. Build MM-OCEAN dataset via multi-agent pipeline with human verification, providing timestamped observations and seven categories of cue-grounding MCQs. Implement a three-tier evaluation (rating, reasoning, grounding) and four failure-mode metrics (Prejudice Rate, Confabulation Rate, Integration-failure Rate, Holistic-grounding Rate). Benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed, 14 open).
AI.result: Across 27 MLLMs, the study finds that 51% of correct ratings are not grounded in retrieved cues (Prejudice Gap), and Holistic-Grounding Rate ranges from 0 to 33.5%, revealing a disconnect between achieving the right score and providing the right justification. The dataset and metrics enable diagnosing grounded social cognition in MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: The work exposes a mismatch between correct Big Five ratings and grounded reasoning, providing a roadmap for developing truly grounded social cognition in MLLMs and highlighting the need for improved integration of observable behavioral evidence in personality reasoning.

title: Video as Natural Augmentation: Towards Unified AI-Generated Image and Video Detection
summary: AI-generated content (AIGC) is rapidly improving, creating an urgent need for detectors that generalize across data sources, deployment pipelines, and visual modalities. A strongly generalizable detector should remain robust under distributional variations. However, we identify a consistent failure mode: SOTA AI-generated image detectors often collapse when applied to frames extracted from videos. Through systematic analysis, we show that this cross-modal gap arises from both entangled synthesis-agnostic video processing shifts, including color conversion, codec compression, resizing, and blur, and model-specific fingerprints introduced by modern video generators. Motivated by these findings, we propose VINA (Video as Natural Augmentation), a unified AIGC detection framework that jointly trains on image and video data. VINA uses video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations and further introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary. Extensive experiments on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks show that VINA delivers bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across nearly all evaluated settings without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

AI.tldr: VINA, a unified cross-modal AIGC detector trained on image and video data with a cross-modal contrastive objective, achieves robust generalization and state-of-the-art results across image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks without dataset-specific tuning.
AI.motivation: Detectors for AI-generated content often fail when applied to video frames due to distributional shifts from video processing and generator fingerprints; robust, generalizable detectors across sources and modalities are urgently needed.
AI.method: Joint image–video training using video frames as natural augmentations and a cross-modal supervised contrastive loss to align image and video representations around a shared real/fake boundary; no heavy, dataset-specific augmentation or tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 14 benchmarks (image, video, in-the-wild) show bidirectional gains, improved robustness and transferability, and state-of-the-art performance across nearly all settings.
AI.conclusion: VINA provides a generalizable, modality-bridging detector that leverages natural video augmentations and cross-modal alignment to improve robustness and transferability without bespoke tuning.

title: EntmaxKV: Support-Aware Decoding for Entmax Attention
summary: Long-context decoding is increasingly limited by KV-cache memory traffic since each generated token attends over a cache whose size grows linearly with context length. Existing sparse decoding methods reduce this cost by selecting subsets of tokens or pages, but are designed for softmax attention, whose dense tails make any truncation discard nonzero probability mass. In contrast, $α$-entmax produces exact zeros, turning sparse decoding from dense-tail approximation into support recovery: if the selected candidates contain the entmax support, sparse decoding remains exact. While recent entmax kernels enable efficient training, they do not address the autoregressive decoding bottleneck, where dense inference still streams the full KV cache before sparsity is known. In this work, we introduce EntmaxKV, an entmax-native sparse decoding framework that exploits sparsity before KV pages are loaded. EntmaxKV combines query-aware page scoring, support-aware candidate selection, and sparse entmax attention. We analyze truncation error through the dropped probability mass $δ$, showing that output error is controlled by $δ$ and vanishes when the entmax support is recovered. We further introduce a Gaussian-aware entmax selector that estimates the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics, adapting the selected budget to the score distribution. Empirically, EntmaxKV drops less probability mass, retains more support tokens, and achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at matched KV budgets. On long-context and language modeling benchmarks, it closely matches full-cache entmax while using a small fraction of the KV cache, achieving up to $3.36\times$ (softmax) and $5.43\times$ (entmax) speedup over full attention baselines at 1M context length. Code available at: https://github.com/deep-spin/entmaxkv.

AI.tldr: EntmaxKV is an entmax-native sparse decoding framework that sparsifies KV-attention before loading cache, enabling exact-sparsity with entmax and delivering substantial speedups for long-context decoding while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-context decoding is bottlenecked by KV-cache memory traffic that grows with context length. Softmax-based sparse methods truncate probability mass but cannot exploit exact zeros. Entmax can produce exact zeros, enabling true sparsity, but decoding bottlenecks remain if sparsity is not exploited early enough.
AI.method: Propose EntmaxKV: (1) query-aware page scoring to rank KV pages prior to loading, (2) support-aware candidate selection to identify tokens within the entmax support, (3) sparse entmax attention to compute over the selected support, (4) Gaussian-aware entmax selector to estimate the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics and adapt the budget. Also provide a truncation-error analysis via dropped mass δ and show error vanishes when entmax support is recovered; evaluate on long-context and language modeling benchmarks against full-cache entmax and softmax-based baselines.
AI.result: EntmaxKV reduces probability mass loss, retains more support tokens, and achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at matched KV budgets. It closely matches full-cache entmax on long-context and language modeling benchmarks while using a small fraction of the KV cache, delivering up to 3.36× speedup over full attention with softmax and 5.43× with entmax at 1M context length.
AI.conclusion: EntmaxKV demonstrates that exploiting entmax sparsity in autoregressive decoding enables efficient long-context generation with minimal accuracy loss, by loading and exploiting sparsity before KV pages are fully loaded and with an adaptive budget via Gaussian-aware selection.

title: Efficient Agentic Reasoning Through Self-Regulated Simulative Planning
comment: Code and model artifacts are available at https://github.com/sailing-lab/sr2am
summary: How should an agent decide when and how to plan? A dominant approach builds agents as reactive policies with adaptive computation (e.g., chain-of-thought), trained end-to-end expecting planning to emerge implicitly. Without control over the presence, structure, or horizon of planning, these systems dramatically increase reasoning length, yielding inefficient token use without reliable accuracy gains. We argue efficient agentic reasoning benefits from decomposing decision-making into three systems: simulative reasoning (System II) grounding deliberation in future-state prediction via a world model; self-regulation (System III) deciding when and how deeply to plan via a learned configurator; and reactive execution (System I) handling fine-grained action. Simulative reasoning provides unified planning across diverse tasks without per-domain engineering, while self-regulation ensures the planner is invoked only when needed. To test this, we develop SR$^2$AM (Self-Regulated Simulative Reasoning Agentic LLM), realizing both as distinct stages within an LLM's chain-of-thought, with the LLM as world model. We explore two instantiations: recording decisions from a prompted multi-module system (v0.1) and reconstructing structured plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs (v1.0), trained via supervised then reinforcement learning (RL). Across math, science, tabular analysis, and web information seeking, v0.1-8B and v1.0-30B achieve Pass@1 competitive with 120-355B and 685B-1T parameter systems respectively, while v1.0-30B uses 25.8-95.3% fewer reasoning tokens than comparable agentic LLMs. RL increases average planning horizon by 22.8% while planning frequency grows only 2.0%, showing it learns to plan further ahead rather than more often. More broadly, learned self-regulation instantiates a principle we expect to extend beyond planning to how agents govern their own learning and adaptation.

AI.tldr: A three-system, self-regulated simulative reasoning framework (SR^2AM) enables efficient, unified planning in LLM-based agents; with two instantiations (v0.1, v1.0), it matches or outperforms much larger models while using far fewer reasoning tokens; RL extends planning horizon and demonstrates scalable self-regulation.
AI.motivation: To overcome inefficiencies of end-to-end reactive policies with uncontrolled planning, by decomposing decision-making into planning (System II), self-regulation (System III), and execution (System I), thereby grounding deliberation in a world model and restricting when/how deeply to plan.
AI.method: Propose and implement a three-system architecture inside an LLM: System II simulative reasoning (world model-based future-state prediction), System III self-regulation (a learned configurator deciding when/how much to plan), and System I reactive execution. Realize SR^2AM as two instantiations: v0.1 (recorded decisions from a prompted multi-module system) and v1.0 (reconstructing structured plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs), trained via supervised learning then reinforcement learning. Evaluate across math, science, tabular analysis, and web information seeking.
AI.result: v0.1-8B and v1.0-30B achieve Pass@1 competitive with substantially larger models (120–355B and 685B–1T) while requiring far fewer reasoning tokens (v1.0-30B uses 25.8–95.3% fewer). RL increases average planning horizon by 22.8% with only a 2.0% rise in planning frequency.
AI.conclusion: Learning-based self-regulation offers a general principle for controlling planning and learning in agents, enabling efficient, scalable agentic reasoning across domains and potentially extending to how agents govern their own learning and adaptation.

title: Interpreting and Enhancing Emotional Circuits in Large Vision-Language Models via Cross-Modal Information Flow
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) represent a significant leap towards empathetic agents, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in emotion understanding. However, the internal mechanisms governing how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives remain largely unexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of visual counterfactuals and the diffuse nature of emotional expression. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework tailored for descriptive emotional reasoning. To this end, we construct a specialized dataset to demystify the emotional circuits underlying the three-stage ``Adapt-Aggregate-Execute'' mechanism. Crucially, we discover a functional decoupling: visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads, but are subsequently translated into narrative generation in deep layers through emotion-general pathways. Guided by these insights, we regulate the emotional information routing to strengthen attention flow and amplify the semantic activation to consolidate expression. Extensive experiments on the comprehensive MER-UniBench demonstrate that our methods significantly improve performance via inference-time intervention, effectively mitigating emotional hallucinations and corroborating the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.

AI.tldr: Proposes a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework to decode and regulate visual emotional processing in LVLMs, revealing a decoupled architecture with middle-layer sentiment-specific aggregation and deep-layer emotion-general narrative generation. Uses inference-time intervention to reduce emotional hallucinations, validated on MER-UniBench.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of understanding of how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives, exacerbated by few visual counterfactuals and diffuse emotional expressions.
AI.method: Introduce a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework; construct a dataset to dissect the Adapt-Aggregate-Execute mechanism; identify a functional decoupling between middle-layer sentiment-specific attention and deep-layer emotion-general pathways; regulate emotional information routing via inference-time interventions to boost attention flow and semantic activation.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant performance gains and reduced emotional hallucinations on MER-UniBench, supporting the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.
AI.conclusion: The work uncovers modular emotional reasoning circuits in LVLMs, offering a tractable method to regulate emotional information routing and improve reliability of emotion-aware generation at inference time.

title: Amplifying, Not Learning: Fine-Tuned AI Text Detectors Amplify a Pretrained Direction
summary: AI text detectors amplify a pretrained typicality axis; they do not construct an AI-vs-human boundary. On raw encoders before any task supervision, projecting onto centroid(AI)-centroid(HC3) achieves NYT-vs-HC3 AUROC 0.806/0.944/0.834 across three architectures (86-106% of the fine-tuned discrimination ceiling: on RoBERTa-base, raw projection exceeds fine-tuning); on RoBERTa-base, full fine-tuning reduces discrimination below raw on both fluent-formal populations tested. The same axis inverts on non-native ESL writing (AUROC 0.06-0.20) -- a falsifiable prediction unique to the typicality reading. A 24-example frozen probe matches full fine-tuning (0.900 vs 0.895). A closed-form Jacobian predictor parameterises axis-manipulating interventions with R^2 = 1.000 universal, lifts ELECTRA-CE deployment TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at FPR = 1%, and transfers to three independently-trained third-party RoBERTa detectors at 16/16 oracle-equivalence (57% NYT-FPR reduction on the OpenAI detector). Scope: encoder family; mechanism magnitude HC3-anchored; population-level shared axis with per-text mechanisms varying across architectures. Three operationally distinct probes -- text-surface caps_rate residualisation, geometric signed-epsilon ablation, closed-form text-pair predictor -- agree at cos 0.74/0.81/1.00 across three architectures, confirming observer-invariance. Under matched-TPR-0.90 evaluation, the published intervention zoo (CC, dealign-f2c) is calibration-equivalent across 27 cells (|Delta AUROC| <= 0.0081), and >= 97% of the LoRA->full-FT bias gap on ELECTRA is calibration shift, not learned representation -- the central claim's prediction confirmed.

AI.tldr: AI detectors rely on a pretrained typicality axis (AI vs human) rather than a robust boundary; simple centroid-based projections achieve strong discrimination across architectures, with subtle effects (e.g., ESL inversion). A closed-form Jacobian predictor enables axis interventions that transfer across models and significantly improve detection metrics, while many observed effects are calibration shifts rather than learned representations; multiple probes converge, supporting observer-invariance and cross-model generalization.
AI.motivation: Test whether AI text detectors truly separate AI-generated vs human-written text or merely exploit a pretrained typicality axis. Explore cross-architecture robustness, falsifiability of predictions, and potential to calibrate/dilate detectors via axis interventions.
AI.method: 1) Project raw encoder embeddings onto the AI centroid – HC3 centroid axis. 2) Evaluate NYT vs HC3 discrimination (AUROC) across three architectures (0.806/0.944/0.834). 3) Compare raw projection performance to fine-tuned discrimination; assess RoBERTa-base with full fine-tuning. 4) Test axis inversion on non-native ESL writing (AUROC 0.06–0.20). 5) Use a 24-example frozen probe to compare with full fine-tuning. 6) Develop a closed-form Jacobian predictor to parameterize axis interventions; measure performance (R^2=1.000) and transfer to other detectors; evaluate TPR uplift at FPR=1% and cross-model transfer. 7) Analyze three operational probes (text-surface caps_rate residualisation, geometric signed-epsilon ablation, closed-form text-pair predictor) for observer-invariance (cosine similarity). 8) Conduct matched-TPR evaluation and compare to published intervention zoo; dissect bias gap as calibration vs learned representation.
AI.result: - The AI vs HC3 axis yields high discrimination on NYT vs HC3 across architectures with AUROCs 0.806/0.944/0.834; this often nears or exceeds the fine-tuned discrimination ceiling (RoBERTa-base case: raw projection beats fine-tuning). - Fine-tuning can reduce discrimination below raw axes in RoBERTa-base. - The axis inverts for ESL writing (AUROC 0.06–0.20), a falsifiable and unique prediction of the typicality view. - A 24-example frozen probe matches full fine-tuning (0.900 vs 0.895). - A closed-form Jacobian predictor fully parameterizes axis interventions (R^2=1.000) and lifts ELECTRA-CE TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at FPR=1%, transferring to three RoBERTa detectors with high oracle-equivalence (16/16) and 57% NYT-FPR reduction on OpenAI detector. - Probes (caps_rate residualisation, signed-epsilon ablation, text-pair predictor) concur across architectures (cos 0.74/0.81/1.00). - Under matched-TPR-0.90, the published intervention zoo is calibration-equivalent across 27 cells; most of LoRA-to-full-FT bias gap is calibration shift rather than learned representation, supporting the central claim.
AI.conclusion: Detectors rely on a shared, pretrained typicality axis rather than constructing a strict AI-vs-human boundary. Axis interventions are predictable, transferable, and largely calibration-driven, with robust cross-architecture support and falsifiable predictions (notably ESL inversion). This mechanism offers a principled, transferable approach for understanding and calibrating AI-detection systems.

title: Superhuman Safe and Agile Racing through Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Autonomous systems have achieved superhuman performance in isolation or simulation, yet they remain brittle in shared, dynamic real-world spaces. This failure stems from the dominant single-agent paradigm for physical applications, where other actors are ignored or treated as environmental noise, preventing effective coordination. Here we show that multi-agent reinforcement learning provides the essential safety scaffolding required for real-world interaction. Using high-speed quadrotor racing as a high-stakes testbed, we train agents to navigate complex aerodynamic interactions and strategic maneuvering with a variable number of racers. Through league-based self-play, agents evolve sophisticated anticipatory behaviors, including proactive collision avoidance, overtaking, and handling multi-agent physical interactions, including aerodynamic downwash. Our agents outperform a champion-level human pilot in multi-player races at speeds exceeding 22 m/s, while simultaneously reducing collision rates by 50 % compared to state-of-the-art single-agent baselines. Crucially, training with diverse artificial agents enables zero-shot generalization to safer human interaction. These results suggest that the path to robust robotic co-existence lies not in isolated safety constraints, but in the rigorous demands of multi-agent interaction. Multimedia materials are available at: https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/marl

AI.tldr: Multi-agent reinforcement learning enables robust, high-speed quadrotor racing with safe real-world interaction, outperforming human champions and surpassing single-agent baselines.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness of single-agent autonomy in dynamic real-world spaces by leveraging multi-agent coordination and interaction safety.
AI.method: League-based self-play training with a variable number of racers in a high-speed quadrotor racing setup; agents learn anticipatory, cooperative and adversarial multi-agent behaviors, including handling aerodynamic downwash; diverse pseudo-agents foster zero-shot human generalization.
AI.result: Agents outperform champion-level human pilots in multi-player races at speeds >22 m/s and reduce collision rates by ~50% versus strong single-agent baselines; zero-shot generalization to safer human interaction is achieved through diverse training.
AI.conclusion: Robust robotic coexistence in shared spaces arises from rigorous multi-agent interaction and self-play, not merely isolated safety constraints; multi-agent training is essential for real-world coordination in autonomous aerial systems.

title: Ratchet: A Minimal Hygiene Recipe for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
comment: 16 pages, 2 figures, 6 tables. Extends arXiv:2605.19576 with the SWE-bench Verified evaluation and a non-divergence analysis (Proposition 1)
summary: Self-evolving skill libraries, pioneered by Voyager, let frozen LLM agents accumulate reusable knowledge without weight updates, yet recent evaluation shows that LLM-authored skills deliver $+0.0$pp over no-skill baselines while human-curated ones deliver $+16.2$pp: the bottleneck is not skill authoring but lifecycle management. We introduce \textbf{Ratchet}, a single-agent loop in which a frozen LLM writes, retrieves, curates, and retires its own natural-language skills. Ratchet integrates four candidate hygiene mechanisms: outcome-driven retirement, a bounded active-cap, meta-skill authoring guidance, and pattern canonicalisation. On MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, Ratchet lifts held-out pass@1 from a $0.258 \pm 0.047$ baseline to a late-window rolling mean of $0.584$ (peak $0.658 \pm 0.042$) across 100 rounds and 3 seeds, a $+0.328 \pm 0.018$ rolling-mean gain where the no-skill control drifts at $+0.002 \pm 0.005$; the same recipe transfers to an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified ($+0.22$ peak lift over 20 rounds). Eight ablations (A1--A8) reveal that the minimal working recipe is smaller than our design suggests: retirement and the meta-skill authoring prior are load-bearing, while explicit deduplication (canonicalisation, cover-guard) is subsumed by the meta-skill itself. A non-divergence proposition shows that bounded cap and retirement threshold together prevent expected performance from drifting below the no-skills floor.

AI.tldr: Ratchet enables a frozen LLM to autonomously write, retrieve, curate, and retire its own natural-language skills within a single-agent loop, augmented by four hygiene mechanisms; it yields substantial performance gains over no-skill baselines and matches human-curated baselines, with ablations clarifying the minimal recipe and stability properties.
AI.motivation: Lifecycle management is the bottleneck in self-evolving skill libraries; autonomous skill lifecycle management is needed for scalable, robust performance gains beyond hand-authored or self-written skills.
AI.method: Implement Ratchet as a single-agent loop where a frozen LLM writes, retrieves, curates, and retires NL skills. Integrate four hygiene mechanisms: outcome-driven retirement, bounded active-cap, meta-skill authoring guidance, and pattern canonicalisation. Evaluate on MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, comparing against no-skill baselines and, for transfer, an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified. Conduct eight ablations (A1–A8) to identify the minimal working recipe and perform a non-divergence analysis.
AI.result: Ratchet raises held-out pass@1 on MBPP+ hard-100 from 0.258 ± 0.047 baseline to a late-window rolling mean of 0.584 (peak 0.658 ± 0.042) over 100 rounds and 3 seeds, i.e., +0.328 ± 0.018, while the no-skill control drifts by +0.002 ± 0.005. The same recipe yields a +0.22 peak lift on SWE-bench Verified over 20 rounds. Ablations show retirement and meta-skill authoring are load-bearing; explicit deduplication is subsumed by meta-skill; a bounded cap with a retirement threshold prevents drift below the no-skills floor.
AI.conclusion: A compact, four-mechanism, single-agent loop suffices to achieve meaningful gains and stability in autonomous skill evolution. Retirement and meta-skill guidance are essential; canonicalisation is less critical, being subsumed by meta-skills. The bound-cap plus retirement threshold provides a non-divergence guarantee, suggesting scalable applicability to other domains.

title: RiT: Vanilla Diffusion Transformers Suffice in Representation Space
summary: Flow matching with $x$-prediction -- regressing the clean data point rather than the ambient velocity -- is known to exploit low-dimensional manifold structure effectively in pixel space \cite{li2025back}. We ask whether a pretrained representation space, while containing a low-dimensional data manifold of comparable intrinsic dimensionality, offers a distribution more favorable for flow-matching learning. Comparing pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features along four geometric axes, we find that pixel and DINOv2 share nearly identical intrinsic dimensionalities (both $\hat{d}\!\approx\!33$) yet DINOv2 exhibits $7.3\times$ higher effective rank, $35\times$ better covariance conditioning, $11.5\times$ lower excess kurtosis, and $1.7\times$ lower on-manifold interpolation error; SD-VAE latents are consistently intermediate, indicating that the advantage stems from representation-learning objectives rather than mere compression. These statistical properties render the flow-matching regression well-conditioned and remove the need for the specialized prediction heads or Riemannian transport used by prior DINOv2 diffusion methods. We propose the \emph{Representation Image Transformer} (RiT): a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained by $x$-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, augmented only by a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint \texttt{[CLS]}-patch modeling. On ImageNet $256{\times}256$, RiT attains FID 1.45 without guidance and 1.14 with classifier-free guidance, outperforming DiT$^\text{DH}$-XL with $19\%$ fewer parameters (676M vs.\ 839M). The resulting ODE is efficiently solvable at coarse discretizations: with classifier-free guidance, $5$ Heun steps already reach FID 2.0 and $10$ steps reach 1.25, without distillation or consistency training. Code at https://github.com/lezhang7/RiT.

AI.tldr: RiT leverages pre-trained DINOv2 features to improve flow-matching by aligning x-prediction with favorable statistics, achieving strong ImageNet FID with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether a pretrained representation space that preserves a low intrinsic dimensionality can yield a distribution more conducive to flow-matching than raw pixels or SD-VAE latents.
AI.method: Compare pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features across four geometric axes (intrinsic dimensionality, effective rank, covariance conditioning, excess kurtosis, on-manifold interpolation error). Propose Representation Image Transformer (RiT): train a vanilla Diffusion Transformer with x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, using a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint [CLS]-patch modeling. Evaluate on ImageNet 256x256 with and without classifier-free guidance; compare to DiT^{DH}-XL; analyze ODE discretization efficiency. Provide code link.
AI.result: DINOv2 features share intrinsic dimensionality with pixels (~d ≈ 33) but exhibit 7.3× higher effective rank, 35× better covariance conditioning, 11.5× lower excess kurtosis, and 1.7× lower on-manifold interpolation error compared with pixel features; SD-VAE latents are intermediate in these statistics. These properties render flow-matching regression well-conditioned and obviate specialized prediction heads or Riemannian transport used in prior DINOv2 diffusion methods. RiT—a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained by x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features with a dimension-aware noise schedule and [CLS]-patch modeling—attains ImageNet 256×256 FID 1.45 without guidance and 1.14 with classifier-free guidance, surpassing DiT^{DH}-XL with 19% fewer parameters (676M vs. 839M). The corresponding ODE is efficiently solvable at coarse discretizations: with guidance, 5 Heun steps reach FID 2.0 and 10 steps reach 1.25 (no distillation or consistency training). Code at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The advantage in flow-matching arises from the representation-learning objectives shaping the geometry and conditioning of the feature space rather than merely compressing the data. Using pretrained DINOv2 features in RiT provides strong, parameter-efficient image synthesis with simple training and fast, low-step ODE solvers.

title: Value-Gradient Hypothesis of RL for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning substantially improves pretrained language models, but it remains understudied why critic-free methods such as PPO and GRPO work as well as they do, and when they should provide the largest gains. We develop a value-gradient perspective of critic-free RL for LLM post-training. First, under a differentiable rollout and additive-noise parameterization, we show that the actor update is value-gradient-like in expectation: the backward pass propagates costates whose conditional expectation equals the value gradient. Second, for discrete transformer policies, we show that autodifferentiation through attention produces empirical costates that approximate this value signal, with an error controlled by the sampling gap and policy entropy. These results motivate a decomposition of RL impact into value gradient signal and reachable reward headroom, yielding a criterion for when RL should be most effective along a pretraining trajectory.

AI.tldr: A value-gradient perspective explains critic-free RL for LLMs: actor updates align with value gradients in expectation under differentiable rollout and additive-noise, and attention autodiff in discrete transformers yields costates that approximate the value signal; RL impact can be decomposed into a value-gradient signal and reachable reward headroom to guide when RL is most beneficial during pretraining.
AI.motivation: To understand why critic-free RL methods like PPO/GRPO perform well in LLM post-training and to identify when they should provide the greatest gains.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical analysis with differentiable rollout and additive-noise parameterization showing the actor update is value-gradient-like in expectation (costates with conditional expectation equal to the value gradient). 2) For discrete transformer policies, demonstrate that autodifferentiation through attention yields empirical costates that approximate the value signal, with error bounded by the sampling gap and policy entropy.
AI.result: The analysis yields a decomposition of RL impact into a value-gradient signal and reachable reward headroom. It provides a criterion for when RL should be most effective along a pretraining trajectory by signaling when the value gradient is informative and reward headroom is available.
AI.conclusion: This framework guides when to apply critic-free RL during LLM pretraining by balancing the strength of the value-gradient signal against the attainable reward headroom; it explains empirical effectiveness of critic-free methods and informs scheduling of RL interventions.

title: GesVLA: Gesture-Aware Vision-Language-Action Model Embedded Representations
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robot manipulation by unifying perception and action. However, existing VLA systems primarily rely on textual instructions and struggle to resolve spatial ambiguity in complex scenes with multiple similar objects. To address this limitation, we introduce gesture as a parallel instruction modality and propose a Gesture-aware Vision-Language-Action model (GesVLA). Our approach encodes gesture features directly into the latent space, enabling them to participate in both high-level reasoning and low-level action generation, and adopts a dual-VLM architecture to achieve tight coupling between gesture representations and action policies. At the data level, we construct a scalable gesture data generation pipeline by rendering hand models onto real-world scene images. This reduces the sim-to-real visual gap while producing rich data with diverse motion patterns and corresponding pointing annotations. In addition, we employ a two-stage training strategy to equip the model with both gesture perception and action prediction capabilities. We evaluate our approach on multiple real-world robotic tasks, including a controlled block manipulation task for validation and more practical scenarios such as product and produce selection. Experimental results show that incorporating gesture consistently improves target grounding accuracy and human-robot interaction efficiency, especially in complex and cluttered environments. Project page: https://gwxuan.github.io/GesVLA/.

AI.tldr: GesVLA introduces gesture as a parallel instruction modality for Vision-Language-Action robotics, enabling gesture features to influence both reasoning and action generation via a dual-VLM architecture and a scalable gesture data pipeline; results show improved target grounding and interaction in cluttered scenes.
AI.motivation: To overcome spatial ambiguity and disambiguate instructions in scenes with multiple similar objects by leveraging human gesture as an additional modality alongside textual instructions in VLA systems.
AI.method: 1) Encode gesture features into the model's latent space to influence high-level reasoning and low-level action generation. 2) Use a dual-VLM (vision-language models) architecture to tightly couple gesture representations with action policies. 3) Build a scalable gesture data pipeline by rendering hand models onto real images to create diverse, annotated data that bridges sim-to-real gaps. 4) Employ a two-stage training strategy to train gesture perception and action prediction components.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world robotics tasks (controlled block manipulation; product/produce selection) shows that incorporating gesture improves target grounding accuracy and human-robot interaction efficiency, especially in complex and cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: Gesture serves as an effective parallel modality that enhances VLA systems for robust manipulation in cluttered scenes. The proposed architecture (dual-VLM, gesture latent integration) and data-generation pipeline enable tighter grounding and more efficient human-robot interaction; a project page is provided for further details.

title: IdleSpec: Exploiting Idle Time via Speculative Planning for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents solve complex tasks by leveraging multi-step reasoning with iterative tool calls and environment interactions, which incur idle time while waiting for observations. Despite the prevalence of idle time in most agentic scenarios, existing works treat it as an unavoidable overhead or propose restricted solutions that overlook varying computational budgets across different tool calls and future observation uncertainty, thereby leading to suboptimal utilization of idle time. In this paper, we introduce IdleSpec, a scalable and generic inference approach that leverages idle-time computation to improve agent performance while minimizing latency overhead. Specifically, IdleSpec iteratively generates plan candidates during idle periods and, once observations become available, aggregates them to guide the next reasoning step. For effective plan generation under observation uncertainty, IdleSpec samples between complementary drafting strategies (i.e., progressive and recovery) from a learned distribution that is updated via posterior feedback. Our experiments demonstrate that IdleSpec significantly improves agent performance in various agentic scenarios by effectively utilizing idle time. In particular, on the GAIA and FRAMES, IdleSpec achieves 55.6% average accuracy with Gemini-2.5-Flash, surpassing the vanilla baseline without idle-time usage by 5.1%. Furthermore, for MLE-Bench, which involves substantial delay from code executions, IdleSpec achieves performance gains of up to 9.1% on the Any Medal rate, highlighting its generalizability to long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: IdleSpec exploits idle computation in LLM-based agents by generating multiple plan candidates during idle periods and aggregating them once observations arrive to inform the next reasoning step, using a learned distribution over drafting strategies (progressive vs. recovery) updated via posterior feedback. It yields substantial gains on long-horizon tasks across GAIA/FRAMES and MLE-Bench.
AI.motivation: Idle time is pervasive in agentic reasoning but is often treated as overhead or fixed latency, ignoring varying computational budgets and future observation uncertainty. Leveraging idle time can reduce latency and improve decision quality across task horizons.
AI.method: During idle periods, IdleSpec iteratively generates plan candidates. After new observations arrive, it aggregates these candidates to guide the next reasoning step. It samples drafting strategies from a learned distribution over progressive and recovery modes, updating this distribution via posterior feedback on outcomes.
AI.result: Empirical gains include 55.6% average accuracy on GAIA and FRAMES with Gemini-2.5-Flash, outperforming the vanilla baseline by 5.1%. On MLE-Bench, IdleSpec achieves up to 9.1% improvement in Any Medal rate, demonstrating robustness to long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: IdleSpec provides a scalable, generic approach to utilize idle time in LLM-based agents, yielding consistent performance gains across diverse, long-horizon tasks with manageable latency overhead.

title: Learning Spatiotemporal Sensitivity in Video LLMs via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Video large language models (Video LLMs) achieve strong benchmark accuracy, yet often answer video questions through shortcuts such as single-frame cues and language priors rather than by tracking spatiotemporal dynamics. This issue is exacerbated in RL post-training, where correctness-only rewards can further reinforce shortcut policies that obtain high reward without tracking video dynamics. We address this by asking a controlled counterfactual question: if the visual world changed while the question remained fixed, should the answer change or stay the same? Based on this view, we propose \textbf{Counterfactual Relational Policy Optimization (CRPO)}, a dual-branch RL framework for improving \emph{spatiotemporal sensitivity}. CRPO constructs counterfactual videos through horizontal flips and temporal reversals, trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and introduces a \textbf{Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR)} between their answers. CRR encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and remain unchanged for static questions. This cross-branch constraint makes it difficult for shortcut policies to be consistently rewarded across both branches. To evaluate this property, we introduce \textbf{DyBench}, a paired counterfactual video benchmark with 3,014 videos covering reversible dynamics, moving direction, and event sequence, together with a strict pair-accuracy metric that prevents fixed-answer shortcuts from inflating scores. Experiments show that CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining competitive general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model, indicating improved spatiotemporal sensitivity rather than stronger reliance on static shortcuts. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/crpo.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: CRPO introduces a dual-branch RL framework with counterfactual videos (horizontal flips and temporal reversals) and a Counterfactual Relation Reward to enforce spatiotemporal sensitivity in video LLMs. Evaluated on DyBench (3,014 videos), it improves spatiotemporal metrics (P-Acc, TimeBlind I-Acc) over base models with competitive general video performance.
AI.motivation: Video LLMs often rely on shortcuts (single-frame cues, language priors) and RL rewards can reinforce such shortcuts. There is a need to promote true spatiotemporal understanding by testing responses to counterfactual changes in the visual world while the question stays the same.
AI.method: Dual-branch RL framework (CRPO) that generates counterfactual videos via horizontal flips and temporal reversals, trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and imposes a Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR) linking their answers. CRR rewards changing answers for dynamic questions and keeping answers for static questions, creating a cross-branch constraint to deter shortcut policies. Evaluation uses DyBench, a paired counterfactual video benchmark with a strict pair-accuracy metric.
AI.result: CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive tasks while maintaining competitive general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO yields +7.7 P-Acc and +8.2 TimeBlind I-Acc on DyBench relative to the base model, indicating improved reliance on actual dynamics rather than static shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual relational supervision via CRPO effectively enhances spatiotemporal sensitivity in video LLMs and reduces shortcut reliance, validated on a dedicated counterfactual benchmark (DyBench). The approach shows promise for robustspatiotemporal reasoning in video understanding.

title: Hierarchical Variational Policies for Reward-Guided Diffusion
summary: Adapting pretrained diffusion models to downstream objectives such as inverse problems often requires expensive test-time guidance or optimization. We propose a principled framework for generating high-quality reward-aligned samples at substantially reduced inference cost. Our approach formulates test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model, where control is amortized into a lightweight yet expressive stochastic policy.
