This formulation naturally supports few-step diffusion sampling: large step sizes enable fast inference, while the learned policy maintains sample quality by providing structured per-step control. The resulting fully amortized sampler achieves a strong quality--speed tradeoff, matching or exceeding recent test-time scaling baselines while requiring significantly less compute. For example, on 4x super-resolution, our method achieves better perceptual quality with more than 5x faster inference compared to the best-performing baseline. We further extend our approach to a semi-amortized regime that combines cheap amortized proposals with limited test-time optimization, achieving state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several challenging inverse problems.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical variational framework for test-time diffusion model adaptation with amortized control, enabling few-step sampling and a strong speed-quality tradeoff; reports state-of-the-art perceptual results on 4x super-resolution and other inverse problems via fully/semi-amortized regimes.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of adapting pretrained diffusion models to downstream inverse problems at test time, while preserving or enhancing sample quality.
AI.method: Formulates test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model in which a lightweight stochastic policy amortizes control over diffusion steps. Enables few-step sampling with large step sizes; fully amortized sampler maintains quality through structured per-step control. Introduces a semi-amortized regime combining cheap proposals with limited test-time optimization.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong quality–speed tradeoffs, matching or exceeding recent test-time baselines with substantially reduced compute. On 4x super-resolution, achieves better perceptual quality with >5x faster inference than the best baseline. Semi-amortized regime yields state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several challenging inverse problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed amortized, hierarchical framework enables efficient, reward-aligned diffusion sampling for downstream objectives, delivering high perceptual quality at reduced inference cost; semi-amortization further pushes to state-of-the-art across multiple tasks.

title: AwareVLN: Reasoning with Self-awareness for Vision-Language Navigation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Project page: https://gwxuan.github.io/AwareVLN/
summary: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to ground language instructions to its own movement within a visual environment. While state-of-the-art methods leverage the reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for end-to-end action prediction, they often lack an explicit and explainable understanding of the relationships between the agent, the instruction, and the scene. Conversely, explicitly building a scene map for heuristic planning is intuitively appealing but relies on additional 3D sensors and hinders large-scale vision-language pre-training. To bridge this gap, we propose AwareVLN, a novel framework that equips the navigation model with a self-aware reasoning mechanism, enabling it to understand the agent's state and task progress in a fully end-to-end and data-driven manner. Our approach features two key innovations: (1) a structural reasoning module that fosters spatial and task-oriented self-awareness, and (2) an automatic data engine with progress division for effective training. Extensive experiments on various datasets in Habitat simulator show our AwareVLN significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art vision-language navigation methods. Project page: https://gwxuan.github.io/AwareVLN/.

AI.tldr: AwareVLN introduces a fully end-to-end self-aware reasoning framework for Vision-Language Navigation, combining a structural reasoning module and an automatic data engine to enable agent-, instruction-, and scene-aware planning without extra 3D sensors.
AI.motivation: VLN models often lack explicit, explainable grounding among the agent, instruction, and the scene; explicit scene maps help planning but require additional 3D sensors and hinder large-scale pre-training. A data-driven, end-to-end self-awareness approach aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Proposes AwareVLN with (1) a structural reasoning module that enforces spatial and task-oriented self-awareness, and (2) an automatic data engine with progress division for effective training, enabling end-to-end learning in Habitat without external 3D mapping.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Habitat-based VLN benchmarks show significant improvements over prior state-of-the-art methods, validating the effectiveness of self-aware reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers explainable, self-aware navigation without additional sensors, boosting performance and scalability for vision-language grounded navigation.

title: ST-SimDiff: Balancing Spatiotemporal Similarity and Difference for Efficient Video Understanding with MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face significant computational overhead when processing long videos due to the massive number of visual tokens required. To improve efficiency, existing methods primarily reduce redundancy by pruning or merging tokens based on importance or similarity. However, these approaches largely overlook a critical dimension of video content, i.e., changes and turning points, and they lack a collaborative model for spatio-temporal relationships. To address this, we propose a new perspective: similarity is for identifying redundancy, while difference is for capturing key events. Based on this, we designed a training-free framework named ST-SimDiff. We first construct a spatio-temporal graph from the visual tokens to uniformly model their complex associations. Subsequently, we employ a parallel dual-selection strategy: 1) similarity-based selection uses community detection to retain representative tokens, compressing static information; 2) temporal difference-based selection precisely locates content-changing points to preserve tokens that capture key dynamic shifts. This allows it to preserve both static and dynamic content with a minimal number of tokens. Extensive experiments show our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches while substantially reducing computational costs. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-SimDiff.

AI.tldr: ST-SimDiff is a training-free framework for efficient video token handling in Multimodal LLMs that uses a spatio-temporal graph and parallel dual-selection (similarity-based token retention via community detection and temporal-difference-based selection) to preserve both static and dynamic content with fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs incur high computational costs when processing long videos due to a large number of visual tokens. Existing pruning/merging methods focus on redundancy and often ignore temporal changes and spatio-temporal relationships, leading to loss of key events and inefficiency.
AI.method: Construct a spatio-temporal graph over visual tokens to model complex associations. Apply a parallel dual-selection strategy: (1) similarity-based selection via community detection to retain representative tokens and compress static information; (2) temporal difference-based selection to locate content-changing points and preserve tokens capturing dynamic shifts. The framework is training-free.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ST-SimDiff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches while substantially reducing computational costs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ST-SimDiff provides a principled, training-free approach to balance redundancy reduction and event preservation in video token processing for MLLMs, achieving better efficiency and effectiveness.

title: Virtual 3D H&E Staining from Phase-contrast Back-illumination Interference Tomography
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) histopathology of unprocessed tissues has the potential to transform disease management by enabling volumetric characterization of tissue microarchitecture and in-vivo assessment. Back-illumination Interference Tomography (BIT) is a new phase microscopy technology that provides rapid, non-destructive volumetric imaging of unprocessed tissues. However, translating BIT volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images remains challenging, particularly due to shift-variant contrast and the absence of quantitative validation benchmarks. We introduce HistoBIT3D, the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset, enabling quantitative evaluation of structural preservation in unsupervised virtual staining against ground-truth nuclear distributions. Using this dataset, we present a novel virtual staining framework that translates BIT volumes with shift-variant contrast into realistic H&E volumes by leveraging bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse to enhance structural fidelity and perceptual realism. Our method achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics while significantly improving 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation. Together, these contributions establish a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing the paradigm of slide-free, volumetric computational histopathology. Our data and code are available at: https://github.com/aasong113/HistoBIT3D_VirtualStaining.

AI.tldr: Introduces HistoBIT3D: a voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence nuclei dataset for evaluating 3D virtual staining of BIT volumes into H&E; proposes a bidirectional multiscale content-consistent framework with cross-domain style reuse, achieving state-of-the-art realism and improved 3D nuclei segmentation; provides data and code.
AI.motivation: To address shift-variant contrast and lack of quantitative benchmarks in translating back-illuminated tomography volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images, enabling quantitative validation of structural preservation.
AI.method: Construct HistoBIT3D by pairing BIT volumes with fluorescence-labeled nuclei; develop a virtual staining model that enforces bidirectional, multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse for faithful H&E translations; evaluate with zero-shot Cellpose nuclei segmentation and standard realism metrics.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics for virtual H&E and significantly improves 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation in zero-shot Cellpose evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Presents a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining in slide-free volumetric computational histopathology; data and code released for community use.

title: Representation Gap: Explaining the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Neural Networks from a Geometric Perspective
summary: Characterizing precisely the asymptotic generalization error of neural networks using parameters that can be estimated efficiently is a crucial problem in machine learning, which relies heavily on heuristics and practitioners' intuition to make key design choices. In order to mitigate this issue, we introduce the Representation Gap, a metric closely related to the generalization error, but admitting better-behaved asymptotic dynamics. Focusing on equivariant diffusion models and leveraging results from optimal quantization and point-process theory, we derive a precise asymptotic equivalent of the Representation Gap and show that it is governed by a single parameter, the \textit{intrinsic dimension} of the task, which is easy to interpret, efficient to estimate, and can be linked to the equivariances of common neural network architectures. We show that this asymptotic dynamic also extends to a broader range of tasks and training algorithms. Finally, we demonstrate empirically that our asymptotic law and intrinsic dimension estimation are accurate on a wide range of synthetic datasets, where these quantities are known, as well as on more realistic datasets, where we obtain results consistent with the related literature.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Representation Gap to study asymptotic generalization error and derives its asymptotic equivalent for equivariant diffusion models. The dynamics are governed by a single parameter, the intrinsic dimension of the task, which is interpretable and easy to estimate; the results extend to other tasks, with empirical validation on synthetic and realistic datasets.
AI.motivation: Provide a robust, efficiently estimable metric for asymptotic generalization behavior in neural networks, moving beyond heuristics. Leverage connections to optimal quantization and point-process theory and exploit equivariant structures in common architectures.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical derivation of the asymptotic equivalent of the Representation Gap for equivariant diffusion models using results from optimal quantization and point-process theory; identify intrinsic dimension as the governing parameter; demonstrate partial extension to a broader class of tasks and training algorithms; validate empirically on synthetic datasets (with known quantities) and more realistic datasets.
AI.result: A precise asymptotic law for the Representation Gap is derived; the asymptotics are governed by the intrinsic dimension, which is interpretable and easy to estimate; the approach and law appear to extend to a broader set of tasks and training regimes; empirical experiments corroborate the theory on synthetic data (where quantities are known) and on real-world-like data in line with related literature.
AI.conclusion: The intrinsic dimension captures the primary drivers of asymptotic generalization dynamics for tasks with equivariant structure, making the Representation Gap a practical metric for design and evaluation; the results point to broader applicability across tasks and training methods, with empirical support.

title: Adapting the Interface, Not the Model: Runtime Harness Adaptation for Deterministic LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents are shaped not only by their language models, but also by the runtime harness that mediates observation, tool use, action execution, feedback interpretation, and trajectory control. While existing agent adaptation methods mainly update model parameters, many failures in deterministic, rule-governed domains stem from mismatches at the model--environment interface. We propose Life-Harness, a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that improves frozen LLM agents without changing model weights or evaluation environments. Life-Harness evolves from training trajectories by converting recurring interaction failures into reusable interventions across environment contracts, procedural skills, action realization, and trajectory regulation, and remains fixed during held-out evaluation. On seven deterministic environments from $τ$-bench, $τ^2$-bench, and AgentBench, Life-Harness improves 116 out of 126 model--environment settings across 18 model backbones, with an average relative improvement of 88.5%. Harnesses evolved only from Qwen3-4B-Instruct trajectories transfer to 17 other models, showing that Life-Harness captures reusable environment-side structure rather than model-specific behavior. These results position runtime interface adaptation as a complementary alternative to model-centric agent training. Code is available at GitHub.

AI.tldr: Life-Harness is a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that fixes recurring environment–model interface failures to significantly improve frozen LLM agents without changing weights, with strong cross-model transfer.
AI.motivation: Deterministic, rule-governed domains often fail due to misalignment between the LLM and the runtime environment; parameter-heavy model fine-tuning is not always possible or desirable, so adapting the runtime interface offers a complementary solution.
AI.method: From training trajectories, Life-Harness converts recurring interaction failures into reusable interventions across four areas: environment contracts, procedural skills, action realization, and trajectory regulation. These interventions are fixed during held-out evaluation and are evolved from trajectories of Qwen3-4B-Instruct, then shown to transfer to 17 other models across seven deterministic benchmarks (τ-bench, τ^2-bench, AgentBench) across 18 model backbones.
AI.result: On 7 deterministic environments over 126 model–environment settings, Life-Harness improves 116 settings, averaging an 88.5% relative improvement. Harnesses evolved from Qwen3-4B-Instruct generalize to 17 other models, indicating environment-side structure generality rather than model-specific behavior.
AI.conclusion: Runtime interface adaptation via Life-Harness is a viable, model-free complement to model-centric training, enabling robust gains across diverse models and environments with reusable interventions.

title: ConvNeXt-FD: A Fractal-Based Deep Model for Robust Biomedical Image Segmentation
summary: Biomedical image segmentation is a critical task in medical diagnosis and treatment planning, enabling precise delineation of anatomical structures and pathological regions. Despite significant advancements, challenges persist due to the inherent variability, noise, and complex morphology present in diverse medical imaging modalities. This paper introduces ConvNeXt-FD, a novel deep learning architecture for robust biomedical image segmentation, built upon a U-Net-like encoder-decoder framework leveraging the powerful ConvNeXt backbone. Our approach integrates a hybrid loss function combining the Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by a differentiable formulation of Fractal Dimension, designed to enhance the model's sensitivity to object boundaries and shape fidelity. We rigorously evaluate ConvNeXt-FD across six distinct biomedical datasets: BUSI (Breast Ultrasound Images), DDTI (Thyroid Ultrasound Images), FluoCells (Fluorescent Cell Images), IDRiD (Diabetic Retinopathy Images for Optic Disc Segmentation), ISIC2018 (Skin Lesion Images), and MoNuSeg (Nuclei Segmentation). Experimental results demonstrate that ConvNeXt-FD, particularly when initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights, achieves competitive and often superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods across various metrics, including Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and False Positive Rate. The integration of ConvNeXt as a strong encoder, coupled with the boundary-aware regularization, proves effective in capturing both high-level semantic features and fine-grained boundary details, leading to more accurate and reliable segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

AI.tldr: ConvNeXt-FD is a U-Net-like biomedical image segmentation model that uses a ConvNeXt backbone with a boundary-aware Fractal Dimension-inspired regularization in a hybrid Dice loss. It shows competitive or superior performance on six diverse datasets, especially with ImageNet pretraining.
AI.motivation: Biomedical image segmentation must handle high variability, noise, and complex morphologies across multiple imaging modalities. There is a need for robust boundary delineation and shape fidelity to improve diagnostic and treatment planning outcomes.
AI.method: An encoder-decoder architecture similar to U-Net, with a ConvNeXt backbone as the encoder. The loss is a hybrid of the Dice coefficient and a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by a differentiable Fractal Dimension. The model is evaluated on six datasets (BUSI, DDTI, FluoCells, IDRiD, ISIC2018, MoNuSeg), with and without ImageNet pretraining.
AI.result: ConvNeXt-FD yields competitive and often superior metrics (Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, FPR) compared to state-of-the-art methods. The boundary-aware term improves boundary precision, and ImageNet pretraining further enhances performance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating a strong ConvNeXt encoder with a boundary-focused regularization improves segmentation accuracy and boundary fidelity across diverse biomedical imaging tasks, yielding robust and reliable performance in challenging contexts.

title: Measuring Cross-Modal Synergy: A Benchmark for VLM Explainability
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) map complex visual inputs to semantic spaces, but interpreting the cross-modal reasoning of VLMs currently relies on post-hoc explainers evaluated via unimodal perturbation metrics. We expose a limitation in this paradigm: because multimodal datasets contain language priors and modality biases, VLMs frequently exhibit cross-modal redundancy, allowing them to answer visual queries using text alone. Consequently, unimodal metrics penalize faithful explainers, triggering an evaluation collapse where visual and textual rankings fundamentally contradict each other. %(Kendall's $τ= -0.06$). To resolve this, we introduce Synergistic Faithfulness ($\mathcal{F}_{syn}$), a scalable metric rooted in the Shapley Interaction Index that strictly isolates the joint Harsanyi dividend between modalities, serving as a highly accurate surrogate ($ρ= 0.92$) while achieving a $24\times$ computational speedup. Evaluating 8 distinct XAI methods across 3 VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets, reveals that explainers proposed for VLMs heavily over-index on visual salience and significantly underperform adapted attention-based methods in capturing true cross-modal synergy. By decoupling visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness, this work provides a rigorous evaluation framework required to safely audit VLM reasoning in high-stakes deployments.

AI.tldr: Proposes Synergistic Faithfulness F_syn, a Shapley-based metric to measure true cross-modal synergy in Vision-Language Models, addressing evaluation collapse in unimodal XAI metrics; shows visual salience alone is insufficient and provides a scalable surrogate with high fidelity and substantial speedup; results across 8 XAI methods, 3 architectures, 3 datasets highlight need to decouple visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness.
AI.motivation: Unimodal perturbation-based evaluations fail to capture joint multimodal contributions due to language priors and modality biases that create cross-modal redundancy, causing explanations to misrank through separate visual/text cues.
AI.method: Introduce F_syn grounded in the Shapley Interaction Index to isolate the joint Harsanyi dividend between modalities. Provide a scalable surrogate with high correlation to true joint contributions (ρ ≈ 0.92) and ~24× speedup. Empirically evaluate 8 XAI methods across 3 VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets.
AI.result: F_syn reveals explainers over-index on visual salience and underperform adapted attention-based methods in capturing true cross-modal synergy. Decoupling visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness yields a robust evaluation framework and improves auditing of VLM reasoning.
AI.conclusion: A rigorous, scalable framework is needed to audit cross-modal reasoning in VLMs. F_syn enables faithful evaluation of cross-modal explanations, decouples modalities, and supports safer deployment in high-stakes settings.

title: Rethinking Token Reduction for Diffusion Models via Output-Similarity-Awareness
summary:
Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve superior image generation quality but suffer from quadratic computational complexity relative to token count. While various token reduction (TR) methods have been proposed to mitigate this cost, they overlook the primary objective of generative models: minimizing recovery error, which requires reflecting output token similarity. They rely solely on input token similarity inherited from reduction-only ViT paradigms, leading to a fundamental misalignment with this objective.
  To bridge this gap, we propose DiTo, a novel TR paradigm that shifts the focus toward output-centric token reduction. Based on the observation that output token similarity is consistently preserved across adjacent timesteps, DiTo utilizes prior-step similarities as an effective proxy to establish token correspondences at a Matching timestep, which are then reused across multiple subsequent Reduction timesteps. To optimize this interleaved scheduling, we propose Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided Interval Scheduling to determine the optimal matching frequency. Furthermore, to mitigate localized approximation errors and resulting blocking artifacts caused by repeated reuse, we propose Frequency-aware Token Matching by incorporating a selection-frequency penalty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

AI.tldr: DiTo introduces an output-centric token reduction paradigm for Diffusion Transformers, using prior-step output similarities for matching timesteps, PMR-guided interval scheduling, and frequency-aware matching to reduce computation while improving image quality, achieving a better speed-accuracy Pareto frontier than prior TR methods.
AI.motivation: Current token reduction methods in diffusion transformers are based on input-token similarity (ViT-style reduction) and do not align with the objective of minimizing recovery error, which depends on output token similarity across timesteps.
AI.method: DiTo performs interleaved scheduling: at a Matching timestep, it uses prior-step output-token similarities to establish correspondences, then reuses these matches across several subsequent Reduction timesteps. It introduces Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided Interval Scheduling to optimize matching frequency and a Frequency-aware Token Matching penalty to discourage over-reuse and reduce artifacts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods, delivering 1.6–3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups and achieving a superior Pareto frontier.
AI.conclusion: Focusing on output-token similarity and adopting an interleaved matching/reduction schedule with PMR-guided frequency selection yields substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing quality, advancing the state-of-the-art in diffusion-model token reduction.

title: TBP-mHC: full expressivity for manifold-constrained hyper connections through transportation polytopes
summary:
Hyper-Connections (HC) improve residual networks by introducing learnable mixing across multiple residual streams, but unconstrained mixing leads to training instability. Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC) address this by enforcing approximate double stochasticity via Sinkhorn normalization, while mHC-lite ensures exact constraints through convex combinations of permutation matrices at the cost of factorial complexity. KromHC reduces this cost using Kronecker-product parameterizations, but restricts the mixing matrices to a structured submanifold of the Birkhoff polytope .
  We propose Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterizations and their Recursive variants (RTBP), which construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with $(n-1)^2$ degrees of freedom. Our approach avoids iterative normalization and combinatorial explosion while preserving full expressivity of the Birkhoff polytope. Empirical results on language model pre-training' demonstrate competitive performance with improved stability and scalability.

AI.tldr: Efficient exact doubly stochastic mixing via TBP/RTBP for hyper-connected residual networks, achieving stability and scalability.
AI.motivation: Unconstrained mixing in Hyper-Connections causes training instability; need expressive yet efficiently computable doubly stochastic mixers within the Birkhoff polytope.
AI.method: Introduce Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterizations and Recursive TBP (RTBP) to construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with (n-1)^2 degrees of freedom, avoiding Sinkhorn iterations and factorial combinatorics, while preserving full Birkhoff expressivity.
AI.result: LM pre-training experiments show competitive performance with improved stability and scalability compared with prior hyper-connection approaches.
AI.conclusion: TBP/RTBP offer a principled, scalable, and expressive framework for hyper-connected residual networks, enabling stable training without normalization bottlenecks.

title: LLM-Metrics: Measuring Research Impact Through Large Language Model Memory
summary: Citation counts remain the dominant metric for assessing research impact, yet they suffer from well-documented limitations: temporal lag, disciplinary bias, and Matthew effects. Here we propose LLM-Metrics, a research-impact assessment metric derived from the parametric memory of large language models (LLMs). The central hypothesis is that high-impact papers receive greater exposure in the academic community, that this exposure enters LLM training data in textual form, and that models consequently form stronger parametric memory of these papers. We designed four types of multiple-choice probes, covering title recognition, author recognition, method recognition, and venue recognition, and evaluated 549 computer science papers published in 2023-2024 across 17 LLMs spanning 0.5B to 72B parameters from six vendors. Of the 17 models, 15 produced positive predictions, 9 of which were significant at p less than 0.05, with an overall Spearman correlation of rho = 0.1495 and p = 0.0004 against citation counts. Three additional findings support the proposed mechanism. First, the predictive signal was stronger for 2024 papers, rho = 0.1880, whose citation counts were near zero at model-training time, reducing the plausibility of a simple reverse-causality explanation. Second, author-recognition probes showed the strongest discriminative power, consistent with an exposure-driven memory mechanism. Third, model scale and predictive power were non-monotonic: a 3B-parameter model, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, with rho = 0.1829, outperformed most larger models, supporting a selective-memory hypothesis in which the limited capacity of smaller models can serve as an effective information filter. LLM-Metrics offers a real-time, cross-disciplinary, citation-independent paradigm for research assessment.

AI.tldr: Proposes LLM-Metrics: a citation-independent, memory-based research-impact metric derived from LLMs' exposure to papers, evaluated via recognition probes across 17 models for 2023-24 CS papers.
AI.motivation: Citation counts are biased by time, discipline, and Matthew effects. A real-time, exposure-based metric could complement or mitigate these limitations by leveraging LLMs' parametric memory.
AI.method: Four multiple-choice probes (title, author, method, venue recognition) were designed. 549 CS papers (2023-2024) were tested across 17 LLMs (0.5B–72B params) from six vendors. Statistical tests (p-values, Spearman rho) assessed correlation with citation counts.
AI.result: 15/17 models yielded positive predictions; 9 significant at p<0.05. Overall Spearman rho=0.1495 (p=0.0004). 2024 papers showed stronger signal (rho=0.1880). Author-recognition had strongest discriminative power. A 3B model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) outperformed many larger models, suggesting limited-capacity models can filter information. Cross-disciplinary, real-time, citation-independent potential.
AI.conclusion: LLM-Metrics offers a novel, exposure-driven metric for research impact, potentially addressing some limitations of citation counts, with evidence supporting the memory-based mechanism and model-scale effects.

title: PointLLM-R: Enhancing 3D Point Cloud Reasoning via Chain-of-Thought
summary: Understanding 3D point clouds through language remains a fundamental challenge in computer graphics and visual computing, due to the irregular structure of point cloud data and the lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has shown strong effectiveness in LLMs and image-based MLLMs, its extension to 3D understanding remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we propose a data-centric framework for constructing large-scale CoT supervision tailored to 3D point cloud understanding. Our framework consists of a two-stage pipeline that first refines point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement, and then synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths through Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Using this approach, we build PoCoTI, a CoT-enhanced point-text instruction-following dataset containing 55K samples with explicit reasoning paths. Fine-tuning PointLLM on PoCoTI yields PointLLM-R, a reasoning-capable 3D multimodal language model. Extensive experiments on generative 3D classification and captioning demonstrate that PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

AI.tldr: Data-centric CoT supervision for 3D point clouds; introduces PoCoTI (55k samples) and PointLLM-R, achieving state-of-the-art in generative 3D classification and captioning with robust generalization.
AI.motivation: Extend chain-of-thought reasoning to 3D multimodal understanding and address irregular point-cloud structure by crafting high-quality, reasoning-rich instruction data.
AI.method: Two-stage: (1) refine point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement; (2) synthesize reasoning paths with Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO); build PoCoTI; fine-tune PointLLM to obtain PointLLM-R.
AI.result: PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance on generative 3D classification and captioning and generalizes well to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogues.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric CoT framework effectively transfers reasoning capabilities to 3D multimodal models, producing high-quality reasoning data and robust, scalable 3D LLMs.

title: I-SAFE: Wasserstein Coherence Metrics for Structural Auditing of Scientific AI Models
summary: Deep learning models are increasingly used in scientific prediction tasks where strong benchmark performance is often interpreted as evidence of scientifically meaningful behavior. This interpretation is fragile, as models may exploit shortcut features, dataset-specific regularities, or distributional biases that are predictive on held-out data but not aligned with domain-relevant structure. To address this limitation, we introduce the \textsc{I-SAFE} (Interventional Secure, Accurate, Fair and Explainable) framework, a post-hoc distributional auditing framework for scientific AI models centered on the Wasserstein Coherence Metric (WCM). Given a trained black-box predictor and an external structural prior encoding domain knowledge about task-relevant input structure, \textsc{I-SAFE} evaluates raw model outputs under structurally guided perturbations of the input. The proposed audit measures output-distribution coherence through three complementary metrics: a Quantile-Based Metric (QBM) for location-level coherence, the WCM for ordinal coherence, and a translation-invariant WCM variant for shape coherence. We instantiate \textsc{I-SAFE} on drug--target interaction (DTI) prediction using the Davis kinase benchmark, KLIFS (Kinase--Ligand Interaction Fingerprints and Structures) binding-pocket annotations, and three sequence-based DTI models: DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, and TAPB. Although the models operate in a comparable predictive regime, \textsc{I-SAFE} reveals substantially different distributional response profiles, a distinction invisible to accuracy-based evaluation. The framework is model-agnostic and applicable to any domain where inputs admit a structured decomposition and an external prior is available.

AI.tldr: Introduces I-SAFE, a post-hoc distributional auditing framework for scientific AI using Wasserstein Coherence Metrics to measure how model outputs coherently respond to structure-guided perturbations, revealing distributional biases unseen by accuracy in drug–target interaction tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the fragility of accuracy as a sole indicator of scientifically meaningful behavior, by auditing models against domain-informed perturbations and distributional coherence.
AI.method: Proposes I-SAFE with three coherence metrics (QBM for location, WCM for ordinal, translation-invariant WCM for shape). Uses structurally guided input perturbations and external priors; demonstrates on DTI benchmarks (Davis, KLIFS) with DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, TAPB. Framework is model-agnostic.
AI.result: Audit reveals substantially different distributional response profiles across models that accuracy alone cannot reveal, demonstrating the value of distributional auditing.
AI.conclusion: I-SAFE generalizes to any domain with structured inputs and external priors, providing a post-hoc tool to validate that model behavior aligns with domain-relevant structure rather than exploiting spurious patterns.

title: Skill Weaving: Efficient LLM Improvement via Modular Skillpacks
summary: Large language models increasingly require specialization across diverse domains, yet existing approaches struggle to balance multi-domain capacities with strict memory and inference constraints. In this work, we introduce SkillWeave, a modular improvement framework that enables LLMs to specialize under fixed memory budgets. SkillWeave partitions full capabilities of a general-purpose model into skillpacks -- lightweight, domain-specific delta modules -- that reorganize and refine the model's internal knowledge. For efficient deployment, SkillWeave integrates SkillZip to compress skillpacks into compact and inference-ready format, enabling strong multi-domain performance with low-latency execution. On multi-task and agentic benchmarks, a 9B SkillWeave model outperforms several baselines and even surpasses a 32B monolithic LLM, while achieving up to 4x speedup.

AI.tldr: SkillWeave introduces modular skillpacks and SkillZip to enable multi-domain specialization of LLMs under fixed memory budgets, achieving strong performance and speedups, even beating larger monolithic models.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of delivering broad, multi-domain capabilities in LLMs while constrained by memory and latency requirements for deployment.
AI.method: Partition the model's capabilities into lightweight domain-specific delta modules called skillpacks, which reorganize and refine internal knowledge; compress skillpacks into an inference-ready format using SkillZip; deploy under a fixed memory budget to achieve multi-domain efficacy with low latency.
AI.result: A 9B SkillWeave model matches or surpasses baselines and a 32B monolithic LLM on multi-task/agentic benchmarks, with up to 4x speedup.
AI.conclusion: Modularization via skillpacks plus compression enables efficient, high-performance multi-domain LLM deployment within tight memory and latency constraints; demonstrates practical advantage over larger, monolithic models.

title: ORBIS: Output-Guided Token Reduction with Distribution-Aware Matching for Video Diffusion Acceleration
summary:
Diffusion Transformer (DiT) has emerged as a powerful model architecture for generating high-quality images and videos. In the case of video DiT, 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increases token length in proportion to the number of frames, sharply increasing computational cost. Token reduction methods mitigate this cost by exploiting spatial redundancy, but existing approaches rely on inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching algorithms, resulting in poor matching quality and only marginal acceleration.
  To overcome these limitations, we propose ORBIS, an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video DiT. ORBIS leverages the output activation from the previous timestep to obtain more accurate inter-token similarity, substantially improving matching quality and enabling a higher token reduction ratio. We further introduce a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm that captures global token distribution and explicitly minimizes token-pair loss for additional gains. To fully hide DATM latency, we design specialized, deeply pipelined hardware and minimize its hardware cost through quantization, occupying only 2.4% of total area with negligible accuracy loss. Extensive experiments show that ORBIS achieves about 2x higher token reduction ratio than the state-of-the-art approach, AsymRnR, while delivering up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

AI.tldr: ORBIS is a software-hardware co-designed accelerator for video Diffusion Transformers (DiT) that uses previous-timestep activations and a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm to improve inter-token similarity estimation, enabling higher token reduction with minimal area cost; yields substantial speedups and energy savings.
AI.motivation: 3D spatio-temporal attention in video DiT causes token-length growth and high compute; existing token-reduction methods suffer from inaccurate similarity estimates and weak matching, limiting acceleration.
AI.method: Develop a SW-HW co-designed accelerator ORBIS. Leverage prior timestep activations for more accurate inter-token similarity; introduce DATM to model global token distribution and minimize token-pair loss; implement deeply pipelined hardware and apply quantization to keep area at 2.4% of total.
AI.result: ORBIS achieves ~2x higher token reduction ratio than AsymRnR, up to 4.5x speedup, and 79.3% energy reduction relative to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.
AI.conclusion: Combining reuse of temporal activation, distribution-aware token matching, and efficient hardware design yields substantial improvements in efficiency for video DiT, enabling higher token reductions with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Correcting Class Imbalance in Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Tabular Classification
summary: Prior-data fitted networks (PFNs) have achieved exceptional performance on tabular classification tasks. However, like other classifiers, their performance can suffer under the effect of class imbalance, resulting in poor performance for rare classes. Several techniques exist which attempt to mitigate the deleterious effect of class imbalance on classification performance, but the in-context learning (ICL) dynamic of PFNs means that loss-based strategies are impossible, and other techniques are unproven. We have adapted several classical techniques addressing class imbalance and analyzed their performance on PFN classification. We observe that thresholding performs exceptionally well because of the calibration characteristics of PFNs, and downsampling performs comparably because of PFNs exceptional limited-data performance, with the additional benefit of reduced computation cost for inference.

AI.tldr: PFNs' class-imbalance mitigation: thresholding excels due to calibration; downsampling is competitive and more efficient; loss-based methods are impractical under ICL.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance degrades PFN classification; PFNs use in-context learning (ICL), which precludes loss-based balancing strategies; investigate whether traditional imbalance techniques transfer to PFNs.
AI.method: Adapted several classical class-imbalance techniques to PFNs and evaluated their performance on PFN classification, with emphasis on thresholding and downsampling.
AI.result: Thresholding performs exceptionally well owing to PFN calibration characteristics; downsampling performs comparably and offers reduced inference cost due to PFN's limited-data performance.
AI.conclusion: Simple imbalance techniques like thresholding and downsampling are effective for PFNs; PFN calibration and data efficiency drive these results; loss-based approaches remain unsuitable under ICL, suggesting direction for practical deployment and further study.

title: stable-worldmodel: A Platform for Reproducible World Modeling Research and Evaluation
summary: World models are central to building agents that can reason, plan, and generalize beyond their training data. However, research on world models is currently fragmented, with disparate codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols hindering reproducibility and fair comparison. Current practice is further limited by three key bottlenecks: fragile one-off codebases, slow video data loading, and the lack of standardized generalization benchmarks. We present stable-worldmodel (swm), an open-source platform for standardized and reproducible world modeling research and evaluation. It delivers (1) a high-performance Lance-based data layer with native support and conversion tools for MP4, HDF5, and LeRobot datasets, (2) clean, well-tested implementations of modern world model baselines and planning solvers, and (3) a broad suite of environments and tasks extended with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors of variation for systematic in-silico evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization. By unifying the full pipeline under a single, scalable framework, \texttt{swm} dramatically reduces research overhead and accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models.

AI.tldr: swm provides a standardized, high-performance open-source platform for world-model research, unifying data, models, and evaluation to improve reproducibility and fair benchmarking.
AI.motivation: World-model research is fragmented across disparate codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols, leading to poor reproducibility and slow progress. There is a need for standardized data access, implementations, and benchmarks to enable fair comparisons and robust evaluation of generalization.
AI.method: Introduce stable-worldmodel (swm), an open-source platform featuring a Lance-based data layer with import/conversion tools for MP4, HDF5, and LeRobot datasets, clean implementations of modern world-model baselines and planning solvers, and a broad suite of environments with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors for systematic in-silico evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization.
AI.result: swm unifies the full pipeline, reducing research overhead and enabling reproducible experiments while providing comprehensive evaluation capabilities across dynamics, control, and generalization tasks.
AI.conclusion: By consolidating data handling, modeling, and evaluation within a single framework, swm accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models.

title: CLORE: Content-Level Optimization for Reasoning Efficiency
summary: Reinforcement learning post-training has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but often produces unnecessarily long, repetitive, or semantically opaque reasoning traces. Existing efficient reasoning methods mainly regulate response length through explicit budgets or length-aware rewards, leaving intermediate reasoning content weakly supervised. We propose CLORE, a content-level optimization framework that improves reasoning efficiency by editing correct on-policy rollouts. CLORE uses an external augmentation model to delete repetitive segments, illegible or task-irrelevant content, and superfluous reasoning after the solution is established, while preserving the final answer. The resulting augmented--original pairs are optimized with an auxiliary reference-free DPO objective alongside standard policy-gradient training. By restricting augmentation to correct trajectories and performing local deletion, CLORE keeps edited rollouts close to the policy distribution and mitigates off-policy mismatch. Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that CLORE improves the accuracy--efficiency trade-off and remains compatible with GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses further show that CLORE reduces repetitive reasoning, illegible content, and post-answer exploration, supporting content-level supervision as a complementary direction to length-level control.

AI.tldr: CLORE is a content-level optimization framework that edits correct on-policy rollouts to delete repetitive, illegible, or task-irrelevant reasoning, improving the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in mathematical reasoning tasks without large off-policy gaps; it uses an external augmentation model to generate augmented-original pairs and trains with a reference-free DPO objective alongside policy gradient, compatible with several existing methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome inefficiencies in RL post-training where reasoning traces are long, repetitive, or opaque, and existing length-control techniques regulate only token budgets rather than content quality; there is a need for content-level supervision to prune non-essential reasoning without harming the final answer.
AI.method: Apply an external augmentation model to post-solution rollouts to delete repetitive, illegible, or task-irrelevant content while preserving the final answer. Generate augmented-original trajectory pairs from correct on-policy rollsouts. Train with an auxiliary reference-free DPO objective in addition to standard policy-gradient optimization, enforcing that augmentation stays local to correct trajectories to minimize off-policy mismatch.
AI.result: CLORE yields a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B and remains compatible with GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses show reductions in repetitive reasoning, illegible content, and post-answer exploration, supporting content-level supervision as a complementary approach to length-level control.
AI.conclusion: Content-level supervision via selective deletion of nonessential reasoning can significantly improve reasoning efficiency without sacrificing accuracy, and can complement existing length-control methods and RLHF approaches in LLM reasoning tasks.

title: Diverse Yet Consistent: Context-Guided Diffusion with Energy-Based Joint Refinement for Multi-Agent Motion Prediction
summary: Deepgenerative models havebecomeapromisingapproach for human motion prediction due to their ability to capture multimodal distributions and represent diverse human be haviors. However, generating predictions that are both di verse and jointly consistent among interacting agents re mains challenging. In addition, most existing approaches are primarily evaluated using single-agent (marginal) met rics, which fail to fully reflect the joint dynamics of multi agent interactions. We propose a diffusion-based frame work that improves multi-agent motion prediction by lever aging rich contextual information from historical trajecto ries. This information is incorporated through a guidance mechanism to enhance the diversity and expressiveness of predicted motions. To further enforce interaction consis tency, we introduce an energy-based formulation that re fines the joint trajectory distribution while preserving the plausibility of individual trajectories. Extensive experi ments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods. No tably, our approach substantially improves both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics on ETH/UCY over strong marginal baselines. Compared with prior joint prediction methods, it delivers significant gains in marginal metrics while maintaining competitive joint performance.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based multi-agent motion prediction framework that uses historical context via guidance and an energy-based refinement to improve both diversity and joint interaction consistency, achieving state-of-the-art performance on ETH/UCY (ADE/FDE, JADE/JFDE) and four benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of generating diverse, jointly consistent multi-agent trajectories while evaluating with joint metrics, overcoming limitations of single-agent (marginal) evaluations.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework with a guidance mechanism that injects information from historical trajectories to boost diversity, combined with an energy-based formulation that refines the joint trajectory distribution while maintaining plausible individual trajectories.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks show consistent improvements over existing methods. Notably, substantial gains in both marginal metrics (ADE/FDE) and joint metrics (JADE/JFDE) on ETH/UCY over strong marginal baselines, and competitive joint performance compared with prior joint methods while achieving larger marginal gains.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively balances diversity and interaction consistency in multi-agent motion prediction by coupling context-guided diffusion with energy-based joint refinement, yielding improved marginal and competitive joint performance across benchmarks.

title: Quantitative coronary calcification analysis for prediction of myocardial ischemia using non-contrast CT calcium scoring
summary: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is widely recognized as an effective tool for cardiovascular risk stratification. This study aimed to develop a novel machine learning framework for predicting myocardial ischemia from routine non-contrast CTCS scans using quantitative coronary calcium assessment. This study analyzed 1,375 patients who underwent both non-contrast CTCS and regadenoson stress cardiac positron emission tomography myocardial perfusion imaging within one year at University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center. A total of 74 variables, including clinical variables, Agatston score, and calcium-omics features, were evaluated. Relevant features were identified using XGBoost with Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). Predictive models were trained and evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. Among 987 patients, 89 (9%) were positive for myocardial ischemia. The final model incorporated the Agatston score, eight calcium-omics features, and age. The proposed model achieved a precision of 98.9+/-3.0%, sensitivity of 79.2+/-8.4, and F1 score of 87.7+/-5.3%. The addition of calcium-omics features significantly improved predictive performance compared with models using clinical variables alone or clinical variables with the Agatston score (p<0.05). Interestingly, the number of calcified arteries, despite being the lowest-ranked feature based on SHAP analysis, showed the strongest association with myocardial ischemia in logistic regression analysis (odds ratio: 3.63, 95% confidence interval: 2.80-4.77, p<0.00001). We developed a machine learning approach for predicting myocardial ischemia using routinely acquired non-contrast CTCS scans. Calcium-omics features provided incremental predictive value beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring and may support more accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework uses calcium-omics features from non-contrast CT calcium scoring to predict myocardial ischemia, improving over traditional Agatston scoring and clinical models; it achieved high precision and substantial sensitivity with SHAP-guided feature selection.
AI.motivation: To leverage routinely acquired non-contrast CTCS data for predicting myocardial ischemia, enabling accessible risk stratification and improving upon Agatston-only models by incorporating calcium-omics features.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 1,375 patients who underwent both non-contrast CTCS and regadenoson stress SPECT MPI within one year. Evaluated 74 variables (clinical data, Agatston score, calcium-omics features). Feature selection via XGBoost with SHAP. 5-fold cross-validation. Final model included Agatston score, eight calcium-omics features, and age.
AI.result: Among 987 analyzed patients, 89 had ischemia. Final model: precision 98.9±3.0%, sensitivity 79.2±8.4, F1 87.7±5.3. Calcium-omics features added predictive value beyond clinical vars and Agatston score (p<0.05). In logistic regression, number of calcified arteries had strong association (OR=3.63, 95% CI 2.80–4.77, p<0.00001) despite being lowest-ranked by SHAP.
AI.conclusion: A machine learning approach using routine CTCS with calcium-omics features can predict myocardial ischemia and provides incremental predictive value beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring, supporting more accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

title: FRED: A Multi-Modal Autonomous Driving Dataset for Flooded Road Environments
summary: The Flooded Road Environments Dataset (FRED) is, to our knowledge, the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset specifically targeting the collection of data from scenarios involving water hazards on the road. The dataset contains images from a 2.3 MP FLIR Blackfly USB3 camera, 64-beam 360$^\circ$ point clouds from an Ouster OS1-64 LiDAR, and data from an iXblue ATLANS-C IMU corrected by a Geoflex RTK GNSS, from five separate locations captured both during and after flooding events. The data has been released in two formats: a KITTI-style format for easy integration with existing data tools, and the RTMaps format for direct replay of the vehicle's data capture. We provide semantic labels to enable the training and evaluation of both single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods for water hazard detection. Position and velocity, as well as data captured under dry conditions, are provided to enable the development of location-based detection methods that may incorporate maps, and to evaluate other tasks such as localisation and SLAM.

AI.tldr: A new multi-modal autonomous driving dataset, FRED, targets water-hazard on-road scenarios. It collects synchronized data from a 2.3 MP FLIR camera, 64-beam OS1-64 LiDAR, and an iXblue IMU corrected by RTK GNSS across five flood-event locations, with KITTI-style and RTMaps formats, semantic labels, and auxiliary dry-condition data to support single-sensor and sensor-fusion water-hazard detection, localization, and SLAM.
AI.motivation: Water-covered roads pose significant perception and localization challenges and are underrepresented in existing datasets. A multi-modal, labeled dataset facilitates development and benchmarking of detection, fusion, and map-based methods under flood conditions.
AI.method: Data collection at five sites during and after flooding. Multi-modal sensors: infrared camera (FLIR), LiDAR (64 beams), and IMU with RTK GNSS. Data released in KITTI-style and RTMaps formats. Semantic labels provided for water hazards. Includes position/velocity and dry-condition data to support location-based detection, localization, and SLAM benchmarking.
AI.result: First dataset explicitly focused on water hazards in autonomous driving. Provides multi-modal data, two practical formats, semantic annotations, and auxiliary metadata to enable training and evaluation of perception, fusion, localization, and SLAM under flood conditions.
AI.conclusion: FRED fills a critical gap by enabling robust research in water-hazard perception and localization, supporting sensor fusion and map-based methods, with potential for expansion to additional sites and modalities.

title: Towards a compositional semantics for quantitative confidence assessment in assurance arguments
comment: Accepted to the 21st European Dependable Computing Conference (EDCC 2026), Canterbury, UK
summary: Assurance arguments provide a clear and structured way to explain why stakeholders should trust that a system satisfies certain properties, yet widely used notations, e.g.Goal Structuring Notation (GSN), typically lack an operational semantics for deriving assurance confidence. Existing approaches address structure and soundness but largely reason over truth values, not over confidence in the justification of claims. Subjective Logic (SL) offers a calculus of belief, disbelief, and uncertainty with operators for combining opinions, enabling confidence propagation under incomplete, conflicting, or subjective evidence. However, existing SL-based approaches do not provide a uniform, compositional semantics that covers all argument elements and relations to enable overall confidence assessment. We propose a confidence semantics that represents argument elements as SL opinions and maps relations between elements to SL operators modelling how confidence flows, effectively turning the argument into an analyzable confidence network. The approach provides explicit warrants, principled handling of context, preserved provenance, and compatibility with GSN, along with practical guidance using an exemplary assurance confidence assessment.

AI.tldr: Introduces a confidence semantics for assurance arguments by modeling argument elements as Subjective Logic opinions and mapping relations to confidence-flow operators, yielding a compositional confidence network compatible with GSN and preserving provenance.
AI.motivation: GSN and similar notations lack an operational, quantitative notion of confidence and fail to propagate justification confidence under incomplete or conflicting evidence; a uniform semantics is needed for reliable assurance assessment.
AI.method: Represent each argument element as an SL opinion; define SL-based operators to propagate confidence along claim–relation links; build a confidence network with explicit warrants and context; ensure provenance is preserved and compatibility with GSN; illustrate with an exemplar assessment.
AI.result: A formal confidence semantics that enables quantitative, compositional confidence reasoning over assurance arguments; practical guidance and an exemplar demonstration; explicit warrants and preserved provenance.
AI.conclusion: This approach fills the gap in operationalizing confidence in assurance arguments, enabling overall confidence assessment and more trustworthy assurance cases, while remaining compatible with GSN.

title: Models Can Model, But Can't Bind: Structured Grounding in Text-to-Optimization
summary: Text-to-optimization requires two separable capabilities: modeling -- choosing the right optimization structure -- and binding -- grounding every coefficient, index, and parameter in the concrete problem data. We study this via Text2Opt-Bench, a scalable benchmark of solver-verified optimization problems spanning 12 categories, from textbook linear programs to stochastic and multi-objective formulations with up to thousands of variables. Across 10+ models, we find that accuracy collapses as instance data grows, even when the formulation itself is simple. We call this the effective binding limit. We address this via a simple inference-time approach, BIND, which externalizes numeric data to structured files so the model binds data programmatically rather than transcribing from the prompt. BIND improves GPT-5-Nano from 59.1% to 82.4% accuracy, matching pass@5 (82.0%) at lower token cost than pass@1, and GPT-5 from 86.2% to 95.8%. Furthermore, we validate our hypothesis by finetuning a model exclusively on binding and show that it outperforms end-to-end SFT and RL across three structurally distinct optimization categories, with a 1.5B binding specialist alone matching a 7B end-to-end baseline.

AI.tldr: Identifies an effective binding limit in text-to-optimization and demonstrates that an inference-time binding mechanism (BIND) and binding-focused finetuning dramatically improve solver accuracy and efficiency across a scalable benchmark.
AI.motivation: To quantify how accuracy in text-to-optimization deteriorates as problem data scales when the model must bind numeric data from prompts, and to examine whether externalizing data can mitigate this bottleneck.
AI.method: Introduce Text2Opt-Bench, a scalable, solver-verified benchmark spanning 12 categories of optimization problems. Propose BIND, an inference-time approach that externalizes numeric data to structured files so the model binds data programmatically. Evaluate across GPT-5 family models and finite-tuning experiments comparing binding-focused training to end-to-end SFT/RL.
AI.result: BIND raises accuracy significantly: GPT-5-Nano from 59.1% to 82.4% (approaching pass@5 of 82.0% with lower token cost than pass@1); GPT-5 from 86.2% to 95.8%. A 1.5B binding-specialist, when finetuned exclusively on binding, matches a 7B end-to-end baseline across three optimization categories.
AI.conclusion: The study validates the effective binding limit and shows that externalizing numeric data at inference and binding-focused finetuning substantially improve performance and data efficiency, enabling smaller models to reach or exceed end-to-end baselines.

title: SGR-Bench: Benchmarking Search Agents on State-Gated Retrieval
comment: Work in Progress. 23 pages, 7 figures, preprint
summary: Recent advances in large language models and tool-using agents have expanded the range of benchmarked web tasks. Yet an important class of specialized retrieval tasks remains undercharacterized. On many specialized data-retrieval websites, answer-bearing evidence becomes accessible only after establishing the correct site-specific retrieval state through filters, views, hierarchies, or scopes. We term this capability state-gated retrieval (SGR). We introduce SGR-Bench, a benchmark for this setting containing 100 expert-curated tasks spanning six source families and 12 public data ecosystems. Each task requires discovering the appropriate website and configuring its site-specific retrieval state to produce a structured answer. SGR-Bench pairs constraint-guided and goal-oriented formulations of the same underlying problems, enabling controlled comparisons between explicit and implicit guidance for state-gated retrieval. We evaluate eight CLI-based agentic LLM systems and three commercial search-agent products. On SGR-Bench, the strongest system reaches only 66.18% item-level F1, while row-level F1 remains much lower. A manual audit of 156 analyzable failed CLI trajectories shows why: agents often reach a relevant web source, but establish the wrong site-specific retrieval state. Retrieval-scope drift (37.2%) and criterion mismatch (27.6%) dominate, whereas final answer composition accounts for only 10.3%. The dataset and single-case evaluation instructions are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PKUAIWeb/SGR-BENCH.

AI.tldr: SGR-Bench introduces a benchmark for state-gated retrieval on specialized data-retrieval sites; 100 tasks across six source families and 12 ecosystems; evaluates LLMs and search agents; highlights that correct site state is critical and many failures stem from retrieval-scope drift and criterion mismatch.
AI.motivation: To study retrieval tasks that require configuring a site-specific retrieval state (filters, views, scopes)—a neglected capability in existing benchmarks—and quantify how existing agents perform under explicit state-gating constraints.
AI.method: Create 100 expert-curated tasks spanning six source families and 12 ecosystems; present both constraint-guided and goal-oriented formulations of the same problems; evaluate eight CLI-based agentic LLMs and three commercial search-agent products; analyze item-level and row-level F1 metrics; perform a manual audit of failed trajectories to identify failure modes.
AI.result: Best system achieves 66.18% item-level F1; row-level F1 is substantially lower; major failure modes include retrieval-scope drift (37.2%) and criterion mismatch (27.6%), with composition errors accounting for 10.3%; dataset and evaluation instructions are released on HuggingFace.
AI.conclusion: State-gated retrieval poses substantial challenges; proper site-state configuration is essential for correct answers; SGR-Bench enables controlled comparisons between constraint-guided and implicit guidance and highlights the gap between discovering a relevant site and configuring its retrieval state correctly.

title: ForeSplat: Optimization-Aware Foresight for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) models offer fast single-pass reconstruction,but scaling them to match per-scene optimization quality is fundamentally hindered by the scarcity of large-scale 3D annotations.A practical compromise is predict-then-refine,where post-prediction optimization compensates for the limited capacity of the feed-forward network.However,standard feed-forward 3DGS is trained solely for zero-step rendering error,ignoring whether its output constitutes a good initialization for the downstream optimizer.We present ForeSplat,an optimization-aware training framework that equips feed-forward 3DGS models to produce initializations explicitly designed for rapid,effective refinement.By offloading part of the scene-modeling burden to the optimizer,ForeSplat substantially reduces the capacity pressure on the feed-forward model,making high-quality reconstruction feasible even with compact networks.At its core is MetaGrad,a lightweight multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that bypasses costly higher-order differentiation through the 3DGS optimizer.MetaGrad unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory,samples anchor states,and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head as a surrogate optimization-aware signal.This fine-tuning adds no inference cost and enables high-quality reconstruction within seconds after a few refinement steps.We instantiate ForeSplat on diverse backbones,including AnySplat,Pi3X,and a distilled variant tailored for edge deployment.Across all tested architectures,a ForeSplat-trained initialization converges in fewer refinement steps and reaches a higher peak reconstruction quality than its vanilla counterpart,even fully converged.The framework consistently bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization,establishing a practical path toward lightweight,high-fidelity 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: ForeSplat is an optimization-aware training framework for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that uses a lightweight MetaGrad to craft initializations tailored for rapid downstream refinement, reducing capacity demands and enabling high-fidelity reconstructions with compact networks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the gap between fast, single-pass 3DGS reconstructions and the limited per-scene optimization quality due to scarce large-scale 3D annotations; standard training optimizes only zero-shot rendering error, not how good an initialization is for subsequent optimization.
AI.method: Introduce MetaGrad: a lightweight, multi-anchor meta-gradient rule that unrolls a short inner refinement trajectory, samples anchor states, and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to train the prediction head; avoids costly higher-order differentiation, adds no inference cost, and guides the model to produce optimization-friendly initializations. Demonstrated across backbones (AnySplat, Pi3X, distilled edge variant).
AI.result: ForeSplat initializations converge in fewer refinement steps and achieve higher peak reconstruction quality than vanilla initializations, across architectures, even when fully optimized; no extra inference cost.
AI.conclusion: ForeSplat bridges amortized prediction and per-scene optimization, enabling practical, lightweight, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction across diverse backbones.

title: PEARL: Unbiased Percentile Estimation via Contrastive Learning for Industrial-Scale Livestream Recommendation
summary: Recommender systems trained on user interaction data are susceptible to behavioral intensity imbalance--a systematic distortion arising from heterogeneous engagement patterns across users. This imbalance skews feedback signals such that observed interactions no longer faithfully reflect true preferences, causing models to disproportionately amplify signals from highly active users while underrepresenting others, which ultimately degrades recommendation quality and robustness at scale. To address this issue, we propose a nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation framework, PEARL, that models relative preference signals instead of absolute engagement magnitudes. Building upon relative advantage debiasing, PEARL leverages real contrastive interaction samples to approximate percentile relationships directly, without relying on auxiliary distribution estimation models. We provide theoretical justification demonstrating that such pairwise comparisons yield unbiased estimates of percentile-based preference signals. For broader applicability, we introduce a prediction-based bootstrapping mechanism for percentile smoothing to handle sparse and discrete feedback, alongside a generalized value-weighted formulation and a co-training strategy to enhance both modeling flexibility and representation learning. Extensive offline experiments demonstrate that PEARL effectively mitigates behavioral bias and consistently improves recommendation performance across multiple ranking targets. Deployed in a production livestream platform with a combined user base of billions, online A/B testing confirms substantial real-world gains: +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, and -6.91% Report Rate.

AI.tldr: PEARL introduces a nonparametric, contrastive percentile framework to debias behavioral intensity imbalance in recommender systems by modeling relative signals via real contrastive samples; it includes percentile smoothing via prediction-based bootstrapping, a generalized value-weighted formulation, and co-training, with strong offline and production-level results.
AI.motivation: User engagement heterogeneity skews observed feedback, causing biased learning and degraded recommendation quality; a percentile-based, relative-signal approach aims to recover true preferences without estimating engagement distributions.
AI.method: Nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation using real contrastive interaction samples to approximate percentile relationships directly, avoiding auxiliary distribution estimation. Includes prediction-based bootstrapping for sparse/discrete feedback, a generalized value-weighted formulation, and a co-training strategy to improve representation learning.
AI.result: Offline experiments show consistent improvement across ranking targets. In production livestream platform, online A/B tests yield improvements: +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, and -6.91% Report Rate.
AI.conclusion: PEARL effectively mitigates behavioral bias and enhances recommendation quality and robustness at scale, with demonstrated real-world impact in a high-traffic production setting.

title: Beyond Euclidean Proximity: Repairing Latent World Models with Horizon-Matched Trajectory Reachability Metrics
summary: Latent world models can contain the state needed for control, yet their terminal-cost interface can expose the planner to the wrong decision-relevant information. In common latent MPC, candidate sequences are ranked by Euclidean distance between predicted terminal and goal latent states; this assumes that raw latent distance weights reachability-relevant variables correctly. We propose trajectory reachability metrics (TRM), a post-hoc terminal-ranking method for fixed latent world models. TRM trains a small pairwise head from logged trajectory structure and uses it as a replacement or hybrid cost; the encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, and evaluation manifests remain fixed. The key design choice is horizon-aware supervision: the metric is trained on broad, balanced temporal separations to match the long-horizon terminal candidate ranking problem. On a hard TwoRoom benchmark, raw latent planning with LeWorldModel (LeWM) reaches 7.0% success, while full-horizon TRM reaches 97.0%; shuffled temporal-label controls stay at 0.0%. The same recipe improves a PLDM baseline from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds, and a short-horizon TRM variant reaches only 35.0% with the 100,000 pair budget. In TwoRoom, we provide mechanistic evidence for why TRM works: XY position is linearly decodable (R^2=0.998), yet raw latent MSE misranks candidates; the XY-probe rowspace accounts for less than 1% of terminal-goal latent MSE but carries most candidate-quality signal; and SCSA audits show that TRM improves the ordering and selected endpoint seen by the planner. On PushT go50/go75, TRM-style task-state metrics improve SCSA ranking and selected final distance more cleanly than closed-loop success, motivating auxiliary hybrid costs in continuous manipulation. TRM is the planner-facing repair, and audits explain when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc horizon-aware terminal reachability metric (TRM) improves latent-MPC planning by replacing or augmenting terminal costs; yields large gains on TwoRoom and PLDM benchmarks, vs raw latent distance.
AI.motivation: Raw latent distances misrank terminal states because they may not align with long-horizon reachability; terminal-cost interfaces can leak wrong information to the planner.
AI.method: Train a small pairwise head on logged trajectories to predict terminal reachability; horizon-aware supervision with broad temporal separations; use as replacement or hybrid cost; keep encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, evaluation fixed.
AI.result: TwoRoom: TRM 97.0% vs 7.0% with raw; shuffled temporal-label controls 0%; PLDM baseline from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds; short-horizon TRM 35.0% with 100,000 pairs; XY position linearly decodable (R^2=0.998); raw latent MSE misranks candidates; XY-probe rowspace carries most candidate-quality signal; SCSA audits show improved ordering and endpoint; PushT go50/go75: TRM improves SCSA ranking and final distance more than closed-loop success.
AI.conclusion: TRM is a planner-facing repair; audits explain when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity; enables long-horizon terminal ranking for latent-world-model planning.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models as Live Strategic Agents: Provider Performance, Hybrid Decomposition, and Operational Gaps in Timed Risk Play
comment: 13 pages, 7 figures. Code and tracked notes: https://github.com/hcekne/risk-game . Public runtime artifact index: https://github.com/hcekne/risk-game/blob/main/docs/article-plans/public_experiment_artifacts.md
summary: Static benchmarks capture only part of how large language models behave in practice. Real systems place models inside repeated loops with time limits, formatting constraints, and failure modes. We study this setting in a timed multi-phase Risk environment with explicit victory targets and repeated planning and execution cycles. In a replicated 32-game cross-provider championship under frozen rules, gemini-3.1-pro-preview won 20 of 32 games against gpt-5.1, claude-opus-4-7, and kimi-k2.6, and the pooled winner distribution differs strongly from an equal-strength null (p approx 1.5 x 10^-5). We then separate planning from execution by standardizing execution on a cheaper Gemini Flash scaffold. Under this design, a pooled 32-game planner bakeoff is consistent with near-equality (p approx 0.821), which indicates that much of the earlier provider spread came from end-to-end system behavior rather than planning alone. To study mechanism, we analyze saved planning and execution traces from the provider championship. Gemini refers to the terminal objective far more often than the other models and increases that focus as victory approaches. Gemini also converts more turns into deep conquest chains, even though it is not the cleanest runtime.
