These results show that live-agent performance depends on objective tracking, execution conversion, cost, and runtime reliability, and they support evaluating LLMs as components in bounded workflows rather than as isolated benchmark respondents.

AI.tldr: Live-agent benchmarks reveal that system-level constraints (objective tracking, execution conversion, cost, runtime reliability) largely drive performance, not pure planning ability. Planner-only comparisons yield near-equality, supporting evaluating LLMs within bounded workflows rather than in isolation.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLMs perform when embedded in timed, multi-phase decision loops and to separate planning quality from end-to-end system behavior in cross-provider evaluations.
AI.method: 1) A timed, multi-phase Risk environment with repeated planning/execution cycles; 32-game cross-provider championship under frozen rules comparing Gemini-3.1-pro-preview with GPT-5.1, Claude Opus, and Kimi-K2.6; statistical comparison to an equal-strength null. 2) Separate planning from execution using a cheaper Gemini Flash scaffold to run a planner bakeoff and assess planning in isolation. 3) Analyze saved planning and execution traces to identify mechanisms driving performance (objective focus, conquest chains, runtime reliability).
AI.result: Gemini-3.1-pro-preview won 20 of 32 games (p ≈ 1.5×10^-5) against GPT-5.1, Claude Opus, and Kimi-K2.6; planner-bakeoff with standardized execution yielded near-equality (p ≈ 0.821), indicating prior performance gaps were largely due to end-to-end system behavior rather than planning alone. Analysis of traces showed Gemini prioritizes terminal objectives as victories approach and generates deeper conquest chains, even if its runtime is not the cleanest.
AI.conclusion: Live-agent performance in bounded workflows depends on objective-tracking, execution conversion, cost, and runtime reliability. Evaluating LLMs as components in bounded workflows provides a more faithful assessment than evaluating isolated prompts in static benchmarks.

title: SO-Mamba: State-Ownership Mamba for Unrolled MRI Reconstruction
summary: Accelerated MRI reconstruction requires recovering missing details while preserving anatomically coherent structures across large spatial regions. State-space models such as Mamba provide efficient long-range modeling, making them attractive learned regularizers for unrolled reconstruction. However, in a data-consistency-coupled unrolled solver, different stages operate on different reconstruction iterates, where the resident carrier should preserve coherent reconstruction content across stages while stage-dependent non-resident evidence is tied to the current update. Treating these roles uniformly can place persistent resident-carrier evidence and update-dependent non-resident evidence into the same recurrent content route. We therefore propose SO-Mamba, a state-ownership Mamba regularizer that assigns reconstruction evidence within each Mamba stage to recurrent residency, state-interface access, and non-state output correction. SO-Mamba implements this ownership rule with a State-Ownership Router (SOR), which constructs a resident carrier for recurrent content and routes non-resident evidence to affine modulation of the B/C state interfaces and an output correction outlet. The resident carrier supplies the Mamba content route, while the non-resident evidence stream adapts the state interfaces and contributes through the output outlet without entering the recurrent content route. We further introduce a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic that separates hidden-state storage from readout expression by measuring outer-band energy in the selective-scan state trajectory and the post-scan Mamba readout. Experiments on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks spanning diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations show that SO-Mamba consistently improves over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: SO-Mamba introduces a state-ownership regularizer for Mamba-based MRI reconstruction that segregates resident recurrent content from non-resident evidence via a State-Ownership Router, improving cross-stage coherence; a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic is proposed; empirical results on five benchmarks show consistent gains over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive efficiency.
AI.motivation: In unrolled data-consistency MRI reconstruction, different stages operate on different iterates, risking dilution of persistent residual information when all evidence shares the same recurrent path. A mechanism to preserve resident content while routing stage-specific evidence is needed.
AI.method: Define SO-Mamba regularizer with resident carrier for recurrent content, and a non-state evidence path modulated at state interfaces and output; implement via State-Ownership Router (SOR) to route non-resident evidence; two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic measuring energy in selective-scan trajectory and post-scan readout.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five public MRI benchmarks across anatomies, sampling patterns, coil configurations shows SO-Mamba consistently outperforms CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive computation time.
AI.conclusion: State ownership in regularizers yields better cross-stage coherence and reconstruction quality in learned unrolled MRI reconstructions; the proposed diagnostic further aids interpretability of internal state dynamics.

title: Machine learning prediction of obstructive coronary artery disease using opportunistic coronary calcium and epicardial fat assessments from CT calcium scoring scans
summary: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is a cost-effective imaging modality widely used to detect coronary artery calcifications. This study aimed to develop an advanced machine learning framework that utilizes quantitative analyses of coronary calcium and epicardial fat from CTCS images to predict obstructive coronary artery disease (CAD). The study population consisted of 1,324 patients from the SCOT-HEART clinical trial who underwent both CTCS and coronary CT angiography. We extracted and analyzed a broad range of features, including 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics features from the CTCS images. Feature selection was conducted using the CatBoost algorithm combined with SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) values. Predictive modeling utilized the CatBoost gradient boosting method, focusing on the most informative features. From an initial set of 424 candidate features, 14 were identified as most predictive through the CatBoost-SHAP method. The top two predictive features originated from fat-omics, with the remaining 12 features derived from calcium-omics. The optimized model achieved robust predictive capabilities, demonstrating a sensitivity of 83.1+/-4.6%, specificity of 93.8+/-1.7%, accuracy of 85.3+/-2.0%, and an F1 score of 73.9+/-3.3%. Inclusion of calcium-omics and fat-omics data significantly improved predictive performance. Notably, the model also showed reliable predictive accuracy in patients with diverse coronary calcium scores, including cases with obstructive CAD despite a zero-calcium score. This innovative approach holds promise for improving clinical decision-making and potentially reducing dependence on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostic procedures, particularly within low-to intermediate-risk patient groups.

AI.tldr: Machine learning on non-contrast CT calcium scoring (CTCS) using calcium-omics and epicardial fat-omics predicts obstructive coronary artery disease (CAD) with high specificity and good sensitivity, including cases with zero calcium score.
AI.motivation: Improve non-contrast imaging-based risk stratification for obstructive CAD and reduce reliance on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostics, especially in low-to intermediate-risk patients.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 1,324 SCOT-HEART participants who underwent CTCS and coronary CT angiography. Extracted 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics features from CTCS. Feature selection via CatBoost with SHAP values; predictive modeling with CatBoost gradient boosting. From 424 candidates, 14 features were selected (2 from fat-omics, 12 from calcium-omics).
AI.result: The optimized CatBoost model achieved sensitivity 83.1±4.6%, specificity 93.8±1.7%, accuracy 85.3±2.0%, and F1 score 73.9±3.3%. Including calcium-omics and fat-omics significantly improved performance. The model maintained predictive accuracy across a range of calcium scores, including obstructive CAD with zero calcium score, suggesting potential to reduce reliance on contrast-enhanced/invasive tests, particularly for low-to intermediate-risk groups.
AI.conclusion: Integrating calcium-omics and epicardial fat-omics from non-contrast CTCS with CatBoost-based feature selection yields accurate prediction of obstructive CAD, enabling improved risk stratification and potential reduction of invasive diagnostic procedures.

title: An Evidence Hierarchy for Bayesian Object Classification via OSINT-Aided Heterogeneous Sensor Fusion
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure; \c{opyright} 2026 The Authors. Submitted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Multisensor Fusion and Integration (MFI 2026). Under review
summary: Heterogeneous sensor fusion is vital for detecting, localizing, and classifying CBRNE threats. However, individual sensors are often only capable of detecting a subset of relevant threats with varying reliability or can even provide only indirect threat indications, making threat classification challenging. Furthermore, high clutter rates on the sensor side present a great challenge for fusion systems. Additionally, the limited availability of high quality datasets hinders the advancement of learning-based detection and classification models in smart sensors. To mitigate these sensor related shortcomings, a context-aware and domain knowledge-enhanced fusion process is proposed. First, a novel evidence hierarchy is established that enables modeling of direct, indicative, and contextual information. Second, contextual information about the environment is introduced into the fusion process, by collecting, processing, and exploiting OSINT inputs. Third, all levels of the evidence hierarchy are used to craft a Bayesian threat type classification mechanism with domain knowledge-informed priors. The proposed methodology is evaluated in simulated scenarios, and the results demonstrate the benefit of the proposed fusion approach in terms of robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, with an overall classification accuracy of up to 95%.

AI.tldr: Proposes a context-aware, domain-knowledge–driven fusion framework for heterogeneous sensors to detect/classify CBRNE threats, using a multi-level evidence hierarchy, OSINT-driven context, and Bayesian priors; evaluated in simulation with up to 95% accuracy and robustness to clutter.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous sensors provide partial, unreliable, or indirect indicators; high clutter and scarce high-quality datasets hinder learning-based detection; need robust fusion incorporating context and domain knowledge.
AI.method: 1) Define evidence hierarchy distinguishing direct, indicative, and contextual information; 2) incorporate OSINT environmental context into fusion; 3) perform Bayesian threat-type classification using priors informed by domain knowledge across hierarchy; evaluation in simulated scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates robustness to clutter and prior mismatch; achieves up to 95% classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware fusion with domain-informed priors improves threat classification performance under clutter and data limitations; promising for smart sensor networks; future work likely includes real-world validation and ablation studies.

title: Unlocking Proactivity in Task-Oriented Dialogue
summary: Proactive task-oriented dialogue (TOD), such as outbound sales, demands a persuasive agent that actively probes the user's concerns and steers the conversation toward acceptance within a bounded number of turns. Yet post-trained LLMs are inherently conservative, and reward-shaping RL (e.g., GRPO) struggles since it only re-weights what an already passive policy samples. We show that conditioning on the user's latent concerns unlocks proactive capability that no amount of sampling can undermine, establishing these concerns as a pivotal training-time signal. To operationalize this finding, we build the \textbf{Cognitive User Simulator}, which models each user as a stratified persona comprising observable external traits and hidden internal concerns. The simulator produces faithful and diverse interactions, while emitting per-turn state dynamics that track persuasion progress. We then introduce \textbf{Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization}, which converts the modeled concerns and the simulation state transition into complementary training objectives: (1) \emph{Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation} that transfers concern-aware behavior from a privileged view of the same policy into its deployable, conversation-only view; and (2) \emph{State-Transition Policy Refinement} ...

AI.tldr: Conditioning dialogue policies on user latent concerns enables proactive, persuasive task-oriented dialogue; this is operationalized via a Cognitive User Simulator and Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization with two objectives: asymmetric self-distillation and state-transition policy refinement.
AI.motivation: Proactive persuasion in bounded TOD is limited by conservative LLMs and reward-shaping RL; latent user concerns provide a pivotal training signal to push the agent toward acceptance within a constraint on turns.
AI.method: Develop a Cognitive User Simulator where each user is a stratified persona with observable traits and hidden concerns; use the simulator to generate faithful, diverse interactions and per-turn state dynamics. Propose Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization comprising (1) Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation transferring concern-aware behavior from a privileged to a deployable policy view, and (2) State-Transition Policy Refinement leveraging simulation transitions to refine the policy.
AI.result: The framework confirms that latent concerns unlock proactive behavior beyond sampling; the simulator produces realistic interactions and progression signals, and the training objectives translate concerns and state dynamics into complementary learning signals that enhance proactive persuasion.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning on user concerns is a pivotal training-time signal for proactive TOD; the Cognitive User Simulator and Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization offer a principled pathway to train more proactive, persuasive agents in bounded dialogue settings.

title: AgroVG: A Large-Scale Multi-Source Benchmark for Agricultural Visual Grounding
summary: Visual grounding, the task of localizing objects described by natural-language expressions, is a foundational capability for agricultural AI systems, enabling applications such as selective weeding, disease monitoring, and targeted harvesting. Reliable evaluation of agricultural visual grounding remains challenging because agricultural targets are often small, repetitive, occluded, or irregularly shaped, and instructions may refer to one, many, or no objects in an image. Evaluating this capability therefore requires jointly testing localization accuracy, target-set completeness, and existence-aware abstention. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{AgroVG}, a multi-source benchmark that formulates agricultural grounding as generalized set prediction: given an image and a referring expression, a model must return all matching target instances or abstain when no target is present. AgroVG contains 10{,}071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from ten source datasets across six target families: crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, and tree canopy. It supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all six families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on sources with reliable instance-level pixel annotations, with queries covering single-target, multi-target, and target-absent regimes. AgroVG further provides task-specific protocols for box-set matching and query-level mask coverage. Zero-shot evaluation of 26 model configurations spanning closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, and specialized grounding systems reveals persistent gaps: the best multi-target Set-$F_1$ reaches only 0.35, and the best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 remains below 0.17. Data and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AgroVG-5172/ .

AI.tldr: AgroVG introduces a large multi-source benchmark for agricultural visual grounding framed as generalized set prediction, emphasizing target completeness and abstention, and reveals substantial performance gaps across models.
AI.motivation: Reliable evaluation of agricultural grounding is challenging due to small, repetitive, occluded, or irregular targets; queries may refer to one or many or no targets, requiring joint testing of localization, target-set completeness, and abstention.
AI.method: AgroVG aggregates 10,071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from 10 sources across six target families (crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, tree canopy); supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all six and instance-mask grounding (T2) where pixel-accurate annotations exist; introduces box-set matching and query-level mask coverage protocols; evaluates 26 models in zero-shot settings (closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, specialized grounding systems) on single-target, multi-target, and target-absent regimes.
AI.result: Dataset shows persistent gaps: best multi-target Set-F1 = 0.35; best positive-query mask success rate (IoU@0.75) < 0.17; data and code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: AgroVG exposes substantial gaps in agricultural grounding and provides a comprehensive benchmark and protocols to drive progress, enabling robust evaluation across localization, cardinality, and existence-aware abstention.

title: On the Sample Complexity of Discounted Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Certainty Equivalents
comment: Accepted to RLC 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2506.00286
summary: We study risk-sensitive reinforcement learning in finite discounted MDPs, where a generative model of the MDP is assumed to be available. We consider a family or risk measures called the optimized certainty equivalent (OCE), which includes important risk measures such as entropic risk, CVaR, and mean-variance. Our focus is on the sample complexities of learning the optimal state-action value function (value learning) and an optimal policy (policy learning) under recursive OCE. We provide an exact characterization of utility functions $u$ for which the corresponding OCE defines an objective that is PAC-learnable. We analyze a simple model-based approach and derive PAC sample complexity bounds. We establish that whenever $u$ does not have full domain $\text{dom}(u)\neq \mathbb{R}$, the corresponding problem is not PAC-learnable. Finally, we establish corresponding lower bounds for both value and policy learning, demonstrating tightness in the size $SA$ of state-action space, and for a more restricted class of utilities, we derive lower bounds that makes the dependence on the effective horizon $\frac{1}{1-γ}$ explicit. Specifically, for $\text{CVaR}_τ$ we show that the correct dependence on $τ$ is $\frac{1}{τ^2}$, thus improving by a factor of $\frac{1}τ$ over state-of-the-art although our bound has a suboptimal dependence on $\frac{1}{1-γ}$.

AI.tldr: Characterizes PAC-learnability and sample complexity for risk-sensitive RL with optimized certainty equivalent (OCE) in finite discounted MDPs; provides upper and lower bounds, shows non-PAC-learnability when the utility's domain is not all real numbers, and gives tight bounds in SA; for CVaR, achieves a 1/τ^2 dependence, improving over prior work but with suboptimal 1/(1-γ) dependence.
AI.motivation: To understand the learnability and sample complexity of risk-sensitive objectives in reinforcement learning when using OCE risk measures, including CVaR, entropic risk, and mean-variance, and to establish tight upper and lower bounds.
AI.method: Analyze finite discounted MDPs with a generative model under a simple model-based approach. Provide an exact characterization of utility functions u for which OCE is PAC-learnable. Derive PAC sample complexity bounds, and prove non-PAC-learnability when dom(u) ≠ R. Establish lower bounds for value and policy learning, with tightness in the SA factor; specialize to CVaR to obtain explicit τ-dependent bounds.
AI.result: The paper shows a complete characterization of when OCE-based objectives are PAC-learnable. It provides upper bounds via a simple model-based method, and matching lower bounds that scale with SA (state-action size). If dom(u) ≠ R, the problem is not PAC-learnable. For CVaR, the correct τ-dependence is 1/τ^2, improving over prior work by 1/τ, though the bound has a suboptimal 1/(1-γ) dependence.
AI.conclusion: OCE-based RL objectives exhibit a sharp learnability boundary determined by the domain of the utility. The results yield tight sample complexity bounds for value and policy learning, with a notable improvement for CVaR’s dependence on τ; horizon dependence remains an area with suboptimal bounds.

title: Diffusion-guided Generalizable Enhancer for Urban Scene Reconstruction
summary: Urban scene reconstruction from real-world observations has emerged as a powerful tool for self-driving development and testing. While current neural rendering approaches achieve high-fidelity rendering along the recorded trajectories, their quality degrades significantly under large viewpoint shifts, limiting the applicability for closed-loop simulation. Recent works have shown promising results in using diffusion models to enhance quality at these challenging viewpoints and distill improvements back into 3D representations. However, they often require costly per-scene optimization, and the distilled representations remain fragile and fail to generalize beyond limited synthesized views. To address these limitations, we propose GenRe, a novel diffusion-guided generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction. GenRe takes as input any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and fixes the deficiencies within a few minutes. By learning to distill generative priors across diverse scenes, GenRe produces robust and high-fidelity representation efficiently that generalizes reliably to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane change). Experiments show that GenRe outperforms existing methods in both quality and efficiency and benefits various downstream tasks, enabling robust and scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: GenRe is a diffusion-guided generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction that improves a pretrained 3D Gaussian representation. It distills generative priors across diverse scenes, requiring only minutes to fix deficiencies, and generalizes to challenging unseen viewpoints, outperforming prior methods in quality and efficiency for robust sensor simulation.
AI.motivation: Current neural rendering methods degrade under large viewpoint shifts and diffusion-based enhancements often require costly per-scene optimization with fragile, non-generalizable distilled representations. There is a need for a fast, generalizable, high-fidelity 3D representation suitable for autonomous driving simulation across diverse viewpoints.
AI.method: GenRe takes a pretrained 3D Gaussian representation as input and uses diffusion priors to distill generative knowledge across diverse urban scenes. It fixes deficiencies within minutes and yields a robust, high-fidelity representation that generalizes to unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane changes) without per-scene optimization.
AI.result: GenRe outperforms existing methods in both visual quality and efficiency, and its representations benefit downstream tasks such as robust and scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving, especially under challenging viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: GenRe offers a fast, diffusion-guided, generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction that reduces the need for expensive per-scene optimization while delivering high-quality, viewpoint-robust 3D representations suitable for autonomous driving simulations.

title: SciCore-Mol: Augmenting Large Language Models with Pluggable Molecular Cognition Modules
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are central to the one-for-all intelligent paradigm, but they face a fundamental challenge when dealing with heterogeneous scientific data such as molecules: the inherent gap between discrete linguistic symbols and topological molecular or continuous reaction data leads to significant information loss and semantic noise in text-based reasoning. We propose SciCore-Mol, a modular framework that bridges this gap through three deeply integrated pluggable cognitive modules: a topology-aware perception module, a latent diffusion-based molecular generation module, and a reaction-aware reasoning module. Each module is coupled to the LLM backbone through learned representation interfaces, enabling richer information exchange than is possible with text-only tool feedback. Our experiments on diverse chemical tasks demonstrate that SciCore-Mol achieves strong comprehensive performance across molecular understanding, generation, reaction prediction, and general chemistry knowledge, with an 8B-parameter open-source system that is competitive with and in several dimensions surpasses proprietary large models. This work provides a systematic blueprint for equipping LLMs with scientific expertise through decoupled, pluggable, and flexibly orchestrated modules, with direct implications for drug design, chemical synthesis, and broader scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: A modular SciCore-Mol framework bridges discrete language with continuous chemical data via topology-aware perception, diffusion-based molecular generation, and reaction-aware reasoning, enabling richer LLM reasoning and generation across chemistry tasks with an 8B open-source model.
AI.motivation: To overcome information loss and semantic noise when using text-only representations for heterogeneous chemical data, by integrating dedicated cognitive modules that handle topology, generation, and reaction reasoning and connect them to the LLM.
AI.method: Three pluggable modules integrated with the LLM through learned representation interfaces: (1) topology-aware perception module to encode molecular topology and related features, (2) latent diffusion-based molecular generation module for chemistry-aware generation, (3) reaction-aware reasoning module to reason about chemical reactions. These modules are decoupled, modular, and orchestrated flexibly with the LLM backbone.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across molecular understanding, generation, reaction prediction, and general chemistry knowledge shows strong performance. The 8B-parameter open-source SciCore-Mol system is competitive with and in some dimensions surpasses proprietary large models.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a systematic blueprint for empowering LLMs with specialized scientific capabilities via decoupled, pluggable modules, enabling broader applications in drug design, chemical synthesis, and scientific discovery.

title: HyLoVQA: Dynamic Hypernetwork-Generated Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Visual Question Answering
summary: Continual Visual Question Answering (VQA) requires learning from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions while preserving past knowledge. Most prior methods adapt by updating a largely shared parameter set. This often leads to cross-level task interference, hindering accurate adaptation to the current task and object. To address this limitation, we propose HyLoVQA. It maintains a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors. The bank stores the content of visual objects and textual tasks, and they are updated using current input features. Conditioned on retrieved anchors, a hypernetwork generates lightweight Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters. This ensures parameter efficiency, allowing the model to adapt to each task and object dynamically. Additionally, we formulate an alignment loss that aligns semantic discrepancies in the feature space with functional changes in the parameter space, thereby constraining LoRA adapters to remain focused on the current task and object. Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA under both standard and compositional settings demonstrate the superiority of HyLoVQA over prior state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: HyLoVQA introduces drift-resilient anchor memory and a hypernetwork that generates LoRA adapters for continual VQA, with an alignment loss, achieving state-of-the-art on VQA v2 and NExT-QA in standard and compositional settings.
AI.motivation: To overcome cross-level task interference and degraded adaptation in continual VQA caused by heavily shared parameters when learning from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions.
AI.method: Maintain a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors capturing visual object content and textual task information, updated with current inputs. Retrieve anchors to condition a hypernetwork that generates lightweight LoRA adapters per task/object. Introduce an alignment loss that links semantic differences in feature space with functional changes in parameter space to keep adapters focused on the current task/object.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA show HyLoVQA outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods under both standard and compositional settings, demonstrating improved adaptability and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: HyLoVQA provides a parameter-efficient, dynamically task/object-specific continual VQA solution by combining anchor-based memory, a hypernetwork for LoRA adapters, and an alignment constraint that aligns semantic and parameter changes.

title: Position: The Time for Sampling Is Now! Charting a New Course for Bayesian Deep Learning
comment: In Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, PMLR 306, 2026
summary: The practical adoption of sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) remains limited, partly due to persistent misconceptions about the feasibility and efficiency of sampling. This position paper argues that SAI has achieved computational parity with optimization-based methods and is at the verge of superseding such methods for effective and efficient inference in BNNs. This development should be in the interest of the whole community, promoting BNNs as a principled paradigm with its long-standing yet unfulfilled promise of providing principled uncertainty quantification for neural networks. SAI can even do more -- yielding superior prediction performance through model averaging, serving as the foundation for a plethora of possible downstream tasks, and providing crucial insights into the landscape of BNNs. In order to make such a change happen and unfold the potential of sampling, overcoming current misconceptions is a necessary first step. The next step is to realign research efforts toward addressing remaining challenges in SAI. In particular, the community must focus on two core problems: sufficient exploration of the posterior landscape and high-fidelity distillation of posterior samples for efficient downstream inference. By addressing conceptual and practical obstacles, we can unlock the full potential of SAI and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

AI.tldr: Sampling-based inference in Bayesian neural networks has reached computational parity with optimization-based methods and is poised to supersede them with improvements in posterior exploration and distillation.
AI.motivation: To challenge misconceptions about the practicality of SAI and advocate for its broader adoption as a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, model averaging, and downstream tasks in Bayesian deep learning.
AI.method: Position/argument paper that analyzes current limitations of SAI, articulates two core research priorities (adequate exploration of the posterior and high-fidelity distillation of samples), and outlines a roadmap for realigning research efforts.
AI.result: The paper argues that SAI can match optimization-based methods in efficiency, yield superior predictions via model averaging, and provide valuable insights into the BNN landscape; achieving these benefits requires addressing exploration and distillation challenges.
AI.conclusion: Call for the community to focus on sufficient posterior exploration and high-fidelity distillation to unlock SAI's potential and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

title: Pre-VLA: Preemptive Runtime Verification for Reliable Vision-Language-Action and World-Model Rollouts
summary: While large vision-language-action (VLA) models and generative world models (WM) have advanced long-horizon embodied intelligence, their practical deployment remains challenged by uncertainty in learning-based action generation. Low-quality actions may cause physical failures during execution or lead to misleading world-model rollouts with redundant rendering costs. To address this issue, we propose Pre-VLA, a unified runtime verification architecture that performs preemptive action validity assessment before physical execution or world-model imagination. Pre-VLA leverages an efficient multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head to predict both safety confidence and critic-derived advantage scores for candidate action chunks. To handle severe class imbalance and unstable boundary decisions, we train Pre-VLA with a multi-task objective combining Focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration. During deployment, a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler filters low-quality actions and triggers adaptive resampling under a limited computation budget. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that Pre-VLA improves the average closed-loop success rate across four suites from 30.79\% to 37.62\% over RynnVLA-002, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

AI.tldr: Pre-VLA provides a pre-execution verifier for VLA and world-model systems that filters low-quality actions using a multimodal backbone and dual-branch head, enabling adaptive resampling under compute limits and boosting success rates.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty in learning-based action generation can cause physical failures and wasteful or misleading world-model rollouts; a lightweight runtime mechanism is needed to validate actions before execution or imagination.
AI.method: Introduce Pre-VLA with a multimodal backbone employing modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head that outputs safety confidence and critic-derived action advantage for candidate action chunks. Train with a multi-task objective combining Focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration to handle class imbalance and unstable decision boundaries. Deploy with a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler that filters poor actions and adaptively resamples within a limited computation budget.
AI.result: On the LIBERO benchmark, Pre-VLA raises the average closed-loop success rate from 30.79% (RynnVLA-002 baseline) to 37.62%, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.
AI.conclusion: Pre-VLA demonstrates effective, low-latency pre-execution validation that improves safety and efficiency in VLA/WM systems, with a practical resampling strategy that respects computational budgets.

title: Cross-domain benchmarks reveal when coordinated AI agents improve scientific inference from partial evidence
summary: Scientific evidence often spans instruments, databases, and disciplines, so no single source records the full phenomenon. This makes it difficult to determine when coordinated AI agents add value over simpler scientific workflows. We evaluate this question with a cross-domain benchmark spanning four scientific tasks: mapping molecular structure into musical representations, detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence, and vetting transiting-exoplanet candidates. Each case uses a frozen evaluation panel, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and stated limitations. The results define three operating regimes. When different disciplines each capture only part of the phenomenon, cross-channel composites improve over single-channel baselines: climate-vector emergence reaches AUROC 0.944 and exoplanet vetting reaches AUROC 0.955. However, the exoplanet workflow is effectively tied with a strong combined-summary baseline, showing that decomposition does not always improve top-line performance. When one signal dominates, as in paradigm-shift detection, coordination mainly improves interpretation and traceability. For molecular sonification, the gain is representational rather than predictive. ScienceClaw x Infinite provides the auditable artifact and provenance layer for this evaluation. The benchmark therefore assigns value to coordination only when the corresponding performance, provenance, or representation claim is supported by explicit comparators.

AI.tldr: A cross-domain benchmark assesses when coordinating AI across channels adds value in science tasks; results reveal three regimes: beneficial when signals are complementary; limited when one signal dominates; representational gains in molecular sonification; provenance and explicit comparators are essential to claim value.
AI.motivation: To determine whether coordinated AI agents provide value beyond traditional, single-source scientific workflows, given fragmentation across instruments, databases, and disciplines, by establishing a validated, auditable cross-domain benchmark.
AI.method: Four tasks spanning different domains: mapping molecular structure into musical representations, detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence, and vetting transiting-exoplanet candidates. A frozen evaluation panel, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and stated limitations are used. Results are interpreted across three regimes of coordination; ScienceClaw x Infinite provides an auditable artifact and provenance layer.
AI.result: Three operating regimes emerge: (1) Complementary signals across disciplines yield improvements over single-channel baselines (e.g., climate-vector emergence AUROC 0.944; exoplanet vetting AUROC 0.955). However, exoplanet workflows can be dominated by a strong combined-summary baseline, limiting gains from decomposition. (2) When one signal dominates (paradigm-shift detection), coordination mainly improves interpretation and traceability. (3) For molecular sonification, gains are primarily representational rather than predictive. The benchmark emphasizes evaluation with explicit comparators to justify coordination value.
AI.conclusion: Coordination across channels provides value only when its claims are supported by explicit performance, provenance, or representation comparisons; the cross-domain benchmark clarifies when cross-channel AI coordination is advantageous and when it is not.

title: GA-VLN: Geometry-Aware BEV Representation for Efficient Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Despite significant progress in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), existing approaches still rely on dense RGB videos that produce excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, resulting in substantial computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning. To address these issues, we introduce the Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV) - a compact, 3D-grounded feature representation that integrates both explicit and implicit geometric cues into multimodal large language model (MLLM) - based navigation systems. We construct BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout that preserves geometric consistency while reducing token redundancy. To further enrich geometric understanding, we incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into the BEV space, injecting structural priors learned from large-scale 3D reconstruction tasks. Together, these complementary cues - explicit depth-based projection and implicit learned priors - yield compact yet spatially expressive representations that substantially improve navigation efficiency and performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating the robustness and data efficiency of the proposed GA-VLN framework.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware BEV representation (GA-BEV) for vision-language navigation that uses RGB-D to create compact 3D-grounded features, fusing explicit depth projection with implicit 3D priors, achieving state-of-the-art data-efficient results in MLLM-based navigation without DAgger or mixed VQA.
AI.motivation: VLN methods suffer from dense RGB video leading to excessive patch tokens and weak explicit spatial structure, causing high computational cost and limited spatial reasoning. A geometry-aware, compact 3D-grounded representation can reduce tokens and improve geometry-aware navigation.
AI.method: 1) Construct BEV spatial maps by projecting RGB-D features into 3D space and aggregating into an agent-centric layout with geometric consistency and reduced token redundancy. 2) Incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into BEV to inject structural priors. 3) Integrate the GA-BEV representation into an MLLM-based navigation system. 4) Achieve results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training.
AI.result: GA-BEV yields compact, spatially expressive representations by combining explicit depth-based projection and implicit 3D priors, leading to improved navigation efficiency and performance, and achieving state-of-the-art results in data-efficient VLN.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-aware BEV framework demonstrates that combining explicit and implicit geometric cues within a BEV space can substantially improve VLN, offering robust, data-efficient performance for MLLM-based navigation.

title: Memory-R2: Fair Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Memory-Augmented LLM Agents
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents enable interactions that extend beyond finite context windows by storing, updating, and reusing information across sessions. However, training such agents with reinforcement learning in multi-session environments is challenging because memory turns the agent's past actions into part of its future environment. Once different rollouts write, update, or delete different memories, they no longer share the same intermediate memory state, making trajectory-level comparisons fundamentally unfair. This violates a key assumption behind group-relative methods such as GRPO, where rollouts are compared as if they were sampled from the same effective environment. Consequently, trajectory-level rewards provide noisy or biased credit signals for long-horizon memory operations. To address this challenge, we introduce Memory-R2, a training framework for long-horizon memory-augmented LLM agents. Its core algorithm, LoGo-GRPO, combines local and global group-relative optimization. The global objective preserves end-to-end learning from long-horizon trajectory-level rewards, while local rerollouts compare different memory-operation outcomes from the same intermediate memory state, yielding fairer group comparisons and more precise supervision for memory construction. Beyond credit assignment, Memory-R2 jointly optimizes memory formation and memory evolution with a shared-parameter co-learning design, where a fact extractor and a memory manager are instantiated from the same LLM backbone through role-specific prompts. To stabilize multi-step RL over long memory horizons, we adopt a progressive curriculum that increases the training horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions. Together, these components provide an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings.

AI.tldr: Memory-R2 provides a training framework for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session RL by addressing biased trajectory credit from memory state changes; combines LoGo-GRPO with local/global group-relative objectives, co-learning memory components, and a progressive horizon curriculum to stabilize training.
AI.motivation: Unfair trajectory-level comparisons arise because memory stores past actions, causing different rollouts to have diverging intermediate memory states and biased credit signals for long-horizon memory operations.
AI.method: Core algorithm LoGo-GRPO with a global objective preserving end-to-end learning and local rerollouts comparing outcomes from the same intermediate memory state. A shared-parameter co-learning design instantiates a fact extractor and a memory manager from the same LLM backbone via role-specific prompts. A progressive curriculum increases training horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions.
AI.result: Proposes Memory-R2 and LoGo-GRPO, detailing components that enable fairer memory-based credit assignment and joint optimization of memory formation/evolution; claims improved stability and more precise supervision for memory construction in long-horizon RL, though explicit empirical metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Memory-R2 offers an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents operating in long-horizon, multi-session settings.

title: Understanding Multimodal Failure in Action-Chunking Behavioral Cloning
summary: Behavioral cloning becomes difficult when the same observation admits several valid actions. We study this problem for action-chunking policies and show that different multimodal parameterizations fail in different ways. For latent-variable policies, posterior-prior regularization makes deployment-time sampling more reliable, but excessive regularization removes the action-conditioned information needed to distinguish demonstrated modes. Reducing this regularization can preserve mode information, but then success depends on whether the prior covers the relevant latent regions. For action-space generative policies, multimodality is constrained by the smoothness of the base-to-action transport: a map with small Lipschitz constant cannot assign substantial probability to many well-separated modes. Covering many modes therefore requires either sharp transitions in base space or off-support bridge regions in action space. Experiments on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulation benchmarks support these mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes multimodal behavior cloning across latent-variable and action-space generative policies, showing distinct failure modes and trade-offs between regularization, prior coverage, and Lipschitz-based transport, with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: When identical observations admit multiple valid actions, standard behavioral cloning struggles to represent multiple modes; understanding how different multimodal parameterizations handle this is essential for robust imitation.
AI.method: The authors analyze two policy families: latent-variable policies with posterior-prior regularization and action-space generative policies constrained by base-to-action transport smoothness. They derive how regularization and prior coverage affect mode preservation, and how Lipschitz constraints limit mode coverage; experiments on synthetic tasks and robotics benchmarks validate the theory.
AI.result: Posterior-prior regularization improves sampling at deployment but excessive regularization removes mode information; reducing regularization preserves modes but relies on priors covering relevant latent regions; in action-space policies, higher multimodality requires sharp base-space transitions or action-space bridges; empirical results support these claims.
AI.conclusion: Different multimodal policy designs exhibit distinct limitations; effective multimodal cloning requires balancing regularization and prior coverage, or leveraging transport properties to enable diverse action modes; these insights guide future design and evaluation of multimodal imitation methods.

title: Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs
summary: Integrating new data into knowledge graphs (KG) typically involves different tasks that are executed within workflows or pipelines There are many possible pipelines for a specific integration problem but there is not yet a general approach to evaluate the overall quality and performance of such pipelines to be able to determine the best choices. We therefore propose a new benchmark KGI-Bench to evaluate integration pipelines that ingest different kinds of input data into an existing KG. We evaluate pipelines by analyzing their output, i.e., the updated KG, with the three complementary quality metrics coverage, correctness and consistency. We also provide benchmark datasets (seed KG, overlapping input data of three formats, reference KG as a ground truth) for the movie domain. To demonstrate the applicability and usefulness of the proposed benchmark, we comparatively evaluate 12 pipelines and analyze their behavior across different input data formats and design choices.

AI.tldr: Introduces KGI-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate knowledge-graph integration pipelines by analyzing the updated KG using coverage, correctness, and consistency; provides seed KG, datasets, and a ground-truth reference in movie domain; compares 12 pipelines across formats.
AI.motivation: There is no general method to evaluate holistic quality of knowledge-graph integration pipelines. A unified benchmark is needed to compare pipelines, understand how design choices and input data formats affect outcomes, and guide selections.
AI.method: Propose KGI-Bench benchmark. Provide benchmark datasets: seed KG, overlapping input data in three formats, and reference KG as ground truth. Evaluation is performed on the updated KG using three complementary quality metrics: coverage, correctness, and consistency. Demonstrate applicability by comparing 12 integration pipelines across input formats and design choices in the movie domain.
AI.result: Shows that the benchmark can differentiate pipeline behavior and reveal how different input data formats and design choices influence outcomes. Provides a practical framework to assess and compare integration pipelines in a reproducible manner.
AI.conclusion: KGI-Bench enables systematic, reproducible evaluation of knowledge-graph integration pipelines, helping to identify strong pipelines and understand the impact of input data formats and design choices.

title: Physiology and Anatomy Aware Inverse Inference of Myocardial Infarction for Cardiac Digital Twin
comment: Early-accepted by MICCAI 2026. This version corresponds to the submitted version. The final version will be available on Springer Link
summary: Accurate localization of myocardial infarction is essential for risk stratification. While LGE-MRI remains the gold standard, it is resource-intensive. Integrating cine MRI with ECG enables a more detailed representation of infarct properties. Existing inverse MI inference methods overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for noninvasive MI localization using cardiac digital twins. To bridge the domain gap between simulation and reality, we introduce an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to synthesize realistic, irregular scars with border zones, mimicking ischemic transmural progression. We then construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, capturing both depolarization and repolarization dynamics. Furthermore, we design a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, spatial extents, and transmuralities. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation, respectively, while further enhancing the interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A noninvasive myocardial infarction localization framework using cardiac digital twins with anatomy-aware infarct synthesis, virtual QRS-T waveform simulation, and a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net); it achieves Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar and 0.5503 for border zone, outperforming prior methods and improving interpretability; code will be released.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of LGE-MRI as a resource-intensive standard and the gaps in existing inverse inference methods that ignore realistic scar morphology and repolarization dynamics, hindering sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability.
AI.method: 1) Anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis to generate realistic, irregular scars with border zones; 2) a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms capturing depolarization and repolarization; 3) Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, extents, and transmuralities.
AI.result: The framework significantly outperforms existing inverse inference methods on infarct localization, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar and 0.5503 for border zone segmentation, while enhancing interpretability of ECG–infarct relationships.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and advantage of a digital-twin, anatomy-aware approach for noninvasive MI localization from cine MRI and ECG, with strong performance and interpretability, and awaits open-source release.

title: MoSA: Motion-constrained Stress Adaptation for Mitigating Real-to-Sim Gap in Continuum Dynamics via Learning Residual Anisotropy
summary: Learning real-world dynamics from visual observations is crucial for various domains. A common strategy is to calibrate simulators by estimating physical parameters, yet accuracy is ultimately bounded by the underlying physical models, which often assume materials are homogeneous and isotropic. Even if reasonable, real-world objects typically exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. After the near-isotropic backbone is well calibrated, these residual effects become the key bottleneck for further closing the real-to-sim gap. Although neural networks can fit dynamics end-to-end, such black-box modeling discards strong physical priors, leading to poor data efficiency and overfitting. Therefore, we propose MoSA, a motion-constrained stress adaptation framework that targets these residual effects to further improve real-to-sim dynamics learning. MoSA uses an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to capture mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. It progressively adapts stresses via microplane-constrained redistribution in a physics-informed cascaded network. We further impose motion constraints by supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field. Experimentally, our learned dynamics achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness, while learning physically meaningful residual anisotropy. Finally, we validate MoSA in a robot manipulation setting, showing that better real-to-sim dynamics modeling translates into more reliable sim-to-real transfer. Project Page is available at https://mercerai.github.io/MoSA/.

AI.tldr: MoSA improves real-to-sim dynamics by learning residual anisotropy on top of an isotropic physics prior using motion-constrained stress adaptation.
AI.motivation: Physics-based simulators are accurate under homogeneous, isotropic assumptions; real materials are mildly anisotropic and heterogeneous, so residual effects after near-isotropic calibration remain the bottleneck for closing the real-to-sim gap.
AI.method: MoSA uses an isotropic baseline as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to capture mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. It progressively adapts stresses via microplane-constrained redistribution in a physics-informed cascaded network and enforces motion constraints by supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field.
AI.result: The method yields superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness, with physically meaningful learned residual anisotropy; validated in a robot manipulation setting, showing improved sim-to-real transfer.
AI.conclusion: MoSA narrows the real-to-sim gap by modeling residual stresses on top of an isotropic baseline, enabling more accurate and robust real-world dynamics learning and transfer; project page available.

title: Meta-Soft: Leveraging Composable Meta-Tokens for Context-Preserving KV Cache Compression
summary: The KV cache used in large language models has linearly growing time complexity, so LLMs face memory blow-up and reduced decoding efficiency when they process long contexts.Current KV Cache eviction has become an important research direction; however, existing methods based on fixed Soft Tokens (e.g., Judge Q) rely on a static parameter set as the query to evaluate the importance of KV pairs, so they cannot adapt dynamically to different input prompts, and they cannot precisely capture complex and changing task relevance.Also, evicted KV pairs are discarded permanently, so this causes irreversible information loss and context breaks. To address this problem, we propose Meta-Soft, a dynamic compression framework based on probe-driven context integration. Specifically, we build a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis matrix $\mathcal{L}$, and we use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to produce differentiable sparse combination weights, so we dynamically synthesize the most targeted $k$ Soft Tokens from the input prompt features.We append these Soft Tokens to the end of the input sequence to probe key information. We also introduce an attention-flow based integration mechanism, which redistributes the semantic information of removed tokens into retained tokens, and this keeps the dropped context information effectively.Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art eviction methods and provides a new solution for KV Cache compression.

AI.tldr: Dynamic KV-Cache eviction via Meta-Soft: a probe-driven, differentiable, context-preserving compression using a learnable basis and Gumbel-Softmax to synthesize targeted soft tokens, with attention-flow to reallocate dropped information; outperforms fixed-soft-token methods.
AI.motivation: KV caches in LLMs grow with context length, causing memory and decoding inefficiency. Fixed token-based eviction (e.g., Judge Q) is inflexible and incurs irreversible information loss; a dynamic, differentiable approach is needed to adapt to prompts and preserve context.
AI.method: Construct a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis L. Use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to generate differentiable sparse weights, synthesizing the best k soft tokens from input features. Append these soft tokens to the input sequence. Introduce an attention-flow mechanism to redistribute information from evicted tokens into retained tokens, preserving dropped semantics.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple datasets show Meta-Soft outperforms existing KV-cache eviction methods, offering a new solution for KV-Cache compression.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, probe-driven context integration enables targeted, differentiable KV-Cache compression that preserves context information and improves decoding efficiency over fixed eviction schemes.

title: Broken Memories: Detecting and Mitigating Memorization in Diffusion Models with Degraded Generations
summary: While diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, their tendency to memorize training data poses significant privacy and copyright risks. In this work, we for the first time identify that memorization induces internal numerical instability, often manifesting as visually ``broken'' artifacts. Inspired by stability analysis in numerical methods, we introduce empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantitatively characterize stable behavior during generation. Leveraging this, we propose a principled, on-the-fly framework for step-wise detection and adaptive mitigation. Our approach suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance, thereby preserving semantic fidelity and image quality. Extensive experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 demonstrate that our method achieves an AUC $>0.999$ detection performance and a $0.0\%$ memorization rate after mitigation with negligible overhead ($\approx0.01$s per image).

AI.tldr: Identifies memorization-induced numerical instability in diffusion models, introduces empirical stability regions from latent update norms, and enables on-the-fly stepwise detection with adaptive mitigation that preserves prompts, achieving near-perfect detection and zero memorization with minimal overhead on Stable Diffusion 1.4.
AI.motivation: Memorization in diffusion models raises privacy and copyright risks. There is a need to detect and mitigate memorization without compromising semantic fidelity or image quality.
AI.method: Define empirical stability regions using latent update norms during generation; implement an on-the-fly, step-wise detection mechanism; apply adaptive mitigation that suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance; evaluate on Stable Diffusion 1.4.
AI.result: Detection performance with AUC > 0.999; memorization rate reduced to 0.0% after mitigation; computational overhead approx 0.01 seconds per image.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stability-based framework effectively detects and mitigates memorization in diffusion models while preserving semantic fidelity and image quality, with demonstrated success on Stable Diffusion 1.4 and potential applicability to other models.

title: Provable Robustness against Backdoor Attacks via the Primal-Dual Perspective on Differential Privacy
summary: Randomized smoothing is a powerful tool for certifying robustness to adversarial perturbations, including poisoning attacks via randomized training and evasion attacks via randomized inference. Extending these guarantees to backdoor attacks, where training and test data are jointly perturbed, remains challenging because training- and test-time randomized mechanisms must be analyzed within a single robustness certificate. We address this by connecting randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy through privacy profiles, which provide a numerical procedure for composing heterogeneous mechanisms. The resulting framework enables tight, modular, end-to-end certification of complex, composed mechanisms while leveraging existing analyses of differentially private mechanisms. We instantiate the framework for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing, deriving joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework. Overall, we provide a principled and general framework for using composite mechanisms to certify robustness under complex threat models that better capture the capabilities of real-world adversaries.

AI.tldr: A general, modular framework for certifying robustness against backdoor attacks by composing randomized smoothing with DP-based mechanisms, enabling end-to-end guarantees for training- and inference-time perturbations; demonstrated on DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing on MNIST and CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Backdoor attacks involve joint perturbations of training and test data, challenging existing robustness certs that treat training and inference separately. A unified, composable framework is needed to provide end-to-end guarantees that reflect real-world adversaries.
AI.method: Develop a framework that connects randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy via privacy profiles, enabling numerical composition of heterogeneous mechanisms. Instantiate for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing to derive joint robustness guarantees.
AI.result: The framework yields joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks and shows effectiveness in experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10.
AI.conclusion: A principled, general framework for certifying robustness under complex threat models using composite mechanisms, enabling tighter, modular end-to-end guarantees for backdoor attacks.

title: Scaling Observation-aware Planning in Uncertain Domains
summary: Deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy on an agent in uncertain domains is a fundamental engineering challenge, in which one balances task achievability against the high costs of hardware and processing. This problem has previously been formalized as the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP), based on the well-known Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) model for decision-making. This work studies (sub-)symbolic techniques to scale solving of decidable fragments of the OOP, namely the Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and the Positional Observability Problem (POP). Besides improving the original approach based on parameter synthesis, we develop a new solving method that identifies sensible observation functions via decomposition of POMDPs, improving performance by 3 and 5 orders of magnitude for instance size and runtime, respectively.

AI.tldr: Scalable solving methods for Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and Positional Observability Problem (POP) within POMDPs, using decomposition-based techniques to identify observation functions; yields large performance gains.
AI.motivation: Enable cost-effective sensing in uncertain domains by solving optimal observability efficiently, enabling trade-offs between task achievability and hardware/processing costs.
AI.method: Extend beyond parameter-synthesis by introducing sub-symbolic techniques and a decomposition-based solving approach that identifies sensible observation functions from POMDP structure, improving scalability for decidable OOP fragments (SSP/POP).
AI.result: Achieves substantial scalability improvements: about 3 orders of magnitude in solvable instance size and about 5 orders of magnitude in runtime.
AI.conclusion: Decomposition-based POMDP solving substantially enhances practical solvability of SSP/POP instances, advancing the feasibility of optimizing sensing hardware deployment under uncertainty; invites further refinement and broader applicability.

title: EasyVFX: Frequency-Driven Decoupling for Resource-Efficient VFX Generation
comment: Accepted by SIGGRAPH 2026. Project page: https://easy-vfx.github.io/
summary: Generating high-fidelity visual effects (VFX) typically demands massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. In this paper, we introduce EasyVFX, a resource-efficient framework that achieves realistic VFX synthesis under stringent constraints. Our core philosophy lies in frequency-domain decomposition: we observe that the complexity of VFX can be significantly mitigated by decoupling high-frequency components, which represent intricate spatial appearances, from low-frequency components that encapsulate global motion dynamics. This spectral disentanglement transforms a high-dimensional learning problem into manageable sub-tasks, thereby lowering the optimization barrier and reducing data dependency. Building upon this insight, we propose a two-stage training paradigm. First, we design a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture. By utilizing a soft routing mechanism, our model assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands, enabling them to cultivate robust priors for appearance and motion dynamics. This specialization allows the model to acquire foundational VFX knowledge with fewer GPU resources. Second, we introduce a Test-Time Training strategy powered by a novel Frequency-constraint Loss. This allows the pre-trained model to swiftly adapt to specific, unseen effects through localized optimizations, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU. Experimental results demonstrate that EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects, proving that frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX.

AI.tldr: A resource-efficient VFX synthesis method EasyVFX using frequency-domain disentanglement and a two-stage training (Freq-MoE and test-time Frequency-constraint Loss) enabling rapid adaptation on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Mitigate heavy data and compute demands in VFX due to coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics; aim for democratization of high-quality effects.
AI.method: Two-stage: (1) Frequency-aware MoE with soft routing to spectral bands, enabling expert specialization per frequency; (2) Test-Time Training with Frequency-constraint Loss to adapt to unseen effects within ~100 steps on one GPU.
AI.result: Produces structurally consistent, visually appealing VFX; demonstrates data- and compute-efficiency via spectral decomposition and specialized priors; shows rapid adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-domain disentanglement is an effective paradigm for resource-efficient VFX synthesis and on-the-fly adaptation, broadening accessibility to professional-grade effects.

title: MMD-Balls as Credal Sets: A PAC-Bayesian Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Test-Time Adaptation
comment: 15 pages, 0 figures. Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Epistemic Intelligence in Machine Learning (EIML@ICML 2026)
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) methods improve model performance under distribution shift but lack formal guarantees connecting shift magnitude to prediction reliability. We develop a PAC-Bayesian framework yielding generalization bounds explicitly parameterized by the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between source and target distributions. Our principal contribution is interpreting MMD-balls around the source distribution as credal sets in Walley's imprecise probability theory, yielding natural epistemic uncertainty quantification. We establish: (i) a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss assumption; (ii) a finite-sample version via MMD concentration; (iii) a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set, with a lower-upper risk decomposition; and (iv) geodesic preservation bounds explaining why kernel-guided adaptation protects local feature geometry. The credal set interpretation separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty and provides a principled decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted.

AI.tldr: PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds for test-time adaptation parameterized by MMD, interpreting MMD-balls around the source as Walley’s credal sets to quantify epistemic uncertainty; provides bounds, concentration, uniform worst-case risk over the credal set, and geodesic preservation insights; offers a principled criterion for when adaptation is warranted.
AI.motivation: TTA methods improve performance under distribution shift but lack formal guarantees linking the shift magnitude to prediction reliability; need formal, data-dependent uncertainty quantification under shift.
AI.method: Derive a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss; establish finite-sample concentration via MMD; obtain a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set with lower-upper risk decomposition; and derive geodesic preservation bounds to explain kernel-guided adaptation effects.
AI.result: Provides explicit MMD-dependent generalization bounds for TTA, a credal-set interpretation separating epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty, finite-sample guarantees, and a decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted by the size of the MMD shift.
AI.conclusion: The credal-set perspective yields principled uncertainty quantification and a decision rule for TTA under distribution shift, linking shift magnitude to reliability and preserving local geometry via kernel guidance.

title: Epicure: Navigating the Emergent Geometry of Food Ingredient Embeddings
summary: We present Epicure, a family of three sibling skip-gram ingredient embeddings retrained from scratch on a multilingual recipe corpus. We aggregate 4.14M recipes from 11 sources spanning seven languages, English, Chinese, Russian, Vietnamese, Spanish, Turkish, Indonesian, German, and Indian-English, and normalise the raw ingredient strings to 1,790 canonical entries via an LLM-augmented pipeline. A 203,508-edge ingredient-ingredient NPMI graph and an 80,019-edge typed FlavorDB ingredient-compound graph, 2,247 typed compound nodes across 15 categories, seed three Metapath2Vec variants that share architecture and hyperparameters and differ only in the random-walk schema: Cooc walks the co-occurrence graph only, Chem walks the typed compound metapaths only, and Core blends both via injected ingredient-ingredient walks at controlled mixing, placing each model at a distinct point on the chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum.

AI.tldr: Epicure trains three sibling skip-gram embeddings for multilingual recipe ingredients, using a large multilingual corpus, canonicalized ingredients, and three Metapath2Vec variants to explore chemistry vs recipe-context representations.
AI.motivation: To capture cross-lingual ingredient semantics and integrate chemical context into embeddings for culinary knowledge extraction, cross-language retrieval, and recipe understanding.
AI.method: Aggregate 4.14M recipes from 11 sources across seven languages; normalize ingredients to 1,790 canonical entries via an LLM-augmented pipeline; build a 203,508-edge ingredient–ingredient NPMI graph and an 80,019-edge typed FlavorDB ingredient–compound graph with 2,247 typed compound nodes across 15 categories; seed three Metapath2Vec variants (Cooc: co-occurrence only; Chem: typed compound metapaths only; Core: mix of both with injected ingredient-ingredient walks) to span chemistry–recipe context spectrum.
AI.result: Three models yield distinct positions along a chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum, providing embeddings that balance culinary context and chemical relationships; no explicit performance metrics are reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The Epicure family enables principled exploration of chemistry-informed and recipe-context-aware multilingual ingredient embeddings, with potential downstream applications in recipe search, ingredient substitution, cross-lingual transfer, and knowledge graph grounding; further work could include evaluation on downstream tasks and ablation on mixing ratios and multilingual coverage.

title: Distributed Image Compression with Multimodal Side Information at Extremely Low Bitrates
summary: Distributed Image Compression (DIC) is crucial for multi-view transmission, especially when operating at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp). Its core challenge is effectively utilizing side information to achieve high-quality reconstruction under strict bitrate budgets. However, existing DIC approaches struggle to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, leading to local blurring and the loss of fine details in the reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose a Multimodal DIC framework (MDIC), which, for the first time, leverages side information in a multimodal manner into the DIC paradigm, effectively preserving fine-grained local details and enhancing global perceptual quality in reconstructed images. Specifically, we introduce a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images to capture shared global semantics. Moreover, we design a feature-mask generator, supervised by a multimodal fine-grained alignment task, to strengthen the exploitation of visual side information. The generated mask serves two purposes: first, it guides the extraction of fine-grained details from losslessly transmitted side information to preserve the semantic consistency of reconstructed details; second, it regulates the extraction of clustered feature representations from the quantized VQ-VAE embeddings, compensating for category information lost under the extreme compression of the primary image. Extensive experiments on the widely used KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates.

AI.tldr: MDIC introduces a multimodal DIC framework using a text-to-image diffusion decoder conditioned on textual side information and a multimodal-aware feature-mask generator to exploit side information, achieving state-of-the-art perceptual quality at ultra-low bitrates on KITTI and Cityscapes.
AI.motivation: DIC at extremely low bitrates (<0.1 bpp) struggles to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, leading to blur and loss of fine details; there is a need to better utilize multimodal side information to preserve semantic consistency and high perceptual quality.
AI.method: Proposes MDIC: a diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images; a supervised feature-mask generator trained via a multimodal fine-grained alignment task to guide extraction of fine-grained details from lossless side information and regulate clustering of VQ-VAE embeddings, compensating for information loss under heavy compression.
AI.result: Experiments on KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes show state-of-the-art perceptual quality at ultra-low bitrates.
AI.conclusion: MDIC demonstrates that integrating multimodal side information with a diffusion-based decoder and feature masking preserves local details and improves global perceptual quality in DIC at extremely low bitrates; validated on standard multi-view datasets.

title: Three Costs of Amortizing Gaussian Process Inference with Neural Processes
summary: Neural processes amortize Gaussian process inference, replacing the exact $O(n^3)$ posterior with a learned $O(n)$ map from context sets to predictive distributions.
