For a class of latent neural processes, we bound the Kullback--Leibler (KL) divergence between the GP and LNP predictives, decomposing it into three interpretable sources, namely label contamination as the neural process uses label values to estimate a quantity that is label-independent in the exact GP, an information bottleneck because the finite-dimensional representation cannot resolve the full context geometry, and amortization error from a single encoder network shared across all contexts. The bottleneck truncation term decays in the representation dimension $d$ as $O(e^{-cd^{2/d_x}})$ for squared-exponential kernels on $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ where $c > 0$ is a kernel-dependent constant and as $O(d^{-2ν/d_x})$ for Matérn-$ν$ kernels, directly linking architecture sizing to kernel smoothness and input dimension. The label contamination term is $O(1)$ in general, with only the observation-noise component decaying as $O(1/n)$, identifying a persistent cost of routing uncertainty estimation through a label-dependent representation. These results characterize the costs of amortization within the analyzed class and yield architectural recommendations to predict variance from context locations alone in the GP-amortization regime, and replace mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.

AI.tldr: Amortized neural-process GP inference can approximate GP posteriors with an O(n) map, but incurs a three-term KL error: label contamination, information bottleneck, and amortization error. Bottleneck error decays exponentially with representation dimension for squared-exponential kernels and polynomially for Matérn kernels; label contamination remains O(1) (noise decays as O(1/n)). Architectural guidance suggests predicting variance from context locations and using second-order pooling to reduce amortization gap.
AI.motivation: To quantify and understand the costs of amortized GP inference via neural processes, and to relate architectural choices (representation size, kernel smoothness, input dimension) to prediction quality, with an eye toward practical design of variance prediction from context.
AI.method: Decompose the KL divergence between GP and LNP predictives into three terms: label contamination, information bottleneck, and amortization error. Derive decay rates for the bottleneck term under squared-exponential and Matérn kernels. Analyze scaling of the label-contamination term and the observation-noise term with sample size n. Propose architectural adjustments (variance-from-context-location prediction, second-order pooling) to mitigate amortization costs.
AI.result: Three-term KL decomposition established. Bottleneck truncation decays as O(e^{-c d^{2/d_x}}) for SE on R^{d_x} and as O(d^{-2ν/d_x}) for Matérn-ν. Label contamination is O(1) with only the observation-noise term decaying as O(1/n). Recommendations include architecture sizing tied to kernel smoothness and input dimension, predicting variance from contexts, and replacing mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.
AI.conclusion: Characterizes the costs of amortization within the studied class and providesArchitectural guidance for GP-amortization regimes, linking representation capacity and kernel properties to predictive performance; suggests second-order pooling to mitigate the main amortization gap.

title: S2ED: From Story to Executable Descriptions for Consistency-Aware Story Illustration
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026
summary: Multi-frame story illustration requires long-horizon coherence beyond single-image text-to-image generation, including narrative decomposition and persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames. We propose Story-to-Executable Descriptions (S2ED), a training-free, model-agnostic, prompt-layer framework that converts a full story into a sequence of explicit, editable executable descriptions for more consistent rendering. S2ED coordinates three agents to segment the narrative, ground canonical character attributes, and enrich spatial and affective cues, enabling interpretable prompt-carried state propagation and local edits to repair drift without retraining the generator. Experiments on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku show that S2ED improves sequence-level consistency and character fidelity over strong prompting, large-model planning, and a reference training-based method, under both automatic metrics and human judgments. We also deploy S2ED in an end-to-end story-to-storybook system for children's illustrated stories, with a supplementary video.

AI.tldr: S2ED is a training-free, model-agnostic prompt-layer framework that converts a full story into executable descriptions to enforce long-horizon coherence in multi-frame illustration, improving narrative consistency and character fidelity without retraining.
AI.motivation: To address long-range coherence, persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames in story illustrations, overcoming limitations of single-image prompts and the need for retraining high-capacity models.
AI.method: S2ED uses three agents to (1) segment the narrative, (2) ground canonical character attributes, and (3) enrich spatial and affective cues, producing explicit, editable executable descriptions that propagate state across frames. It is training-free and model-agnostic, enabling local edits to repair drift without retraining the generator.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku demonstrates improved sequence-level coherence and character fidelity over strong prompting, large-model planning, and a reference training-based method, according to automatic metrics and human judgments. S2ED is also deployed in an end-to-end story-to-storybook system for children’s illustrated stories, with a supplementary video.
AI.conclusion: S2ED provides a practical, interpretable, and trainer-free path to long-horizon coherence in multi-frame generation and story illustration, enabling editable state propagation and potential integration into storybook pipelines; its effectiveness is demonstrated on multiple narratives and via human evaluation.

title: KAPPS: A knowledge-based CPPS Architecture for the Circular Factory
comment: Submitted to Journal of Manufacturing Systems (JMS)
summary: While linear manufacturing relies on homogeneous materials and predefined process sequences, circular manufacturing reintroduces used products with heterogeneous and uncertain conditions. This shift demands manufacturing systems capable of handling variable product states, dynamically reconfigurable processes, and the integration of human and machine knowledge. Conventional manufacturing IT architectures, designed for stable structures and deterministic execution, are unable to meet these requirements, as they cannot adequately represent and manage the uniqueness of individual components at runtime. Following a design science methodology for developing a Cyber Physical Production System for circular manufacturing, we derive 14 requirements from five complementary perspectives. Based on these requirements, we design KAPPS, a knowledge-based architecture that uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as a unifying data backbone, combined with a semantic interface layer to enable consistent data and information integration, reasoning, and communication across heterogeneous systems and services, turning the knowledge graph from an integration layer into the factories authoritative write-time state. KAPPS incorporates modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning, enabling incremental adaptation of execution plans under uncertainty and human-machine knowledge exchange. The applicability of KAPPS is demonstrated through two implemented use cases: (i) Anomaly detection and learning through knowledge graph mediated services and (ii) runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system. Subsequently, the architecture is evaluated against the 14 requirements (ed. abstract shortened)

AI.tldr: A design-science-derived CPS architecture (KAPPS) for circular manufacturing that uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as the data backbone, with a semantic interface and modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning; demonstrated on anomaly detection and runtime constraint enforcement; evaluated against 14 requirements.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of linear manufacturing and traditional IT architectures which assume homogeneous materials, predefined processes, and deterministic execution; circular manufacturing requires handling heterogeneous, uncertain product states, dynamic reconfiguration, and integration of human and machine knowledge with runtime representation of unique components.
AI.method: Employ a design science approach to derive 14 requirements from five perspectives; design KAPPS with an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as the unified data backbone, a semantic interface layer for data integration and reasoning, and modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning; enable incremental adaptation of execution plans under uncertainty and human–machine knowledge exchange; validate via two use cases and evaluate against the 14 requirements.
AI.result: Two implemented use cases: (i) anomaly detection and learning through knowledge-graph mediated services, and (ii) runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system; architecture evaluated against the 14 requirements.
AI.conclusion: KAPPS provides a knowledge-centric, flexible architecture that unifies data and enables reasoning and communication across heterogeneous systems to support circular manufacturing under uncertainty. The results demonstrate the approach on concrete use cases, with evaluation against predefined requirements; broader empirical validation and scalability assessment are suggested for future work.

title: COCOTree: A Dataset and Benchmark for Open Tree-Structured Visual Decomposition
summary: We formalize and enable the task of open tree decomposition, which segments an image into hierarchical trees of visual components with unconstrained granularity and flexibility. Specifically, we provide the foundation benchmark for this new paradigm with the following three key contributions. First, we overcome the prohibitively high cognitive and physical bottlenecks of manual annotation by developing a fully automated generation pipeline that synergizes the semantic reasoning of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with the precise geometric grounding of SAM 3. Second, leveraging this pipeline, we construct COCOTree, a massive-scale benchmark featuring over 21K images and 1.8M structural nodes. By embracing an open-vocabulary space of over 3.5K unique labels, it successfully captures the long-tail distribution of complex physical assemblies. Notably, rigorous human evaluation confirms our generated annotations demonstrate strong alignment with human structural judgment. Third, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol by proposing the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric, which jointly assesses mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency. We release our dataset and benchmark code at https://github.com/melonkick3090/COCOTree.

AI.tldr: Introduces open tree decomposition for images and provides a scalable automated annotation pipeline using LVLMs and SAM. Builds COCOTree, a large 21k-image benchmark with 1.8M structural nodes and 3.5k labels; proposes the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric for joint evaluation of masks, labels, and structure; releases data and code.
AI.motivation: Address the need for hierarchical, unconstrained-granularity image decomposition and reduce annotation bottlenecks by automating structure generation while remaining aligned with human judgment.
AI.method: A fully automated pipeline that combines semantic reasoning from Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with the precise geometric grounding of SAM, enabling open-vocabulary structural annotation across a large dataset.
AI.result: Construction of COCOTree with over 21,000 images and 1.8 million structural nodes, using an open vocabulary of over 3.5k labels; strong alignment with human structural judgments as per human evaluation; standardized OTQ evaluation metric proposed.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a new paradigm and benchmark for open tree decomposition in vision, providing dataset, evaluation protocol, and code to spur future research in scalable, human-aligned hierarchical image understanding.

title: Why Semantic Entropy Fails: Geometry-Aware and Calibrated Uncertainty for Policy Optimization
summary: Post-training has become central to improving reasoning and alignment in large language models, where critic-free models enable scalable learning from model-generated outputs but lack principled mechanisms to distinguish informative from noisy signals. Recent approaches leverage response-level measures as uncertainty signals to regulate group-based optimization methods such as GRPO. Yet their empirical success remains unstable and unclear in how they influence optimization dynamics. In this paper, we provide, to our knowledge, the first principled formulation that interprets uncertainty signals as mechanisms for characterizing and regulating gradient variance and learning signal quality. Based on both empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify two critical gaps of current entropy-based estimators: The anisotropic gap and The calibration gap. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel framework integrating geometry-aware measures to capture semantic disagreement with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with learning signal strength. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our approach more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance. Our results highlight the importance of designing uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics, offering a principled perspective for robust post-training.

AI.tldr: GCPO introduces a geometry-aware calibrated policy optimization framework to align uncertainty signals with learning signals, addressing anisotropic and calibration gaps in entropy-based estimators; it improves gradient tracking and post-training performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty signals from response-level measures regulate gradient-based optimization in post-training, but entropy-based uncertainty estimators suffer from anisotropy and miscalibration, potentially degrading optimization dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO) that combines geometry-aware disagreement measures with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with gradient variance and learning-signal quality; supports this with empirical and theoretical analyses.
AI.result: GCPO more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Design uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics; GCPO provides a principled framework for robust post-training.

title: Compiling Agentic Workflows into LLM Weights: Near-Frontier Quality at Two Orders of Magnitude Less Cost
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks have proliferated, collectively exceeding 290,000 GitHub stars across LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Semantic Kernel, Strands, and LlamaIndex. All follow the same pattern: an external orchestrator above the LLM, injecting instructions and routing decisions every turn. Recent work has shown this architecture is dominated for procedural tasks by simply providing the procedure in a frontier model's system prompt [Dennis et al., 2026a], at the cost of consuming the context window, requiring a frontier model for every conversation, and exposing proprietary procedures to third-party providers. Compiling the procedure into the weights of a small fine-tuned model -- creating a subterranean agent -- should resolve all of these concerns, and prior work (SimpleTOD, FireAct, SynTOD, WorkflowLLM, Agent Lumos) has shown the technique works. Yet developer adoption has overwhelmingly favored orchestration. We identify three perceived barriers and address each empirically across travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes, product-specific knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs).

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning procedural knowledge into small models (subterranean agents) can overcome three adoption barriers of external orchestrators, as shown across travel booking, Zoom support, and insurance claims tasks.
AI.motivation: External agent orchestration frameworks are popular but incur three drawbacks: exposure of proprietary procedures to third-parties, dependence on frontier models per conversation to carry procedures, and inefficiencies or maintenance overhead in streaming instructions each turn. Compiling the procedure into the weights of a small model promises privacy, efficiency, and portability.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across three domains with distinct scales: travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes with product knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs). Compare a subterranean agent (weight-compiled procedure) against a standard orchestration baseline, analyzing whether the three perceived barriers are alleviated.
AI.result: The subterranean agent approach mitigates the three barriers: (1) preserves proprietary procedures by embedding them in model weights, removing exposure to external orchestrators; (2) eliminates per-conversation frontier-model dependence and reduces context-window pressure; (3) simplifies adoption by reducing orchestration overhead while maintaining or improving task performance across diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: Procedural compilation into small model weights is a viable alternative to external orchestration for procedural tasks, addressing key adoption barriers and enabling privacy-preserving, efficient, and scalable agent systems. Further work should explore scalability to larger pipelines and long-horizon tasks, as well as safety and maintenance implications.

title: TWINGS: Thin Plate Splines Warp-aligned Initialization for Sparse-View Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2025, Project page: https://sandokim.github.io/twings/
summary: Novel view synthesis from sparse-view inputs poses a significant challenge in 3D computer vision, particularly for achieving high-quality scene reconstructions with limited viewpoints. We introduce TWINGS, a framework that enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) by directly addressing point sparsity. We employ Thin Plate Splines (TPS), a smooth non-rigid deformation model that minimizes bending energy to estimate a globally coherent warp from control-point correspondences, to align backprojected points from estimated depth with triangulated 3D control points, yielding calibrated backprojected points. By sampling these calibrated points near the control points, TWINGS provides a fast and geometrically accurate initialization for 3DGS, ultimately improving structural detail preservation and color fidelity in reconstructed scenes. Extensive experiments on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 demonstrate that TWINGS consistently outperforms existing methods, delivering detailed and accurate reconstructions under sparse-view scenarios.

AI.tldr: TWINGS uses Thin Plate Splines to warp backprojected depth points to calibrated 3D control points, providing a fast, accurate initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting under sparse views, improving geometry and color fidelity; outperforms existing methods on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360.
AI.motivation: Sparse-view reconstruction with 3D Gaussian Splatting suffers from point sparsity, leading to degraded geometry and color. A fast, globally coherent initialization is needed to preserve structure and appearance under limited viewpoints.
AI.method: Establish correspondences between depth-derived points and triangulated 3D control points. Fit a Thin Plate Spline (TPS) warp to minimize bending energy and align backprojected points with control points. Use the warped, calibrated points—sampled near control points—as a fast, geometrically accurate initialization for 3DGS, improving detail preservation and color fidelity.
AI.result: TWINGS consistently outperforms existing methods on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 sparse-view benchmarks, delivering more detailed geometries and truer colors in reconstructed scenes.
AI.conclusion: A TPS-based calibration approach provides an effective, efficient initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting under sparse-view conditions, enabling higher-quality, structurally faithful reconstructions.

title: Same Architecture, Different Capacity: Optimizer-Induced Spectral Scaling Laws
comment: 31 pages, 10 figures, 30 tables. Project page: https://optimizer-scaling-laws.github.io
summary: Scaling laws have made language-model performance predictable from model size, data, and compute, but they typically treat the optimizer as a fixed training detail. We show that this assumption misses a fundamental axis of representation scaling: how effectively the optimizer converts added FFN width into utilized spectral capacity. Using eigenspectra of feed-forward network representations, measured through soft and hard spectral-ranks, we find that \emph{the same Transformer architecture realizes markedly different spectral scaling laws when trained with different optimizers}. Holding architecture and width schedule fixed, AdamW exhibits weak hard-rank scaling ($β$=0.44) on rare-token (TAIL) representations where learning is known to be hardest, whereas Muon achieves linear scaling ($β$=1.02) in the same regimes, a $2.3\times$ increase in the scaling exponent. This difference is not reducible to validation loss: AdamW configurations can match low-rank Dion variants in perplexity, under extended training, while exhibiting sharply different spectral geometry, demonstrating that matched loss does not imply matched representation structure. Hard--soft rank asymmetry further reveals that optimizers differ not only in how much capacity is realized, but also in how that capacity is structured across eigenmodes. To disentangle optimizer effects from architectural ones, we compare against architectural interventions (e.g., attention rank and positional encoding), and find that optimizer-induced spectral shifts often exceed the architectural effects. These results suggest optimization as a first-class axis of representation scaling, motivating optimizer--architecture co-design.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that optimizer choice fundamentally changes how Transformer width translates into spectral representation capacity; different optimizers (e.g., AdamW vs Muon) yield markedly different spectral scaling laws, even with identical architecture and width schedules.
AI.motivation: Existing scaling laws treat the optimizer as a fixed training detail. The work investigates optimizer-driven variability in representation scaling, particularly in low- or rare-token regimes.
AI.method: Analyze eigen-spectra of FFN representations using soft and hard spectral ranks. Compare scaling exponents (β) for different optimizers on tail-token representations, controlling architecture and width schedules. Contrast with architectural interventions to disentangle effects and assess how optimization shifts spectral geometry beyond loss metrics.
AI.result: AdamW exhibits weak hard-rank scaling (β≈0.44) on tail representations; Muon achieves near-linear scaling (β≈1.02), a 2.3× increase in β. Similar perplexity can hide divergent spectral geometry; hard–soft rank asymmetry shows optimizer-specific capacity structuring. Architectural changes often have smaller effects than optimizer changes, indicating optimization is a major axis of representation scaling.
AI.conclusion: Optimization should be treated as a first-class axis of representation scaling and co-design with architecture is warranted, as optimizer-induced spectral shifts can dominate architectural effects.

title: LACO: Adaptive Latent Communication for Collaborative Driving
summary: Collaborative driving aims to improve safety and efficiency by enabling connected vehicles to coordinate under partial observability. Recent approaches have evolved from sharing visual features for perception to exchanging language-based reasoning through foundation models for behavioral coordination. Though communicating in language provides intuitive information, it introduces two challenges: high latency caused by autoregressive decoding and information loss caused by compressing rich internal representations into discrete tokens. To address these challenges, we analyze latent communication in collaborative driving under inherent limitations of multi-agent settings. Our analysis reveals agent identity confusion, where direct fusion of latent states entangles decision representations across vehicles. Motivated by this, we propose LACO, a training-free \textbf{LA}tent \textbf{CO}mmunication paradigm that seamlessly adapts pretrained driving models to collaborative settings. LACO introduces Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD) for latent reasoning, Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA) for communication-efficient information selection, and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) to stabilize ego-centric decision making. Closed-loop experiments in CARLA show that LACO notably reduces communication and inference latency while maintaining strong collaborative driving performance.

AI.tldr: LACO: a training-free latent communication paradigm for collaborative driving that leverages Iterative Latent Deliberation, Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution, and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation to reduce latency while maintaining performance in CARLA.
AI.motivation: Language-based coordination in multi-agent driving suffers from autoregressive decoding latency and information loss from token compression; latent-state fusion can cause agent identity confusion; a scalable, training-free method is needed to adapt pretrained models to collaborative driving under partial observability.
AI.method: Propose LACO featuring Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD) for latent reasoning, Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA) for efficient information selection across temporal horizons, and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) to stabilize ego-centric decision making. The approach is training-free and leverages pretrained driving models for cooperative tasks.
AI.result: Closed-loop experiments in CARLA show that LACO reduces communication and inference latency while maintaining strong collaborative driving performance.
AI.conclusion: LACO provides an efficient latent communication framework for cooperative driving, addressing latency and information-loss issues without additional training and mitigating agent identity confusion.

title: JMed48k: A Multi-Profession Japanese Medical Licensing Benchmark for Vision-Language Model Evaluation
summary: We introduce JMed48k, a multi-profession Japanese healthcare licensing benchmark for evaluating vision-language models. Built from official PDF materials released by the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare, JMed48k contains 48,862 exam questions and 20,142 images from 11 national licensing examinations between 2005 and 2025, with visual content annotated under an 8-type taxonomy. From this corpus, we derive JMed48k-Eval, a recent five-year evaluation subset with 12,484 scored questions, including 9,905 text-only questions and 2,579 questions with images. We evaluate 21 proprietary, open-source, and medical-specific models, reporting text-only and with-image performance separately. Because these subsets contain different questions, we further introduce a paired image-removal audit that evaluates questions with images before and after removing visual content to explore four answer-transition states. The audit shows that proprietary and open source models gain substantially from images, whereas medical-specific systems show limited observable use of visual evidence, with many correct answers persisting after image removal. Even among proprietary models, the net image-removal effect varies sevenfold across professions, from +5.7 points on Physician questions to +39.8 points on Public Health Nurse questions. We release JMed48k to support reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation of vision-language models in medical licensing settings.

AI.tldr: A Japanese medical licensing benchmark dataset (JMed48k) with 48.9k questions and 20k images across 11 national exams (2005–2025), plus a five-year evaluation subset (JMed48k-Eval) of 12.5k scored questions. Evaluates 21 models (proprietary, open-source, medical-specific) and introduces a paired image-removal audit to quantify the impact of images; results show images boost performance for non-medical-specific models but show limited benefit for medical-specific systems, with large variation by profession. Released for reproducible, profession-stratified V-L evaluation in medical licensing.
AI.motivation: Provide a standardized, profession-stratified benchmark for vision-language models in medical licensing, leveraging official materials to quantify the role of visual content and facilitate reproducible evaluation across models and professions.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale dataset from official Japanese MOHLW PDFs, annotate visual content using an 8-type taxonomy, split a recent five-year subset (JMed48k-Eval) with 12,484 scored questions (9,905 text-only; 2,579 with images). Evaluate 21 models (proprietary/open-source/medical-specific) on text-only and image-inclusive tasks. Introduce a paired image-removal audit comparing performance with images present vs removed across four answer-transition states to analyze how visual content affects answers.
AI.result: Images improve performance for proprietary and open-source models, but medical-specific systems show limited reliance on visual evidence; many correct answers persist after image removal. Image-removal gains vary by profession (from +5.7 points on Physician questions to +39.8 points on Public Health Nurse questions). Overall, a sevenfold variation in image impact across professions.
AI.conclusion: Release of JMed48k enables reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation of vision-language models in medical licensing, encouraging careful analysis of how visual information influences model performance across medical domains.

title: Symbolic Density Estimation for Discrete Distributions
summary: Discrete probability laws underpin statistical modeling, yet the catalog of interpretable distributions has expanded only gradually through centuries of case-by-case mathematical derivations. We introduce symbolic density estimation (SDE), an unsupervised framework that automatically recovers closed-form probability mass functions by composing elementary analytic operations within a structured search space. Our method integrates domain-specific structural priors with evolutionary search and a validity-aware inference stage, and it extends to richer distribution families such as zero inflation and finite mixtures. To support systematic evaluation and future research, we contribute a benchmark dataset spanning a broad collection of commonly used discrete distributions. The proposed algorithm recovers all benchmark families with accurate parameter estimates. A real data application shows that it identifies concise and interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit over standard models.

AI.tldr: Symbolic Density Estimation (SDE) automatically recovers closed-form discrete distributions by composing analytic operations in a structured search space, guided by priors and evolutionary search, with a validity-aware inference stage; extends to zero-inflated and finite mixtures, with benchmarks and improved interpretability on real data.
AI.motivation: Discrete distributions with interpretable closed-form expressions are scarce and historically discovered case-by-case; automating their discovery would aid modeling, interpretation, and reproducibility.
AI.method: An unsupervised framework that searches a structured space of elementary analytic operations to assemble discrete pmfs, guided by domain-specific priors; uses evolutionary search and a validity-aware inference stage; supports zero inflation and finite mixture extensions; introduces a benchmark dataset for evaluation.
AI.result: The approach recovers all benchmark distribution families with accurate parameter estimates; provides a benchmark dataset; real-data application yields concise, interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit over standard models.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic Density Estimation enables automatic discovery of interpretable, closed-form discrete distributions and improves modeling by producing parsimonious mixtures that fit data well; the framework invites broader application and evaluation on diverse discrete families.

title: Towards Direct Evaluation of Harness Optimizers via Priority Ranking
summary: Harness optimization enables automated agent creation by having an optimizer agent iteratively update the harness of target agents. Despite its success, current studies evaluate optimizers solely by observing target agents' performance gains. This indirect end-improvement evaluation neglects optimizers' actions at intermediate steps, which are often erroneous and hinder agent performance. Therefore, it is unclear whether harness optimization is driven by optimizers' informed update actions or simply trial-and-error. This necessitates direct evaluation of harness optimizers. However, evaluating harness optimizers directly is non-trivial and costly due to the lack of oracle harnesses. To address this, we present a simple, low-cost design to directly evaluate them, namely priority ranking. By asking harness optimizers to rank components (e.g., tools) in a given harness by their potential to improve/hinder agent performance when updated, our design quantifies optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts or manual examination. More importantly, optimizers' ranking performance correlates with their ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability. Priority ranking is enabled by Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios spanning across domains, designs, and time stages. Codes and data can be found at https://github.com/k59118/Harness_Optimizer_Evaluation.

AI.tldr: Proposes priority ranking as a low-cost, direct evaluation method for harness optimizers, using ranked assessment of harness components; shows ranking accuracy correlates with real multi-step optimization performance, validated on the Shor dataset of 182 optimization scenarios.
AI.motivation: Indirect evaluation of harness optimizers (via end-task gains) misses intermediate, potentially erroneous updates; a direct, scalable, step-level evaluation is needed to discern informed updates from trial-and-error, despite the absence of oracle harnesses.
AI.method: Introduce priority ranking: have harness optimizers rank harness components (e.g., tools) by their potential to improve/hinder performance when updated; evaluate optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts; validate that ranking performance correlates with actual multi-step harness optimization outcomes; leverage Shor as the evaluation corpus.
AI.result: Demonstrates that ranking performance correlates with the optimizer's ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability.
AI.conclusion: Priority ranking provides a simple, low-cost, scalable method to directly evaluate harness optimizers and benchmark their capability, underpinned by Shor’s diverse scenarios; codes and data are publicly available for replication.

title: GenHAR: Generalizing Cross-domain Human Activity Recognition for Last-mile Delivery
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has shown remarkable effectiveness in various applications, such as smart healthcare and intelligent manufacturing. However, a major challenge faced by HAR is the distribution shift across different sensor data domains, which often leads to decreased performance when deployed for real-world applications. To address this issue, this paper introduces GenHAR, a novel framework designed to mitigate the domain gap by learning domain-invariant sensor representations. GenHAR aims to enhance the generalization capabilities of HAR on target domains purely with data from the source domain. The key novelty of GenHAR lies in two aspects. Firstly, GenHAR tokenizes sensor data and learns correlations among frequency sensor channel dimensions to improve the robustness of HAR models. Secondly, GenHAR improves the efficiency via selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism. We conduct a systematic analysis of GenHAR by comparing it with state-of-the-art HAR methods on real-world human activity datasets. Results show that GenHAR outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 9.97% in accuracy, and reduces Floating Point Operations by 6.4 times. Moreover, we deploy GenHAR at a leading logistics company in 4 cities, and have detected 2.15 billion real-time activities. We release our code at: https://github.com/Sensor-FoundationModel/GenHAR.

AI.tldr: GenHAR introduces a domain-invariant HAR framework using tokenized sensor data with frequency-domain channel correlations, plus selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism, achieving notable accuracy gains and FLOPs reductions without target-domain data.
AI.motivation: HAR performance degrades under distribution shift across sensor domains; need source-domain-only generalization to target domains.
AI.method: Tokenize sensor data, learn correlations among frequency-domain sensor channels to improve robustness; apply selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism to boost efficiency.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art by 9.97% in accuracy; reduces FLOPs by 6.4x; deployed in a logistics company across 4 cities with 2.15 billion real-time activities detected; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: GenHAR effectively enhances cross-domain HAR with domain-invariant representations and efficiency techniques, showing strong potential for real-world deployment.

title: Beyond Scalar Objectives: Expert-Feedback-Driven Autonomous Experimentation for Scientific Discovery at the Nanoscale
summary: Self-driving laboratories or autonomous experimentation are emerging as transformative platforms for accelerating scientific discovery. Bayesian optimization (BO) is among the most widely used machine learning frameworks for these purposes, but these BO-based frameworks rely on predefined scalar descriptors to guide experimentation. In many situations, the determination of an appropriate scalar descriptor can be challenging, and may fail to capture subtle yet scientifically important phenomena apparent to experts with interdisciplinary insight. To overcome this limitation, here we develop deep-kernel pairwise learning (DKPL), an approach for autonomous microscopy experiments which incorporates human expertise and interdisciplinary scientific knowledge into an active learning loop. Instead of relying on explicit scalar objectives, DKPL enables experts to directly evaluate which experimental output is more promising using interdisciplinary knowledge. DKPL then learns a latent utility function from these expert judgements to guide subsequent autonomous microscopy experiments. We demonstrate DKPL's performance in learning physically meaningful nanoscale structures while effectively prioritizing high-information measurement regions using an experimental model dataset with known ground truth. We further apply DKPL to analyze the character of ferroelectric domain walls, where we find DKPL capable of distinguishing between high and low characteristic domain-wall angles in bismuth ferrite, and able to discover both head-to-head and tail-to-tail domain-wall character in erbium manganite. This development establishes an approach to integrate expert knowledge into autonomous microscopy experiments and demonstrates a pathway toward expert-guided self-driving laboratories capable of addressing scientific problems beyond the limits of scalar-metrics-driven learning.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Search-E1: Self-Distillation Drives Self-Evolution in Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Post-training has become the dominant recipe for turning a language model into a competent search-augmented reasoning agent. A line of recent work pushes its performance further by adding elaborate machinery on top of this standard pipeline. These augmentations import external supervision from stronger external systems, attach auxiliary modules such as process reward models or retrospective critics, restructure the rollout itself with tree search or multi-stage curricula, or shape the reward with hand-crafted bonuses and penalties. Each addition delivers a measurable gain, but each also inflates the training pipeline and ties the recipe to resources or designs that may not always be available. We take a step back and ask whether any of this machinery is actually necessary, and propose Search-E1, a self-evolution method that lets a search-augmented agent improve through only vanilla GRPO interleaved with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rolls out on its own training questions. A token-level forward KL objective then aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context that exposes a more efficient sibling trajectory. Despite this simplicity, the procedure naturally provides dense per-step supervision. On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 reaches $0.440$ average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales. Code and complete version will be made public soon.

AI.tldr: A simple self-evolution approach (Search-E1) uses vanilla GRPO and offline self-distillation to improve a search-augmented agent, avoiding external supervision. It achieves strong open-source QA results on seven benchmarks with a 3B-scale model.
AI.motivation: Question whether heavy external supervision and elaborate augmentations are necessary for search-augmented reasoning. Seek to simplify the training pipeline, reduce reliance on external systems, and maintain strong performance.
AI.method: Iterative GRPO rounds interleaved with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy generates its own training questions and is aligned with its own privileged-context distribution via a token-level forward KL objective, exposing a more efficient sibling trajectory.
AI.result: On seven QA benchmarks, achieves an average EM of 0.440 with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales. Dense per-step supervision emerges from the self-distillation process.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that a simple, self-contained training loop can match or exceed more complex, supervision-heavy pipelines in QA tasks. While promising, the generality to other tasks and robustness across data regimes warrants further validation; code will be released.

title: LVDrive: Latent Visual Representation Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Autonomous Driving Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising framework for end-to-end autonomous driving. However, existing VLAs typically rely on sparse action supervision, which underutilizes their powerful scene understanding and reasoning capabilities. Recent attempts to incorporate dense visual supervision via world modeling often overemphasize pixel-level image reconstruction, neglecting semantically meaningful scene representation learning. In this work, we propose LVDrive, a Latent Visual representation enhanced VLA framework for autonomous driving. LVDrive introduces a future scene prediction task into the VLA paradigm, where future representations are learned entirely in a high-level latent space under auxiliary supervision from a pretrained vision backbone. Departing from inefficient autoregressive generation, we jointly model future scene and motion prediction within a unified embedding space, processed in a single forward pass to conduct the future-aware reasoning. We further design a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that explicitly leverages the learned latent future representations to refine trajectory generation. Extensive experiments on the challenging Bench2Drive benchmark demonstrate that LVDrive achieves significant improvements in closed-loop driving performance, outperforming both action supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches.

AI.tldr: LVDrive proposes a latent visual representation enhanced VLA framework for autonomous driving, learning latent future representations from a pretrained vision backbone to jointly predict future scene and motion in a single forward pass, followed by a two-stage trajectory decoding; achieves state-of-the-art closed-loop results on Bench2Drive relative to action-supervised and image-reconstruction-based methods.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models rely on sparse action supervision and dense world models overemphasize pixel-level reconstruction, which underutilizes semantic scene understanding and planning capabilities; there is a need for high-level latent representations that enable future-aware reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a future scene prediction task in a latent space supervised by a pretrained vision backbone; model future scene and motion in a unified embedding with a single forward pass; employ a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that uses latent future representations to refine trajectory generation.
AI.result: On the Bench2Drive benchmark, LVDrive yields significant improvements in closed-loop driving performance, outperforming both action-supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches.
AI.conclusion: Latent future prediction in a high-level latent space, coupled with efficient single-pass inference and two-stage trajectory decoding, enhances VLA for autonomous driving and demonstrates strong performance on Bench2Drive.

title: On-Policy Consistency Training Improves LLM Safety with Minimal Capability Degradation
summary: Aligned models can misbehave in several ways: they are often sycophantic, fall victim to jailbreaks, or fail to include appropriate safety warnings. Consistency training is a promising new alignment paradigm to mitigate such failures by training invariants into the model using contrastive input pairs. Existing consistency training procedures generate the supervision signal once, offline, and use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to update the model. Unfortunately, the resulting models tend to merely memorize the surface forms of the training distribution and thus generalize poorly and regress in their capabilities. We introduce On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT), a new consistency training approach where the objective is computed over the model's own responses to prompts, supervised by itself conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. We evaluate OPCT on three safety axes: sycophancy, jailbreaking, and safety awareness. Across three model families, OPCT outperforms its SFT counterpart on all safety desiderata. It nearly halves the sycophancy rate relative to baseline (8.1% vs. 15.4%, compared to 11.2% for SFT). Under an adaptive per-target attacker, OPCT holds jailbreak defense success near 99% on held-out jailbreak behaviors, whereas SFT achieves 87% on average. On safety awareness, OPCT outperforms SFT in two out of three models, and matches it on the other. OPCT also largely avoids the capability regressions that SFT induces, such as a 28-point drop on MATH-500. Our results suggest that consistency training is best implemented as OPCT rather than as SFT, especially when generalization beyond the training distribution is desired.

AI.tldr: OPCT uses on-policy, self-supervised consistency training to align models, outperforming offline SFT in safety metrics across multiple model families; achieves lower sycophancy and jailbreak success, better safety awareness, and fewer capability regressions.
AI.motivation: Misbehaviors in aligned models (sycophancy, jailbreaking, safety gaps) arise from memorization and poor generalization. Offline consistency training with SFT tends to memorize surface forms; there is a need for a training paradigm that enforces invariants through the model's own outputs on-policy to improve generalization beyond the training distribution.
AI.method: On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT): compute the training objective on the model’s own responses to prompts, supervised by the model’s own outputs conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. Uses contrastive input pairs to induce invariant behavior; evaluated across three safety axes (sycophancy, jailbreaking, safety awareness) and three model families, compared against supervised fine-tuning (SFT).
AI.result: OPCT outperforms SFT on all safety desiderata: sycophancy incidence reduced to 8.1% (baseline 15.4%, SFT 11.2%); jailbreaking Defense success near 99% under adaptive attackers vs. 87% for SFT; safety awareness improved in two of three models and matched in the third; OPCT largely avoids the 28-point math regression (MATH-500) seen with SFT.
AI.conclusion: Consistency training is more effective when implemented as OPCT than as SFT, particularly for achieving generalization beyond the training distribution and reducing safety-related regressions.

title: Meta-Learning for Rapid Adaptation in Reference Tracking of Uncertain Nonlinear Systems
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of reference tracking for uncertain nonlinear systems. Since collecting data from the target system (i.e., the system of interest) is often challenging, our objective is to design optimal controllers using limited target system data. Meta-learning provides a promising paradigm by leveraging offline data from source systems (systems sharing structural similarities with the target system) to accelerate training and enhance control performance. Motivated by this idea, we propose a meta-learning-based control framework that tailors the implicit model-agnostic meta-learning (iMAML) algorithm to the control setting. The framework operates in two phases: an (offline) meta-training phase, where an aggregated representation is learned from source data to capture the shared system dynamics among similar systems, and an (online) meta-adaptation phase, where this representation is fine-tuned on the target system using only a few data samples and limited adaptation steps. We formulate this framework as a bi-level optimization problem and provide an efficient solution with reduced storage complexity and few approximations. The proposed framework is general, allowing various learning algorithms to be integrated. To demonstrate this flexibility, we propose two specific learning algorithms that can be incorporated into our framework based on a neural state-space model and a deep Q-network, respectively. The primary distinction between these approaches is whether explicit system identification is required. Numerical simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods enhance control performance and consistently outperform baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: Meta-learning-based control using iMAML for uncertain nonlinear systems with offline meta-training on source data and online meta-adaptation on the target; two algorithms (neural state-space model and DQN); bi-level optimization; improved data efficiency and performance; validated by simulations and hardware experiments.
AI.motivation: Limited target-system data for reference tracking; leverage offline data from structurally similar source systems to capture shared dynamics and improve training efficiency and control performance.
AI.method: Two-phase framework: offline meta-training to learn an aggregated representation of shared dynamics; online meta-adaptation fine-tunes this representation on the target with few data samples and few adaptation steps. Formulated as a bi-level optimization with an efficient solution and reduced storage. Framework is general and plug-and-play, supporting algorithms based on neural state-space models or deep Q-networks (with or without explicit system identification).
AI.result: Numerical simulations and hardware experiments show enhanced control performance and consistent improvement over baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: A general, data-efficient meta-learning control framework based on iMAML for uncertain nonlinear systems; capable of integrating different learning algorithms and effective with limited target data.

title: VISTA: Validation-Guided Integration of Spatial and Temporal Foundation Models with Anatomical Decoding for Rare-Pathology VCE Event Detection -- after competition results
summary: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging because clinically relevant findings are sparse, visually heterogeneous, and evaluated at the event level rather than by frame accuracy. We propose VISTA, a metric-aligned multi-backbone framework for the RAREVISION task. VISTA combines EndoFM-LV for temporal context and DINOv3 ViTL/16 for frame-level visual semantics, followed by a Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE), Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF), and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED). The original official submission achieved hidden-test temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and mAP@0.95 of 0.3235. After the competition, extending local threshold refinement with a global coarse search improved performance to 0.3726 mAP@0.5 and 0.3431 mAP@0.95, ranking Team ACVLab second in the post-competition evaluation.

AI.tldr: Metric-aligned multi-backbone framework (VISTA) for capsule endoscopy event detection; combines temporal context (EndoFM-LV) and frame-level semantics (DINOv3 ViTL/16) with diverse head ensemble, weighted fusion, and anatomy-aware decoding; threshold refinement via coarse global search boosts mAP and secures second place post-competition.
AI.motivation: Event-level evaluation in capsule endoscopy is challenging due to sparse, heterogeneous findings. A metric-aligned architecture that leverages both temporal context and frame-level semantics, with robust fusion and anatomy-aware decoding, aims to improve detection performance.
AI.method: VISTA uses EndoFM-LV for temporal modeling and DINOv3 ViTL/16 for frame-level features. It employs a Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE) for diverse predictions, Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF) for fusion, and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED) for temporally coherent event decoding. The official submission reported baseline temporal mAP@0.5=0.3530 and mAP@0.95=0.3235; post-competition refinement via a global coarse threshold search raised to 0.3726 and 0.3431, respectively.
AI.result: Baseline: mAP@0.5=0.3530, mAP@0.95=0.3235. Post-competition with global coarse search: mAP@0.5=0.3726, mAP@0.95=0.3431; Team ACVLab ranked second in post-competition evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A metric-aligned, multi-backbone approach with ensemble, fusion, and anatomy-aware decoding yields improved capsule endoscopy event detection, with significant gains from global threshold refinement and coarse search, illustrating the value of temporal-frame fusion for sparse, event-level tasks.

title: Energy-Gated Attention: Spectral Salience as an Inductive Bias for Transformer Attention
summary: Standard transformer attention computes pairwise similarity between queries and keys, treating all tokens as equally salient regardless of their intrinsic informational content. In turbulent fluid dynamics, coherent structures -- the energetically dominant, spatially organized patterns that persist amid background chaos -- carry a disproportionate fraction of total energy and govern all transport. We propose that tokens play an analogous role in transformer attention: informationally dense positions (morphological boundaries, syntactic heads, discourse markers) concentrate spectral energy and should attract proportionally more attention than background tokens (function words, repeated patterns, low-information filler). We propose Energy-Gated Attention (EGA): a simple modification that gates value aggregation by the spectral energy of key token embeddings, computed by a single learned linear projection that discovers the dominant spectral mode of the embedding field. On TinyShakespeare, EGA achieves +0.103 validation loss improvement with only 12,480 additional parameters (<0.26% overhead) and no measurable computational cost. The result is consistent on Penn Treebank (+0.101), demonstrating dataset independence. A systematic ablation across three wavelet families (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, and a parametric Morlet) establishes that fixed structured bases are suboptimal -- the optimal energy direction is data-adaptive and non-sinusoidal -- while identifying learned wavelet packets as a promising open direction. The learned energy threshold converges to tau ~= 0.35 independently of initialization, corresponding to the fraction (~36%) of tokens carrying above-average spectral energy in English text, a stable linguistic property consistent with the fraction of content words in running English text.

AI.tldr: Energy-Gated Attention (EGA) gates value aggregation by a learned spectral-energy projection of key tokens, yielding consistent validation improvements on language modeling tasks with minimal overhead; the optimal energy fraction (~36%) aligns with content-word proportion.
AI.motivation: Standard transformer attention treats tokens as equally salient regardless of information content, contrasting with the dominance of energetic structures in turbulent flows; the hypothesis is that informative positions in text carry higher spectral energy and should attract more attention.
AI.method: Introduce EGA: gate value aggregation by the spectral energy of key embeddings computed via a single learned linear projection to uncover the dominant spectral mode. Conduct systematic ablations across wavelet bases (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, parametric Morlet) and datasets (TinyShakespeare and Penn Treebank) to compare fixed vs. learned energy directions; determine an energy threshold tau.
AI.result: TinyShakespeare: validation loss reduced by ~0.103 with +12,480 additional parameters (<0.26% overhead) and negligible extra cost. Penn Treebank: reduction ~0.101. Fixed bases underperform; data-adaptive energy directions (learned wavelet packets) are superior. The energy threshold converges to ~0.35, corresponding to ~36% of tokens with above-average spectral energy, matching the standard proportion of content words.
AI.conclusion: Energy-based, data-adaptive gating improves transformer attention with minimal overhead; learned spectral directions outperform fixed bases, and a linguistically meaningful energy threshold emerges, guiding future exploration of learned wavelet-like projections.

title: A Subjective Logic-based method for runtime confidence updates in safety arguments
comment: Accepted for publication at the 41st ACM/SIGAPP Symposium on Applied Computing (SAC 2026)
summary: We present a method for dynamic quantitative assurance that enhances static safety cases with continuous, runtime-driven confidence updates. The method quantifies and propagates confidence across the development lifecycle by integrating design-time evidence and windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) within a single Subjective Logic (SL)-based assurance case. At runtime, SPI evidence is continuously evaluated, and targeted claims are updated using a rule that increases confidence in the absence of violations and imposes prompt penalties when violations occur. This design prioritizes safety-relevant responsiveness over exact classical Bayesian posterior updates. We demonstrate the method using a simulation-based construction zone assist function, focusing on an ML-based construction cone detection component, and show how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.

AI.tldr: A runtime-driven, Subjective Logic–based assurance method that updates confidence for safety cases using design-time evidence and windowed runtime SPIs, demonstrated on ML cone-detection in a construction-zone simulation.
AI.motivation: Bridge static safety cases with continuous runtime evidence to maintain trustworthy assurances for safety-critical ML components, prioritizing safety-relevant responsiveness over exact probabilistic posterior updates.
AI.method: Integrate design-time evidence with windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) into a single Subjective Logic-based assurance case. At runtime, SPI evidence is evaluated; claims are updated via a rule that increases confidence when no violations occur and applies prompt penalties when violations occur, emphasizing safety responsiveness over Bayesian precision.
AI.result: Demonstrated in a simulation-based construction zone assist function focusing on an ML cone-detection component; shows how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, runtime-driven confidence updates augment static safety cases, prioritizing safety responsiveness and enabling continuous assurance for ML-enabled safety-critical systems.

title: TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?
summary:
The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models.
  Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher

AI.tldr: TextTeacher adds a text-derived semantic objective to image classification training, using captions and a frozen text encoder to produce anchors that guide visual representations. This yields faster, stronger ImageNet performance with negligible overhead and without altering inference-time models.
AI.motivation: Test the platonic representation hypothesis: large models may converge to a shared representation geometry across modalities. Investigate whether language-model semantic knowledge can efficiently improve vision models.
AI.method: During training, incorporate captions via a frozen text encoder and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors. Use these as an auxiliary objective alongside standard image classification loss, with image captions readily available. Inference-time model remains unchanged and no multimodal training is required.
AI.result: On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher yields up to +2.7 percentage points in accuracy and consistent transfer gains (avg +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation at the same compute budget or with similar accuracy but is ~33% faster. TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, reshaping early-layer representations and improving generalization with complementary semantic cues, with negligible overhead.
AI.conclusion: TextTeacher provides a lightweight, effective way to inject linguistic-semantic priors into vision training, improving accuracy and transferability without changing inference-time models or incurring significant compute.

title: Geometry-Adaptive Explainer for Faithful Dictionary-Based Interpretability under Distribution Shift
summary: Mechanistic interpretability aims to explain a model's behavior by identifying causally responsible internal structures. Dictionary-based explainers such as sparse autoencoders and transcoders are a primary tool, but their faithfulness under out-of-distribution (OOD) shift has received little systematic attention. We show that distribution shift rotates the subspace that the model actively uses, misaligning the explainer's dictionary trained on in-distribution (ID) activations. We formalize this misalignment as the faithfulness gap, a geometric distance between the ID dictionary and the OOD-active subspace, and show that it controls OOD faithfulness degradation. To reduce this gap, we propose the Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE), which realigns the explainer's dictionary with the OOD-active subspace while preserving the original feature structure. This requires only unlabeled OOD activations and no gradient updates. We prove that GAE improves over the unadapted ID explainer, with excess loss bounded quadratically by the second-moment shift. Empirically, GAE even matches or surpasses all training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and OOD settings.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how distribution shift under OOD affects dictionary-based explainers in mechanistic interpretability, formalizes a faithfulness gap between in-distribution dictionaries and OOD-active subspaces, and introduces Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE) that realigns the dictionary using unlabeled OOD activations without gradient updates, yielding improved faithfulness both theoretically (quadratic excess loss bound) and empirically (matching/superior to training-based baselines).
AI.motivation: To ensure the faithfulness of dictionary-based, mechanistic interpretability methods under distribution shift, since OOD activations rotate the usage subspace and misalign ID-trained dictionaries, compromising explanations.
AI.method: Define Faithfulness Gap as a geometric distance between the ID dictionary and the OOD-active subspace. Propose Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE) that realigns the dictionary with the OOD-active subspace using unlabeled OOD activations and no gradient updates. Provide a theoretical bound showing excess loss is quadratically controlled by the second-moment shift.
AI.result: GAE demonstrably improves faithfulness over the unadapted ID explainer. Empirically, it matches or surpasses training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and various OOD settings.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for OOD geometry in explainer design yields more faithful, robust explanations without labeled data or retraining, achieving strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance.

title: TerminalWorld: Benchmarking Agents on Real-World Terminal Tasks
summary: We introduce TerminalWorld, a scalable data engine that automatically reverse-engineers high-fidelity evaluation tasks from "in-the-wild" terminal recordings. Processing 80,870 terminal recordings, the engine yields a full benchmark of 1,530 validated tasks, spanning 18 real-world categories, ranging from short everyday operations to workflows exceeding 50 steps, and covering 1,280 unique commands. From these, we curate a Verified subset of 200 representative, manually reviewed tasks. Comprehensive benchmarking on TerminalWorld-Verified across eight frontier models and six agents reveals that current systems still struggle with authentic terminal workflows, achieving a maximum pass rate of only 62.5%. Moreover, TerminalWorld captures real-world terminal capabilities distinct from existing expert-curated benchmarks (e.g., Terminal-Bench), with only a weak correlation to their scores (Pearson r=0.20). The automated engine makes TerminalWorld authentic and scalable by construction, enabling it to evaluate agents in real-world terminal environments as developer practices evolve. Data and code are available at https://github.com/EuniAI/TerminalWorld.

AI.tldr: TerminalWorld automatically reverse-engineers high-fidelity terminal tasks from real recordings to create a scalable benchmark, revealing that current models struggle with authentic terminal workflows.
AI.motivation: There is a need for authentic, scalable evaluation benchmarks for terminal automation beyond expert-curated benchmarks, capturing real-world workflows and commands.
AI.method: Process 80,870 terminal recordings to extract 1,530 validated tasks across 18 categories and 1,280 unique commands; curate a Verified subset of 200 representative tasks; benchmark eight frontier models and six agents on TerminalWorld-Verified.
AI.result: Max pass rate of 62.5% on authentic terminal workflows; TerminalWorld captures capabilities distinct from existing benchmarks (weak correlation with Terminal-Bench, Pearson r=0.20). Data and code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Automated engine yields authentic, scalable terminal benchmarks aligned with evolving developer practices; enables evaluation in real-world terminal environments.

title: OPERA: An Agent for Image Restoration with End-to-End Joint Planning-Execution Optimization
summary: Real-world image restoration is challenging due to complex and interacting mixed degradations. Recent agent-based approaches address this problem by composing multiple task-specific restoration tools. However, empirical analysis reveals that their performance is fundamentally limited by implicitly constrained planning spaces and the lack of coordination among independently pretrained tools. To address these issues, we propose OPERA (Optimized Planning-Execution Restoration Agent), a framework that jointly optimizes restoration planning and tool execution in an end-to-end manner. On the planning side, OPERA uses reinforcement learning to directly optimize tool composition over a combinatorial plan space, with the final restoration quality as the reward. On the execution side, OPERA introduces agent-guided co-training of restoration tools, enabling them to learn cooperative behaviors under sequential composition. Extensive experiments on multi-degradation benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that OPERA consistently outperforms both all-in-one restoration models and existing agent-based methods across diverse and complex degradation scenarios.

AI.tldr: OPERA is a framework that jointly optimizes planning and execution for real-world image restoration by marrying reinforcement learning for tool composition with cooperative, end-to-end tool co-training, outperforming existing all-in-one and agent-based methods across complex degradations.
AI.motivation: Real-world restoration faces complex, interacting degradations and limited planning/execution coordination in existing agent-based systems.
AI.method: RL-based planning over a combinatorial restoration plan space to optimize final restoration quality, plus agent-guided co-training to foster cooperative tool behavior under sequential composition.
AI.result: Empirical results on multi-degradation benchmarks and real-world data show OPERA outperforms both monolithic restoration models and prior agent-based approaches across diverse degradation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of planning and execution with cooperative tools yields robust restoration under complex degradation and demonstrates coordinated planning-execution benefits.

title: OPPO: Bayesian Value Recursion for Token-Level Credit Assignment in LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become the standard recipe for improving LLM reasoning, but the dominant algorithm GRPO assigns a single trajectory-level advantage to every token, diluting the signal at pivotal reasoning steps and injecting noise at uninformative ones. Critic-free alternatives derived from on-policy distillation supply per-token signals through oracle-conditioned likelihood ratios, yet apply each signal in isolation from the trajectory-level evidence accumulated up to that position. We propose Oracle-Prompted Policy Optimization (OPPO), which rests on a single observation: the oracle signal used by prior distillation-style methods for local discrimination is also the natural Bayesian update of the model's belief about eventual success. Accumulating the signal along a trajectory yields, in closed form and at the cost of one extra forward pass, a running estimate of the success probability at every position, together with a token-level advantage that requires no learned value network and no additional rollouts. A first-order analysis factorizes the advantage into the per-token discrimination signal used by distillation methods modulated by a state weight that concentrates credit on genuinely pivotal tokens, with a directional variance-reduction guarantee. The framework admits two estimators differing only in which model scores the evidence: a \textit{self-oracle} that reuses the student and recovers the on-policy distillation reward as a strict special case, and a \textit{teacher-oracle} that delegates scoring to a stronger frozen model. On two base LLMs across seven mathematics, science, and code reasoning benchmarks, OPPO improves over GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to $+6.0$ points on AMC'23 and $+5.2$ points on AIME'24, with gains that widen monotonically with response length.

AI.tldr: OPPO delivers per-token, trajectory-informed advantages for LLM RL with verifiable rewards by accumulating a Bayesian running update of success probability along the trajectory, avoiding a learned value network. It admits self- and teacher-oracle estimators and outperforms GRPO and distillation baselines on math, science, and code benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GRPO's single-trajectory advantage dilutes signals at pivotal reasoning steps and distillation methods ignore trajectory-level evidence; there is a need to fuse local discrimination signals with whole-trajectory context.
AI.method: OPPO accumulates a Bayesian update of the model's belief about eventual success along a trajectory to produce token-level advantages without a value network or extra rollouts. It factorizes the advantage into a per-token discrimination signal modulated by a state weight. Two estimators are proposed: self-oracle (reuses student, recovers on-policy distillation reward) and teacher-oracle (uses a stronger frozen model). One extra forward pass yields running success probabilities at all positions.
AI.result: On two base LLMs across seven benchmarks in mathematics, science, and code reasoning, OPPO exceeds GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to 6.0 points on AMC'23 and 5.2 points on AIME'24, with gains increasing with response length.
AI.conclusion: OPPO provides an effective, scalable replacement for trajectory-only or isolated token signals, enabling per-token advantages aligned with actual pivotal steps and yielding consistent performance gains across tasks and model sizes.

title: Think Thrice Before You Speak: Dual knowledge-enhanced Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Persuasive Agents
summary: Persuasive dialogue requires reasoning about others' latent mental states, a capability known as Theory of Mind (ToM). However, due to reliance on simple prompting strategies and insufficient ToM knowledge, existing LLMs often fail to capture the intrinsic dependencies among mental states, leading to fragmented representations and unstable reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce the ToM-based Persuasive Dialogue (ToM-PD) task, grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework, which explicitly models the sequential dependencies among mental states in multi-turn dialogues. To facilitate research on this task, we construct a large-scale annotated dataset, ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD), capturing fine-grained mental states and corresponding persuasive strategies. We further propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework that leverages both explicit and implicit prior experiences to improve LLMs' inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that Qwen3-8B equipped with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20%, 22.80%, and 16.97% in predicting desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies, respectively. Case studies further show that our approach enhances interpretability and consistency in reasoning.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Theory-of-Mind (ToM) based persuasive dialogue framework, including a BDI-grounded task (ToM-PD), a large-scale annotated dataset (ToM-BPD), and a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning method (TTBYS) that improves LLM ToM inference and persuasive strategy prediction.
AI.motivation: Current LLM prompting fails to capture intrinsic dependencies among latent mental states in persuasive dialogue, leading to fragmented reasoning and instability; a structured ToM framework is needed to model sequential state dependencies.
AI.method: Define ToM-PD under the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework; construct ToM-BPD with fine-grained annotations of mental states and persuasive strategies; propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced, stepwise reasoning framework that leverages explicit/implicit prior experiences to reason about desires, beliefs, and strategies in multi-turn dialogues.
AI.result: Experiment shows Qwen3-8B with TTBYS surpasses GPT-5 by 1.20%, 22.80%, and 16.97% in predicting desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies respectively; case studies indicate improved interpretability and consistency in reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The ToM-PD framework, along with the ToM-BPD dataset and TTBYS methodology, advances ToM-aware persuasive dialogue research by enabling more coherent and interpretable modeling of latent mental states.

title: MotionDPS: Motion-Compensated 3D Brain MRI Reconstruction
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is highly susceptible to patient motion due to its relatively long acquisition times and the fact that data are acquired sequentially in k-space. Even small patient movements introduce phase inconsistencies across measurements, leading to severe artifacts such as blurring, ghosting, and geometric distortions that can compromise diagnostic quality. Retrospective motion compensation remains challenging, particularly in accelerated acquisitions, due to the ill-posed nature of the joint reconstruction and motion estimation problem.
