In this work, we propose a unified Bayesian framework for motion-compensated 3D MRI that jointly estimates the anatomical image, rigid-body motion parameters, and coil sensitivity maps directly from motion-corrupted k-space data. Our approach integrates pretrained 3D complex-valued score-based diffusion models as expressive anatomical image priors within a physics-based forward model. Inference is performed by alternating diffusion posterior image updates with efficient proximal optimization steps for motion and coil sensitivity estimation, enabling fully unsupervised reconstruction without the need for paired motion-free training data. Experiments on simulated and real-motion brain MRI datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improved image quality and motion robustness compared to state-of-the-art classical and learning-based motion correction techniques, particularly in the presence of severe motion and high acceleration.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian framework for motion-compensated 3D MRI that jointly estimates image, rigid motion, and coil maps using diffusion-model priors in a physics-based forward model; unsupervised reconstruction from motion-corrupted k-space data.
AI.motivation: MRI motion artifacts are severe, especially in accelerated acquisitions; joint ill-posed reconstruction and motion estimation are challenging; need unsupervised methods that do not require paired motion-free data.
AI.method: Unified Bayesian approach that alternates diffusion-posterior image updates with proximal steps to estimate motion parameters and coil sensitivities; integrates pretrained 3D complex-valued score-based diffusion models as anatomical priors within the forward model; operates directly on motion-corrupted k-space data.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and real brain-motion data show improved image quality and robustness to motion and high acceleration compared to classical and learning-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a fully unsupervised, diffusion-prior–driven solution for motion-compensated MRI, leveraging physics-based reconstruction; demonstrates strong performance, with potential limitations in computation and diffusion-model dependency.

title: The Illusion of Reasoning: Exposing Evasive Data Contamination in LLMs via Zero-CoT Truncation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning abilities across a wide range of tasks, but data contamination undermines the objective evaluation of these capabilities. This problem is further exacerbated by malicious model publishers who use evasive, or indirect, contamination strategies, such as paraphrasing benchmark data to evade existing detection methods and artificially boost leaderboard performance. Current approaches struggle to reliably detect such stealthy contamination. In this work, we uncover a critical phenomenon: a model's generated reasoning steps actively mask its underlying memorization. Inspired by this, we propose the Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP), a novel black-box detection method that deliberately truncates the entire Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process to expose latent shortcut mappings. To further isolate memorization from the model's intrinsic problem-solving capabilities, ZCP compares the model's zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark against an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset. Furthermore, we introduce Contamination Confidence, a metric that quantifies both the likelihood and severity of contamination, moving beyond simple binary classifications. Extensive experiments on both previously identified contaminated models and specially fine-tuned contaminated models demonstrate that ZCP robustly detects both direct and evasive data contamination. The code for ZCP is accessible at https://github.com/Yifan-Lan/zero-cot-probe.

AI.tldr: Introduces Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP), a black-box detector for hidden data contamination in LLM benchmarks by truncating chain-of-thought and comparing zero-shot performance on original vs isomorphically perturbed data, plus a Contamination Confidence score to quantify risk.
AI.motivation: Data contamination in LLM benchmarks, including evasive strategies like paraphrasing benchmark data, undermines objective evaluation of reasoning abilities. There is a need for robust, black-box detection methods beyond existing detectors that can reveal memorization hidden behind reasoning steps.
AI.method: ZCP truncates the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process to reveal latent memorization and compares zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark to an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset. The approach introduces Contamination Confidence, a metric that estimates both the likelihood and severity of contamination. Evaluations are conducted on contaminated and specially fine-tuned contaminated models with publicly available code provided.
AI.result: ZCP effectively detects both direct and evasive data contamination, outperforming baseline detectors in identifying memorization masked by reasoning steps; results are demonstrated on multiple contaminated models and fine-tuned variants.
AI.conclusion: ZCP offers a practical, black-box method and a quantitative contamination confidence metric to improve the detection and assessment of data contamination in LLM evaluation, with code available for replication.

title: Spreadsheet-RL: Advancing Large Language Model Agents on Realistic Spreadsheet Tasks via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Mingyuan served as the project lead. Banghao, Yining, and Mingyuan contributed equally to this work, with more junior authors listed before senior authors. All data and code releases are maintained by the corresponding authors at UIUC and are not affiliated with Meta
summary:
Spreadsheet systems (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) play a central role in modern data-centric workflows. As AI agents grow increasingly capable of automating complex tasks, such as controlling computers and generating presentations, building an AI-driven spreadsheet agent has emerged as a promising research direction. Most existing spreadsheet agents rely on specialized prompting over general-purpose LLMs; while this design has potentials on simple spreadsheet operations, it struggles to manage the complex, multi-step workflows typical of real-world applications.
  We introduce Spreadsheet-RL, a reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning framework designed to train specialized spreadsheet agents within a realistic Microsoft Excel environment. Spreadsheet-RL features an automated pipeline for scalable collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums, as well as domain-specific evaluation tasks in areas such as finance and supply chain management, which we compile into the new Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark dataset. It also includes a Spreadsheet Gym environment designed for multi-turn RL: Spreadsheet Gym exposes extensive Excel functionality through a Python sandbox, along with a refined harness that incorporates a comprehensive tool set and carefully designed tool-routing rules for spreadsheet tasks. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that Spreadsheet-RL substantially enhances AI agent's performance on both general and domain-specific spreadsheet tasks: it improves Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's Pass@1 on SpreadsheetBench from 12.0% to 23.4%, and raises Pass@1 from 8.4% to 17.2% on our curated Domain-Spreadsheet dataset. These results highlight Spreadsheet-RL's strong potential for generalization and real-world adoption in spreadsheet automation, and broadly, its promise for advancing LLM-based interactions with data interfaces in everyday work.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement-learning fine-tuning framework (Spreadsheet-RL) that trains specialized Excel agents, with an automated dataset pipeline (start-goal spreadsheets) and a multi-turn RL environment (Spreadsheet Gym); shows significant Pass@1 gains on SpreadsheetBench and Domain-Spreadsheet.
AI.motivation: Real-world spreadsheet automation involves multi-step workflows that are hard for general prompting; there is a need for dedicated RL-based agents with scalable data collection and realistic environments.
AI.method: Design Spreadsheet Gym to expose Excel through a Python sandbox; automated pipeline collects paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums; domain tasks (finance, supply chain) assembled into Domain-Spreadsheet; RL fine-tuning of model (Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507) with tool routing rules and a diverse toolset.
AI.result: Pass@1 improvements: SpreadsheetBench 12.0% -> 23.4%; Domain-Spreadsheet 8.4% -> 17.2%.
AI.conclusion: Spreadsheet-RL demonstrates improved performance and generalization, showing potential for real-world spreadsheet automation and broader data-interface AI interactions; suggests RL-based approaches can enhance LLM-based spreadsheet tasks.

title: AesFormer: Transform Everyday Photos into Beautiful Memories
summary: In everyday photography, aesthetically appealing moments are often captured with structural flaws (e.g., composition, camera viewpoint, or pose) that existing retouching and portrait enhancement methods cannot fix. We formulate Aesthetic Photo Reconstruction (APR) as improving a photo's aesthetic quality via structural reconstruction while preserving subject identity and scene semantics. Although recent advances in image editing models make APR feasible, they often lack aesthetic understanding, yielding edits that are semantically plausible yet aesthetically weak. To address this, we propose AesFormer, a two-stage framework that decouples aesthetic planning from image editing. In Stage 1, an aesthetic action model (AesThinker) analyzes the input along seven progressive photographic dimensions and outputs executable editing actions; we further apply GRPO-A to encourage broad exploration over diverse action plans beyond SFT. In Stage 2, an action-conditioned editor (AesEditor) performs structural edits guided by these actions. To support APR, we build a video-based corpus-mining pipeline (VCMP) and construct AesRecon, a benchmark of 9,071 strictly aligned (poor, good) image pairs. Experiments show that AesFormer substantially improves APR performance and is competitive with Nano Banana Pro. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/AesFormer_ICML2026.

AI.tldr: AesFormer enables aesthetic photo reconstruction via a decoupled two-stage framework (aesthetic planning + image editing), introducing AesThinker with seven photographic dimensions and GRPO-A for diverse action exploration, followed by an action-conditioned editor. It supports APR with a VCMP-driven corpus and AesRecon benchmark; results show notable improvement and competitiveness with Nano Banana Pro; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where existing retouching methods fix perceptual flaws but lack aesthetic understanding, risking semantic plausibility over aesthetics; preserve identity/semantics while enhancing aesthetics.
AI.method: Stage 1: aesthetic action model analyzes input along seven dimensions to output editing actions; GRPO-A expands beyond supervised fine-tuning; Stage 2: action-conditioned editor executes edits guided by actions; VCMP pipeline to mine video-based data; AesRecon benchmark with 9,071 aligned (poor, good) image pairs.
AI.result: Significant APR performance gains; competitive with Nano Banana Pro; robust across settings (as per experiments).
AI.conclusion: A two-stage decoupled framework improves APR by combining explicit aesthetic planning with editing, supported by a dedicated corpus and benchmark; code available.

title: When to Switch, Not Just What: Transition Quality Prediction in Clash Royale
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables; Accepted at IEEE Conference on Games (CoG) 2026
summary: In competitive games, players frequently switch strategies after losing streaks, yet our analysis of 926,334 match records from 34,619 Clash Royale players reveals a counterintuitive pattern: switching frequency is inversely associated with the win rate, with effects that vary substantially across players and situational contexts. We attribute this to a limitation common in many prior recommendation systems, which evaluate strategies by expected quality while overlooking the behavioral cost of switching and individual differences in switching propensity. We refer to this implicit premise as the Zero Switching Cost Assumption. To address this, we reformulate strategy recommendation as a transition-level decision problem and instantiate it as TQP (Transition Quality Predictor), a three-stage pipeline structured as Who -> When -> What. PersonaGate suppresses recommendations for players whose strategic consistency is empirically associated with superior outcomes. TimingGate identifies moments when switching is likely to yield a net benefit over staying, using a subtype- and state-matched baseline to control for natural win-rate recovery. ScoreFusion ranks candidate strategies by combining an adoptability signal with predicted transition quality (delta WR). We further introduce SwitchGap, an evaluation metric that measures a policy's discriminative quality without treating observed player choices as optimal ground truth. This property is particularly important because the most frequent switchers record the lowest win rates. The full pipeline achieves a SwitchGap of +10.4 percentage points at a recommendation rate of 5.4%, and loss-triggered switchers, despite being the lowest-performing group, benefit the most from subtype-conditioned guidance.

AI.tldr: A transition-aware strategy-recommendation framework for competitive games that replaces a zero-switching-cost assumption with a Who-When-What pipeline (TQP), showing improved discriminative policy and win-rate outcomes.
AI.motivation: Conventional recommender systems optimize for expected quality while ignoring switching costs and individual propensities, leading to counterproductive switching behavior observed in large-scale Clash Royale data.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: Who (PersonaGate) filters players by consistency; When (TimingGate) identifies beneficial switch moments using subtype/state-mmatched baselines; What (ScoreFusion) ranks strategies by adoptability and delta WR; introduces SwitchGap evaluation metric.
AI.result: Achieved SwitchGap of +10.4 percentage points at 5.4% recommendation rate; loss-triggered switchers gain the most from subtype-conditioned guidance; observed inverse relation between switching frequency and win rate, with substantial heterogeneity across players and contexts.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for switching costs and individual differences yields more effective strategy recommendations than zero-switching-cost baselines; the framework provides discriminative policy evaluation and practical improvements for guided switching in competitive play.

title: AtelierEval: Agentic Evaluation of Humans & LLMs as Text-to-Image Prompters
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) systems increasingly rely on upstream prompters, either humans or multimodal large language models (MLLMs), to translate user intent into detailed prompts. Yet current benchmarks fix the prompt and only evaluate T2I models, leaving the prompting proficiency of this upstream component entirely unmeasured. We introduce AtelierEval, the first unified benchmark that quantifies prompting proficiency across 360 expert-crafted tasks. Grounded in a cognitive view, it spans three task categories and instantiates tasks using a taxonomy of real-world challenges, with a dual interface for both humans and MLLMs. To enable scalable and reliable evaluation, we propose AtelierJudge, a skill-based, memory-augmented agentic evaluator. It produces subjective and objective scores for prompt-image pairs, achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts, approaching human performance. Extensive experiments benchmark 8 MLLMs against 48 human users across 4 T2I backends, validate AtelierEval as a robust diagnostic tool, and reveal the superiority of mimicry over planning, advocating for an image-augmented direction for future prompters. Our work is released to support future research.

AI.tldr: A unified, scalable benchmark (AtelierEval) assesses upstream prompting proficiency across 360 tasks for humans and MLLMs, using a memory-augmented evaluator (AtelierJudge). Results show strong alignment with human expertise (Spearman 0.79), reveal mimicry beats planning, and advocate image-augmented prompting directions; validated across 8 MLLMs, 48 humans, and 4 T2I backends.
AI.motivation: Current prompts are fixed when evaluating T2I models, leaving prompting proficiency of upstream components unmeasured. A unified, scalable benchmark is needed to quantify prompting skill for both humans and MLLMs across diverse real-world tasks.
AI.method: Three task categories grounded in a cognitive taxonomy; 360 expert-crafted tasks; dual interface for humans and MLLMs; AtelierJudge, a memory-augmented, agentic evaluator, produces subjective and objective scores for prompt-image pairs; correlation with human experts (Spearman r=0.79); evaluation across 8 MLLMs, 48 humans, 4 T2I backends.
AI.result: AtelierEval reliably diagnoses prompting ability; AtelierJudge achieves high agreement with human experts (r=0.79). Mimicry-based prompting outperforms planning-based prompting; image-augmented prompting direction favored for future prompters; the benchmark demonstrates robustness and serves as a diagnostic tool.
AI.conclusion: First unified benchmark for prompting proficiency in T2I pipelines; released for future research; suggests practical guidance (favor mimicry and image-augmentation) and scalability across humans and MLLMs.

title: Accelerating Vision Foundation Models with Drop-in Depthwise Convolution
summary: Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available.

AI.tldr: Replace select attention heads in ViTs with depthwise convolution-based layers to accelerate inference while preserving feature extraction, aided by strategies to identify replaceable heads and a fine-tuning procedure. Achieves 17-20% speedup on classification and segmentation with minimal accuracy loss; validated with theory, experiments, and benchmarks; implementation available.
AI.motivation: ViTs are powerful but expensive at inference due to transformer-based backbones. The work seeks practical acceleration for large pretrained vision models by leveraging convolution-like behavior in some attention heads to reduce compute without sacrificing downstream performance.
AI.method: Introduce a depthwise-convolution-based layer as a drop-in replacement for selected attention heads identified by simple criteria. Combine with a fine-tuning procedure to recover any task-specific performance loss. Validate on image classification and segmentation; perform derivations, experiments, and efficiency benchmarks; provide reference implementation.
AI.result: As a result, the method delivers 17-20% inference speedup with minimal degradation in downstream performance across tasks, while maintaining the feature extraction capabilities of the original ViT backbone.
AI.conclusion: Selective replacement of convolution-like attention heads with depthwise conv modules is an effective and practical approach to accelerate pretrained ViTs, with general applicability to classification and segmentation and availability of code for reproducibility.

title: Noise Schedule Design for Diffusion Models: An Optimal Control Perspective
summary: We develop a principled framework for analyzing and designing noise schedules in diffusion models. We show that one can recast this design problem as an optimal control problem, whose state is the Fisher information of the diffusion process which evolves according to an ODE and the control input is the noise schedule. The objective of the optimal control problem is a functional involving the Fisher information, which is shown to be an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler sampling error. By solving this optimal control problem, we obtain sufficient conditions on noise schedules under which state-of-the-art $\tilde{\mathcal{O}} (d/n)$ sampling error is achievable, where $d$ is the data dimension and $n$ is the number of discretization steps. While existing theoretical work also prove that $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/n)$ sampling error bounds are achievable, these results hold for specific noise schedules, which do not include the schedules used in practice. Under a further parametric assumption on the data distribution, we show that one can obtain closed-form expressions for the noise schedules. These noise schedules generalize standard empirical schedules such as exponential and sigmoid schedules by allowing additional parameters that can be tuned. Systematically tuning the parameters of these schedules yields new schedules that achieve superior FID scores on image generation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A principled optimal-control framework for diffusion-noise scheduling: state is Fisher information, control is the noise schedule; derives upper bounds on KL sampling error leading to O(d/n) guarantees; provides closed-form schedules under parametric data distributions; extends standard schedules and improves FID via tunable parameters.
AI.motivation: To understand and optimize the design of diffusion noise schedules in order to achieve near-optimal sampling efficiency and quality in high-dimensional data generation.
AI.method: Model diffusion as an ODE with state = Fisher information; control input = noise schedule. Define an objective functional of the Fisher information that upper-bounds the KL sampling error. Solve the optimal-control problem to obtain sufficient conditions for noise schedules achieving tilde O(d/n) sampling error. Under a parametric data-distribution assumption, derive closed-form noise schedules that generalize exponential and sigmoid schedules. Parameter tuning yields better empirical performance (FID) on image benchmarks.
AI.result: Provided sufficient conditions for achieving tilde O(d/n) sampling error with diffusion schedules. Derived closed-form schedules under parametric distribution assumptions, broadening beyond specific pre-existing schedules. Demonstrated that tunable parameters yield improved FID scores on image generation benchmarks compared to standard schedules.
AI.conclusion: A unified, principled approach to diffusion-noise scheduling via optimal control, yielding theoretically grounded schedules with practical improvements. Highlights: (i) provable sampling-efficiency guarantees under broad conditions, (ii) ability to obtain closed-form, tunable schedules under data-model assumptions, (iii) empirical gains on image benchmarks. Limitations and extensions include assessing tightness of bounds, robustness to model misspecification, and scalability to complex data modalities.

title: Claw AI Lab: An Autonomous Multi-Agent Research Team
comment: Project page and code are available at https://github.com/Claw-AI-Lab/Claw-AI-Lab
summary: We present Claw AI Lab, a lab-native autonomous research platform that advances automated research from a hidden prompt-to-paper pipeline into an interactive AI laboratory. Rather than centering the system around a single agent or a fixed serial workflow, we allow users to instantiate a full research team from one prompt, with customizable roles, collaborative workflows, real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume control through a unified dashboard. The platform also supports distinct research modes for exploration, multi-agent discussion, and reproduction, making autonomous research substantially more steerable and laboratory-like in practice. A key practical contribution of Claw AI Lab lies in its Claw-Code Harness, which connects local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feeds execution artifacts back into the research loop. As a result, the harness improves not only execution integration, but also experimental completion and result integrity: experiments are easier to inspect, iterate on, and faithfully transfer into final papers, reducing common failure modes such as partial runs and malformed result reporting. In our internal evaluation on five AI research case studies, using AutoResearchClaw as the baseline, Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges on idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality. We view Claw AI Lab as an early step toward a new paradigm: autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure.

AI.tldr: Claw AI Lab extends autonomous research by providing a lab-like, multi-agent AI research platform with a unified dashboard, customizable roles, and a Claw-Code Harness that links local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments. It emphasizes interactive, steerable research, robust execution, and reproducibility, evaluated favorably against AutoResearchClaw in five case studies.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of single-agent or linear autonomous research pipelines by offering an interactive, reliable, and extensible laboratory-style platform that supports collaborative research workflows and reproducible experiments.
AI.method: Develop a platform (Claw AI Lab) enabling instantiable research teams from a single prompt with roles, collaboration workflows, monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume capabilities; provide distinct research modes (exploration, multi-agent discussion, reproduction); introduce Claw-Code Harness to connect local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feedback artifacts into the research loop; evaluate on five AI research case studies using AutoResearchClaw as baseline.
AI.result: The platform yielded improved idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality according to AI expert judges; it enhances execution integration and result integrity, simplifies inspection and iteration, and reduces failure modes like partial runs and malformed reporting.
AI.conclusion: Claw AI Lab represents an early step toward autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure, potentially shifting how research is conducted by enabling lab-like, steerable autonomous workflows.

title: EventGait: Towards Robust Gait Recognition with Event Streams
summary: Gait recognition enables non-intrusive, privacy-preserving identification but suffers in uncontrolled environments due to illumination and motion sensitivity of conventional cameras. In this work, we explore gait recognition using event cameras, which offer microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, naturally capturing robust dynamic cues and suppressing static noise. Existing event-based approaches typically aggregate event streams into event images over long time windows, thereby discarding fine-grained motion dynamics critical for gait recognition. Therefore, we propose \textbf{EventGait}, an end-to-end dual-stream framework that separately models motion and shape while preserving the advantages of events. Our dynamic stream leverages a Mixture of Spiking Experts (MoSE) with diverse neuron constants for robust dynamic perception across complex motion and illumination scenes, while the static stream learns dense shape representations via Cross-modal Structure Alignment (CroSA) with large vision foundation models. To address the absence of large-scale event-based gait datasets, we introduce a synthesis pipeline and release two new benchmarks: SUSTech1K-E and CCGR-Mini-E. Extensive experiments have shown that event-based gait recognition not only achieves results comparable to camera-based gait recognition under normal conditions but also significantly outperforms it in low-light scenarios. Our approach sets a new state of the art on both synthesized and real-world event-based gait benchmarks, highlighting the robustness and potential of event-driven gait analysis. The code and datasets are released at https://github.com/QUEAHREN/EventGait.

AI.tldr: EventGait presents an end-to-end dual-stream gait recognition framework for event cameras: a dynamic stream using Mixture of Spiking Experts (MoSE) to capture motion under challenging illumination, and a static stream using Cross-modal Structure Alignment (CroSA) with large vision foundation models for shape; introduces synthetic data and two benchmarks; achieves state-of-the-art results, especially in low-light, and releases code.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of conventional cameras in gait recognition under uncontrolled environments—illumination variability and motion sensitivity—by leveraging event cameras' high temporal resolution and high dynamic range. Existing event-based methods aggregate events into long-lived images, losing fine-grained motion cues necessary for gait recognition. The work aims to preserve dynamic information while leveraging events.
AI.method: Propose EventGait, a dual-stream architecture. Dynamic stream: Mixture of Spiking Experts (MoSE) with diverse neuron constants for robust dynamic perception across varying motion/illumination. Static stream: Cross-modal Structure Alignment (CroSA) leveraging large vision foundation models to learn dense shape representations from events. Addresses data scarcity by a synthesis pipeline and introduces two benchmarks: SUSTech1K-E and CCGR-Mini-E.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that event-based gait recognition matches camera-based gait under normal lighting and significantly outperforms it in low-light scenarios. EventGait sets new state-of-the-art on both synthesized and real-world event-based gait benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the robustness and potential of event-driven gait analysis, enabling reliable gait recognition in challenging conditions. The authors release code and datasets to facilitate further research.

title: CausalGuard: Conformal Inference under Graph Uncertainty
summary: Estimating treatment effects from observational data requires choosing an adjustment set, but valid adjustment depends on an unknown causal graph. Graph misspecification can cause under-coverage, while graph-agnostic conformal wrappers may regain nominal coverage only through large padding. We introduce CausalGuard, a structure-weighted conformal framework that calibrates after aggregating graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes. Candidate DAGs are proposed from an LLM-derived edge prior, pruned by conditional-independence tests, and reweighted by Bayesian Information Criterion. A composite nonconformity score then calibrates the posterior-weighted pseudo-outcome. CausalGuard provides distribution-free finite-sample marginal coverage for this aggregated pseudo-outcome; under causal identification, overlap, conditional-mean nuisance stability, and concentration on target-aligned valid adjustment strategies, its conditional mean converges to the true Conditional Average Treatment Effect. Across five benchmarks, CausalGuard attains mean coverage above the nominal 90% level for the directly evaluable target and reduces width when graph-agnostic conformal baselines require large padding. Stress tests show that CausalGuard suppresses invalid collider adjustment and remains stable under misspecified priors when the retained candidate set is data-supported.

AI.tldr: CausalGuard is a structure-weighted conformal method that calibrates causal effect estimates under uncertain causal graphs by aggregating graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes using LLM-priors, CI pruning, and BIC reweighting, yielding finite-sample, distribution-free coverage and improved efficiency over graph-agnostic baselines.
AI.motivation: Graph misspecification in adjustment sets leads to under-coverage, while graph-agnostic conformal methods demand large padding to achieve nominal coverage. A framework that combines plausible graphs with principled calibration can provide reliable, efficient inference for treatment effects from observational data.
AI.method: Propose candidate DAGs from an LLM-derived edge prior; prune by conditional-independence tests; reweight by Bayesian Information Criterion; compute graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes; form a composite nonconformity score; apply conformal calibration to achieve distribution-free finite-sample marginal coverage for the aggregated pseudo-outcome; analyze under identification assumptions (overlap, nuisance stability, etc.) for convergence to CATE.
AI.result: Empirically, across five benchmarks, mean coverage exceeds the nominal 90% for the evaluable target and the method reduces the width compared with graph-agnostic conformal baselines that require large padding; stress tests show suppression of invalid collider adjustment and stability under misspecified priors when the retained candidates are data-supported.
AI.conclusion: CausalGuard provides valid, finite-sample coverage for aggregated, graph-informed pseudo-outcomes and improves efficiency over graph-agnostic approaches, with robustness to misspecification given data-supported candidate graphs.

title: WorkstreamBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on End-to-End Spreadsheet Tasks in Finance
summary: LLM agents are increasingly expected to carry out end-to-end workflows, producing complete artifacts from high-level user instructions. To meet enterprise needs, frontier AI labs have developed agents that can construct entire spreadsheets from scratch. This is especially relevant in finance, where core workflows such as financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis are commonly conducted through spreadsheets. Yet, existing spreadsheet benchmarks do not measure this advanced capability, focusing instead on question-answering or single-formula edits. To address this gap, we provide one of the first evaluations of agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks, focusing on economically critical financial workflows such as modeling and scenario analysis. Since deliverables therein are routinely reviewed and revised by multiple stakeholders, judging their quality necessarily involves high-level criteria such as readability or ease of modification. To reflect the multidimensional nature of solution quality, we develop an evaluation taxonomy comprising three dimensions: Accuracy, Formula, and Format, each comprising fine-grained criteria that reflect professional standards. The Claude family leads the benchmark and produces the most professional-looking outputs in our qualitative review, but even the strongest agents frequently fall short of professional finance standards and degrade sharply as the difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations. This suggests that current agents are not yet able to reliably produce professional-quality spreadsheets at the level of complexity real-world workflows demand.

AI.tldr: One of the first evaluations of LLM agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks in finance; introduces a three-dimensional evaluation taxonomy (Accuracy, Formula, Format); Claude-family agents show the most professional outputs but still underperform professional finance standards, with quality dropping as task complexity increases beyond a few chained calculations.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of benchmarks for end-to-end spreadsheet generation by LLM agents in enterprise finance, where spreadsheets are central to modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis.
AI.method: Develop an evaluation taxonomy with three dimensions (Accuracy, Formula, Format) and fine-grained criteria. Construct end-to-end spreadsheet tasks around financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis. Evaluate agents (notably Claude family) on their ability to generate complete spreadsheets from high-level instructions, followed by qualitative assessment of readability and modifiability. Analyze performance across tasks of increasing complexity.
AI.result: Claude-family agents produced the most professional-looking spreadsheets in qualitative reviews but generally failed to meet professional finance standards. Performance deteriorated as the number of chained calculations increased, indicating limited reliability for complex real-world workflows.
AI.conclusion: Current agents are not yet capable of reliably producing professional-quality, complex spreadsheets for real-world financial workflows. There is a need for better benchmarking, more robust modeling of multi-step financial tasks, and possibly improvements in agent reliability and collaboration-friendly outputs.

title: One Sentence, One Drama: Personalized Short-Form Drama Generation via Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Existing approaches for digital short-drama production typically rely on one-shot LLM generated scripts and loosely coupled pipelines, which fail to satisfy three key requirements of short-drama generation: (1) narrative pacing, resulting in weak hooks, insufficient escalation, and unattractive endings; (2) spatial consistency, leading to drifting scene layouts and inconsistent character positions across clips; and (3) production-level quality control, requiring extensive manual review and correction across script and visual stages. We present One Sentence, One Drama, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that transforms a user's single-sentence idea into a fully produced short drama through structured intermediate modules and iterative refinement. Our approach is built upon three key components: (1) a multi-agent debate-based story generation module that enforces short-drama pacing and narrative coherence; (2) a 3D-grounded first-frame generation mechanism that establishes a shared spatial reference for consistent character positioning and scene layout across clips; and (3) multi-stage reviewer loops that perform comprehensive error detection and targeted revision across script, visual, and video generation stages. We also introduce scene-level BGM matching and scene transition planning to improve the audience's immersive experience. To systematically evaluate this task, we introduce Short-Drama-Bench, a benchmark that extends standard video quality metrics with short-drama-specific criteria. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing pipelines in narrative quality, cross-clip consistency, and overall viewing experience.

AI.tldr: Proposes One Sentence, One Drama, a hierarchical multi-agent system that turns a single sentence into a full short drama via (i) debate-based story generation for pacing, (ii) a 3D-grounded first frame for cross-clip spatial consistency, and (iii) multi-stage reviewer loops for script/visual/video QA, plus scene-level BGM and transitions; introduces Short-Drama-Bench and shows superiority over pipelines in narrative quality and cross-clip consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing digital short-drama pipelines rely on one-shot LLM-generated scripts and loosely coupled stages, leading to poor narrative pacing, spatial drift, and heavy manual review requirements. There is a need for structured, end-to-end systems that enforce pacing, spatial consistency, and production-grade quality control.
AI.method: A hierarchical multi-agent framework with three core modules: (1) a multi-agent debate-based story generation module to enforce short-drama pacing and coherence; (2) a 3D-grounded first-frame generation mechanism to establish a shared spatial reference and maintain character positioning across clips; (3) multi-stage reviewer loops that detect errors and perform targeted revisions across script, visuals, and video generation. Additional components include scene-level BGM matching and scene transition planning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Short-Drama-Bench shows significant improvements over existing pipelines in narrative quality, cross-clip consistency, and overall viewing experience.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively transforms a user-provided single sentence into a fully produced short drama via structured intermediate modules and iterative refinement, delivering improved pacing, spatial coherence, and production-grade quality.

title: Optimal Guarantees for Auditing Rényi Differentially Private Machine Learning
summary: We study black-box auditing for machine learning algorithms that claim R \ 'enyi differential privacy (RDP) guarantees. We introduce an auditing framework, based on hypothesis testing, that directly estimates Rényi divergence between neighboring executions using the Donsker-Varadhan (DV) variational estimator. Our analysis yields explicit and non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing via class-restricted DV estimators, separating statistical estimation error from algorithmic privacy leakage. We prove matching minimax lower bounds showing that, up to logarithmic factors, our sample-complexity guarantees are information-theoretically optimal, thereby establishing the first optimal guarantees for auditing RDP via DV estimators. Empirically, we instantiate our framework for auditing DP-SGD in a fully black-box setting. Across MNIST and CIFAR-10, and over a wide range of privacy regimes, our auditors produce a strong overall improvement on empirical RDP lower bounds compared to prior state-of-the-art black-box methods especially at small and moderate Rényi orders where accurate auditing is most challenging.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hypothesis-testing framework to audit Rényi DP using Donsker-Varadhan estimators; achieves non-asymptotic, tight confidence intervals and minimax-optimal sample complexity; empirically improves RDP lower-bounds for DP-SGD on MNIST/CIFAR-10, especially at small/moderate Rényi orders.
AI.motivation: There is a need to verify privacy claims of ML algorithms that assert RDP guarantees, even in black-box settings. Existing auditing methods lack non-asymptotic guarantees and optimal sample efficiency, and cannot cleanly separate estimation error from actual privacy leakage. The work aims to provide statistically sound, information-theoretically optimal auditing of RDP.
AI.method: Develop a hypothesis-testing auditing framework that directly estimates Rényi divergence between neighboring executions via Donsker-Varadhan variational estimators. Introduce class-restricted DV estimators to separate statistical estimation error from algorithmic leakage, derive explicit non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing, and prove minimax lower bounds showing near-optimal sample complexity. Apply the framework in a fully black-box setting to DP-SGD and validate on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across various privacy regimes.
AI.result: Non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing are obtained; established information-theoretic optimality of the auditing procedure up to logarithmic factors via minimax lower bounds; empirically, the method yields stronger empirical RDP lower bounds than prior black-box methods, particularly for small/moderate Rényi orders across MNIST and CIFAR-10.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first (up to log factors) optimal guarantees for auditing RDP using DV estimators and demonstrates practical effectiveness in fully black-box DP-SGD auditing on standard benchmarks, suggesting broad applicability and improved reliability of privacy claims.

title: Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse Forecasts When It Matters Most
summary: We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a structure common in finance and epidemiology. On these tasks, more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts. The pattern appears on ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a contamination-free, simulated-world benchmark we release, in forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control, and replicates in real-world datasets on COVID-19, measles, housing markets, and hyperinflation. A per-quantile decomposition shows the failure concentrates at the upper tail, which more capable models shift upward to track aggressive extrapolations of growth, while the lower tail stays put. A within-family study of Llama-3.1 shows that both model scale and post-training independently contribute to this effect. Domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration. This inverse scaling does not appear on single-threshold metrics common in LLM forecasting benchmarks, reversing the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on identical outputs. Single-threshold scoring at conventional cutoffs misses the upper-tail cost; tail-inclusive scoring reverses the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on the same outputs. We recommend that LLM forecasting evaluations use continuous (and unbounded) measures of accuracy alongside bounded binary threshold metrics.

AI.tldr: Inverse scaling in LLM forecasting: more capable models worsen upper-tail calibration on superlinear, regime-change time-series; tail-heavy forecasts improve with higher capacity, while lower tail remains similar. Requires tail-aware, continuous evaluation; single-threshold metrics miss the effect.
AI.motivation: To understand how model scale and post-training affect distributional forecasts for time-series with superlinear growth and regime-change risk, with relevance to finance and epidemiology.
AI.method: Evaluate forecasting performance across model scales on ForecastBench-Sim synthetic SIR with a linear control, and replicate on real datasets (COVID-19, measles, housing markets, hyperinflation). Use per-quantile decomposition to analyze tail behavior; conduct within-family study of Llama-3.1 to separate scale vs post-training effects; compare continuous vs single-threshold metrics.
AI.result: More capable models exhibit worse upper-tail calibration (inverse scaling) on forecasts with superlinear growth and regime-change risk; lower tail remains relatively unchanged. This pattern persists in real data. Both model scale and post-training contribute to the effect; domain knowledge does not reliably correct calibration. Single-threshold metrics hide the effect by ranking outputs similarly, while tail-inclusive metrics reveal reversed capability–accuracy relationship on same outputs.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation of LLM forecasting should employ continuous, unbounded accuracy measures in addition to bounded threshold metrics to reveal tail risks and avoid misinterpreting model capability. Tail-aware evaluation is crucial for forecasting tasks with regime-change and heavy-tailed dynamics.

title: Flow-based Gaussian Splatting for Continuous-Scale Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution
summary: High-resolution remote sensing images (RSIs) are crucial for Earth observation applications, yet acquiring them is often limited by sensor constraints and costs. In recent years, generative super-resolution (SR) methods, particularly diffusion models, have made significant progress. However, they typically require slow iterative inference with 40--1000 steps and exhibit limited flexibility in continuous-scale SR settings. To address these issues, we propose FlowGS, a generative reconstruction framework for arbitrary-scale SR of RSIs. FlowGS models the high-frequency detail representations between high- and low-resolution images and learns a continuous probability flow from noise to detail priors via flow matching (FM) constrained by shortcut consistency, thereby reducing generative complexity and improving inference efficiency. Additionally, we employ 2D Gaussian splatting to construct a continuous feature field, thereby enabling flexible reconstruction at arbitrary query locations. Experimental results show that FlowGS delivers competitive perceptual quality compared with existing methods in both continuous-scale and fixed-scale SR settings, with substantially improved inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: FlowGS is a continuous-scale, efficient generative framework for super-resolution of remote sensing images, using flow matching to model high-frequency detail transfer and 2D Gaussian splatting to enable arbitrary query locations, achieving competitive perceptual quality with improved inference speed.
AI.motivation: Conventional diffusion-based SR methods for RSIs are slow due to iterative sampling (40–1000 steps) and lack flexibility for arbitrary scales. There is a need for fast, continuous-scale SR that preserves details and is efficient for remote sensing applications.
AI.method: FlowGS learns a continuous probability flow from noise to detail priors by flow matching constrained by shortcut consistency, modeling high-frequency detail representations between HR and LR RSI pairs. It uses 2D Gaussian splatting to build a continuous feature field for querying at arbitrary locations, enabling arbitrary-scale reconstruction.
AI.result: FlowGS delivers competitive perceptual quality compared with existing methods in both continuous-scale and fixed-scale SR settings, while substantially improving inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FlowGS reduces generative complexity and inference time in RSI SR, enabling flexible, arbitrary-scale reconstruction with strong perceptual quality.

title: SCI-Defense: Defending Manipulation Attacks from Generative Engine Optimization
summary: LLM-based ranking systems are vulnerable to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) attacks, where adversaries inject semantic signals into product descriptions to artificially boost rankings. We propose SCI-Defense, a three-component defense framework combining Perplexity detection (PPL), Semantic Integrity Scoring (SIS), and Inter-Candidate Detection (ICD). SIS evaluates four manipulation dimensions: Authority Attribution (AA), Narrative Purposiveness (NP), Comparative Claims (CA), and Temporal Claims (TC). Evaluated on 600 Amazon product descriptions across 6 categories, SCI-Defense achieves Precision=1.000 and FPR=0.000, with Recall of 1.000, 0.952, and 0.830 against String, Reasoning, and Review attacks respectively. On 600 MS MARCO web passages, String attacks are blocked with perfect recall while Review attacks yield near-zero recall, as web passages lack the persuasion-oriented signals that SIS targets in product descriptions. We demonstrate that existing defenses -- PPL-only filters, SafetyClf content classifiers, and paraphrasing -- achieve zero recall against semantic manipulation attacks. We further demonstrate new attacks such as Specification Amplification and Use-Case Saturation can expose semantic relevance manipulation as a structural defense blind spot that suggests directions for future research.

AI.tldr: Three-component defense (PPL, SIS, ICD) against semantic GEO attacks in LLM-based ranking. Evaluated on 600 Amazon product descriptions across 6 categories and 600 MS MARCO web passages. Reports perfect precision (1.000) and zero false positives (FPR 0.000); recalls: 1.000 (String), 0.952 (Reasoning), 0.830 (Review) on Amazon; MS MARCO shows perfect recall for String and near-zero recall for Review. Claims existing defenses fail to detect semantic manipulation; introduces new attack vectors (Specification Amplification, Use-Case Saturation) to expose blind spots and outlines future research directions.
AI.motivation: GEO attacks manipulate product descriptions to artificially boost LLM-based rankings, threatening integrity and trust. Need robust, multi-faceted defenses beyond single-signal filters.
AI.method: SCI-Defense combines Perplexity detection (PPL), Semantic Integrity Scoring (SIS) across four manipulation dimensions (Authority Attribution, Narrative Purposiveness, Comparative Claims, Temporal Claims), and Inter-Candidate Detection (ICD). Evaluated on 600 Amazon descriptions (6 categories) and 600 MS MARCO web passages; comparison against PPL-only, SafetyClf, and paraphrasing defenses; includes analysis of new attack types (Specification Amplification, Use-Case Saturation).
AI.result: On 600 Amazon descriptions: Precision=1.000, FPR=0.000; Recall=1.000 (String), 0.952 (Reasoning), 0.830 (Review). On 600 MS MARCO passages: String attacks blocked with perfect recall; Review attacks near-zero recall. Existing defenses showed zero recall against semantic manipulation. Demonstrates blind spots exposed by new attacks; supports need for integrated defense.
AI.conclusion: Integrated defense (PPL+SIS+ICD) effectively mitigates GEO attacks in tested settings, outperforming existing defenses. Findings suggest strong generalization across domains but reveal structural blind spots (semantic relevance manipulation) and point to directions for future research to bolster robustness against evolving attacks.

title: Forecasting Scientific Progress with Artificial Intelligence
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded in scientific discovery, yet whether it can anticipate scientific progress remains unclear. To study this question, we introduce a temporally grounded evaluation framework for forecasting scientific progress under controlled knowledge constraints. We present CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary and event-level benchmark that evaluates scientific forecasting in AI systems through feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. Across 4,760 scientific events, we observe systematic and domain-dependent limitations in current frontier models. While models can identify plausible research directions from competing candidates, they fail to reliably predict whether scientific advances will be realized and systematically misestimate when they will occur. Performance is highly heterogeneous across domains, with the timing of AI progress more predictable than advances in biology, chemistry, and physics. Performance is largely insensitive to whether events occur before or after the training cutoff, suggesting these limitations cannot be explained solely by knowledge exposure in training data. Under controlled information access, additional pre-cutoff knowledge improves performance but does not close the gap to full-information settings, which becomes more pronounced for high-citation advances. Models also exhibit systematic overconfidence and strong response biases, indicating unreliable uncertainty estimation. Taken together, current AI systems fall short as predictive tools for scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not translate into reliable forecasting, and performance benefits more from post-event information than from forward-looking prediction.

AI.tldr: A temporally grounded benchmark (CUSP) shows AI can propose plausible research directions but struggles to forecast whether and when scientific advances will occur, with strong domain dependence and persistent miscalibration.
AI.motivation: Evaluate the capacity of AI to forecast scientific progress under controlled knowledge constraints and quantify the limitations of frontier models.
AI.method: Introduce CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary, event-level benchmark with 4,760 scientific events. Tasks include feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. Compare AI systems under pre-cutoff and post-cutoff information access to assess the role of prior knowledge and information leakage.
AI.result: Models identify plausible research directions but rarely predict the realization or timing of advances. Performance varies by domain; timing is more predictable than in biology, chemistry, or physics. Pre-cutoff knowledge helps but does not close the gap to full information, especially for high-citation advances. Post-event information yields more benefit. Models show overconfidence and response biases, with unreliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.conclusion: Current AI systems are insufficient predictors of scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not reliably translate into forecasting accuracy, and gains come more from post-event information than forward-looking prediction.

title: Balancing Uncertainty and Diversity of Samples: Leveraging Diversity of Least, High Confidence Samples for Effective Active Learning
summary: Deep learning models, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs), have achieved state-of-the-art performance on various computer vision tasks such as object classification, detection, segmentation, generation, and many more. However, these models are data-hungry as they require more training data to learn millions or billions of parameters. Especially for supervised learning tasks, curating a large number of labeled samples for model training is an expensive and time-consuming task. Active Learning (AL) has been used to address this problem for many years. Existing active learning methods aim at choosing the samples for annotation from a pool of unlabeled samples that are either diverse or uncertain. Choosing such samples may hinder the model's performance as we pool based on one dimension, i.e., either diverse or uncertain. In this paper, we propose four novel hybrid sampling methods for pooling both easy and hard samples, which are also diverse. To verify the efficacy of the proposed methods, extensive experiments are conducted using high and low-confidence samples separately. We observe from our experiments that the proposed hybrid sampling method, Least Confident and Diverse (LCD), consistently performs better compared to state-of-the-art methods. It is observed that selecting uncertain and diverse instances helps the model learn more distinct features. The codes related to this study will be available at https://github.com/XXX/LCD.

AI.tldr: Hybrid active-learning sampling combines uncertainty and diversity; LCD method achieves superior performance by selecting both easy and hard, diverse samples.
AI.motivation: DL models are data-hungry and require large labeled datasets. Traditional AL methods optimize along a single dimension (uncertainty or diversity), which can limit performance; a hybrid approach aims to leverage both to improve learning.
AI.method: Introduces four novel hybrid sampling strategies for pool-based active learning in vision tasks, including LCD (Least Confident and Diverse); experiments analyze high- and low-confidence samples separately; code will be released.
AI.result: LCD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art AL methods; selecting uncertain and diverse instances leads to learning of more distinct features and better performance across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid uncertainty-diversity sampling is effective for vision AL; LCD stands out as a strong method; the approach is validated empirically and code will be made available.

title: Dynamic Mixture of Latent Memories for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Achieving self-evolution in intelligent agents requires the continual accumulation of new knowledge across changing task sequences without forgetting previously acquired abilities. Existing approaches either internalize knowledge by updating model parameters, which induces catastrophic forgetting, or rely on external memory, which fails to genuinely enhance the model's intrinsic capabilities. We propose MoLEM, a generative mixture of latent memory framework based on a dynamic mixture-of-experts (MoE). We treat multiple experts as independent carriers to generate memory. A router selects and weights experts through key-query matching, and the aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process. The base model for reasoning remains entirely frozen, with all experiential knowledge internalized into the additional modules, avoiding catastrophic forgetting. For continual learning, each training stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder that selects the appropriate routing group at inference, and inputs that match no stage fall back to the pretrained model. Experiments train the framework on continual-learning sequences spanning math, science, and code domains. After training, we evaluate the framework on the corresponding test sets to measure task learning and competence preservation across continual adaptation stages. After the full continual-learning sequence, our method improves the average accuracy by 10.40% over the Vanilla pretrained baseline, while none of the competing methods consistently exceed this baseline across different training orders.

AI.tldr: MoLEM introduces a generative mixture of latent memory using a dynamic MoE to store experiential knowledge in auxiliary modules while keeping the base model frozen, enabling continual learning across task sequences without catastrophic forgetting; routing selects memory experts via key-query matching, with lightweight autoencoders guiding routing per stage; shows ~10.4% average accuracy gain over the vanilla pretrained baseline across math, science, and code domains.
AI.motivation: Address continual learning without forgetting: traditional parameter-update methods cause forgetting; external memory helps but doesn't enhance intrinsic capabilities. Propose internal latent memory via a dynamic mixture of experts to preserve knowledge within dedicated modules, enabling continual adaptation while keeping the base model unchanged.
AI.method: A generative mixture of latent memory framework (MoLEM) built on a dynamic mixture-of-experts (MoE). Multiple experts act as independent memory carriers. A router uses key-query matching to select and weight experts; aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process. The base model remains frozen; experiential knowledge is stored in the memory modules. For continual learning, each training stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder that selects the appropriate routing group at inference; inputs that match no stage fallback to the pretrained model.
AI.result: Experiments on continual-learning sequences spanning math, science, and code domains. After training, evaluation on test sets measures task learning and competence preservation across stages. MoLEM improves average accuracy by 10.40% over the vanilla pretrained baseline, and competing methods do not consistently exceed this baseline across different training orders.
AI.conclusion: MoLEM demonstrates effective continual learning by internalizing experience in latent memory modules while preserving the frozen base model, achieving significant gains over the vanilla pretrained baseline. The dynamic routing and stage-aware autoencoder enable selective memory use and robust knowledge retention across task orders.

title: Beyond the Org Chart: AI and the Transformation of Invisible Work
summary: An increasing number of news and research articles report that AI adoption is allowing professionals to blur and extend the boundaries of their corporate roles. With the goal of understanding how work processes might be changing in an AI-forward company, we interviewed 24 product-focused individuals at a large technology firm about how AI has impacted their own work, their work within their product team, and their professional interactions. Our conversations suggest that AI is not only changing formal role responsibilities and collaborations between those roles, but also changing informal cultural practices like professional mentoring that are key to helping professionals settle in their positions, stay engaged with their work, and grow their careers. Some of these changes are positive, such as smoother collaboration between peers, but other changes are more nuanced and put the typical career growth opportunities, like receiving feedback from professional networks and promoting leadership and mentorship, at risk. We propose steps that AI companies can take to make the invisible work more visible. Additionally, we propose efforts that individuals and leaders can take to support their colleagues through AI transformation while preserving healthy company cultures that support diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions.

AI.tldr: AI adoption reshapes tasks, roles, and informal practices; a qualitative study highlights benefits and risks, and outlines steps to reveal invisible work and sustain healthy collaboration.
AI.motivation: Understand how AI-forward work environments alter professional roles, team dynamics, and informal mentorship, and implications for career growth.
AI.method: Qualitative study based on 24 semi-structured interviews with product-focused professionals at a large tech firm; thematic analysis of impact on formal responsibilities, collaboration, and informal cultural practices.
AI.result: AI changes both formal roles and informal mentoring/feedback networks; some outcomes improve cross-peer collaboration, others threaten career progression and leadership development; proposes organizational and individual actions to surface invisible work while preserving culture.
AI.conclusion: Addressing invisible work and culture is essential in AI transformations; implement steps for companies and individuals to support diverse thinking, collaboration, and mentorship during AI adoption.

title: Enhancing Multimodal Large Language Models for Safety-Critical Driving Video Analysis
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC 2026)
summary: Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in general visual understanding. However, their application to safety-critical driving scenarios remains limited by an inability to accurately perceive and reason about rare high-stakes dynamic events, such as collisions or near-collisions. To address this, we introduce a pipeline that enhances MLLM perception by fusing downsampled video frames with synchronized high-frequency telematics data (IMU and GPS) and semantic insights from specialized computer vision models. Our pipeline generates high-quality pseudo-labels, including descriptive captions and question-answer pairs, specifically designed to train MLLMs to identify and describe Safety-Critical Events (SCEs) in real-world driving footage. We show the effectiveness of our approach fine-tuning the open-source QwenVL-2.5 model via DoRA adapters: our experiments demonstrate significant improvements in identifying and explaining safety-critical events, with fewer than 50M trainable parameters and limited computational budget.

AI.tldr: Fuses downsampled video, high-frequency telematics (IMU+GPS), and CV model outputs to generate pseudo-labels for training MLLMs to identify and describe safety-critical driving events; fine-tunes QwenVL-2.5 with DoRA adapters using under 50M trainable parameters and modest compute.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical driving events are rare and challenging for MLLMs to perceive/reason about; improved multimodal perception and label quality are needed to reliably detect and explain such events.
AI.method: Integrate downsampled video frames with synchronized IMU and GPS data and semantic CV outputs; generate high-quality pseudo-labels (descriptions and QA pairs) for training MLLMs; fine-tune QwenVL-2.5 via DoRA adapters, maintaining a small trainable parameter budget (~<50M) and limited computational resources.
AI.result: The approach yields significant improvements in identifying and explaining safety-critical events with a constrained parameter budget (<50M) and limited compute.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, multimodal labeling-and-finetuning pipeline can substantially enhance MLLMs' ability to perceive and articulate safety-critical events in driving scenarios.

title: ChronoMedicalWorld: A Medical World Model for Learning Patient Trajectories from Longitudinal Care Data
summary: Long-horizon clinical simulation -- predicting how a patient's physiology evolves over years under specified interventions -- is central to chronic-disease care, yet existing electronic health record (EHR) models are predominantly discriminative, and general-purpose large language models drift under repeated interventions. We propose the \textbf{ChronoMedicalWorld Model (CMWM)}, an action-conditioned latent world-model framework for learning patient trajectories from longitudinal care data. CMWM couples a joint-embedding state encoder with a wide action encoder that admits both structured intervention indicators and free-text communication embeddings, and trains a recurrent latent transition module under a six-term objective: next-observation supervision, next-latent prediction, SIGReg latent regularisation, and three physiology-aware shape priors (slope, continuity, large-jump penalty). A closed-loop rollout-prefix protocol matches training to deployment, so the model is optimised against the same multi-step error it exhibits at inference. As a concrete case study, we instantiate CMWM for annual estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) trajectory forecasting in chronic kidney disease (CKD). On a 2{,}232-patient nephrology cohort, the CKD instantiation achieves a dynamic-50\% history rollout test mean absolute error (MAE) of 7.384 and root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 10.256, against 7.964 and 11.069 for a tuned GPT-5.5 structured-prompting baseline ($-7.28\%$ MAE, $-7.35\%$ RMSE), with the gain dominated by the dialogue portion of patient--health-coach communication. The framework is not CKD-specific: its architecture, loss design, and training protocol apply to any chronic condition that can be cast as periodic clinical state interleaved with structured and conversational interventions.

AI.tldr: Action-conditioned latent world-model (CMWM) for long-horizon clinical simulation; combines structured and free-text interventions with physiology-aware priors; outperforms a GPT-based baseline in CKD eGFR trajectory forecasting; generalizable to other chronic diseases.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of discriminative EHR models and drift-prone LLMs for long-horizon patient trajectories; enable stable, multi-step prediction under interventions.
AI.method: CMWM architecture: joint-embedding state encoder; wide action encoder for structured indicators and free-text embeddings; recurrent latent transition; six-term objective including next observation/latent, SIGReg regularization, and slope/continuity/large-jump priors; closed-loop rollout-prefix training; applied to annual eGFR in CKD.
AI.result: In a 2,232-patient CKD cohort, dynamic-50% history rollout MAE 7.384 and RMSE 10.256, vs GPT-5.5 tuned baseline 7.964 MAE and 11.069 RMSE; ~7% improvements; gains largely from dialogue portion.
AI.conclusion: The approach is architecture- and training-protocol-agnostic for chronic conditions; can be applied to any periodic clinical state with structured and conversational interventions.

title: AMEL: Accumulated Message Effects on LLM Judgments
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures, 6 tables. Single author. Code, data (75,898 deduplicated API responses), and analysis pipeline at https://github.com/chutapp/amel
summary:
Large language models are routinely used as automated evaluators: to review code, moderate content, or score outputs, often with many items passing through one conversation. We ask whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent judgments, an effect we call the accumulated message effect on LLM judgments (AMEL). Across 75,898 API calls to 11 models from 4 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source models), we present identical test items in isolation or following histories saturated with predominantly positive or negative evaluations.
  Models shift toward the conversation's prevailing polarity (d = -0.17, p < 10^-46). The effect concentrates on items where the model is genuinely uncertain at baseline (d = -0.34 for high-entropy items, vs d = -0.15 when the baseline is deterministic). Bias does not grow with context length: 5 prior turns and 50 produce the same shift (Spearman |r| < 0.01; OLS slope p = 0.80). And there is a negativity asymmetry: paired per item, negative histories induce 1.62x more bias than positive (t = 13.46, p < 10^-39, n = 2,481). Scaling helps but does not solve it (Anthropic: Haiku -0.22 to Opus -0.17; OpenAI: Nano -0.34 to GPT-5.2 -0.17).
  Three follow-ups narrow the mechanism. The token probability distribution shifts continuously, not at a threshold. The negativity asymmetry has both token-level and semantic components, though attributing the balance is exploratory at our sample sizes. Position does not matter: five biased turns anywhere in a 50-turn history produce the same shift. The simplest fix for evaluation pipelines is a fresh context per item; when batching is unavoidable, balancing the history helps.

AI.tldr: AMEL biases LLM judgments through accumulated conversation polarity; negative histories bias more; effect persists across model types and context length; mitigated by fresh context or balanced batching.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether prior conversation history biases LLM evaluation judgments and its implications for using LLMs as automated evaluators.
AI.method: Analyzed 75,898 API calls across 11 models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source; tested identical items in isolation vs after histories biased positive/negative; quantified shift in judgments; examined entropy, number of turns, position; tested scaling across models; three follow-ups on mechanism.
AI.result: Overall effect d = -0.17; negative histories yield 1.62x more bias than positive; larger effect for high-entropy items (d = -0.34) than deterministic (d = -0.15); bias does not grow with 5 vs 50 turns; effect persists across scale (Haiku to Opus; Nano to GPT-5.2); negativity asymmetry with token-level and semantic components; position in history does not matter.
AI.conclusion: To mitigate, use fresh context per item or balance history when batching; scaling alone won’t solve AMEL; more work needed to fully understand token/semantic mechanisms.

title: Event-Illumination Collaborative Low-light Image Enhancement with a High-resolution Real-world Dataset
summary: Event-based low-light image enhancement (LIE) methods mainly focus on incorporating high dynamic range (HDR) information from events while overlooking the essential global illumination in images and the inherent noise sensitivity of event signals in real-world scenarios. To address these issues, we propose EIC-LIE, an event-illumination collaborative LIE framework. Concretely, we first design an Event-Illumination Collaborative Interaction (EICI) module, which contains two key processes: forward gathering, which gathers HDR features across varying lighting conditions, and backward injection, which provides complementary content for illumination and event representations. Next, we introduce an Illumination-aware Event Filter (IAEF) that dynamically reduces event noise based on brightness statistics derived from images. Additionally, we build a beam-splitter-based hybrid imaging system to collect high-quality event-image pairs with temporal synchronization from dynamic scenes, providing the first high-resolution, real-world event-based LIE dataset. Extensive experiments show that our EIC-LIE outperforms state-of-the-art methods on five real-world and synthetic datasets, significantly surpassing previous methods with improvements of up to 1.24dB in PSNR and 0.069 in SSIM. The code and dataset are released at https://github.com/QUEAHREN/EIC-LIE.

AI.tldr: Proposes EIC-LIE, an event-illumination collaborative framework for low-light image enhancement that integrates an Event-Illumination Collaborative Interaction (EICI) module and an Illumination-aware Event Filter (IAEF), plus a beam-splitter-based imaging system to collect aligned event-image data. Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on real-world and synthetic datasets and releases code and data.
AI.motivation: Current event-based LIE methods emphasize HDR information from events but neglect global illumination cues and are sensitive to event noise in real-world scenes. There is a need for integrated handling of illumination content and noise, as well as high-quality real-world datasets for event-based LIE.
AI.method: Introduce EICI with two sub-processes: forward gathering (accumulate HDR features across varying lighting) and backward injection (provide complementary content for illumination and event representations). Add Illumination-aware Event Filter (IAEF) to dynamically suppress event noise using brightness statistics from images. Build a beam-splitter-based hybrid imaging system to capture synchronized high-resolution event-image pairs, enabling a real-world LIE dataset. Train and evaluate on five real-world and synthetic datasets, comparing against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.result: The method outperforms existing approaches on five datasets, with improvements up to 1.24 dB in PSNR and 0.069 in SSIM. Code and dataset are released at the provided repository URL.
AI.conclusion: EIC-LIE effectively leverages event-illumination collaboration and noise-aware processing to enhance low-light images in dynamic scenes and provides a high-quality real-world dataset for the community.

title: An Improved Adaptive PID Optimizer with Enhanced Convergence and Stability for Deep Learning
summary:
Optimization is essential in deep learning. The foundational method upon which most optimizers are built is momentum-based stochastic gradient descent. However, it suffers from two key drawbacks. First, it has noisy and varying gradients, and second, it has an overshoot phenomenon. To address noisy gradients, Adam was proposed, which remains the most widely used adaptive optimizer. To address the overshoot phenomenon, a control-theory-based PID optimizer was proposed. To tackle both the limitations within a single framework, several variants of Adaptive PID (AdaPID) have recently been proposed.
  Although AdaPID performs well, it still inherits two critical drawbacks from Adam, namely convergence and stability issues. In this work, we address both these limitations. To fix the convergence issue, we uniquely integrate the idea of using a non-increasing effective learning rate into AdaPID (originally proposed in AMSGrad, an extension of Adam). To fix the stability issue, we innovatively integrate a gradient difference based modulation factor into AdaPID (originally proposed in DiffGrad, another extension of Adam). Combining both these ideas in AdaPID, results in our novel IAdaPID-ADG optimizer.
  We evaluate our proposed optimizer on multiple datasets, including benchmark datasets (MNIST and CIFAR10) and real-world datasets (IARC and AnnoCerv). The IAdaPID-ADG substantially outperforms all competing optimizers. Additionally, we perform an ablation study on the MNIST dataset to demonstrate the contribution of each added component.
