This work contributes to the field by first studying which characteristics of a score are most relevant for search and by defining a systematic method to build query datasets from any annotated corpus. We also consider diverse methods for content-based search on music score images, ranging from transcription-based approaches relying on Optical Music Recognition (OMR), to a transcription-free Transformer model trained to recognize queries directly from score images, and a text-prompted Large Language Model. Our experiments evaluate these models on four corpora exhibiting diverse characteristics in terms of dataset size, image quality, and typesetting mechanisms. Overall, each method excels under different conditions: OMR-based pipelines achieve higher in-domain retrieval, whereas transcription-free models handle domain variability more effectively.

AI.tldr: This work compares content-based search methods for music score images, including OMR-based pipelines, transcription-free transformers, and text-prompted LLMs, and proposes a dataset-building approach from annotated corpora.
AI.motivation: Content-based search for score images is underexplored compared to text metadata, hindering retrieval and accessibility of digitized scores.
AI.method: Define a systematic method to construct query datasets from annotated corpora; evaluate multiple approaches (OMR-based, transcription-free Transformer, and text-prompted LLM) on four diverse corpora varying in size, image quality, and typesetting.
AI.result: No single method dominates; OMR-based pipelines yield higher in-domain retrieval while transcription-free models show better robustness to domain variability across different corpora.
AI.conclusion: For robust music score retrieval, leverage OMR-based approaches when in-domain data is available and conditions are favorable; employ transcription-free models for cross-domain generalization; a standardized pipeline for building query datasets is feasible and valuable.

title: Can Breath Biomarkers Causally Influence Blood Glucose? Investigating VOC-Mediated Modulation in Diabetes
summary: Diabetes is a global health burden, and early detection is critical for timely intervention. This study explores a non-invasive, data-driven framework to identify individuals at risk of diabetes using Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) and lifestyle variables. We use causal inference techniques to estimate the impact of VOCs such as acetone, isopropanol, isoprene, and ethanol on blood glucose levels. Additionally, we designed a classifier to distinguish diabetics from non-diabetics using non-invasive markers. We created a risk-based ranking system for individuals in the "gray zone," and identified natural clusters in the population using Gaussian Mixture Model. Our results suggest that specific VOCs exhibit a strong causal influence on glucose levels and that machine learning models can reliably classify and stratify individuals at high risk. This integrated causal-explainable analysis can support the development of tool for non-invasive early screening of diabetes.

AI.tldr: Non-invasive VOC-based framework using causal inference and ML to detect, classify, and risk-stratify diabetes, enabling early screening.
AI.motivation: Diabetes imposes a global burden; need for non-invasive, explainable tools leveraging VOCs and lifestyle data to identify at-risk individuals.
AI.method: Estimate causal effects of VOCs (acetone, isopropanol, isoprene, ethanol) on blood glucose; train classifier on non-invasive markers to separate diabetics from non-diabetics; develop gray-zone risk ranking; apply Gaussian Mixture Model to reveal natural clusters.
AI.result: Some VOCs show strong causal influence on glucose; ML classifier reliably distinguishes diabetics; risk ranking and clustering enable stratification of high-risk individuals; demonstrates potential for non-invasive early screening.
AI.conclusion: An integrated causal–explainable framework supports development of non-invasive early screening tools for diabetes.

title: MuKV: Multi-Grained KV Cache Compression for Long Streaming Video Question-Answering
comment: To appear at CVPR'26. Code is available at https://github.com/IMBALDY/MuKV
summary: Long streaming video QA remains challenging due to growing visual tokens and limited reasoning length of large language models (LLMs). KV-caching stores the Key-Value (KV) of the historical tokens via LLM prefill and enables more efficient streaming QA. However, existing methods cache every one or two frames, causing redundant memory usage and losing fine-grained spatial details within frame or temporal contexts across frames. This paper proposes MuKV, a method that features a multi-grained KV cache compression module and a semi-hierarchical retrieval approach to improve both efficiency and accuracy for long streaming VideoQA. For the offline KV cache, MuKV extracts visual representations at patch-, frame-, and segment-levels. The multiple levels of granularity preserve both local cues and global temporal context, while maintaining efficiency with a dual signal token compression mechanism guided by self-attention and frequency. For online QA, MuKV designs a semi-hierarchical retrieval method to retrieve relevant KV caches for answer generation. Experiments on long-streaming VideoQA benchmarks show that MuKV significantly improves answer accuracy, without sacrificing memory and online QA efficiency. Moreover, our compression mechanism alone brings consistent benefits across answer accuracy, memory, and QA efficiency over baselines, showcasing highly effective contribution.

AI.tldr: MuKV introduces multi-grained KV cache compression and semi-hierarchical retrieval to enhance long streaming VideoQA, balancing memory efficiency with fine-grained spatial-temporal detail.
AI.motivation: Long streaming VideoQA suffers from growing visual tokens and limited LLM reasoning length; caching every frame is wasteful and degrades fine-grained temporal/spatial information.
AI.method: Offline KV cache: extract patch-, frame-, and segment-level visual representations; dual-signal token compression guided by self-attention and frequency. Online QA: semi-hierarchical retrieval to fetch relevant KV caches. Evaluated on long-streaming VideoQA benchmarks.
AI.result: Significant improvements in answer accuracy without sacrificing memory or online QA efficiency. The proposed compression mechanism yields consistent gains across accuracy, memory, and QA efficiency compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: MuKV demonstrates effective multi-granularity KV caching and semi-hierarchical retrieval to enable accurate and efficient long streaming VideoQA, with compression driving robust performance gains.

title: Aerodynamic force reconstruction using physics-informed Gaussian processes
summary: Accurate modeling of aerodynamic loads is essential for understanding and predicting the responses of complex structural systems. However, these models often rely on simplifications of the true physical forces, introducing assumptions that can limit their accuracy. Validating such models becomes particularly challenging in the presence of noisy or incomplete data. To address this, we introduce a probabilistic physics-informed machine learning approach designed to reconstruct the underlying aerodynamic loads from noisy measurements of structural dynamic responses. The model avoids overfitting, eliminates the need for regularization schemes, and allows for the use of heterogeneous and multi-fidelity data during the training process. The efficacy of the approach is demonstrated through the reconstruction of aerodynamic loads on the Great Belt East Bridge, simulated under a linear unsteady assumption. Results show a strong agreement between true and predicted loads, particularly related to root mean squared errors, magnitude, phase angle and peak values of the signals. The method for load reconstructing holds broad applicability, such as modeling validation, future load estimation, and structural damage prognosis.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic physics-informed ML framework to reconstruct aerodynamic loads from noisy structural responses, avoiding overfitting, supporting heterogeneous/multi-fidelity data, validated on the Great Belt East Bridge under linear unsteady assumptions, with strong agreement in RMSE, magnitude, phase, and peak values; applicable to validation, future load estimation, and damage prognosis.
AI.motivation: Accurately inferring aerodynamic loads is difficult due to simplifications in load models and noise/incompleteness in measurements; robust validation and load estimation require methods that fuse data while respecting physics.
AI.method: A probabilistic physics-informed machine learning approach that infers underlying aerodynamic loads from measured structural dynamic responses. The model leverages physical constraints, handles noisy data, does not require regularization, and can fuse heterogeneous and multi-fidelity datasets during training.
AI.result: Applied to the Great Belt East Bridge under a linear unsteady assumption, the method reconstructs aerodynamic loads with strong agreement to the true loads, delivering favorable RMSE, magnitude, phase angle, and peak-value metrics.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers broad applicability for modeling validation, future load estimation, and structural damage prognosis, while mitigating overfitting and avoiding regularization, and accommodating diverse data sources.

title: Exposing Vulnerabilities in Visible-Infrared VLMs: A Unified Geometric Adversarial Framework with Cross-Task Transferability
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong performance across diverse multimodal tasks, but their adversarial robustness in visible-infrared (VIS-IR) scenarios remains underexplored. This gap is critical because VIS-IR sensing is widely used in real-world perception systems to support reliable understanding under challenging imaging conditions. To address this cross-modal threat setting, we propose CFGPatch, a curved-edge fractal geometric adversarial patch framework for attacking VIS-IR VLMs. CFGPatch builds on triangular fractal geometry and replaces rigid straight-edged primitives with Bezier-curved elements, preserving multi-scale fractal self-similarity while introducing smoother contours, richer directional variation, and more flexible shape deformation. In addition, we design a modality-specific Fraser-spiral rendering mechanism to inject fine-grained texture distortions and misleading perceptual cues into visible and infrared images. By coupling global curved-fractal geometry with local spiral-based appearance interference, CFGPatch disrupts both shape perception and texture interpretation. We further adopt expectation over transformation (EOT) to improve robustness against common image-level transformations. Extensive experiments show that CFGPatch effectively fools VIS-IR VLMs and consistently outperforms standard patch baselines in attack effectiveness and robustness. Moreover, adversarial samples optimized for zero-shot classification transfer well to image captioning and visual question answering, demonstrating strong cross-task transferability and generalizability across downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: We present CFGPatch, a curved-edge fractal adversarial patch framework for VIS-IR vision-language models (VLMs). It combines Bezier-curved fractal geometry with Fraser-spiral texture rendering and uses Expectation Over Transformation (EOT) to achieve state-of-the-art attack effectiveness and strong cross-task transfer to image captioning and VQA.
AI.motivation: Visual-language models in visible-infrared (VIS-IR) sensing are underexplored regarding adversarial robustness. VIS-IR perception is critical in real-world conditions, and cross-modal threats can degrade reliability. The work aims to understand and improve attack strategies in this cross-modal setting and assess cross-task transferability.
AI.method: CFGPatch constructs curved-edge fractal patches by replacing straight primitives with Bezier-curved elements that preserve multi-scale fractal self-similarity (triangular fractal geometry). It introduces Fraser-spiral rendering to inject fine-grained texture distortions in both visible and infrared channels. The global curved-fractal patch is coupled with local spiral-based appearance interference, and EOT is employed to enhance robustness to image-level transformations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show CFGPatch outperforms standard patch baselines in attack effectiveness and robustness on VIS-IR VLMs. Patches optimized for zero-shot classification also transfer well to downstream tasks, including image captioning and visual question answering (VQA).
AI.conclusion: CFGPatch reveals a potent cross-modal adversarial vulnerability in VIS-IR VLMs and demonstrates notable cross-task generalizability, suggesting both a valuable attack framework and a need for robust defenses in multimodal VIS-IR systems.

title: Short-Term-to-Long-Term Memory Transfer for Knowledge Graphs under Partial Observability
summary: Reinforcement learning under partial observability requires deciding what information to retain, yet most memory-based approaches do not explicitly model short-term-to-long-term transfer of symbolic observations. We study this transfer process in a temporal knowledge-graph memory setting and cast it as a neuro-symbolic value-based decision problem: for each observed triple, the agent chooses whether to keep or drop it before long-term insertion. To handle variable-sized short-term buffers, we use a per-item Q-learning design with shared parameters and a practical temporal-difference update over matched items across consecutive steps. On the RoomKG benchmark at long-term memory capacity 128, learned transfer decisions outperform symbolic and neural baselines, including symbolic baselines with temporal annotations and history-based LSTM/Transformer baselines. Across transfer-policy ablations, a lightweight local short-term-only variant performs best, and step-level behavior shows that the policy keeps navigation- and query-relevant facts while discarding lower-value candidate facts, supporting explicit and interpretable memory decisions under memory constraints.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic memory-management approach for RL under partial observability uses per-item Q-learning to decide which temporal knowledge-graph triples to keep, improving long-term retrieval and performance under memory constraints on RoomKG.
AI.motivation: RL with partial observability requires deciding what information to retain; existing memory models lack explicit short-term-to-long-term transfer of symbolic observations. This work models that transfer in a temporal knowledge-graph memory as a decision problem.
AI.method: Per-item Q-learning with shared parameters and TD updates over matched items across consecutive steps to handle variable-sized short-term buffers; evaluation on RoomKG with memory capacity 128; comparisons against symbolic and neural baselines, including baselines with temporal annotations and LSTM/Transformer histories; ablations including a lightweight local short-term-only variant.
AI.result: Learned transfer decisions outperform symbolic and neural baselines; the local short-term-only variant performs best among transfer-policy ablations; step-level analysis shows the policy preserves navigation- and query-relevant facts while discarding lower-value candidates, enabling interpretable memory decisions under constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed neuro-symbolic memory-management approach yields explicit, interpretable control over memory under capacity limits and improves long-term decision-making in partially observable RL on RoomKG.

title: Detection of Virus and Small Cell Patches in Foci Images Using Switchable Convolution and Feature Pyramid Networks
summary: Accurate detection and counting of virus patches in focus-forming unit (FFU) images, also known as foci images, are important for quantifying viral infection and analyzing cellular structures. This task is challenging because biomedical targets often vary substantially in size, density, contrast, and shape. In this paper, we propose an enhanced YOLOv2-based detector that integrates a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) to improve multi-scale feature representation. We also incorporate a switchable atrous convolution mechanism to adapt the receptive field for fine-grained targets in dense microscopy images. The proposed method is evaluated on biomedical foci image datasets for virus patch and small cell patch detection. For small cell patch detection, the model achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 40.5% at a 25% Intersection over Union (IoU) threshold. For FFU virus patch detection, the model achieves an mAP of 68%. These results indicate that combining FPN-based feature fusion with switchable convolution improves the suitability of YOLOv2 for specialized biomedical object detection tasks

AI.tldr: An enhanced YOLOv2 detector with a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) and switchable atrous convolution improves detection of virus patches and small cell patches in FFU images, achieving 68% mAP for virus patches and 40.5% mAP for small cell patches at IoU 0.25.
AI.motivation: Biomedical focus-forming unit (FFU) images exhibit large variability in patch size, density, contrast, and shape, making accurate detection and counting challenging. Standard detectors struggle with multi-scale and dense targets.
AI.method: Extend YOLOv2 with an FPN to enhance multi-scale feature representation and integrate switchable atrous convolution to adapt the receptive field for fine-grained, dense targets. Evaluate on FFU datasets for virus patches and small cell patches.
AI.result: For small cell patch detection, mAP = 40.5% at IoU = 0.25. For FFU virus patch detection, mAP = 68%. These results suggest that FPN-based feature fusion combined with switchable convolution improves YOLOv2's suitability for specialized biomedical detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: Combining FPN-based fusion with switchable convolution enhances detection performance of YOLOv2 in challenging biomedical microscopy images, particularly for multi-scale and dense targets such as virus patches and small cell patches.

title: Algebraic Machine Learning for Small-to-Medium Datasets Is Competitive against Strong Standard Baselines
summary: Symbolic methods are generally not considered competitive with strong modern learners on realistic supervised tasks. We evaluate Algebraic Machine Learning (AML), a framework that learns through subdirect decomposition of algebraic structure rather than numerical optimization, against standard baselines on image and tabular classification across varying training-set sizes. We find that AML trained only on training data without using validation or cross-validation outperforms a family of cross-validated baseline methods including CNNs on small to medium image datasets (50--2000 training examples). On tabular datasets in the same size range, XGBoost is overall the best performing method, but AML is nonetheless comparable to methods incorporating task-specific biases such as LightGBM and random forests. AML achieves this competitive performance across two very different types of datasets using a generic algebraic inductive bias, rather than the modality-specific biases built into standard baselines like CNNs for images or XGBoost for tabular data, and requires no cross validation because it has no task-dependent hyperparameters to tune.

AI.tldr: AML uses algebraic substructure learning to compete with strong learners without task-specific hyperparameters; outperforms cross-validated baselines on small-to-medium image datasets and remains competitive on tabular data where XGBoost leads.
AI.motivation: Test whether algebraic machine learning (AML) with a generic algebraic inductive bias can match or exceed modern supervised learners across modalities and data-sizes, without cross-validation or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Evaluate AML against standard baselines (CNNs for images; XGBoost, LightGBM, random forests for tabular data) across varying training-set sizes (50–2000 examples). AML uses subdirect decomposition of algebraic structure and requires no task-dependent hyperparameters.
AI.result: AML trained only on training data without validation outperforms cross-validated baselines including CNNs on small-to-medium image datasets (50–2000 examples). On tabular datasets, XGBoost is generally best, but AML is comparable to LightGBM and random forests; AML achieves this with a generic algebraic inductive bias and no cross-validation.
AI.conclusion: Algebraic Methods like AML can be competitive with strong baselines across diverse data regimes using a generic inductive bias, offering performance without task-specific hyperparameter tuning or cross-validation.

title: PIU: Proximity-guided Identity Unlearning in ID-Conditioned Diffusion Models
summary: Identity-conditioned diffusion models enable high-quality and identity-consistent face generation, but they also raise severe privacy concerns, as models may continue to synthesize individuals despite their right to be forgotten. While machine unlearning has been extensively studied for concept and data removal, identity unlearning remains largely unexplored, particularly in models conditioned directly on identity embeddings rather than text prompts. In this work, we study identity unlearning in Arc2Face, a state-of-the-art identity-conditioned latent diffusion model for face generation, and introduce Proximity-guided Identity Unlearning (PIU), an anchor-guided framework for identity unlearning. Specifically, we formulate identity removal as an identity replacement objective that reassigns the source identity to a selected anchor identity in the learned identity space, and we complement it with a proximity-based anchor selection strategy motivated by the geometry of ArcFace representations. We further show that effective unlearning can be achieved through localized fine-tuning of a small subset of identity-sensitive cross-attention layers. Experiments across many target identities show that our framework effectively suppresses generation of the target identity while preserving realism and identity consistency for retained identities, as validated by improved performance on unlearning and image-quality metrics, together with qualitative evaluation. The source code for the PIU framework is publicly available at https://github.com/edgarcancinoe/piu_unlearning .

AI.tldr: PIU introduces Proximity-guided Identity Unlearning for Arc2Face by replacing a target identity with an anchor identity using ArcFace-aware proximity, achieved through localized fine-tuning of identity-sensitive cross-attention layers to suppress the target while preserving other identities and image quality.
AI.motivation: Identity unlearning in identity-conditioned diffusion models is essential for privacy (right to be forgotten). Prior work focuses on concept/data unlearning or text-conditioned models, leaving identity embeddings largely unexplored; a practical method is needed to remove specific identities without harming other identities or realism.
AI.method: Proximity-guided Identity Unlearning (PIU) formulates identity removal as an identity replacement objective that reassigns the source identity to a selected anchor identity in the learned identity space. It uses a proximity-based anchor selection strategy leveraging ArcFace geometry and performs localized fine-tuning on a small subset of identity-sensitive cross-attention layers in Arc2Face.
AI.result: Empirically, PIU effectively suppresses generation of target identities while preserving realism and identity consistency for retained identities. It improves unlearning and image-quality metrics and is validated through qualitative and quantitative evaluation across many target identities. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: PIU provides a practical, annotation-efficient framework for identity unlearning in identity-conditioned diffusion models, enabling targeted identity removal with minimal impact on other identities and image quality; it complements privacy-preserving ML by enabling identity unlearning in Arc2Face-like systems.

title: One-Way Policy Optimization for Self-Evolving LLMs
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a promising paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, the sparsity of binary verifier rewards often leads to low efficiency and optimization instability. To stabilize training, existing methods typically impose token-level constraints relative to a reference policy. We identify that such constraints penalize deviations indiscriminately; this can flip verifier-determined direction when the policy attempts to outperform the reference, thereby suppressing gains. To resolve this, we propose One-Way Policy Optimization (OWPO), a method based on the principle of decoupling optimization direction from update magnitude. In OWPO, the verifier dictates the update direction, while the reference policy serves only to adjust the magnitude. Specifically, OWPO applies asymmetric reweighting: it performs Accelerated Alignment for inferior deviations (where the policy lags behind the reference) and Gain Locking for superior deviations (where the policy surpasses the reference). Furthermore, by incorporating iterative reference updates, OWPO creates a ``Ratchet Effect'' that continuously consolidates gains. Experimental results demonstrate that OWPO outperforms strong baselines, including DAPO, OPD, and MOPD, breaking the bottleneck of fixed priors to enable continuous self-evolution without reliance on external reference models.

AI.tldr: One-Way Policy Optimization (OWPO) for RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) decouples update direction from magnitude using asymmetric reweighting, yielding improved stability and gains over DAPO/OPD/MOPD and enabling self-evolution via iterative reference updates (Ratchet Effect).
AI.motivation: Binary verifier rewards are sparse; token-level constraints relative to a reference policy can indiscriminately penalize beneficial deviations and suppress gains, hindering efficiency and learning stability.
AI.method: OWPO propagates the verifier's suggested update direction while using a reference policy to scale the update magnitude. It uses asymmetric reweighting: Accelerated Alignment for inferior deviations and Gain Locking for superior deviations. It includes iterative reference updates to create a Ratchet Effect that consolidates gains across iterations.
AI.result: OWPO outperforms strong baselines (DAPO, OPD, MOPD) on RLVR tasks, breaking the bottleneck of fixed priors and enabling continuous self-evolution without external references.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling direction and magnitude via OWPO with ratcheting yields stable, progressive improvements in RLVR, offering a robust alternative to traditional constraint-based methods.

title: Robustness of breast lesion segmentation under MRI undersampling improves with k-space-aware deep learning
summary:
Purpose: To assess whether breast lesion segmentation can be learned directly from acquired MRI k-space, and whether doing so improves robustness when data are accelerated or noisy.
  Materials and Methods: This retrospective study used public breast dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI (DCE-MRI) datasets with acquired and synthetic k-space, together with a within-dataset synthetic control. We compared four 3D U-Net variants: a hybrid k-space-to-image model, a native k-space model, and magnitude and complex image-space baselines. Models were evaluated under increasing undersampling and added complex Gaussian k-space noise. The primary outcome was patient-level Dice similarity coefficient under cross-validation, with the hybrid model prespecified as the main comparison against the magnitude image-space baseline.
  Results: At full sampling, the hybrid and image-space models performed similarly. As acceleration increased, the hybrid model retained substantially more segmentation accuracy and significantly outperformed the magnitude image-space baseline across moderate to high undersampling levels. The same pattern was observed when noise was added directly to k-space: the hybrid model degraded more slowly, whereas the image-space baseline failed under heavier noise. This advantage was reproduced in the within-dataset synthetic control. Feature analysis suggested that the k-space stage and image-space stage played complementary roles, with frequency-domain filtering concentrated before image-domain lesion localization.
  Conclusion: K-space-aware deep learning improves the robustness of breast lesion segmentation under MRI undersampling and k-space noise, while matching image-space methods at full sampling.

AI.tldr: A k-space–aware 3D U-Net enhances breast lesion segmentation robustness under undersampling and k-space noise, while matching performance at full sampling.
AI.motivation: Learning segmentation directly from k-space can improve robustness to MRI undersampling and noise by exploiting frequency-domain information before image reconstruction.
AI.method: Retrospective study on public breast DCE-MRI datasets with both acquired and synthetic k-space. Compared four 3D U-Net variants: a hybrid k-space-to-image model, a native k-space model, and magnitude and complex image-space baselines. Evaluation under increasing undersampling and added complex Gaussian k-space noise. Primary outcome: patient-level Dice with cross-validation; hybrid model preset as main comparison against magnitude image-space baseline. Included a within-dataset synthetic control. Image-domain and frequency-domain stages analyzed for complementary roles.
AI.result: Under full sampling, hybrid and image-space models perform similarly. With higher undersampling, the hybrid model retains substantially more segmentation accuracy and outperforms the magnitude image-space baseline across moderate to high undersampling. When noise is added to k-space, the hybrid model degrades more slowly; the image-space baseline fails under heavier noise. Synthetic control replicated results. Feature analysis suggests complementary roles of k-space and image-space stages, with frequency-domain filtering placed before image-domain localization.
AI.conclusion: K-space–aware deep learning boosts robustness of breast lesion segmentation under undersampling and k-space noise, while matching image-space methods at full sampling.

title: 3D LULC classification using multispectral LiDAR and deep learning: current and prospective schemes
summary: Land Use Land Cover (LULC) classification is essential for national 3D mapping, geospatial analysis, and sustainable planning. Multispectral (MS) LiDAR provides synchronized spatial-spectral information, and deep learning (DL) enables 3D point cloud semantic segmentation; however, adoption is limited by the lack of publicly available urban and suburban MS LiDAR datasets aligned with National Mapping and Cadastral Agencies (NMCAs) classification schemes. This study addresses these gaps by introducing L1 and L2 NMCA-aligned LULC classification schemes and a new benchmark MS LiDAR dataset. We evaluate seven state-of-the-art DL models and perform spectral ablation studies at both levels of detail. Results show that Point Transformer V3 achieves the best performance, with mIoU of 79.4% (L1, 8 classes) and 58.9% (L2, 20 classes) using a dual-wavelength LiDAR system (532 nm and 1064 nm). Ablation results show that multispectral information improves performance over geometry-only inputs, with gains of 1.1 percentage points at L1 and 7.8 points at L2. These results highlight the value of LiDAR reflectance for fine-grained material discrimination and support the evolution of NMCA LULC schemes toward higher semantic detail. The Loosdorf-MSL dataset contributes a new benchmark for consistent national and international LULC mapping.

AI.tldr: Introduces NMCA-aligned LULC schemes and a new MS LiDAR benchmark dataset (Loosdorf-MSL) for urban/suburban areas, tests seven DL models on dual-wavelength data, with Point Transformer V3 achieving top mIoU (L1: 79.4%, L2: 58.9%), and shows spectral data improves accuracy, especially at finer detail (L2).
AI.motivation: To address the lack of publicly available, NMCA-aligned MS LiDAR datasets for LULC and to provide a standardized benchmark enabling consistent national/international mapping.
AI.method: Two-level LULC labeling (L1: 8 classes, L2: 20 classes) aligned with NMCA schemes. Use dual-wavelength MS LiDAR (532 nm and 1064 nm). Evaluate seven state-of-the-art DL models with spectral ablation studies to quantify the contribution of reflectance vs geometry.
AI.result: Point Transformer V3 yields the best performance: mIoU 79.4% (L1, 8 classes) and 58.9% (L2, 20 classes). Spectral information provides gains of 1.1 percentage points at L1 and 7.8 points at L2 over geometry-only inputs. The Loosdorf-MSL dataset serves as a new benchmark for national/international LULC mapping.
AI.conclusion: Multispectral LiDAR reflectance significantly enhances fine-grained LULC discrimination and supports advancing NMCA schemes toward higher semantic detail; Loosdorf-MSL provides a valuable, standardized benchmark for LULC mapping.

title: Maestro: Reinforcement Learning to Orchestrate Hierarchical Model-Skill Ensembles
summary: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) and modular skills has endowed autonomous agents with increasingly powerful capabilities. Existing frameworks typically rely on monolithic LLMs and fixed logic to interface with these skills. This gives rise to a critical bottleneck: different LLMs offer distinct advantages across diverse domains, yet current frameworks fail to exploit the complementary strengths of models and skills, thereby limiting their performance on downstream tasks. In this paper, we present Maestro (Multimodal Agent for Expert-Skill Targeted Reinforced Orchestration), a Reinforcement Learning (RL)-driven orchestration framework that reframes heterogeneous multimodal tasks as a sequential decision-making process over a hierarchical model-skill registry. Rather than consolidating all knowledge into a single model, Maestro trains a lightweight policy to dynamically compose ensembles of frozen expert models and a two-tier skill library, deciding at each step whether to invoke an external expert, which model-skill pair to select, and when to terminate. The policy is optimized via outcome-based RL, requiring no step-level supervision. We evaluate Maestro across ten representative multimodal benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, chart understanding, high-resolution perception, and domain-specific analysis. With only a 4B orchestrator, Maestro achieves an average accuracy of 70.1%, surpassing both GPT-5 (69.3%) and Gemini-2.5-Pro (68.7%). Crucially, the learned coordination policy generalizes to unseen models and skills without retraining: augmenting the registry with out-of-domain experts yields a 59.5% average on four challenging benchmarks, outperforming all closed-source baselines. Maestro further maintains high computational efficiency with low latency. The source code is available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/Maestro.

AI.tldr: Maestro proposes an RL-driven orchestration framework that dynamically composes frozen expert models and a two-tier skill library to tackle heterogeneous multimodal tasks, achieving strong performance and generalization with a lightweight 4B orchestrator.
AI.motivation: Current frameworks rely on monolithic LLMs and fixed interfaces, missing the complementary strengths of diverse models/skills across domains; a flexible, outcome-based RL policy can optimally allocate tasks to specialized systems.
AI.method: Maestro models the problem as sequential decision-making over a hierarchical registry of frozen expert models and pooled skills. A lightweight policy learns to (i) invoke external experts, (ii) select a model-skill pair, and (iii) terminate, using outcome-based reinforcement learning that requires no step-level supervision.
AI.result: On ten multimodal benchmarks, Maestro with a 4B orchestrator attains 70.1% average accuracy, surpassing GPT-5 (69.3%) and Gemini-2.5-Pro (68.7%). The coordination policy generalizes to unseen models/skills, with registry augmentation yielding 59.5% average on four hard benchmarks, outperforming all closed-source baselines. It also maintains low latency.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, RL-driven orchestration over a registry of frozen experts and skills is effective for multimodal tasks, enabling strong performance and cross-domain generalization without retraining the large model, and is computationally efficient.

title: 4D-GSW: Kinematic-Aware Spatio-Temporal Consistent Watermarking for 4D Gaussian Splatting
comment: 9 pages main paper, 7 figures, 18 pages in total
summary: While 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has revolutionized high-fidelity dynamic reconstruction, safeguarding the intellectual property of these assets remains an open challenge. Conventional steganographic techniques often neglect the underlying kinematic manifolds, triggering non-physical artifacts such as severe temporal flickering and "FVD collapse". To address this, we propose \textbf{4D-GSW}, a kinematic-aware watermarking framework designed to embed robust copyright information while preserving high spatio-temporal consistency. Unlike prior 4D steganography that primarily focuses on opacity-guided invisibility, our approach explicitly addresses the physical coherence of motion trajectories. We introduce a \textbf{Spatio-Temporal Curvature (STC)} metric to identify "Dynamic Instants," adaptively gating watermark gradient injection to shield critical motion manifolds from non-physical perturbations. To ensure global coherence across complex deformations, we formulate a joint \textbf{HMM-MRF energy minimization} model that synchronizes watermark phases within both temporal trajectories and spatial neighborhoods. Furthermore, an \textbf{anisotropic gradient routing} mechanism ensures that watermark embedding remains strictly decoupled from photometric reconstruction fidelity. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the superior performance of our method in robustly hiding watermarks while resisting various attacks and maintaining high rendering quality and spatiotemporal consistency.

AI.tldr: A kinematic-aware watermarking framework, 4D-GSW, for 4D Gaussian Splatting that embeds robust copyright data without compromising motion coherence, leveraging STC-based dynamic-instants gating, HMM-MRF energy minimization for global phase synchronization, and anisotropic gradient routing to decouple watermark from photometric fidelity.
AI.motivation: Protect intellectual property of 4D dynamic assets (4D Gaussian Splatting) while avoiding non-physical artifacts caused by conventional steganography that neglect motion manifolds, such as temporal flickering and FVD collapse.
AI.method: Define Spatio-Temporal Curvature (STC) to identify Dynamic Instants and gate watermark gradient injection to protect critical motion manifolds. Use a joint HMM-MRF energy minimization to synchronize watermark phases across temporal trajectories and spatial neighborhoods for global coherence. Employ anisotropic gradient routing to ensure watermark embedding remains decoupled from photometric reconstruction fidelity.
AI.result: Experiments show robust watermark hiding under attacks while preserving rendering quality and spatiotemporal consistency; the method outperforms baselines by maintaining motion coherence and resisting degradation from steganographic perturbations.
AI.conclusion: 4D-GSW provides a kinematic-aware watermarking solution for 4D Gaussian Splatting that preserves physical coherence of motion while embedding robust copyright data, with STC gating, HMM-MRF synchronization, and anisotropic gradient routing as core components.

title: IKNO: Infinite-order Kernel Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have achieved significant success in modern scientific computing due to their flexibility and strong generalization capabilities. Existing models, however, primarily rely on first-order kernel integral approximations, which severely limit their expressivity. To address this, we propose the Infinite-order Kernel Neural Operator (IKNO), which constructs neural operators via infinite-order kernel integrals and admits an elegant closed-form finite approximation. We develop two complementary infinite-order neural operator constructions: IKNO-Vanilla, which applies the full-kernel resolvent on the product grid via Kronecker eigendecomposition, and IKNO-TP, an alternative tensor-product operator that composes per-axis resolvents. Furthermore, we develop fast computation schemes for both variants of IKNO, which achieve outstanding global information aggregation while maintaining high computational efficiency. Empirically, we evaluate our IKNO on both time-dependent and time-independent benchmarks with arbitrary input shapes, including large-scale industrial datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the IKNO method consistently achieves the SOTA accuracy with significant improvements on nearly all benchmark datasets while maintaining scalability to very large point clouds.

AI.tldr: IKNO introduces infinite-order kernel neural operators to overcome the expressivity limits of first-order kernel approximations, with two concrete variants (IKNO-Vanilla and IKNO-TP) and fast computation schemes, achieving state-of-the-art results on diverse time-dependent/independent benchmarks and scalable to large point clouds.
AI.motivation: Existing neural operators rely on first-order kernel integral approximations, which limit expressivity and performance on complex or irregular input shapes and large-scale data. An infinite-order kernel framework can capture richer interactions and offer better generalization and accuracy across varied discretizations.
AI.method: Develop two infinite-order neural operator constructions: IKNO-Vanilla uses a full-kernel resolvent on a product grid via Kronecker eigendecomposition; IKNO-TP is a tensor-product operator composing per-axis resolvents. Derive a closed-form finite approximation and implement fast computation schemes to enable global information aggregation with high efficiency.
AI.result: Empirically, IKNO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across time-dependent and time-independent benchmarks with arbitrary input shapes, including large-scale industrial datasets, and demonstrates strong scalability to very large point clouds.
AI.conclusion: The infinite-order kernel framework in IKNO provides a powerful, scalable alternative to first-order neural operators, delivering superior expressivity and accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency across diverse datasets and input geometries.

title: Bernini: Latent Semantic Planning for Video Diffusion
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and diffusion models have each reached remarkable maturity: MLLMs excel at reasoning over heterogeneous multimodal inputs with strong semantic grounding, while diffusion models synthesize images and videos with photorealistic fidelity. We argue that these two families can be unified through a simple division of labor: MLLMs perform semantic planning, while diffusion models render pixels from high-level semantic guidance and low-level visual features. Building on this idea, we propose Bernini, a unified framework for video generation and editing. An MLLM-based planner predicts the target semantic representation directly in the ViT embedding space, and a DiT-based renderer synthesizes pixels conditioned on this plan, augmented by text features and, for editing, source VAE features for detail preservation. Because semantics serve as the interface, the planner and renderer can be trained separately and only lightly co-trained, preserving the pretrained strengths of both components while keeping training efficient. To better handle multiple visual inputs, we introduce Segment-Aware 3D Rotary Positional Embedding (SA-3D RoPE), and further incorporate chain-of-thought reasoning in the planner to better transfer understanding into generation. Bernini achieves state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of video generation and editing benchmarks, with the MLLM's pretrained understanding translating into strong generalization on challenging editing tasks.

AI.tldr: Bernini unifies MLLMs and diffusion-based renderers for video generation/editing by using an MLLM planner to produce semantic targets in ViT space and a DiT-based renderer to generate pixels from that plan, with SA-3D RoPE for multi-input handling and chain-of-thought reasoning in planning; achieves state-of-the-art results while allowing modular training.
AI.motivation: To leverage the complementary strengths of multimodal LLMs (semantic reasoning and grounding) and diffusion renderers (photorealistic pixel synthesis) by decoupling planning (semantics) from rendering (pixels), enabling efficient training and strong generalization, especially in editing tasks.
AI.method: A two-component system: (1) an MLLM-based planner that predicts target semantic representations directly in Vision Transformer (ViT) embedding space; (2) a DiT-based renderer that synthesizes pixels conditioned on the semantic plan, with additional text features and, for editing, source VAE features to preserve detail. The framework introduces Segment-Aware 3D Rotary Positional Embedding (SA-3D RoPE) to handle multiple visual inputs, and incorporates chain-of-thought reasoning in the planner to improve transfer of understanding to generation. Training is modular: planner and renderer can be trained separately and lightly co-trained.
AI.result: Bernini achieves state-of-the-art performance across a broad set of video generation and editing benchmarks. The pretrained understanding of the MLLM translates into strong generalization on challenging editing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Semantic interfaces enable modular training of planning and rendering components, preserving pretrained strengths while keeping training efficient. The approach benefits from explicit semantic planning and robust pixel rendering, with SA-3D RoPE and chain-of-thought reasoning enhancing multi-input handling and transfer of understanding to generation.

title: From Sequential Nodes to GPU Batches: Parallel Branch and Bound for Optimal $k$-Sparse GLMs
summary: GPUs have significantly accelerated first-order methods for large-scale optimization, especially in continuous optimization. However, this success has not transferred cleanly to problems with discrete variables, combinatorial structure, and nonlinear objectives, such as certifying optimal solutions for cardinality-constrained generalized linear models. Major challenges include the sequential processing of heterogeneous nodes in branch and bound (BnB) and frequent data movement between the CPU and GPU. We propose a simple, generic, and modular CPU--GPU framework that processes multiple BnB nodes in batches on GPUs. The framework is built around a small set of GPU-efficient routines and uses padding together with lightweight custom kernels to handle irregular node data structures. Experiments show one to two orders of magnitude speedups and zero optimality gap on challenging instances. The framework can also be extended to collect the entire Rashomon set, enabling downstream statistical analysis such as variable-importance analysis and model selection under secondary user-specific measures (e.g., AUC in classification).

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated CPU-GPU batching framework for branch-and-bound in cardinality-constrained generalized linear models; achieves 10x–100x speedups with zero optimality gap; supports Rashomon-set collection for downstream analysis.
AI.motivation: Discrete optimization with branch-and-bound remains CPU-bound due to heterogenous node processing and costly CPU-GPU data transfers. A generic, modular GPU-friendly framework is needed to batch and accelerate BnB nodes.
AI.method: A simple, modular CPU–GPU framework that batches multiple BnB nodes for GPU processing. It relies on a small set of GPU-efficient routines and uses padding with lightweight custom kernels to manage irregular node data structures, enabling efficient handling of discrete optimization on GPUs.
AI.result: Experiments report one to two orders of magnitude speedups (approximately 10x–100x) and zero optimality gap on challenging instances.
AI.conclusion: The framework is simple, generic, and extensible to broader discrete optimization tasks. It can be extended to collect the entire Rashomon set for downstream statistical analyses, such as variable-importance analysis and model selection under user-defined metrics.

title: QuantSR+: Pushing the Limit of Quantized Image Super-Resolution Networks
summary: Low-bit quantization is widely used to compress super-resolution (SR) models and reduce storage and computation costs for deployment on resource-limited devices. However, when SR models are pushed to ultra-low precision (2-4 bits), performance can drop sharply due to diminished representational capacity and the detail-sensitive nature of SR. To address these issues, we propose QuantSR+, a unified framework that improves quantization operators, network design, and training optimization, achieving better trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency than prior low-bit SR methods. QuantSR+ mainly relies on three technical contributions: (1) Redistribution-driven Bit Determination (RBD), which reshapes quantization distributions in both forward and backward passes to preserve representation fidelity; (2) Quantized Slimmable Architecture (QSA), which begins with an over-parameterized model and progressively prunes less critical blocks to meet efficiency budgets while pushing the accuracy performance; and (3) Slimming-guided Function-localized Distillation (SFD), which enforces block-aware feature alignment via a direct loss and a progressive, function-local training schedule to capture quantization effects better and speed up convergence. Extensive experiments show that QuantSR+ achieves state-of-the-art performance against both specialized quantized SR methods and generic quantization approaches. For SwinIR-S on Urban100 (x4), it improves PSNR by 0.29 dB over the 2-bit SOTA baseline. Meanwhile, it delivers strong efficiency gains at 2-bit, reducing operations by up to 87.9% and storage by 89.4%. QuantSR+ is effective for both convolutional and transformer-based SR models, indicating broad applicability.

AI.tldr: A unified low-bit super-resolution quantization framework (QuantSR+) with three contributions— Redistribution-driven Bit Determination (RBD), Quantized Slimmable Architecture (QSA), and Slimming-guided Function-localized Distillation (SFD)—achieves superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs vs. prior low-bit SR methods, with notable PSNR gains and substantial compute/storage reductions for CNN and transformer SR models.
AI.motivation: Ultra-low precision (2-4 bits) SR suffers from reduced representational capacity and detail sensitivity. There is a need to reshape quantization distributions, adapt architecture under budget constraints, and align block-wise features to preserve SR quality during quantization.
AI.method: (1) Redistribution-driven Bit Determination (RBD): reshapes quantization distributions in forward and backward passes to preserve representation fidelity; (2) Quantized Slimmable Architecture (QSA): starts from an over-parameterized model and progressively prunes less critical blocks to meet efficiency budgets while maintaining accuracy; (3) Slimming-guided Function-localized Distillation (SFD): enforces block-aware feature alignment via a direct loss and a progressive, function-local training schedule to capture quantization effects and accelerate convergence.
AI.result: QuantSR+ achieves state-of-the-art performance versus both specialized quantized SR methods and generic quantization approaches. For SwinIR-S on Urban100 (x4), it reports a PSNR gain of 0.29 dB over the 2-bit SOTA baseline. At 2-bit, it yields up to 87.9% fewer operations and 89.4% less storage. The approach is effective for both convolutional and transformer-based SR models.
AI.conclusion: QuantSR+ provides a broadly applicable, high-accuracy, high-efficiency solution for ultra-low-bit SR quantization, delivering strong accuracy gains with substantial efficiency improvements across SR backbones.

title: Bandit Convex Optimization with Gradient Prediction Adaptivity
summary: Bandit convex optimization (BCO) is a fundamental online learning framework with partial feedback, where the learner observes only the loss incurred at the chosen decision point in each round. In this work, we investigate whether optimistic gradient predictions can improve worst-case regret guarantees in a prediction-adaptive manner. Specifically, given gradient predictions $m_t$, we seek regret bounds that scale with the cumulative prediction error $S_T=\sum_{t=1}^T \|\nabla f_t(x_t)-m_t\|^2.$ We first establish a negative result: under the single-point feedback protocol, an unavoidable $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ regret lower bound persists even when $S_T=o(T)$, showing that the variance of gradient estimation fundamentally obscures the benefit of accurate predictions. To overcome this barrier, we propose \emph{Two-Point Variance-Reduced Optimistic Gradient Descent} (TP-VR-OPT) for the two-point feedback setting. The key idea is a novel variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with the prediction error rather than the gradient norm. This yields a regret bound of $O\big(\sqrt{d\,\mathbb{E}[S_T]}\big),$ where $d$ is the decision dimension. Complementing this result, we establish an information-theoretic lower bound that scales as $Ω(\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[S_T]})$, providing a fundamental characterization of the best achievable prediction-adaptive regret and showing that TP-VR-OPT is optimal up to a factor of $\sqrt d$. We further develop adaptive variants that eliminate the need for prior knowledge of $\mathbb{E}[S_T]$ or the horizon $T$, and extend our framework to non-stationary environments, establishing dynamic regret guarantees that adapt simultaneously to the cumulative prediction error and the comparator path length.

AI.tldr: Optimistic gradient predictions help bandit convex optimization (BCO) under two-point feedback via a variance-reduced estimator, achieving regret O(sqrt(d E[S_T])) and near-optimality (lower bound Ω(sqrt(E[S_T])); single-point feedback cannot beat Ω(sqrt(T)); adaptive and non-stationary extensions are developed.
AI.motivation: To determine whether gradient predictions can meaningfully reduce worst-case regret in BCO in a prediction-adaptive manner, and to quantify the dependency on the cumulative prediction error S_T. The work also aims to characterize optimal rates and provide adaptive and non-stationary extensions.
AI.method: Introduce Two-Point Variance-Reduced Optimistic Gradient Descent (TP-VR-OPT) with a novel variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with the prediction error. Establish regret upper bound O(√(d E[S_T])) and an information-theoretic lower bound Ω(√(E[S_T])). Develop adaptive variants that remove the need for prior knowledge of E[S_T] or horizon T, and extend to non-stationary environments with dynamic regret guarantees.
AI.result: Regret bound: O(√(d E[S_T])); lower bound: Ω(√(E[S_T])); TP-VR-OPT is optimal up to a √d factor; single-point feedback retains a Ω(√T) barrier even when S_T=o(T); adaptive methods remove dependence on E[S_T] and T; non-stationary extensions yield dynamic regret that adapts to S_T and comparator path length.
AI.conclusion: Two-point feedback combined with optimistic gradient predictions and variance reduction yields a near-optimal, prediction-adaptive BC optimization framework. The work identifies a fundamental gap for single-point feedback and provides adaptive and non-stationary extensions, marking a near-complete characterization of the problem under the proposed setting.

title: VEELA: A Clinically-Constrained Benchmark for Liver Vessel Segmentation in Computed Tomography Angiography
summary: Accurate segmentation of hepatic and portal vessels in contrast-enhanced computed tomography angiography (CTA) remains challenging due to complex vascular topology, peripheral visibility limitations, and acquisition-induced ambiguities. While existing public datasets offer valuable benchmarks, few include clinically realistic annotation constraints. We introduce VEELA (Vessel Extraction and Extrication for Liver Analysis), a rigorously curated liver vessel dataset derived from 40 CTA scans inherited from the CHAOS grand-challenge cohort. All vessels were manually delineated slice-by-slice under multi-expert consensus, using a strict visibility-driven annotation policy and avoiding anatomically inferred interpolation. This design explicitly captures anatomical variability and imaging-related uncertainty. As a continuation of the CHAOS challenge, VEELA enables reproducible cross-benchmark evaluation while extending the scope to fine-grained hepatic and portal vessel segmentation. We further establish a standardized benchmarking framework and analyze complementary evaluation metrics, including topology-aware (clDice), overlap-based (IoU), boundary-sensitive (NSD), and geometry-aware (area, length) measures. Our results demonstrate that different metrics capture distinct aspects of vascular integrity, underscoring the necessity of multi-perspective evaluation for clinically meaningful vessel segmentation. VEELA is publicly released to facilitate reproducible research and support the development of robust vascular segmentation methods. Researchers can access the evaluation metrics, dataset, and submission platform at https://www.synapse.org/Synapse:syn65471967.

AI.tldr: VEELA is a rigorously annotated liver vessel dataset derived from CHAOS CTA scans, with multi-expert consensus and a visibility-driven policy, enabling cross-benchmark evaluation and multi-metric assessment (clDice, IoU, NSD, area/length) for hepatic/portal vessel segmentation.
AI.motivation: To address the gap of clinically realistic annotation constraints in public datasets and to capture anatomical variability and imaging uncertainty, enabling reproducible cross-benchmark evaluation for hepatic/portal vessel segmentation.
AI.method: Manual slice-by-slice delineation by multiple experts under a visibility-driven annotation policy, avoiding interpolation to preserve uncertainty; derived from 40 CTA scans from the CHAOS cohort; standardized benchmarking framework established.
AI.result: Demonstrates that topology-aware, overlap-based, boundary-sensitive, and geometry-aware metrics capture distinct aspects of vascular integrity; underscores the need for multi-perspective evaluation; VEELA and its metrics/platform are publicly released for reproducible benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: VEELA enables reproducible cross-benchmark evaluation and supports development of robust hepatic/portal vessel segmentation methods; publicly released with access to evaluation metrics, dataset, and submission platform.

title: Reinforced Graph of Thoughts: RL-Driven Adaptive Prompting for LLMs
summary: Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a generalized form of recent prompting paradigms for large language models (LLMs), has been shown to be useful for elaborate problem solving. By executing a graph of operations, thoughts of the LLM are structured as an arbitrary graph, forming the actual graph of thoughts. Originally, the graph of operations is defined manually, which requires in-depth knowledge about the solution of the problem to solve. Such a static graph of operations is rigid and therefore lacks adaptability. We propose Reinforced Graph of Thoughts (RGoT), an automated approach to the GoT prompting paradigm that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to adaptively generate a graph of operations from a human-defined set. Results indicate that, under certain constraints, it is possible to construct graphs of operations adaptively to the task's complexity in an automated way.

AI.tldr: RGoT automates the Graph of Thoughts prompting by using reinforcement learning to adaptively generate a graph of operations from a predefined operator set, addressing the static, manually crafted nature of GoT and enabling task-complexity–driven adaptation.
AI.motivation: Overcome the rigidity and manual design of static GoT graphs by enabling adaptive, learned assembly of operation graphs that match problem complexity.
AI.method: An RL-based framework that selects and composes a graph of operations from a human-defined operator vocabulary to form a graph of thoughts for solving tasks. The policy is trained to optimize problem-solving performance, allowing the graph structure to adapt to task demands.
AI.result: Evidence suggests that, under certain constraints, the graph of operations can be generated adaptively to the task’s complexity, indicating feasibility of automated, complexity-aware GoT prompting within the defined constraints.
AI.conclusion: RGoT offers a scalable, automated extension of GoT prompting by learning how to structure reasoning graphs. It highlights potential gains in adaptivity and task-aligned reasoning, while emphasizing the role of defined constraints and operator vocabularies; future work should address efficiency, generalization, and robust evaluation across tasks.

title: GazePrior: Zero-Shot AR/VR Eye Tracking via Learned 3D Gaze Reconstruction
summary: Eye tracking (ET) is a foundational technology for advanced AR/VR applications. However, training ET models for every new ET device is challenging: real data collection is costly and time-consuming, while existing synthetic data generation methods lack realism. To remove the need for additional data collection while maintaining data quality, we introduce a data-driven 3D prior that models the distribution of human eyes across diverse identities, gaze directions, and light settings. This model, which we coin GazePrior, then enables sparse-input 3D reconstruction of annotated data collected with previous ET devices, which can in turn be rendered from the cameras of any target ET device. Our approach synthesizes data with the realism, diversity and ground-truth accuracy of real data collection without its prohibitive costs. Our experiments demonstrate that ET models trained with our synthesized data outperform previous zero-shot methods, achieving higher accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: A data-driven 3D eye prior (GazePrior) enables cross-device eye-tracking model training by reconstructing annotated data from existing devices and rendering for target devices, reducing data collection costs while preserving realism and ground-truth accuracy.
AI.motivation: Training ET models for every new device is costly and time-consuming; synthetic data often lacks realism and ground-truth accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce GazePrior, a data-driven 3D prior that models the distribution of human eyes across identities, gaze directions, and light settings. Use sparse-input 3D reconstruction of annotated data from previous ET devices, then render data for any target device.
AI.result: Synthesized data matches realism, diversity, and ground-truth accuracy of real data; ET models trained on this data outperform prior zero-shot methods, with higher accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: A data-driven 3D prior enables cost-effective, high-quality ET data generation across devices, reducing the need for new data collection and facilitating cross-device model transfer.

title: Survive or Collapse: The Asymmetric Roles of Data Gating and Reward Grounding in Self-Play RL
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning trains language models on their own generated tasks, co-evolving a proposer and solver without human labels. Recent systems report strong reasoning gains, but collapse and instability are widely observed and poorly understood. The dominant response treats this as a reward-design problem. We argue instead that self-play stability is governed by two distinct levers: a data-level gate that decides which proposer-generated tasks enter the training pool, and the reward signal that updates the policy on tasks already admitted. Through controlled experiments on a Python output-prediction task and a deterministic-DSL twin task that strips pretraining priors, output ambiguity, and executor noise, we find the two levers are asymmetric. A strict gate is sufficient for stability under every reward variant we test, including a self-consistency reward with no access to ground truth; while no reward variant is sufficient once the gate is removed. This asymmetry exposes a counter-intuitive coupling we call the Grounded Proposer Paradox: a proposer with ground-truth access accelerates collapse faster than an ungrounded one when paired with a self-consistency solver, by concentrating training on clean tasks that form the fastest path to a spurious self-consistent attractor. Replacing the binary gate with a continuous strictness parameter $\varepsilon$ further reveals a two-stage phase transition: training-side metrics decouple at low $\varepsilon$, while validation accuracy holds until $\varepsilon$ is much higher. Data-level gating, not reward calibration, is the binding constraint on self-play stability.

AI.tldr: Data-level gating (which tasks enter the training pool) is the primary determinant of self-play stability; reward shaping cannot compensate for the absence of gating. A strict gate stabilizes training across reward variants, while removing the gate leads to collapse regardless of reward. The Grounded Proposer Paradox and a continuous gate parameter (epsilon) reveal a two-stage phase transition, with data gating as the binding constraint.
AI.motivation: Disentangle the separate roles of data selection (gate) and reward signal in self-play training of proposer-solver systems, to understand what drives stability and collapse.
AI.method: Controlled experiments on a Python output-prediction task and a deterministic DSL twin task that removes pretraining priors, output ambiguity, and executor noise. Compare binary data gates (on/off) versus various reward schemes, including self-consistency, and vary a continuous gate strictness parameter epsilon. Assess stability, training dynamics, and validation performance.
AI.result: A binary data gate suffices for stability across tested reward variants; removing the gate yields instability regardless of reward design. The Grounded Proposer Paradox shows that a proposer with ground-truth access accelerates collapse when paired with a self-consistency solver by concentrating training on clean tasks that lead to a spurious self-consistent attractor. Introducing a continuous gate parameter epsilon reveals a two-stage phase transition: training-side metrics decouple at low epsilon while validation accuracy remains stable until higher epsilon. Overall, data-level gating is the binding constraint on self-play stability, more critical than reward calibration.
AI.conclusion: Prioritize data-level gating when designing self-play systems; reward design alone cannot ensure stability. The results highlight a surprising interaction (Grounded Proposer Paradox) and suggest a phase-transition regime controlled by gate strictness, with practical implications for constructing robust self-play curricula.

title: AgroTools: A Benchmark for Tool-Augmented Multimodal Agents in Agriculture
summary: Agricultural decision-making increasingly requires multimodal systems that can transform visual observations into reliable, executable actions. However, existing agricultural multimodal benchmarks mainly evaluate final-answer correctness and provide limited support for assessing whether models can use external tools to complete precision-sensitive workflows. In this paper, we introduce AgroTools, a benchmark for evaluating tool-augmented multimodal agents in agriculture. AgroTools contains 539 question-answer instances paired with 1,097 heterogeneous agricultural images, spanning five task families and an executable environment of 14 agricultural tools. Each query is annotated with structured tool-use traces, enabling a dual-view evaluation of both process-level execution quality and outcome-level task success. We benchmark 9 open-source and 4 closed-source multimodal large language models on AgroTools. Results show that current models remain far from reliable in agricultural tool-use settings, with clear bottlenecks in tool planning, argument generation, execution recovery, and final-answer synthesis. We hope AgroTools will support future research on multimodal agents for high-precision agricultural applications. The benchmark and evaluation are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/AgroTools/AgroTools.

AI.tldr: AgroTools introduces a tool-augmented multimodal benchmark for agriculture, with 539 QA instances over 1,097 images, 14 executable tools, and 5 task families. It supports dual-view evaluation via structured tool-use traces and compares 13 multimodal LLMs. Results reveal significant gaps in planning, argumentation, execution recovery, and synthesis, highlighting bottlenecks in current models and providing a resource to push precision-agriculture AI research.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal benchmarks in agriculture emphasize final-answer accuracy and lack evaluation of external tool usage in precision workflows. There is a need to assess whether models can plan, select, and execute tool-based steps reliably to achieve high-precision agricultural tasks.
AI.method: Construction of AgroTools: 539 QA pairs paired with 1,097 heterogeneous agricultural images, spanning five task families; an executable environment with 14 agricultural tools; annotated structured tool-use traces enabling dual-view evaluation of process-level execution quality and outcome-level task success; evaluation of 9 open-source and 4 closed-source multimodal LLMs across the benchmark.
AI.result: Models underperform in tool-augmented settings, with clear bottlenecks in tool planning, argument generation, execution recovery, and final-answer synthesis; AgroTools provides a dataset and benchmark to quantify these weaknesses and guide future improvements.
AI.conclusion: AgroTools is a valuable resource for advancing multimodal agents in high-precision agriculture, enabling rigorous evaluation of tool use and supporting research toward reliable, executable agricultural AI systems; the benchmark and evaluation are publicly available.

title: Can Transformers Learn to Verify During Backtracking Search?
summary: Backtracking search underlies classical constraint solvers, planners, and theorem provers. Recent transformer-based reasoning systems explore search trees over their own intermediate steps. A common training recipe fits an autoregressive next-token loss on offline solver traces. The model's input at each step is a cumulative trace of all prior decisions. The optimal continue-or-backtrack predictor depends only on the current search state, since two trajectories reaching the same state admit the same viable continuations. We show that decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative traces fail this requirement in two ways: the trace can scatter state features across many positions (scattered retrieval), and the predictor can condition on the trajectory rather than the state (history entanglement). We address scattered retrieval with localization, a trace-level fix that rewrites each decision block to expose state features locally. We address history entanglement with Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask that enforces state-based decisions structurally without modifying training data, objective, or parameters. We focus on reactive verification, after propagation has exposed a contradiction. We test SSA on 3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, and backtracking parsing. On same-state pairs that differ only in prior history, SSA emits identical decisions while a cumulative-trained causal baseline does not. Our contribution is a diagnostic of transformer behavior on serialized trajectory data, paired with a structural fix. Pretrained language models that search over their own reasoning steps may face the same failure. Our analysis opens up inference-time context clearing as a candidate way to apply the same isolation without retraining.

AI.tldr: Decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative search traces exhibit two failure modes: scattered retrieval of state features and history entanglement, causing decisions to depend on history rather than the current search state. The authors propose localization to expose state features locally and Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask, to enforce state-based decisions without data/ objective/ parameter changes. Empirical tests on 3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, and backtracking parsing show SSA yields identical decisions for same-state pairs despite different histories, addressing the two failure modes. This suggests inference-time context clearing as a promising, retraining-free approach for such models.
AI.motivation: To diagnose why decoder-only transformers trained on serialized solver traces fail to condition decisions on the current state and to develop structural fixes that isolate state information during decision making without altering training data or objectives.
AI.method: Introduce two remedies: (1) localization to rewrite decision blocks so state features are exposed locally within the trace, addressing scattered retrieval; (2) Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask that enforces state-centric decisions without modifying data, loss, or parameters. Evaluate these on reactive verification tasks (3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, backtracking parsing) against a baseline trained on cumulative traces.
AI.result: SSA eliminates history-based variation in decisions for same-state pairs, while cumulative-trace baselines exhibit history entanglement; localization improves exposure of state features; results demonstrated across multiple domains, supporting the proposed diagnosis and fixes.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a diagnostic of transformer behavior with serialized trajectory data and offers a structural fix (localization + SSA) to enforce state-based decision making. It also suggests inference-time context clearing as a potential retraining-free strategy for pretrained LMs that search over their own reasoning steps.

title: Translating Signals to Languages for sEMG-Based Activity Recognition
summary: Surface electromyography (sEMG) signal-based activity recognition has attracted increasing research attention in recent years. To develop accurate sEMG signal-based activity recognizers, numerous approaches have been proposed. Some studies focus on designing larger and more expressive model architectures to enhance the representational capacity of sEMG signals, while others aim to enrich model priors through large-scale pretraining, thereby improving recognition performance. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable generalization and reasoning capabilities in natural language processing, whose implicit knowledge, learned from extensive linguistic descriptions of actions, opens new possibilities for interpreting sEMG signals and inferring activity intentions. Motivated by this, we propose LLM-sEMG, a novel framework that leverages LLMs as sEMG activity recognizers. Within this framework, we design a language-oriented mapping mechanism that converts continuous sEMG sequences into sEMG language, integrating several strategies to further facilitate the signal-to-language mapping process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves highly accurate sEMG signal-based activity recognition using large language models.

AI.tldr: LLM-sEMG: using large language models as sEMG activity recognizers via a language-oriented mapping from sEMG sequences to sEMG language, achieving strong recognition performance.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of scaling model capacity and pretraining in sEMG recognition; leverages LLMs' generalization and implicit action knowledge to interpret sEMG signals and infer intents.
AI.method: Introduce a language-oriented mapping mechanism to convert continuous sEMG sequences into sEMG language; integrate strategies to facilitate mapping; use LLMs as recognizers; perform extensive experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates highly accurate sEMG activity recognition with LLMs; extensive experiments show strong performance.
AI.conclusion: LLM-sEMG demonstrates viability of language-based interpretation for biosignal recognition and highlights broader potential of LLMs in sEMG analysis.

title: ARC-STAR: Auditable Post-Hoc Correction for PDE Foundation Models
summary: Partial differential equation (PDE) foundation models are pretrained networks that forecast how physical fields like velocity and pressure evolve from a single reusable solver. On unfamiliar flows their predictions drift step by step, errors concentrate in a few regions, yet retraining destabilizes the network and uniform post-hoc correction overlooks this spatial concentration. To address this, we propose a frozen-solver post-hoc correction framework, Adaptive Risk-Calibrated Spatial Triage for Auditable Refinement (ARC-STAR). ARC-STAR organizes correction into three stages: a global corrector removes broad solver bias, a blockwise local refiner cleans the post-global residual, and, at deployment, a label-free score routes refinement to high-risk blocks under a compute budget. The framework is designed to be (i) frozen-host, preserving the pretrained solver without fine-tuning; (ii) auditable, with global and local stages trained and evaluated separately for measurable contributions; and (iii) budget-aware, using a blockwise interface that either refines the full field or routes limited compute to high-risk regions. Across five flow benchmarks spanning ten regime cells, ARC-STAR is the only method that cuts velocity rollout error by at least 36x over raw Poseidon on every cell. The global stage reduces raw host error by 91-99%, and the local stage further reduces the remaining post-global residual by up to 94.4%. Our code implementation is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/arc_star.

AI.tldr: ARC-STAR is a frozen-solver, post-hoc correction framework for PDE foundation models that uses a global corrector, blockwise local refiner, and a budget-aware routing stage to auditable, compute-constrained refinement. It preserves the pretrained solver (no fine-tuning), and achieves large error reductions across multiple flow regimes, outperforming Poseidon on all cells.
AI.motivation: PDE foundation models exhibit error drift on unfamiliar flows with spatially concentrated errors; retraining destabilizes models and uniform post-hoc corrections miss spatial concentration. There is a need for an auditable, frozen-solver, budget-aware post-hoc correction framework.
AI.method: Three-stage correction: (1) global corrector to remove broad solver bias; (2) blockwise local refiner to clean the post-global residual; (3) at deployment, a label-free score routes refinement to high-risk blocks under a compute budget. The approach is frozen-host (no fine-tuning), auditable (stages trained/evaluated separately), and budget-aware (blockwise interface for full-field or selective refinement).
