Using k6 with 50-1000 concurrent virtual users, we find that replica count must be matched to the physical CPU thread count of the host: scaling from 3 to 12 replicas on a 12-thread CPU reduces p95 latency from 3.3s to 1.41s (57.3% reduction) and eliminates all request failures, while over-provisioning to 24 or 48 replicas degrades performance due to scheduler contention. To our knowledge this U-shaped scaling behavior has not been quantified previously for clinical AI inference workloads. We do not claim prospective clinical validation. Source code and deployment manifests are available at https://github.com/nucleusai/sepsisai-orchestrator.

AI.tldr: Open-source SepsisAI-Orchestrator platform for bedside deployment of a validated LightGBM sepsis model; demonstrates CPU-thread–matched replica scaling; single-source deployment with CDA preprocessing, NoSQL storage, REST inference, and Streamlit dashboard; reveals a U-shaped latency scaling with replicas and no prospective clinical validation.
AI.motivation: Translate clinical ML models into bedside use by addressing deployment barriers: heterogeneous data representations, standardized workflows, and concurrency/latency constraints in hospitals.
AI.method: Develop an integrated platform (CDA preprocessing, NoSQL storage, LightGBM REST service, Streamlit dashboard) orchestrated with Docker/Kubernetes. Reuse a validated LightGBM model; perform load testing with k6 (50–1000 concurrent users) varying replica counts and host CPU threads to assess latency (p95) and failure rate.
AI.result: Replication aligned with CPU threads yields best latency and zero failures (3→12 replicas on a 12-thread CPU: p95 latency 3.3s→1.41s; 57.3% reduction). Over-provisioning to 24/48 replicas increases contention and degrades performance, producing a U-shaped scaling curve not previously quantified for clinical AI inference.
AI.conclusion: The work provides infrastructure and empirical characterization for deploying clinical AI at scale; does not claim prospective clinical validation; source code and deployment manifests are publicly available.

title: Case-Aware Medical Image Classification with Multimodal Knowledge Graphs and Reliability-Guided Refinement
summary: Deep learning has brought significant progress to medical image classification, yet most existing methods still rely on isolated visual evidence and cannot effectively leverage similar cases or external knowledge. In clinical practice, diagnosis is typically supported by historical similar cases and their associated symptoms. To simulate this diagnostic process, we propose a framework that performs case-aware reasoning using multimodal knowledge graphs for explainable medical image diagnosis. Given an input image, our method constructs a multimodal knowledge graph from adaptively retrieved similar cases, enabling more effective utilization of related samples. We further introduce a knowledge propagation and injection mechanism, where an image-centric Graph Attention Network propagates knowledge semantics to obtain case-based features, followed by a bidirectional cross-modal attention mechanism that injects these features into visual representations for cross-modal alignment. To mitigate noisy retrieval, we design a confidence-calibrated decision refinement scheme that estimates the reliability of each retrieved case by jointly considering prediction confidence and sample similarity, adaptively adjusting its contribution to the final prediction and providing interpretable case-level evidence. Extensive experiments on multiple medical imaging datasets show that our approach consistently outperforms strong baselines, and ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component. The source code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MKG-CARE-8B7B.

AI.tldr: A framework for explainable medical image diagnosis using case-aware reasoning with multimodal knowledge graphs; retrieves similar cases, propagates and injects knowledge into image representations, uses confidence calibration to handle noisy retrieval, and achieves improved performance with interpretability.
AI.motivation: Medical image diagnosis often relies on historical similar cases and external knowledge; current methods rely on isolated visual evidence and fail to leverage related samples; need explainable, case-based reasoning using multimodal graphs.
AI.method: Construct multimodal knowledge graphs from adaptively retrieved similar cases; an image-centric Graph Attention Network propagates knowledge semantics to derive case-based features; bidirectional cross-modal attention injects these features into visual representations; a confidence-calibrated decision refinement estimates reliability of each retrieved case using prediction confidence and sample similarity to adjust contributions; provides interpretable case-level evidence.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple medical imaging datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines; ablations validate component contributions; code released.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables effective, explainable case-based reasoning for medical image diagnosis by integrating multimodal knowledge graphs and attention-based fusion, robust to noisy retrieval and supported by extensive experiments.

title: Riemannian geometry meets fMRI: the advantages of modeling correlation manifolds and eigenvector subspaces
summary: Correlation matrices are fundamental summaries of functional brain networks, yet standard analyses often treat entries independently, ignoring the curved geometry of correlation space. Existing geometric methods frequently lack closed-form operations or depend on arbitrary region ordering, limiting scalability. We introduce a scalable geometric framework with two components: (i) the Off-log metric, a smooth transformation mapping correlation matrices to symmetric zero-diagonal matrices. This enables closed-form expressions for distances, Frechet means, and linear models, allowing standard statistical modeling without complex manifold optimization. (ii) Grassmannian subspace discrimination, which compares subjects via principal-angle distances between eigenvector subspaces, resolving inherent sign and basis ambiguities. Both components integrate into standard machine-learning workflows for inference, regression, and classification. Validated across two clinical cohorts (Parkinson's and psychosis) and three ageing fMRI datasets, the Off-log metric increased sensitivity in permutation tests and matched or exceeded Riemannian and Euclidean baselines in classification. Brain-age prediction performance was comparable, with Riemannian metrics excelling in two of three cohorts. The Grassmannian method consistently outperformed Euclidean baselines, highlighting disease-relevant networks. Overall, geometry-aware representations improve sensitivity and predictive performance while remaining straightforward to deploy at scale.

AI.tldr: A scalable geometry-aware framework for functional brain connectivity matrices combining an Off-log metric with Grassmannian subspace discrimination; provides closed-form statistics and robust, symmetric matrix comparisons, improving sensitivity and prediction across several brain-imaging cohorts.
AI.motivation: Standard analyses treat correlation matrices as if entries were independent, ignoring the curved geometry of correlation space; existing geometric methods suffer from lack of closed-form operations or arbitrary region ordering, limiting scalability.
AI.method: Two components: (i) Off-log metric mapping correlation matrices to symmetric zero-diagonal matrices to enable closed-form distances, Frechet means, and linear models; (ii) Grassmannian subspace discrimination using principal-angle distances between eigenvector subspaces to resolve sign/basis ambiguities; integration into standard ML workflows for inference, regression, and classification.
AI.result: Off-log metric increased sensitivity in permutation tests and matched/exceeded Riemannian and Euclidean baselines in classification; brain-age prediction was comparable, with Riemannian metrics excelling in two of three cohorts; Grassmannian method consistently outperformed Euclidean baselines; disease-relevant networks highlighted and geometry-aware representations improved performance and scalability.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware framework for correlation-based brain networks yields more sensitive statistical inference and competitive predictive performance while remaining straightforward to deploy at scale; the two components can be integrated into routine brain-imaging analyses to handle correlation geometry effectively.

title: MOTOR: A Multimodal Dataset for Two-Wheeler Rider Behavior Understanding
summary: Two-wheelers account for a disproportionately high share of road fatalities in the Global South. Research on two-wheeler rider behavior, however, lags far behind four-wheelers, where multimodal datasets have driven major advances in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). To address this gap, we present the MOtorized TwO-wheeler Rider (MOTOR) dataset, the first large-scale, multi-view, multimodal resource dedicated to two-wheelers in dense, unstructured traffic. MOTOR comprises 1,629 sequences (25+ hours of video data) collected from 16 riders and integrates synchronized front, rear, and helmet videos, rider eye-gaze from wearable trackers, on-road audio, and telemetry (GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope). Rich annotations capture traffic context, rider state, 12 riding maneuvers spanning conventional and unconventional behaviors, and legality labels (Legal, Illegal, Unspecified). We benchmark rider behavior recognition and maneuver legality classification using state-of-the-art video action recognition backbones (CNN and Transformer-based), extended with multimodal fusion, and find that combining RGB, gaze, and telemetry consistently yields the best performance. MOTOR thus provides a unique foundation for advancing safety-critical understanding of two-wheeler riding. It offers the research community a benchmark to develop and evaluate models for behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and intelligent transportation systems. Dataset and code is available at https: //varuniiith.github.io/MOTOR-Dataset/

AI.tldr: A large-scale, multimodal dataset for motorcycle/scooter rider behavior in dense traffic (MOTOR): 1,629 sequences (~25+ hours) from 16 riders, with synchronized front/rear/helmet video, rider eye-gaze, on-road audio, and telemetry (GPS/IMU); rich annotations for traffic context, rider state, 12 maneuvers (conventional/unconventional), and legality labels; baseline results show RGB+gaze+telemetry fusion yields best performance for rider behavior recognition and legality classification; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in high-quality, multimodal datasets for two-wheeler behavior in the Global South, where rider fatality risk is high and research lags behind four-wheelers; enable safety-critical analysis, behavior understanding, and legality-aware prediction to inform ADAS and ITS.
AI.method: Data collection in dense, unstructured traffic with multi-view video (front/rear/helmet), wearable eye-gaze tracking, audio, and GPS/IMU telemetry; comprehensive annotations including traffic context, rider state, 12 maneuvers (covering conventional and unconventional behaviors), and legality (Legal/Illegal/Unspecified); evaluation using state-of-the-art CNN and Transformer backbones with multimodal fusion to benchmark behavior recognition and legality classification.
AI.result: Fusion of RGB, gaze, and telemetry consistently yields the best performance in rider behavior recognition and legality classification, demonstrating the value of multimodal data; the dataset provides a benchmark for behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and ITS applications; dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: MOTOR establishes a foundational resource for advancing safety-critical understanding of two-wheeler riding, enabling research on behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and ITS, and offering a benchmark for future models and evaluations.

title: Learning Causal Orderings for In-Context Tabular Prediction
summary:
In-context learning for tabular data sets strong predictive standards in observational settings; it however primarily relies on correlational structure, which becomes unreliable under distribution shift or intervention. While established methods to discover causal structure exist, they are often focused on structure identifiability and decoupled from the predictive architectures that could benefit from them.
  To bridge these perspectives, we study how to simultaneously infer and enforce causal structure in the form of topological variable orderings into tabular prediction. Unlike standard architectures, our model TabOrder uses causal order-constrained attention, basing predictions only on features that precede a target under a learned causal order. Similar to causal discovery methods, TabOrder learns the optimal variable ordering in an unsupervised manner through a likelihood-based objective. We justify this choice under standard functional model classes and also study how sample missingness, a common challenge in tabular data, interacts with causal direction identification. Empirically, we confirm that TabOrder recovers accurate variable orderings while addressing prediction and imputation tasks, as well as gives insight into real-world biological data under intervention.

AI.tldr: TabOrder learns and enforces a causal variable ordering in tabular prediction by constraining attention to preceding features, enabling unsupervised order discovery and robust prediction/imputation under shift and interventions.
AI.motivation: To address reliance on correlational structure in in-context learning for tabular data and to bridge causal discovery with predictive architectures by learning a causal order that informs predictions.
AI.method: Introduce TabOrder with causal order-constrained attention; predictions depend only on features preceding the target in a learned order; jointly learns the order via a likelihood-based objective; analyzes missingness effects on causal direction identification.
AI.result: Empirically, TabOrder recovers accurate variable orderings; performs well on prediction and imputation tasks; yields insights on real-world biological data under intervention.
AI.conclusion: Simultaneous inference and enforcement of causal structure in tabular prediction improves robustness to distribution shift and interventions and provides interpretability through learned causal ordering.

title: FashionLens: Toward Versatile Fashion Image Retrieval via Task-Adaptive Learning
summary: Fashion image retrieval is a cornerstone of modern e-commerce systems. A unified framework that supports diverse query formats and search intentions is highly desired in practice. However, existing approaches focus on narrow retrieval tasks and do not fully capture such diversity. Therefore, in this work, we aim to develop a unified framework capable of handling diverse realistic fashion retrieval scenarios, achieving truly versatile fashion image retrieval. To establish a data foundation, we first introduce U-FIRE, a comprehensive benchmark that consolidates fragmented fashion datasets into a unified collection, supplemented by two manually curated datasets for testing generalization. Building upon this, we propose FashionLens, a unified framework based on Multimodal Large Language Models. To handle divergent matching objectives, we design a Proposal-Guided Spherical Query Calibrator that dynamically shifts query representations into task-aligned metric spaces via adaptive spherical linear interpolation. Additionally, to mitigate the optimization imbalance caused by varying task complexities and data scales, we develop a Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling strategy that automatically re-weights tasks based on realtime learning difficulty and the data scale prior. Experiments on U-FIRE show that FashionLens achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse retrieval scenarios and generalizes robustly to unseen tasks. The data and code are publicly released at https://github.com/haokunwen/FashionLens.

AI.tldr: Introduces FashionLens, a unified fashion image retrieval framework built on Multimodal LLMs, addressing diverse query formats and retrieval intents; supported by a unified benchmark (U-FIRE) and two held-out test sets; achieves state-of-the-art results and demonstrates generalization to unseen tasks.
AI.motivation: Current fashion retrieval systems are task-specific and struggle to handle diverse real-world queries. A unified framework with robust data foundation and adaptive mechanisms is needed to support multiple retrieval objectives in a single system.
AI.method: A unified framework (FashionLens) built on Multimodal Large Language Models. Key components include a Proposal-Guided Spherical Query Calibrator that adapts query representations to task-specific metric spaces via adaptive spherical linear interpolation, and a Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling strategy that re-weights tasks in real time according to learning difficulty and data scale. Evaluation is conducted on the U-FIRE benchmark, which consolidates fragmented datasets, plus two manual test sets for generalization.
AI.result: FashionLens achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse retrieval scenarios on U-FIRE and shows robust generalization to unseen tasks; code and data are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a versatile, scalable approach to fashion image retrieval, unifying multiple tasks under a single model with adaptive mechanisms to balance objectives; it has strong empirical support on a comprehensive benchmark and generalization tests.

title: Physics-Informed Generative Solver: Bridging Data-Driven Priors and Conservation Laws for Stable Spatiotemporal Field Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing continuous physical fields from sparse measurements is a central inverse problem, but data-driven generative models can produce states that violate governing dynamics. We introduce a physics-informed generative solver that separates stable prior learning from inference-time enforcement of conservation laws. Martingale-Regularized Score Matching regularizes score pretraining with a Score Fokker-Planck constraint, yielding a dynamically stable prior. Physics-Informed Implicit Score Sampling then guides denoising trajectories by gradients of physical residuals, projecting samples toward admissible manifolds without retraining. In acoustics, the method co-generates pressure and particle velocity from sparse sensors, enabling dense virtual arrays that suppress spatial aliasing. The same framework generalizes to real-world ERA5 meteorological fields under extreme sparsity. Together, this work establishes a rigorous and generalizable paradigm for solving high-dimensional inverse problems, bridging the gap between generative artificial intelligence and first-principles science.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed generative solver that separate prior learning from inference-time physics constraints, using martingale-regularized score matching and physics-informed sampling to reconstruct high-dimensional fields from sparse data; demonstrated in acoustics and meteorology.
AI.motivation: Data-driven generative models can violate governing dynamics when reconstructing continuous fields from sparse measurements. There is a need to decouple stable prior learning from dynamics enforcement at inference time to ensure physically admissible reconstructions.
AI.method: 1) Martingale-Regularized Score Matching (regularizes score pretraining with a Score Fokker-Planck constraint to produce a dynamically stable prior). 2) Physics-Informed Implicit Score Sampling guides denoising trajectories via gradients of physical residuals, projecting samples onto admissible manifolds without retraining. 3) Applied to acoustics to co-generate pressure and velocity from sparse sensors, enabling dense virtual arrays and reduced spatial aliasing. 4) Generalizes to ERA5 meteorological fields under extreme sparsity.
AI.result: The framework yields a dynamically stable prior and a sampling procedure that produces physically consistent reconstructions; co-generated fields suppress spatial aliasing in acoustics and succeed on sparse meteorology data, illustrating a generalizable paradigm bridging generative AI with first-principles science.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a rigorous, generalizable methodology for high-dimensional inverse problems, enabling physics-consistent reconstruction from sparse data without retraining, and extending to multiple domains.

title: GeoWeaver: Grounding Visual Tokens with Geometric Evidence before Scene Reasoning
summary: Spatio-temporal reasoning in vision-language models requires visual representations that preserve physical geometry rather than merely semantic appearance. Recent multimodal models incorporate geometric information through structural branches, 3D-aware supervision, reasoning-stage fusion, or long-horizon memory. While these approaches demonstrate the importance of geometry for spatial intelligence, they typically treat geometric cues as a shared signal across all visual tokens. We note that this overlooks a finer-grained challenge: different visual tokens require different geometric evidence depending on their spatial roles. To address this limitation, we introduce GeoWeaver, a pre-reasoning geometric grounding framework that treats geometry as a representational prerequisite for spatio-temporal reasoning. GeoWeaver constructs a multi-level geometry bank from a frozen geometry encoder and performs token-adaptive geometric evidence allocation, enabling each visual token to retrieve the most relevant geometric abstractions. The selected evidence is incorporated into visual tokens via a residual grounding operation prior to language modeling, yielding geometry-grounded representations for downstream reasoning. Extensive evaluations on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoWeaver consistently enhances geometry-aware reasoning while retaining general multimodal capabilities. This indicates that geometric information yields the greatest benefit not as a late-fusion auxiliary signal but as a fundamental prerequisite that shapes the representational foundation on which large language models perform reasoning. All source code and models will be released at https://github.com/yahooo-m/GeoWeaver .

AI.tldr: GeoWeaver enables token-adaptive geometric grounding for spatio-temporal V-L reasoning by building a multi-level geometry bank from a frozen encoder and integrating evidence via a residual grounding step before language modeling, improving geometry-aware reasoning while preserving multimodal capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models often treat geometric cues as a shared signal across all visual tokens, neglecting that different tokens require different geometric evidence based on their spatial roles; there's a need for geometry to serve as a representational prerequisite for spatio-temporal reasoning.
AI.method: Construct a multi-level geometry bank from a frozen geometry encoder; perform token-adaptive allocation of geometric evidence to visual tokens; apply a residual grounding operation to fuse selected geometry into tokens prior to language modeling, yielding geometry-grounded representations for downstream reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on spatial reasoning benchmarks show GeoWeaver consistently enhances geometry-aware reasoning while maintaining general multimodal capabilities, indicating geometry acts as a fundamental prerequisite rather than a late-fusion signal.
AI.conclusion: Geometry information is most beneficial when it shapes the foundational representations for LMs in multimodal spatio-temporal tasks; GeoWeaver demonstrates this by adaptive geometry grounding and residual integration, with source code and models to be released.

title: From Snapshots to Trajectories: Learning Single-Cell Gene Expression Dynamics via Conditional Flow Matching
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) provides high-dimensional profiles of cellular states, enabling data-driven modeling of cellular dynamics over time. In practice, time-resolved scRNA-seq is collected at only a few discrete time points as unpaired snapshot populations, leaving substantial temporal gaps. This motivates trajectory inference at unmeasured time points. Existing methods mainly follow two directions, optimal-transport (OT) alignment provides distribution-level matching between observed snapshots, while continuous-time generative models support forecasting via learned dynamics. However, two challenges remain: (i) unpaired snapshots render local transitions between adjacent time points ambiguous, leading to unstable supervision; and (ii) long-horizon prediction relies on repeated integration, where small modeling errors compound and cause distribution drift. To address these challenges, we propose single-cell Flow Matching (scFM), a latent generative framework based on coupling-conditioned flow matching. First, we compute entropically regularized OT couplings between adjacent snapshots and use them to construct soft, weighted flow-matching targets for learning time-dependent velocity fields. Second, we learn bidirectional velocity fields and leverage their consistency to refine couplings and improve temporal coherence under sparse supervision. Third, we introduce distribution-level alignment and latent dynamic regularization to anchor long rollouts and mitigate drift. Experiments on real-world time-series scRNA-seq datasets show that scFM consistently improves distributional prediction performance for both temporal interpolation and extrapolation. Moreover, scFM yields more accurate trajectory reconstruction and temporally coherent visualizations where intermediate time points are absent, indicating a more faithful recovery of underlying temporal gene expression dynamics.

AI.tldr: A latent generative framework, scFM, uses entropically regularized optimal-transport couplings to define soft flow targets for learning time-dependent velocity fields in scRNA-seq data. It also trains bidirectional velocity fields with consistency checks and adds distribution-level alignment and latent regularization to stabilize long-horizon predictions, improving interpolation/extrapolation and trajectory reconstruction from sparse timepoints.
AI.motivation: Time-resolved scRNA-seq provides only a few unpaired snapshots, leaving temporal gaps and ambiguous local transitions between adjacent time points. Long-horizon predictions suffer from error accumulation (drift) when rolling out dynamics via learned models. There is a need for methods that simultaneously align distributions across time and learn coherent, time-resolved dynamics.
AI.method: Compute entropically regularized OT couplings between adjacent snapshots to create soft, weighted flow-matching targets for learning time-dependent velocity fields. Learn bidirectional velocity fields and enforce their consistency to refine couplings and increase temporal coherence under sparse supervision. Introduce distribution-level alignment and latent dynamic regularization to anchor long rollouts and mitigate drift.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world time-series scRNA-seq datasets show that scFM consistently improves distributional prediction for both temporal interpolation and extrapolation. It also yields more accurate trajectory reconstruction and temporally coherent visualizations when intermediate time points are absent, indicating a faithful recovery of underlying temporal gene expression dynamics.
AI.conclusion: scFM provides a robust, coupling-conditioned flow-matching framework that addresses unpaired snapshots and drift, achieving improved distributional predictions and coherent trajectories in time-resolved scRNA-seq analyses.

title: Cell Phantom Video Generation in Elliptical Fourier Descriptor Domain
comment: 6 pages, Accepted at the International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2026
summary: Training Deep Neural Networks for tracking individual cells in biomedical videos requires a large amount of annotated data. The annotation of videos for cell tracking is very time consuming and often requires domain expertise; this explains the limited availability of public annotated data to address important medical problems like tissue repair or cancer treatment. Generating synthetic videos along with their Ground Truth annotations is a promising solution that relies, as a foundational first step, on the synthesis of single cell annotations (or phantoms). Phantoms need to be time consistent, as they have to replicate biological processes that are specific to the cell types. In this work, we propose a novel framework for generating videos of cell phantoms in the Elliptical Fourier Descriptors (EFDs) domain, a compact and geometrically interpretable representation for 2D closed contours. We represent the cell phantom evolution as a multivariate time series of EFD coefficients, introducing a strong prior for cell morphology and enabling the efficient generation of sequences that evolve coherently in time. Our experimental validation proves that modelling the temporal evolution in EFD space enables the generation of biologically plausible phantom videos. Our method can be used in generative pipelines for synthesizing annotated data for cell tracking, thus strongly mitigating the annotation effort for creating new datasets. Our code is available for download here: https://github.com/FrancescoBenedetto99/efd-cell-video-gen.

AI.tldr: A framework to generate biologically plausible, time-consistent cell phantoms in Elliptical Fourier Descriptor (EFD) space for augmenting annotated cell-tracking data; demonstrates coherent temporal evolution and realistic videos; code released.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of annotated biomedical video data for cell tracking due to time-consuming, expert-limited annotation; synthetic data with realistic phantoms can mitigate data bottlenecks and enable robust model training.
AI.method: Encode 2D cell contours as Elliptical Fourier Descriptor coefficients; model the evolution of these coefficients as a multivariate time series to produce temporally coherent phantom sequences; impose morphology priors to ensure biologically plausible shapes and dynamics; generate videos of phantoms with coherent temporal evolution.
AI.result: Demonstrates that modeling temporal evolution in EFD space yields biologically plausible phantom videos, enabling their use in data-generation pipelines for cell tracking; supports efficient synthesis of annotated datasets; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: An EFD-based temporal modeling framework is effective for generating coherent, annotated cell-tracking video data, reducing annotation burden and facilitating synthetic data pipelines.

title: A Boundary-Layer Mechanism for One-Third Scaling in Online Softmax Classification
summary: Hard-label classification is usually trained with smooth surrogate losses, most prominently softmax cross-entropy. We isolate an asymptotic mechanism by which this mismatch between smooth surrogate and discrete labels produces power-law learning curves in an online teacher-student model. After subtracting the mean logit, the thermodynamic-limit dynamics close in centered variables: a growing centered student-teacher alignment $D$ and the residual student variance $Δ$. At late times, examples away from teacher decision boundaries are already classified confidently and contribute exponentially little. Only boundary layers of width $O(D^{-1})$ remain active, while the noise of fixed-learning-rate online gradient descent maintains a nonzero $Δ$. As a function of the training time $α$ the late-time solution yields a $α^{-1/3}$ power law not only for the test loss but also for the generalization error $ε_g$, i.e., one minus test accuracy. This is much slower than the $α^{-1}$ Bayes-optimal reference for the same model. We further show that learning-rate schedules can improve the generalization error towards a $ε_g \sim α^{-1/2}$ power law. Simulations support the predicted order parameter dynamics and learning curves. Controlled experiments with correlated Gaussian inputs and whitened pretrained features show that data structure can dominate transients. Therefore, our result is an asymptotic, complementary mechanism rather than an alternative to spectral explanations of neural scaling laws.

AI.tldr: Isolates an asymptotic mechanism by which mismatch between hard-label training and smooth surrogate losses yields power-law learning curves in an online teacher-student setting; finds late-time α^{-1/3} scaling for test loss and generalization error, with possible improvement to α^{-1/2} via learning-rate schedules; explains boundary-layer dynamics in centered variables and that data structure can dominate transients; complements spectral explanations of neural scaling laws.
AI.motivation: Explain why training with smooth surrogate losses for hard-label classification produces slow, power-law learning curves and connect this behavior to order-parameter dynamics in an online teacher-student model; position the result as complementary to spectral (eigenvalue) explanations.
AI.method: Thermodynamic-limit analysis of an online teacher-student model; subtract mean logit to obtain centered dynamics with growing alignment D and residual variance Δ; analyze boundary layers of width O(D^{-1}); derive α^{-1/3} scaling for test loss and generalization error; explore learning-rate schedules and their impact (α^{-1/2}); support with simulations and controlled experiments using correlated Gaussian inputs and whitened pretrained features.
AI.result: Late-time solution exhibits α^{-1/3} decay for both test loss and generalization error; learning-rate schedules can push toward α^{-1/2} scaling; boundary-layer mechanism explains persistent nonzero Δ; data structure can dominate transients; simulations corroborate the order-parameter dynamics and learning curves.
AI.conclusion: The reported mechanism is asymptotic and complementary to spectral explanations of neural scaling laws; it explains power-law learning curves arising from surrogate-loss vs. hard-label mismatch in an online setting and highlights the role of boundary-layer dynamics and data structure in shaping generalization trajectories.

title: VGenST-Bench: A Benchmark for Spatio-Temporal Reasoning via Active Video Synthesis
comment: 82 pages, 91 figures (7 in main paper, 84 in appendix). Project page: https://zinosii.github.io/VGenST-Bench/
summary: Spatio-temporal reasoning is a core capability for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) operating in the real world. As such, evaluating it precisely has become an essential challenge. However, existing spatio-temporal reasoning benchmark datasets primarily rely on static image sets or passively curated video data, which limits the evaluation of fine-grained reasoning capabilities. In this paper, we introduce VGenST-Bench, a video benchmark that employs generative models to actively synthesize highly controlled and diverse evaluation scenarios. To construct VGenST-Bench, we propose a multi-agent pipeline incorporating a human quality control stage, ensuring the quality of all generated videos and QA pairs. We establish a comprehensive 3x2x2 video taxonomy, encompassing Spatial Scale, Perspective, and Scene Dynamics to span diverse scenarios. Furthermore, we design a hierarchical task suite that decouples low-level visual perception from high-level spatio-temporal reasoning. By shifting the paradigm from passive curation to active synthesis, VGenST-Bench enables fine-grained diagnosis of spatio-temporal understanding in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A novel video benchmark VGenST-Bench for spatio-temporal reasoning in MLLMs; uses generative active synthesis, a multi-agent pipeline with human QC, a 3x2x2 taxonomy (Spatial Scale, Perspective, Scene Dynamics), and a hierarchical task suite to decouple perception from reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on static images or passively-curated videos, hindering fine-grained evaluation of MLLMs' spatio-temporal capabilities.
AI.method: Active video synthesis with generative models, a multi-agent pipeline plus human quality control, a 3x2x2 taxonomy, and a hierarchical task suite that separates low-level perception from high-level reasoning.
AI.result: Provides a scalable framework and benchmark design enabling fine-grained diagnosis of spatio-temporal understanding in MLLMs; empirical results are not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Shifts evaluation from passive data curation to active, controllable synthesis, enabling precise diagnosis of spatio-temporal understanding in MLLMs.

title: Partial Fusion of Neural Networks: Efficient Tradeoffs Between Ensembles and Weight Aggregation
summary: Ensembles of neural networks typically outperform individual networks but incur large computational costs, whereas weight aggregation produces less costly, yet also less accurate, aggregate models. We introduce partial fusion of networks, which interpolates between ensembles and weight aggregation and thus allows for a flexible tradeoff between computational cost and performance. A direct way to achieve this is to extend existing weight aggregation methods based on neuron-level similarity between different networks, where partial fusion then only aggregates weights of neurons which are most similar. We showcase one particular method to jointly identify which neurons are most similar and match them via partial optimal transport. Further, we consider the more general perspective of weight aggregation and partial fusion as generalized pruning of ensemble models, where neurons cannot just be deleted, but also linearly combined. Finally, we show that generalized pruning applied to a single network yields similar benefits as partial fusion by allowing for a tradeoff between isolating, deleting, and linearly combining neurons based on similarity. Our code is available at https://github.com/Fabian-Mor/partial_fusion_nn.

AI.tldr: Partial fusion interpolates between ensembles and weight aggregation by selectively matching and combining neurons across networks, enabling a tunable tradeoff between accuracy and computation.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of ensembles while preserving performance, and to unify weight aggregation with neuron-level matching through a flexible framework.
AI.method: Extend weight aggregation with neuron-level similarity; identify and match similar neurons using partial optimal transport; implement generalized pruning where neurons can be deleted or linearly combined; apply the framework to ensembles and to single networks.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates a tunable tradeoff between accuracy and compute by selectively fusing neurons across models, and generalizes to pruning within a single network; the authors provide code for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Partial fusion provides a flexible, principled way to interpolate between ensembles and dense weight-averaging, enabling efficient, scalable model compression through neuron-level similarity and partial fusion.

title: SegGuidedNet: Sub-Region-Aware Attention Supervision for Interpretable Brain Tumor Segmentation
summary: Accurate segmentation of brain tumour sub-regions from multi-parametric MRI is critical for treatment planning yet remains challenging due to morphological variability, class imbalance, and overlapping appearances of tumour regions across imaging sequences. We propose SegGuidedNet, a three-dimensional residual encoder--decoder network introducing a novel SegAttentionGate module that explicitly supervises the decoder to produce spatially discriminative attention maps for each tumour sub-region necrotic core, peritumoral oedema, and enhancing tumour via a lightweight auxiliary loss, adding less than 0.2% parameter overhead. This sub-region supervision maintains decoder discriminability between visually ambiguous classes while providing free-of-cost spatial interpretability at inference without any post-hoc explanation method. Evaluated independently on BraTS2021 and BraTS2023 GLI across 251 held-out subjects each, SegGuidedNet achieves mean Dice of 0.905 (ET= 0.873, TC=0.906, WT=0.935) and 0.897 (ET=0.859, TC=0.902, WT=0.931) respectively, surpassing ensemble-based nnU-Net and HNF-Netv2 as a single model and approaching Swin UNETR a 10-model ensemble within 2--4 Dice points at a fraction of the inference cost. The consistency of results across two benchmark editions further confirms the generalisability of the proposed approach, offering competitive accuracy with built-in interpretability in a lightweight, clinically practical framework.

AI.tldr: SegGuidedNet is a 3D residual encoder–decoder model with a SegAttentionGate and a lightweight auxiliary loss to supervise sub-region segmentation (necrotic core, peritumoral edema, enhancing tumor) for brain tumor MRI. It delivers competitive Dice scores on BraTS2021/2023 GLI, surpasses certain baselines as a single model, offers built-in spatial interpretability with minimal parameter overhead, and approaches Swin UNETR ensembles at lower inference cost.
AI.motivation: Brain tumor sub-region segmentation is essential for treatment planning but challenged by morphological variability, severe class imbalance, and visually overlapping sub-regions across MRI sequences. The work targets discriminative, spatially informative segmentation with interpretable attention maps that can be produced during inference without extra post-hoc tools.
AI.method: A 3D residual encoder–decoder network (SegGuidedNet) incorporating SegAttentionGate modules. The SegAttentionGate provides spatial attention maps for each tumor sub-region via a lightweight auxiliary loss that adds less than 0.2% parameter overhead. Sub-region supervision enforces discriminability among ET, TC, WT without requiring post-hoc explanations, yielding free interpretability at inference.
AI.result: On BraTS2021 and BraTS2023 GLI (251 held-out subjects each), SegGuidedNet achieves mean Dice of 0.905 (ET=0.873, TC=0.906, WT=0.935) and 0.897 (ET=0.859, TC=0.902, WT=0.931). It surpasses ensemble-based nnU-Net and HNF-Netv2 as a single model and approaches Swin UNETR (a 10-model ensemble) within 2–4 Dice points while offering lower inference cost.
AI.conclusion: SegGuidedNet provides competitive sub-region segmentation accuracy with built-in interpretability and extremely low parameter overhead, demonstrating generalizability across BraTS editions and offering a clinically practical, lightweight alternative to large ensembles.

title: VeriScale: Adversarial Test-Suite Scaling for Verifiable Code Generation
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for software engineering, constructing high-quality benchmarks is crucial for evaluating not just the functional correctness, but also the formal verifiability of generated code. However, existing benchmarks are limited by the quantity and quality of positive and negative test cases, leading to an overestimation of model capabilities in generating specifications and implementations. To address this, we propose VeriScale, a novel framework driven by the adversarial implementations. It consists of two stages: test-suite expansion to construct diverse and challenging test cases, and test-suite reduction to distill them into compact yet discriminative suites. While VeriScale is general, we instantiate it on Verina to construct VerinaPlus, which expands the original test suites by over 83$\times$, and VerinaLite, a lightweight 14$\times$ variant. Our experiments across eight state-of-the-art LLMs demonstrate that VerinaPlus exposes substantial model weaknesses hidden by the original benchmark, evidenced by sharp score drops on both SpecGen and CodeGen tasks, whereas VerinaLite maintains this discriminative power at a fraction of the evaluation cost. The enhanced benchmarks and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/XiaoyangLiu-sjtu/VeriScale.

AI.tldr: VeriScale is a framework to build more discriminative benchmarks for LLM-based software engineering by adversarial test-case expansion and reduction. It instantiates Verina into VerinaPlus (83x expansion) and VerinaLite (14x). Evaluations across eight SOTA LLMs show VerinaPlus uncovers weaknesses hidden by existing benchmarks (score drops on SpecGen and CodeGen), while VerinaLite preserves discriminative power at lower cost; code is open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks suffer from limited positive/negative test cases, causing overestimation of LLM capabilities in generating specifications and implementations, especially for formal verifiability. A scalable framework for expanding and pruning test suites is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage process: (1) test-suite expansion to generate diverse, challenging cases; (2) test-suite reduction to produce compact, discriminative suites. When instantiated on Verina, it yields VerinaPlus (~83x expansion) and VerinaLite (~14x). Evaluation across eight state-of-the-art LLMs on SpecGen and CodeGen tasks.
AI.result: VerinaPlus reveals substantial model weaknesses not visible in the original benchmark, with sharp score drops on SpecGen and CodeGen; VerinaLite retains discriminative power at a fraction of evaluation cost.
AI.conclusion: VeriScale offers a general approach to improve benchmark quality for LLM-driven software engineering tasks; the authors provide enhanced benchmarks and source code publicly.

title: Beyond Chamfer Distance: Granular Order-aware Evaluation Metric For Online Mapping
summary: Online map estimation is a crucial component of autonomous driving systems that reduces the reliance on costly high-definition maps. State-of-the-art (SOTA) methods commonly predict map elements as ordered sequences of points that form polylines and polygons. The evaluation of these methods relies predominantly on mean average precision (mAP) based on thresholded Chamfer distance (CD). This framework lacks sensitivity to point ordering and provides limited granularity in assessing geometric quality, making it difficult to distinguish which methods truly excel over others. In this work, we address these limitations on two fronts. For the single-instance similarity measure, we introduce sequence optimal sub-pattern assignment (SOSPA), an order-aware metric that enables fine-grained evaluation of individual geometries while satisfying all metric axioms. For the multi-instance evaluation framework, we propose polyline localisation and detection (PLD), a soft metric that jointly captures detection quality and geometric accuracy, replacing the hard thresholding of mAP with a principled soft assignment. Through evaluations on nuScenes, we demonstrate that PLD effectively ranks SOTA online mapping methods (MapTRv2, StreamMapNet, MapTracker) while providing a decomposed error analysis. This analysis identifies detection capability as the dominant bottleneck in current methods, revealing a performance trend that mAP fails to capture. Code for evaluation using our metrics will be released.

AI.tldr: Introduces order-aware SOSPA and soft PLD metrics for online map estimation; shows improved ranking of SOTA methods and uncovers detection as the bottleneck; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation relies on thresholded Chamfer distance and mAP, which ignore point ordering and offer coarse, non-axiomatic similarity; there is a need for fine-grained, order-aware and soft multi-instance evaluation.
AI.method: Single-instance: SOSPA, an order-aware assignment metric that satisfies metric axioms; Multi-instance: PLD, a soft assignment-based metric combining detection quality with geometric accuracy, replacing hard mAP thresholding. Evaluated on nuScenes across MapTRv2, StreamMapNet, MapTracker.
AI.result: PLD effectively ranks SOTA methods and enables decomposed error analysis; reveals detection as the primary bottleneck, a trend not captured by mAP.
AI.conclusion: Proposes SOSPA and PLD as principled evaluation tools for online map estimation; facilitates nuanced performance diagnostics; code will be released.

title: ASAP: Attention Sink Anchored Pruning
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) face severe computational bottlenecks due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention at high resolutions. Existing token reduction methods rely on local metrics - such as single-layer attention scores - that are inherently vulnerable to the attention sink phenomenon, where uninformative tokens are paradoxically preserved over salient foreground objects. We propose ASAP (Attention Sink Anchored Pruning), a training-free framework that recasts this sink as a feature. Modeling ViT information flow as a Lazy Random Walk, ASAP identifies the sink as a dominant accumulator of probability mass. By computing the diffusion distance to the sink within the cumulative transition matrix, ASAP partitions tokens via Radial Diffusion Clustering and compresses background redundancy through Transition Weight Pooling in a single shot. Extensive experiments across image, video, and vision-language tasks demonstrate ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art methods, accelerating throughput by up to 48% while maintaining - or even exceeding - baseline accuracy.

AI.tldr: ASAP is a training-free token pruning framework for Vision Transformers (ViTs) that treats attention sink as a diffusion sink. It uses Lazy Random Walk diffusion to locate the dominant accumulator of probability mass, then applies Radial Diffusion Clustering and Transition Weight Pooling to prune background tokens. It achieves up to 48% throughput gains with accuracy on par with or exceeding baselines across image, video, and vision-language tasks.
AI.motivation: ViTs incur quadratic self-attention complexity at high resolutions. Existing token-reduction approaches rely on local metrics (e.g., single-layer attention scores) that can misprioritize salient foreground tokens due to the attention sink phenomenon.
AI.method: Model ViT information flow as a Lazy Random Walk and identify the sink as a dominant accumulator of probability mass. Compute the diffusion distance to the sink within the cumulative transition matrix, then partition tokens via Radial Diffusion Clustering and compress background via Transition Weight Pooling in a single shot. The framework is training-free.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across image, video, and vision-language tasks show ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art token reduction methods, delivering up to 48% throughput acceleration while maintaining or exceeding baseline accuracy.
AI.conclusion: ASAP provides a robust, training-free token-pruning approach that generalizes across modalities (image, video, vision-language), effectively mitigating the attention sink and reducing compute without sacrificing accuracy.

title: SceneAligner: 3D-Grounded Floorplan Localization in the Wild
summary: Many public buildings provide floorplans with a "you are here" indicator to help visitors orient themselves. Floorplan localization seeks to computationally replicate this capability by determining where visual observations were captured within a floorplan. However, existing methods typically assume controlled small-scale environments and precise vectorized floorplans, limiting their ability to operate in large-scale buildings and rasterized floorplans. In this work, we present an approach for performing floorplan localization in the wild by grounding the task in a reconstructed 3D representation of the scene. Given an unconstrained image collection, our method reconstructs a gravity-aligned 3D scene and projects it into a 2D density map that serves as a floorplan proxy. Floorplan localization is then formulated as aligning this proxy with the input floorplan via a 2D similarity transform. To bridge the appearance gap between density maps and architectural floorplans, we adapt a 2D foundation model to learn cross-modal correspondences, introducing a fine-tuning scheme that encourages semantically aligned matches while preserving structural consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements over prior methods, including in extremely sparse settings with as little as a single input image. Our code and data will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: A scalable floorplan localization method for the wild that reconstructs a gravity-aligned 3D scene from unconstrained images, projects it into a 2D density map as a floorplan proxy, and aligns this proxy to a raster floorplan via a 2D similarity transform; it also employs cross-modal fine-tuning of a 2D foundation model to bridge density maps and architectural floorplans, promoting semantic alignment with preserved structure.
AI.motivation: Existing floorplan localization methods assume controlled, small-scale environments and precise vectorized floorplans, limiting applicability to large buildings and raster floorplans.
AI.method: Reconstruct a gravity-aligned 3D scene from unconstrained image collections; project the scene into a 2D density map serving as a floorplan proxy; solve a 2D similarity transform to align the proxy with the input floorplan; fine-tune a 2D foundation model to learn cross-modal correspondences, encouraging semantically aligned matches while preserving structural consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial improvements over prior methods, including in extremely sparse settings with as little as a single input image.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables floorplan localization in the wild and will publicly release code and data.

title: Boundary-targeted Membership Inference Attacks on Safety Classifiers
summary: Safety classifiers are essential safeguards within generative AI systems, filtering harmful content or identifying at-risk users when interacting with large language models. Despite their necessity, these models are trained on sensitive datasets including discussions of self-harm and mental health, raising important, yet poorly understood, privacy concerns. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) allow adversaries to infer membership of examples used to train models. In this work, we hypothesize that identifying the examples on which the classifier is least confident are informative for an adversary to infer membership. This reflects a localized failure of generalization, where the model relies on memorization to resolve ambiguity in the training set. To investigate this, we introduce a new boundary-targeted selection strategy that identifies low confidence examples that amplify the signal of an examples membership within a training set. Our experimental results show that an adversary can recover 19\% of the conversations a safety classifier flagged as indicating user distress, at a 5\% false-positive rate, on a classifier fine-tuned for detecting a user who may require emotional support. This is $3.5$ times more than attacking using state-of-the-art MIA methods alone. Finally, we characterize the boundary laying examples and show that content-based filtering is ineffective for protection, and existing noise strategies can effectively mitigate susceptibility of these examples.

AI.tldr: A boundary-targeted membership inference attack reveals privacy leakage in safety classifiers: low-confidence, boundary examples reveal membership, achieving 19% recovery at 5% FPR on a distress-detection classifier, 3.5x stronger than prior MIA baselines; content-based filtering insufficient for defense; noise-based mitigations can reduce susceptibility.
AI.motivation: Assess privacy risks when training safety classifiers on sensitive conversations (e.g., self-harm/mental health) and determine whether low-confidence, boundary examples provide a stronger signal for membership inference.
AI.method: Introduce a boundary-targeted selection strategy that prioritizes low-confidence training examples to amplify membership signals. Evaluate leakage by attacking a safety classifier fine-tuned to detect users needing emotional support; compare against state-of-the-art MIAs; analyze the nature of boundary examples; assess mitigation via content-based filtering and noise strategies.
AI.result: The attacker recovers 19% of conversations flagged as distress at a 5% FPR, achieving 3.5x improvement over baselines. Boundary examples carry strong membership signal; existing content-based defenses are ineffective; noise-based strategies can mitigate susceptibility.
AI.conclusion: Privacy vulnerability exists in safety classifiers due to boundary/low-confidence training samples. Targeted MIAs outperform generic attacks, but defense via noise additions or data sanitization shows promise; further work needed to generalize findings and strengthen defenses.

title: Rethinking Noise-Robust Training for Frozen Vision Foundation Models: A Cross-Dataset Benchmark with a Case Study of Small-Loss Failure
summary: Frozen Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with lightweight classification heads are increasingly used in medical imaging because they offer efficient and reproducible deployment. Yet noisy-label learning methods for this frozen-feature regime remain poorly understood, and most existing methods still rely on a small-loss assumption inherited from end-to-end training. We present a controlled benchmark of eight noisy-label methods across five medical datasets, three backbones, two noise types, and five noise rates (150 conditions, 6,000 training runs), evaluated with balanced accuracy. The benchmark shows that there is no universal winner: Friedman ranking over the 150 conditions yields $χ^2 = 333.2$ ($p = 4.77 \times 10^{-68}$), ELR wins the most conditions (49/150), while CUFIT attains the best mean rank (2.51). The practical cost of method choice grows sharply with noise severity, from 4.5pp on clean data to 18.8pp at asymmetric 40\% noise. To explain these benchmark-level patterns, we revisit the small-loss assumption in a representative high-risk regime. Under frozen DINOv2 features, clean and noisy loss distributions overlap by 53--61\%, and matched-rate clean-sample detection shows that prediction agreement is markedly more stable than loss ranking under asymmetric noise (3pp vs.\ 13pp precision drop). On ISIC2019 with asymmetric 40\% noise, Co-Teaching reaches 68\% overall accuracy while collapsing to 35.1\% balanced accuracy with zero recall on three minority classes. Together, these results recast noisy-label learning for frozen VFMs as a regime-aware method-selection problem rather than a search for a single dominant algorithm. We conclude with evidence-based guidance and a low-regret feature-space selector for practical recommendation.

AI.tldr: A benchmark study of eight noisy-label methods for frozen vision foundation models (VFMs) in medical imaging, showing no universal winner; method effectiveness depends on noise type/rate and dataset, with regime-aware selection and a feature-space selector recommended.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in understanding noisy-label learning when using frozen VFMs with lightweight heads, where the small-loss assumption from end-to-end training may not hold; provide a large-scale, regime-aware benchmark to guide method choice.
AI.method: Controlled benchmark across five medical datasets, three backbones, two noise types, and five noise rates (150 conditions, ~6k training runs) using balanced accuracy. Evaluates eight methods (e.g., ELR, CuFiT, Co-Teaching, etc.). Analyses include Friedman test (χ2) over conditions, loss distribution comparisons under frozen DINOv2 features, and prediction-agreement vs loss ranking under asymmetric noise. Includes ISIC2019 with 40% asymmetric noise as a case study. Also derives a low-regret feature-space selector for practical recommendations.
AI.result: No universal winner: Friedman χ2=333.2, p≈4.8e-68; ELR wins most conditions (49/150) while CUFIT has best mean rank (2.51). Noise severity greatly impacts cost (4.5 percentage points improvement on clean data to 18.8pp at 40% asymmetric noise). Under frozen DINOv2, clean/noisy loss distributions overlap by 53–61%; prediction agreement is more stable than loss ranking under asymmetry (≈3pp vs 13pp precision drop). On ISIC2019 with 40% asymmetric noise, Co-Teaching reaches 68% overall but only 35.1% balanced accuracy on minority classes. Conclusion: noisy-label learning in frozen VFMs is regime-dependent; favors regime-aware algorithm selection over a single dominant method; provide evidence-based guidance and a feature-space selector for practical recommendation.
AI.conclusion: The study reframes noisy-label learning for frozen VFMs as a regime-aware selection problem rather than seeking a single best algorithm, and offers guidance and a practical feature-space selector to assist practitioners.

title: Target-Aligned Bellman Backup for Cross-domain Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cross-domain offline reinforcement learning (CDRL) aims to improve policy learning in a target domain by leveraging data collected from a source domain. Existing works typically assess the transferability of source-domain data by measuring its similarity to target-domain transitions, and implicitly perform transition-level selection. Transitions that are considered similar are assigned higher weights or rewards, while dissimilar ones are down-weighted. However, transition-level similarity does not necessarily imply consistency in long-term returns. Even visually or dynamically similar transitions may lead to significantly different outcomes in the target domain, which can mislead policy learning and degrade performance. To address this issue, we revisit the fundamental objective of policy learning. Since policy optimization ultimately relies on Bellman targets to evaluate the quality of decisions, we propose to assess the transferability of source-domain transitions based on their alignment with target-domain Bellman targets, rather than superficial transition similarity. Based on this insight, we propose a method termed Target-Aligned Bellman Backup (TABB), which selectively leverages source-domain data by measuring their contribution to accurate Bellman target estimation in the target domain. We evaluate TABB across a broad range of cross-domain offline RL settings with highly limited target-domain data. Experimental results show that TABB consistently achieves strong performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Target-Aligned Bellman Backup (TABB) for cross-domain offline RL; selects source transitions by their alignment with target Bellman targets rather than surface transition similarity, yielding robust improvements when target data are scarce.
AI.motivation: Transition-level similarity does not guarantee consistent long-term returns; policy learning relies on Bellman targets. Aligning data transfer with target Bellman backups should improve decision quality in the target domain.
AI.method: Introduce TABB, which measures and leverages each source transition's contribution to accurate Bellman target estimation in the target domain, selectively incorporating source data based on this alignment instead of transition similarity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse cross-domain offline RL settings with highly limited target data shows that TABB consistently achieves strong performance.
AI.conclusion: Transfer evaluation should be anchored to alignment with target Bellman targets; TABB demonstrates robust improvements in CDRL when target data are scarce.

title: Enhancing Gaze Reasoning in Vision Foundation Models for Gaze Following
summary: Gaze following requires both scene understanding and gaze reasoning to localize the gaze target of an in-scene person. Recently, vision foundation models (VFMs) have demonstrated strong performance on this task, enabling simpler architectures while outperforming prior methods. However, we observe a key limitation of VFM-based approaches: while VFMs substantially improve scene understanding, they contribute little to gaze reasoning. As a result, existing methods often rely on semantically salient objects rather than true gaze cues, leading to degraded performance when targets are not salient. To address this, we propose a novel training mechanism to enhance gaze reasoning in VFMs for gaze following. Our method includes: (1) a head-conditioned local LoRA, which enables localized adaptation to preserve scene token learning while improving head token learning for gaze reasoning; and (2) an out-of-cone penalty, which injects gaze cues into head tokens while aligning them with scene tokens. Experiments on the GazeFollow and VAT datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with particularly strong improvements when gaze targets are not semantically salient. Our findings offer valuable insights for advancing future gaze following research. We will release the code once the paper is accepted.

AI.tldr: Proposes head-conditioned local LoRA and out-of-cone penalty to boost gaze reasoning in vision foundation models for gaze following, achieving state-of-the-art on GazeFollow and VAT, esp. for non-salient targets.
AI.motivation: VFMs improve scene understanding but weak on gaze reasoning; current methods rely on semantically salient objects leading to failure when targets lack salience.
AI.method: Introduce head-conditioned local LoRA to adapt only head tokens while preserving scene token learning; introduce out-of-cone penalty to inject gaze cues into head tokens and align with scene tokens; train VFMs with these components for gaze following.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on GazeFollow and VAT datasets; notable improvements when gaze targets are not semantically salient; code to be released after acceptance.
AI.conclusion: Shows that targeted fine-tuning mechanisms can enhance gaze reasoning in VFMs without sacrificing scene understanding; supports future gaze following research; potential for broader gaze-related tasks.

title: Towards Explainability of SLMs by investigating Token Level Activation
summary: Transformer-based language models such as BERT having 110M+ parameters have revolutionized natural language understanding, yet their internal mechanisms remain largely opaque to researchers and practitioners. Traditional attention-based interpretability methods often emphasize structurally important but semantically weak tokens such as punctuation marks rather than meaningful semantic relationships. This work introduces a lightweight and model-agnostic framework for quantifying token-level representational importance using hidden-state activation strengths at Layer 8 of BERT. The proposed Activation Flow Network (AFN) framework computes Token Activation Strength using the L2 norm of Layer-8 hidden representations, enabling direct ranking of semantically salient tokens. The study further introduces a threshold-based activation bucket formulation that partitions tokens into HIGH-activation and LOW-activation groups using an empirical upper-quartile activation boundary. Experimental observations demonstrate that semantically meaningful content words consistently occupy the HIGH-activation bucket and dominate representational activation shifts, while structurally supportive tokens contribute comparatively less. The results suggest that Layer 8 acts as a critical semantic consolidation zone balancing structural and semantic information processing. By revealing how activation magnitudes concentrate around semantically informative tokens, this work provides an interpretable and computationally efficient alternative to attentioncentric analysis, contributing toward transforming BERT from a "black box" into a more transparent "glass box" model for natural language understanding.

AI.tldr: Activation Flow Network (AFN) quantifies token-level semantic activation in BERT by using Layer-8 hidden-state L2 norms to rank tokens, revealing high-activation content words and proposing Layer 8 as a semantic consolidation zone, offering a lightweight alternative to attention-based interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address the opacity of BERT representations and the bias of attention-based interpretability toward structurally important but semantically weak tokens; to provide a lightweight, model-agnostic, and computationally efficient framework for identifying semantically salient tokens.
AI.method: Compute Token Activation Strength as the L2 norm of Layer-8 hidden representations. Rank tokens by this strength and use an empirical upper-quartile threshold to partition tokens into HIGH-activation and LOW-activation buckets. Analyze the distribution of semantic content vs. function/structure tokens across buckets and assess activation shifts.
AI.result: Semantically meaningful content words consistently occupy the HIGH-activation bucket and dominate representational activation shifts; structurally supportive tokens show comparatively lower activation. Layer 8 appears to act as a semantic consolidation zone balancing structural and semantic information processing.
AI.conclusion: Activation magnitudes concentrate around semantically informative tokens, making AFN a transparent and computationally efficient alternative to attention-centric analyses and contributing to the glass-box interpretation of BERT.

title: GLeVE: Graph-Guided Lesion Grounding with Proposal Verification in 3D CT
summary: Grounding radiology report descriptions to 3D CT volumes is essential for verifiable clinical interpretation, yet remains challenging due to the semantic-spatial gap between free-text narratives and volumetric anatomy. Existing report-assisted and vision-language grounding methods typically rely on phrase-level alignment or dense pixel supervision, resulting in limited lesion-wise correspondence and suboptimal localization accuracy. We propose GLeVE, a graph-guided lesion grounding framework with anatomical prior verification and octree-based autoregressive refinement. GLeVE treats each lesion description as an atomic semantic unit and encodes organ attribution, attributes, and inter-lesion relations through relation-aware graph reasoning to produce discriminative lesion-wise queries. Anatomy-aware proposal generation with region-level verification enforces one-to-one text-lesion alignment, while hierarchical octree refinement progressively improves boundary delineation. Experiments on AbdomenAtlas 3.0 demonstrate consistent gains over classical multimodal foundation models and report-supervised baselines in both segmentation accuracy and lesion-level localization.

AI.tldr: A graph-guided lesion grounding framework (GLeVE) with anatomy priors and octree refinement that improves lesion-wise grounding and segmentation in 3D CT volumes.
AI.motivation: Bridging the semantic-spatial gap between free-text radiology reports and volumetric CT anatomy; existing methods rely on phrase-level alignment or dense supervision, limiting lesion-wise correspondence and localization accuracy.
AI.method: Treat each lesion description as an atomic semantic unit and encode organ attribution, attributes, and inter-lesion relations via relation-aware graph reasoning to produce discriminative lesion-wise queries. Use anatomy-aware proposal generation with region-level verification to enforce one-to-one text-lesion alignment, followed by hierarchical octree refinement for progressive boundary delineation.
AI.result: On AbdomenAtlas 3.0, GLeVE achieves consistent gains over classical multimodal foundation models and text-supervised baselines in both segmentation accuracy and lesion-level localization.
AI.conclusion: GLeVE effectively grounds radiology narratives to 3D CT volumes by leveraging anatomy priors, graph reasoning among lesions, and octree-based refinement for accurate, lesion-wise localization and segmentation.

title: Efficient Higher-order Subgraph Attribution via Message Passing
summary: Explaining graph neural networks (GNNs) has become more and more important recently. Higher-order interpretation schemes, such as GNN-LRP (layer-wise relevance propagation for GNN), emerged as powerful tools for unraveling how different features interact thereby contributing to explaining GNNs. GNN-LRP gives a relevance attribution of walks between nodes at each layer, and the subgraph attribution is expressed as a sum over exponentially many such walks. In this work, we demonstrate that such exponential complexity can be avoided. In particular, we propose novel algorithms that enable to attribute subgraphs with GNN-LRP in linear-time (w.r.t. the network depth). Our algorithms are derived via message passing techniques that make use of the distributive property, thereby directly computing quantities for higher-order explanations. We further adapt our efficient algorithms to compute a generalization of subgraph attributions that also takes into account the neighboring graph features. Experimental results show the significant acceleration of the proposed algorithms and demonstrate the high usefulness and scalability of our novel generalized subgraph attribution method.

AI.tldr: Proposes linear-time algorithms for GNN-LRP subgraph attributions by leveraging distributive properties and message passing, avoiding exponential sums over walks; extends to a generalized attribution that includes neighboring feature contributions; empirically demonstrates acceleration and scalability.
AI.motivation: Explainability of GNNs with higher-order attributions is hindered by exponential complexity since subgraph attributions sum over exponentially many walks; a scalable method is needed for practical analysis and interpretation.
AI.method: Develop linear-time algorithms derived via message passing that exploit distributive properties to compute higher-order GNN-LRP quantities directly; adapt the framework to a generalized subgraph attribution that also accounts for neighboring graph features.
AI.result: Experiments show significant acceleration of the proposed algorithms and demonstrate the usefulness and scalability of the generalized subgraph attribution method.
AI.conclusion: Efficiently computable higher-order GNN explanations are feasible; the proposed linear-time, distributive-property-based approach makes subgraph attributions scalable and extendable to include neighbor features.

title: H-Flow: Self-supervised Human Scene Flow via Physics-inspired Joint Multi-modal Learning
summary: Parametric human models capture global pose but cannot represent the non-rigid surface dynamics of clothing and soft tissue. Generic scene flow estimates dense motion but breaks down on articulated bodies, where pixel-level supervision is also intractable to acquire. We introduce H-Flow, a dense human scene flow that captures both skeletal kinematics and surface deformation. A unified multi-head transformer estimates flow from monocular video, jointly predicting pose and depth as companion outputs. The challenge lies in the lack of supervision. In place of unattainable labels, we anchor the network in the physics of human motion, encoding geometric, structural, and biomechanical priors as cross-modal training objectives. We further introduce DynAct4D, a high-fidelity synthetic benchmark providing dense flow annotations across diverse subjects, garments, and motions. On standard benchmarks, H-Flow outperforms scene-flow and parametric baselines, and generalizes zero-shot to in-the-wild video. Code, models, and the DynAct4D benchmark will be released upon publication

AI.tldr: H-Flow is a dense monocular human scene flow model that jointly estimates pose, depth, and surface deformation using a unified transformer, trained with physics-based cross-modal priors in lieu of labels; DynAct4D provides a high-fidelity synthetic benchmark for dense flow across diverse subjects and motions; results show state-of-the-art performance and zero-shot generalization to in-the-wild video; code and benchmark to be released.
AI.motivation: Parametric human models capture global pose but miss non-rigid surface dynamics of clothing/soft tissue; generic scene flow is unreliable on articulated bodies and acquiring pixel-level supervision is impractical; a supervision-efficient method that jointly models pose and surface deformation is needed.
AI.method: A unified multi-head transformer ingests monocular video and outputs dense scene flow plus companion pose and depth predictions. Training leverages cross-modal objectives encoding geometric, structural, and biomechanical priors as physics-informed supervision, replacing unattainable labels. DynAct4D is introduced as a high-fidelity synthetic benchmark with dense flow annotations across diverse subjects, garments, and motions.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks, H-Flow surpasses both conventional scene-flow methods and parametric baselines. It also generalizes zero-shot to in-the-wild video.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed, cross-modal training enables accurate dense human scene flow without full supervision, demonstrating the viability of integrating kinematic and surface deformation modeling. DynAct4D provides a rigorous synthetic benchmark for evaluation; code, models, and the benchmark are slated for release.

title: Hybrid Kolmogorov-Arnold Network and XGBoost Framework for Week-Ahead Price Forecasting in Australia's National Electricity Market
comment: The 24th IEEE International Conference on Industrial Informatics, 2026
summary: Accurate electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for market participants to support operational planning and risk management, yet remains challenging due to strong volatility, nonlinear dynamics, and frequent extreme price spikes. These challenges are particularly pronounced in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), where high renewable penetration further increases uncertainty. This paper investigates week-ahead electricity price forecasting and proposes a hybrid KAN+XGBoost framework that integrates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with tree-based learning. The proposed approach combines the global nonlinear representation capability of KAN with the local robustness of XGBoost to capture both long-term dependencies and short-term price fluctuations. Experiments are conducted on real-world NEM data using an expanding window evaluation strategy. The results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms benchmark methods, including SARIMAX, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), standalone KAN, and XGBoost, reducing MAE by approximately 12% compared to XGBoost and by over 50% compared to a naive baseline. The results suggest that hybrid learning strategies provide an effective and robust solution for electricity price forecasting in highly dynamic electricity markets.

AI.tldr: Hybrid KAN+XGBoost for week-ahead EPF in the NEM; reports MAE improvements over SARIMAX, LSTM, standalone KAN, and XGBoost, with ~12% gain over XGBoost and >50% over naive baseline.
AI.motivation: To address the strong volatility, nonlinear dynamics, and extreme price spikes in electricity prices, especially in the NEM with high renewable penetration, by improving week-ahead forecast accuracy for market participants.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid architecture that combines Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with XGBoost. KAN provides global nonlinear representation, while XGBoost offers local robustness. The model is evaluated using an expanding window strategy on real-world NEM data.
