AI.result: The hybrid model outperforms benchmark methods (SARIMAX, LSTM, standalone KAN, and XGBoost). Reported MAE reductions: ~12% versus XGBoost and >50% versus a naive baseline.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid learning strategies that blend global nonlinear representation with local robustness are effective for EPF in highly dynamic electricity markets such as the Australian NEM.

title: AtomicMotion: Learning Human Motion From Different Human Parts
summary: Accurately reconstructing full-body poses from sparse head and hand trajectories is a foundational challenge for immersive AR/VR telepresence. Current methods often struggle with error accumulation and unnatural joint coordination, primarily because they treat the human body as a monolithic entity, thereby failing to capture the fine-grained ``atomic intents'' embedded in subtle signal variations and overlooking the inherent structural topology. To bridge this gap, we present AtomicMotion, a framework designed to decouple and re-integrate body dynamics through three core innovations. First, we introduce a logical body partitioning scheme that decomposes the skeleton into five distinct clusters based on functional intent; this ensures that each partition preserves internal joint synergies while isolating local motion primitives. Second, to robustly map sparse inputs to high-dimensional poses, we employ a masked full-body pre-conditioning strategy during training, forcing the model to internalize global skeletal topology and latent kinematic constraints. Finally, addressing the limitations of vanilla spatial attention, which often ignores fixed physiological connectivity, we propose Kinematic Attention. By embedding the classical kinematic tree structure into the attention mechanism, we ensure biological plausibility in the synthesized motions. Extensive evaluations on the AMASS dataset demonstrate that AtomicMotion significantly outperforms existing baselines, yielding higher reconstruction fidelity and superior biomechanical realism.

AI.tldr: AtomicMotion decouples body dynamics into five functional clusters, uses masked pre-conditioning, and introduces Kinematic Attention to improve full-body pose reconstruction from sparse head/hand cues, achieving improved fidelity and biomechanical realism on AMASS.
AI.motivation: Current full-body reconstruction from sparse inputs suffers from error accumulation and unrealistic joint coordination due to treating the body as a single entity and ignoring skeletal topology.
AI.method: Three pillars: (1) logical body partitioning into five clusters by functional intent; (2) masked full-body pre-conditioning during training to enforce global topology and latent kinematic constraints; (3) Kinematic Attention that embeds the kinematic tree into attention to enforce physiological connectivity.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on AMASS show AtomicMotion significantly outperforms baselines in reconstruction fidelity and biomechanical realism.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled approach to decoupled-reintegration of body dynamics, enabling more accurate, plausible motion synthesis for AR/VR telepresence; future work may address scalability to varied skeletons, real-time performance, and broader action domains.

title: A Posterior-Predictive Variance Decomposition for Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty in Wind Power Forecasting
summary: Accurate wind power forecasting requires reliable uncertainty quantification, yet most existing methods report a single predictive uncertainty that conflates epistemic and aleatoric sources. This paper applies the law of total variance to the joint setting of heteroscedastic neural network regression and Bayesian posterior approximation, deriving an explicit decomposition of total uncertainty (TU) into aleatoric (AU) and epistemic (EU) components. The resulting estimators are compatible with standard posterior-approximation methods and with $β$-NLL training to regulate the mean--variance learning trade-off. A wind power--specific evaluation framework is proposed to validate disentanglement without access to ground-truth uncertainty labels, comprising three modules: controlled synthetic experiments to verify responses to heteroscedastic noise and distribution shift; data-property--driven validation on a real-world wind turbine SCADA dataset; and dataset-size scaling experiments to examine the predicted asymptotic behavior of EU. Across synthetic and real-world experiments, the decomposed AU and EU components respond in theoretically consistent directions to noise structure, distributional shift, and training-scale variation, supporting the theoretical consistency and operational utility of the proposed decomposition and evaluation protocol.

AI.tldr: Decomposition of total predictive uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components for wind power forecasting using law of total variance, with estimators compatible with Bayesian posterior approximations and beta-NLL training; wind-specific evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: Current predictive uncertainty often conflates epistemic and aleatoric sources, hindering interpretation and decision-making. A clear decomposition enables better calibration, risk assessment, and targeted improvements (data/model).
AI.method: Derive TU = AU + EU using the law of total variance in the setting of heteroscedastic neural network regression with Bayesian posterior approximation. Propose estimators for AU and EU compatible with standard posterior-approximation methods and with beta-NLL training to balance mean–variance learning. Develop a wind power–specific evaluation framework with three modules: synthetic controlled experiments (noise and distribution shift), data-property–driven validation on real-world wind turbine SCADA data, and dataset-size scaling to study EU asymptotics.
AI.result: Across synthetic and real data, AU and EU respond in directions consistent with changes in noise, distribution shift, and training scale, supporting theoretical consistency and utility of the decomposition and evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: A principled, implementable decomposition of predictive uncertainty into AU and EU within common Bayesian deep-learning frameworks is demonstrated, with a dedicated wind-forecasting evaluation protocol enabling disentanglement without ground-truth uncertainty labels; the framework is extensible to other domains and models.

title: From Baseline to Follow-Up: Counterfactual Spine DXA Image Synthesis in UK Biobank Using a Causal Hierarchical Variational Autoencoder
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at the 48th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC 2026)
summary: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is widely used for large-scale skeletal assessment, yet learning controllable and interpretable factor-specific anatomical variation remains challenging. We propose a metadata-conditioned causal hierarchical variational autoencoder (CHVAE) for causally consistent generation of anteroposterior (AP) spine DXA images from the UK Biobank (UKB). The model is trained on 3,743 raw AP spine scans from the first imaging visit and conditioned on basic participant attributes and lumbar morphometry. Causal consistency is evaluated in a baseline-to-follow-up setting using abduction--action--prediction (AAP): latent variables are abducted from baseline images, age is intervened to the repeat-imaging value, and the resulting counterfactual follow-up morphometry is compared with observed repeat-imaging measurements. Results show strong absolute-level agreement for key vertebral morphometry variables under age intervention, supporting intervention-aligned synthesis of anatomically plausible DXA images.

AI.tldr: A metadata-conditioned causal hierarchical VAE (CHVAE) generates causally-consistent AP spine DXA images from UK Biobank; conditioned on basic attributes and lumbar morphometry; causal validity tested via abduction–action–prediction (AAP) with age intervention; reports strong morphometry agreement under intervention.
AI.motivation: Enable controllable, interpretable, and causally consistent generation of anatomically plausible DXA images to support large-scale skeletal phenotyping and causal inference.
AI.method: Train a CHVAE conditioned on participant attributes and lumbar morphometry using 3,743 AP spine scans from UKB. Assess causal consistency via AAP: abduce latent variables from baseline, intervene on age to follow-up value, generate counterfactual follow-up morphometry, compare to observed follow-up measurements.
AI.result: Observed strong absolute-level agreement for key vertebral morphometry variables under age intervention, indicating intervention-aligned, anatomically plausible synthesis of DXA images.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of metadata-conditioned, causally-consistent image synthesis in DXA and supports use of latent factors for interpretable skeletal variation; potential for improved large-scale phenotyping and causal inference.

title: Cross-Species RSA Reveals Conserved Early Visual Alignment but Divergent Higher-Area Rankings Across Human fMRI and Macaque Electrophysiology
summary: Does the relationship between learning rules and brain alignment generalize across species? We extend our prior finding that untrained CNNs match backpropagation at human V1 by testing the same five learning rules against macaque electrophysiology. The rules are backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP), and an untrained random-weights baseline. The macaque data come from two datasets: MajajHong2015 (V4/IT, 3,200 stimulus presentations, 88/168 neurons) and FreemanZiemba2013 (V1/V2, 135 stimuli, 102/103 neurons). Using RSA with identical model weights from our human study, we find: (1) all models achieve higher alignment with macaque early visual cortex (rho = 0.15-0.30 at V1/V2) than with human fMRI (rho = 0.01-0.08), consistent with the higher signal-to-noise ratio of electrophysiology; (2) STDP and PC produce the highest macaque V1/V2 alignment (rho ~ 0.30 and 0.28), consistent with their leading position among trained rules in human V1; (3) at IT, learning rule rankings show no detectable correlation across species (Kendall's tau = 0.00, p = 1.00), though this null result is expected given that n = 5 provides power only at tau = +/-1.0, and is further confounded by stimulus set differences; (4) a pretrained ResNet-50 (ImageNet) achieves rho = 0.25 at macaque IT, substantially above all custom CNN conditions (rho = 0.07-0.14), suggesting IT alignment is limited by model capacity and training data rather than by the learning rule. Noise ceilings, multi-seed variability (5 seeds), and a stimulus-control analysis are reported. These results demonstrate that early visual alignment is robust across species, while higher-area alignment is modulated by model capacity and stimulus domain.

AI.tldr: Early visual alignment across species is robust; STDP and predictive coding best match macaque V1/V2; IT alignment limited by model capacity/training data; pretrained CNNs outperform custom CNNs at IT.
AI.motivation: Assess whether the link between biological learning rules and brain alignment generalizes from humans to macaques across the visual hierarchy.
AI.method: Use identical model weights from a human study to evaluate five learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP, random) against macaque electrophysiology from MajajHong2015 (V4/IT) and FreemanZiemba2013 (V1/V2) using RSA. Compare with human fMRI, report noise ceilings and multi-seed variability; include stimulus-control analyses; benchmark against a pretrained ResNet-50 (ImageNet).
AI.result: (1) All models show higher alignment with macaque early visual cortex (rho≈0.15–0.30 at V1/V2) than with human fMRI (rho≈0.01–0.08). (2) STDP and PC yield the highest V1/V2 alignment (rho≈0.30 and 0.28). (3) IT: learning-rule rankings show no detectable cross-species correlation (Kendall’s tau=0.00, p=1.00; power limited with n=5). (4) A pretrained ResNet-50 attains rho=0.25 at IT, well above custom CNNs (rho=0.07–0.14), suggesting IT alignment is limited by model capacity/training data rather than the learning rule. Noise ceilings, multi-seed variability (5 seeds), and stimulus-control analyses are reported.
AI.conclusion: Early visual alignment is robust across species, while higher-area alignment depends on model capacity and the stimulus domain.

title: What Does the Caption Really Say? Counterfactual Phrase Intervention for Compositional Data Selection in Vision-Language Pretraining
summary: CLIP-style contrastive pretraining typically curates web-scale image-text pairs using sample-level filtering signals, often based on pair-level alignment. We show that this signal saturates: once coarse mismatches are removed, stricter global filtering no longer tracks the compositional supervision provided by the retained captions. The reason is structural - a global score conflates whether a pair is broadly plausible with whether the individual object, attribute, and relation phrases inside the caption materially support the image-text match. The latter is what compositional generalization demands, yet pair-level filters are blind to it. We address this with Counterfactual Phrase Intervention (CPI), a phrase-level curation framework that converts controlled nonce-token substitutions into image-conditioned phrase-sensitivity scores. CPI uses global alignment only for coarse mismatch removal, then ranks the surviving pool by whether caption phrases measurably affect the image-text score under controlled substitution. We frame CPI as a first-order phrase-sensitivity signal rather than a grounding or identification result, and evaluate it at CC3M scale. Ranking by this signal yields a 50%-data subset that improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +1.91 over the full-data baseline and +1.00 over alignment-only filtering at matched budget, while improving SugarCrepe overall and preserving general transfer. CPI is loss-orthogonal: applied unchanged to NegCLIP, it further improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +3.84, with additional CE-CLIP gains in the main text.

AI.tldr: CPI introduces phrase-level supervision for image-text contrastive pretraining to overcome global-filter saturation by leveraging controlled nonce substitutions to measure phrase sensitivity, yielding better compositional generalization without sacrificing transfer.
AI.motivation: Global, pair-level filtering in CLIP-style pretraining saturates after removing coarse mismatches; it fails to capture compositional cues tied to individual object/attribute/relation phrases, hindering compositional generalization.
AI.method: Counterfactual Phrase Intervention (CPI): first use global alignment for coarse mismatch removal, then rank surviving captions by phrase-level sensitivity. This is done by controlled nonce-token substitutions to measure how caption phrases affect the image-text score, producing a first-order phrase-sensitivity signal rather than grounding.
AI.result: On CC3M-scale data, selecting ~50% of data via CPI improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +1.91 over full-data baseline and +1.00 over alignment-only filtering at same budget; SugarCrepe improves, general transfer preserved. CPI is loss-orthogonal and also yields gains when applied to NegCLIP (+3.84 on VL-CheckList-VG Relation) and CE-CLIP gains in main text.
AI.conclusion: Phrase-level, counterfactual curation via CPI provides a complementary, loss-orthogonal signal that enhances compositional generalization and evaluation metrics in image-text pretraining without sacrificing general transfer; it generalizes beyond standard alignment-based filtering.

title: SegCompass: Exploring Interpretable Alignment with Sparse Autoencoders for Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. 15 pages, 9 figures, 6 tables
summary: While large language models provide strong compositional reasoning, existing reasoning segmentation pipelines fail to transparently connect this reasoning to visual perception. Current methods, such as latent query alignment, are end-to-end yet opaque "black boxes". Conversely, textual localization readout is merely readable, not truly interpretable, often functioning as an unconstrained post-hoc step. To bridge this interpretability gap, we propose SegCompass, an end-to-end model that leverages a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to forge an explicit, interpretable, and differentiable alignment pathway. Given an image-instruction pair, SegCompass first generates a chain-of-thought (CoT) trace. The core of our method is an SAE that maps both the CoT and visual tokens into a shared, high-dimensional sparse concept space. A query codebook selects salient concepts from this space, which are then spatially grounded by a slot mapper into a multi-slot heatmap that guides the final mask decoder. The entire model is trained jointly, unifying reinforcement learning for the reasoning path with standard segmentation supervision. This SAE-driven interface provides a "white-box" connection that is significantly more traceable than latent queries and more coherent than textual readouts. Extensive experiments on five challenging benchmarks demonstrate that SegCompass matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance. Crucially, our visual and quantitative analyses show a strong correlation between the quality of the learned sparse concepts and final mask accuracy, confirming that SegCompass achieves superior results through its enhanced and inspectable alignment. Code is available at https://github.com/ZhenyuLU-Heliodore/SegCompass.

AI.tldr: SegCompass introduces a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to align chain-of-thought reasoning with visual tokens in a shared sparse concept space, enabling an interpretable, differentiable pathway for visual segmentation. It uses a query codebook and slot mapper to ground concepts into a multi-slot heatmap feeding a mask decoder, trained end-to-end with RL for reasoning and standard segmentation loss. It achieves state-of-the-art-like performance on five benchmarks and provides observable correlations between concept quality and mask accuracy, with code released.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between strong reasoning capabilities of large language models and transparent visual perception alignment. Existing approaches (e.g., latent query alignment) are opaque end-to-end, while textual readouts are readable but not truly interpretable. A lightweight, differentiable, and interpretable bridge is needed to connect reasoning traces to grounded visual representations.
AI.method: An end-to-end model, SegCompass, that: (1) generates a chain-of-thought (CoT) trace for an image-instruction pair; (2) uses a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to map both CoT tokens and visual tokens into a shared high-dimensional sparse concept space; (3) employs a query codebook to select salient concepts; (4) uses a slot mapper to produce a multi-slot heatmap; (5) feeds the heatmap into a final mask decoder; (6) trains jointly with reinforcement learning to optimize the reasoning path and standard segmentation supervision, yielding a white-box, interpretable alignment between CoT and visual concepts.
AI.result: SegCompass matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance on five challenging segmentation benchmarks. Visual and quantitative analyses show a strong correlation between the quality of the learned sparse concepts and final mask accuracy, indicating that the improved alignment directly drives segmentation performance.
AI.conclusion: The SAE-driven interface provides a transparent, differentiable bridge between reasoning and perception, achieving superior interpretability relative to latent-query methods and more coherent than plain textual readouts, while delivering competitive segmentation performance. The approach enables inspectable alignment between CoT and visual concepts, and code availability facilitates reproducibility.

title: SEGA: Spectral-Energy Guided Attention for Resolution Extrapolation in Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion transformers (DiTs) have emerged as a dominant architecture for text-to-image generation, yet their performance drops when generating at resolutions beyond their training range. Existing training-free approaches mitigate this by modifying inference-time attention behavior, often through Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) extrapolation combined with attention scaling. However, these strategies apply a uniform and content-agnostic scaling across RoPE components with distinct frequency characteristics, inducing a trade-off between preserving global structure and recovering fine detail. We introduce SEGA, a training-free method that dynamically scales attention across RoPE components according to the latent's spatial-frequency structure at each denoising step. This adaptive scaling improves both structural coherence and fine-detail fidelity. Experiments show that SEGA consistently improves high-resolution synthesis across multiple target resolutions, outperforming state-of-the-art training-free baselines.

AI.tldr: SEGA is a training-free method that adaptively scales diffusion-attention across RoPE components using the latent spatial-frequency structure at each denoising step, boosting high-resolution image synthesis and beating prior training-free baselines.
AI.motivation: Diffusion transformers suffer performance drops when generating beyond their training resolution. Existing training-free fixes apply uniform, content-agnostic RoPE scaling, trading off global coherence and fine detail; a content-aware, frequency-guided approach is needed.
AI.method: SEGA dynamically scales attention across RoPE components at each denoising step based on the latent's spatial-frequency content, without requiring training.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in high-resolution synthesis across multiple target resolutions, outperforming state-of-the-art training-free baselines.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, frequency-aware RoPE scaling during inference enhances both structural coherence and fine detail in high-resolution generation without additional training.

title: AMUSE: Anytime Muon with Stable Gradient Evaluation
summary: Modern deep learning commonly relies on AdamW with prescribed learning rate schedules, but recent works challenge both components: Schedule-Free optimization removes explicit schedules via iterate averaging, and Muon improves the update geometry by orthogonalizing momentum for matrix parameters. Despite Muon's strong empirical performance, its underlying mechanism remains partially understood. We study Muon through the river-valley loss landscape, where useful training progress occurs along a flat, low-curvature bulk subspace (the river), while high-curvature dominant directions form steep valley walls that induce oscillations. We empirically show that while Muon's orthogonalization accelerates river progress by increasing the bulk component, it also amplifies dominant-direction noise, causing oscillatory trajectories. Building on this, we propose Anytime MUon with Stable gradient Evaluation (AMUSE), which integrates Muon's rapid bulk progress with the stabilizing effect of Schedule-Free averaging. AMUSE uses a time-varying interpolation coefficient that initially evaluates gradients near the fast Muon sequence for rapid adaptation, then gradually shifts toward the stable averaged sequence to suppress valley-wall oscillations. As a result, AMUSE requires no learning rate schedules and supports anytime training. Across vision tasks and large language model pretraining, AMUSE consistently improves the performance-iteration Pareto frontier over (Schedule-Free) AdamW and Muon.

AI.tldr: AMUSE combines Muon's fast bulk progress with Schedule-Free averaging through a time-varying interpolation, enabling schedule-free training and improved performance-efficiency (Pareto frontier) across vision and LLM tasks.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the need for learning-rate schedules and the oscillations caused by high-curvature directions by uniting fast bulk descent (Muon) with gradient averaging stability (Schedule-Free).
AI.method: Introduce Anytime MUon with Stable gradient Evaluation (AMUSE): use a dynamic interpolation coefficient that starts near Muon-like rapid gradient updates and gradually shifts toward the averaged sequence, blending fast adaptation with stabilized convergence; requires no learning-rate schedules and supports anytime training; evaluated on vision benchmarks and large-language-model pretraining, compared against Schedule-Free AdamW and Muon.
AI.result: AMUSE consistently improves the performance-iteration Pareto frontier relative to Schedule-Free AdamW and Muon across tested vision tasks and LLM pretraining, indicating better efficiency-stability trade-offs and avoiding valley-wall oscillations while maintaining rapid bulk progress.
AI.conclusion: AMUSE achieves schedule-free, anytime training by harmonizing Muon’s rapid bulk progress with stable gradient averaging, yielding robust optimization and improved Pareto efficiency on diverse large-scale tasks.

title: From Abstraction to Instantiation: Learning Behavioral Representation for Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often suffer from performance degradation under distribution shifts, as they struggle to learn generalized behavior representations across varying environments. While existing approaches attempt to construct behavior representations through action-centric latent variables, they are often limited by short-horizon temporal fragmentation and static execution-alignment, leading to inconsistent behaviors in complex scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{BehaviorVLA}, a framework that facilitates robust manipulation through the learning of a temporally coherent behavioral representations. Our approach features two symmetric components: (1) the \textbf{Visuomotor Behavior Encoder (VBE)}, which utilizes a causal Mamba-based architecture to aggregate long-horizon trajectory information into a unified behavior representation; and (2) the \textbf{Phase-conditioned Behavior Decoder (PBD)}, which decodes this representation into precise actions by dynamically aligning task-level priors with real-time execution progress. Experiments on RoboTwin 2.0, LIBERO, and CALVIN demonstrate state-of-the-art success rates of 58\%, 98\%, and 4.36 (Avg.Len), respectively. Notably, in real-world sim-to-real transfer, BehaviorVLA matches the performance of OpenVLA-OFT using only 50\% of the demonstration data, showcasing its superior data efficiency and generalization.

AI.tldr: A temporally coherent visuomotor framework (BehaviorVLA) with VBE and PBD enables robust VLA manipulation under distribution shifts, achieving state-of-the-art results and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models often degrade under distribution shifts due to short-horizon, action-centric representations and static execution alignment; there is a need for temporally coherent behavior representations that generalize across environments.
AI.method: Two symmetric modules: (1) Visuomotor Behavior Encoder (VBE) with a causal Mamba-based architecture that aggregates long-horizon trajectory information into a unified behavior representation; (2) Phase-conditioned Behavior Decoder (PBD) that decodes the representation into actions by dynamically aligning task-level priors with real-time execution progress.
AI.result: State-of-the-art success rates: RoboTwin 2.0 58%, LIBERO 98%, CALVIN 4.36 (Avg.Len). In real-world sim-to-real transfer, BehaviorVLA matches OpenVLA-OFT with only 50% of demonstrations, indicating superior data efficiency and generalization.
AI.conclusion: BehaviorVLA offers robust, data-efficient manipulation by learning temporally coherent behavior representations, achieving strong generalization under distribution shifts and competitive real-world performance.

title: Don't Forget the Critic: Value-Based Data Rehearsal for Multi-Cyclic Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Data rehearsal has emerged as a leading approach for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in Continual Reinforcement Learning (CRL). However, existing work remains confined to policy gradient frameworks, regularizing only actors due to the performance degradation incurred by critic regularization. This actor-centric approach overlooks the potential of data rehearsal for value function approximation. Moreover, existing evaluations in CRL rarely consider multi-cyclic environments where task sequences repeat, a critical real-world scenario that exacerbates forgetting and plasticity. We investigate data rehearsal for Deep Q-Networks using Q-value regularization in multi-cyclic settings and propose Qreg+NWLU which introduces two simple modifications: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values throughout training, and (2) "No-Wait" regularization that applies immediately rather than after the first task. Together, these modifications yield improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer over Qreg and conventional CRL methods within value function approximation settings.

AI.tldr: Q-value data rehearsal for DQN in CRL with multi-cycle tasks, via continuous Q-value rehearsal and No-Wait regularization, improving forgetting and transfer vs Qreg.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in continual RL remains a problem, with prior work focusing on policy-gradient actors. Exploring data rehearsal for value-function approximation in multi-cycle environments addresses a realistic challenge where tasks repeat.
AI.method: Propose Qreg+NWLU: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values during training, (2) No-Wait regularization that applies immediately rather than after the first task.
AI.result: Demonstrates improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer compared to Qreg and standard CRL methods within value-function approximation settings.
AI.conclusion: Two simple modifications enable effective data-rehearsal-based value-function regularization in CRL for multi-cycle tasks, improving efficiency and mitigation of forgetting and enhancing transfer.

title: Slimmable ConvNeXt: Width-Adaptive Inference for Efficient Multi-Device Deployment
comment: Accepted at Mobile AI Workshop 2026 (CVPR'26 Workshop)
summary: Deploying vision models across devices with varying resource constraints, or even on a single device where available compute fluctuates due to battery state, thermal throttling, or latency deadlines, typically requires training and maintaining separate models. Width-adaptive inference addresses this by training a single set of shared weights containing multiple nested subnetworks of increasing capacity, but prior CNN-based approaches required switchable batch normalization, while recent scalable methods have focused on Vision Transformers. We present Slimmable ConvNeXt, which shows that ConvNeXt's modern design, specifically LayerNorm and inverted bottlenecks, makes it particularly suited for channel-width slimming, eliminating the normalization overhead of classical slimmable networks and producing a simpler training pipeline than both prior CNN and ViT approaches. On ImageNet-1k, Slimmable ConvNeXt-T with 3 subnetworks achieves 80.8% top-1 accuracy at 4.5 GMACs and 77.4% at 1.2 GMACs, trained from scratch for 600 epochs. At comparable compute, this exceeds HydraViT's 6-head subnetwork (78.4% at 4.6 GMACs) by 2.4 percentage points and its 3-head configuration (73.0% at 1.3 GMACs) by 4.4 percentage points, while also outperforming MatFormer-S (78.6%) and SortedNet-S (78.2%) at the same GMACs. Scaling to Slimmable ConvNeXt-B further improves maximum accuracy to 82.8% at 15.35 GMACs.

AI.tldr: Slimmable ConvNeXt demonstrates effective width-adaptive CNN by training a single weight set with multiple nested subnetworks, achieving state-of-the-art-like results on ImageNet across widths, and surpassing several baselines at matching GMACs; ConvNeXt-B reaches 82.8% top-1 at 15.35 GMACs.
AI.motivation: To handle devices with diverse or fluctuating compute without maintaining multiple models; width-adaptive inference enables a single model with nested subnetworks, avoiding separate BN switching issues and enabling simpler training than prior CNN or ViT approaches.
AI.method: Apply Slimmable (channel-width slimming) to ConvNeXt using LayerNorm and inverted bottlenecks to avoid normalization overhead; train a single model with multiple nested subnetworks; evaluate on ImageNet-1k; compare to HydraViT, MatFormer-S, SortedNet-S; scale to ConvNeXt-B.
AI.result: 3-subnet ConvNeXt-T achieves 80.8% top-1 at 4.5 GMACs and 77.4% at 1.2 GMACs; outperforms HydraViT (78.4% at 4.6 GMACs; 73.0% at 1.3 GMACs for 6-head and 3-head) and MatFormer-S/SortedNet-S at same GMACs; ConvNeXt-B reaches 82.8% top-1 at 15.35 GMACs.
AI.conclusion: Modern CNN designs like ConvNeXt, when paired with slimming via LayerNorm and inverted bottlenecks, are well-suited for width-adaptive inference, offering simpler training and competitive or superior accuracy across widths compared to ViT-based scalable methods.

title: Swift Sampling: Selecting Temporal Surprises via Taylor Series
summary: While most frames in long-form video are redundant, the critical information resides in temporal surprises: moments where the actual visual features deviate from their predicted evolution. Inspired by the human brain's predictive coding, we introduce Swift Sampling, an elegant, training-free frame selection algorithm that automatically identifies high-information moments in a video. Specifically, we model a video as a differentiable trajectory in the visual latent space and compute the velocity and acceleration of its features. Then, we apply Taylor expansion to project the expected path of subsequent frames. Frames that diverge sharply from this predicted manifold are identified as temporally surprising frames and selected for sampling. Unlike prior training-free methods that rely on auxiliary networks or video-specific hyperparameter tuning, Swift Sampling is incredibly lightweight, adding only 0.02x additional computational cost over baseline making it 30x cheaper overhead than leading baselines. Across three long-video question answering benchmarks and 10 different downstream tasks, Swift Sampling outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines. It is especially powerful for long videos with limited frame budgets improving accuracy by up to +12.5 points.

AI.tldr: Lightweight, training-free frame sampling (Swift Sampling) identifies temporally surprising frames via predictive coding in latent space, using velocity/acceleration and Taylor projection to select informative frames with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Long-form videos are highly redundant; need high-information moments to facilitate downstream tasks like long-video QA without expensive frame sampling.
AI.method: Model video as a differentiable trajectory in a visual latent space; compute velocity and acceleration; apply Taylor expansion to predict next frames; select frames that diverge from predicted manifold; no training required; computational overhead ~0.02x baseline; about 30x cheaper than leading baselines.
AI.result: On three long-video QA benchmarks and 10 downstream tasks, outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines; improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points for long videos under tight frame budgets.
AI.conclusion: Swift Sampling offers an efficient, effective, training-free frame sampling strategy that reduces computational cost while improving accuracy, suitable for long-video applications.

title: Lost in Tokenization: Fundamental Trade-offs in Graph Tokenization for Transformers
summary: Transformers have become a central architecture for graph learning, but their application to graphs requires first choosing a tokenization: a graph-to-token map that determines which structural information is exposed at the input. In this work, we show that this choice is a fundamental component of transformer expressivity. We examine three tokenizations that serve as building blocks for many existing graph tokenizations: spectral, random-walk, and adjacency tokenizations. We prove that different tokenizations induce distinct depth regimes: the same graph computation may be realizable by a shallow transformer under one tokenization, while requiring substantially larger depth under another. For example, we prove that random-walk tokenization is lossy for any walk length, making it impossible in general to recover the graph from it, and that while spectral tokenization is lossless, it is ill-conditioned for local tasks. We further show that although both random-walk and spectral tokenizations are derived from adjacency information, it is impossible for a limited-depth transformer to convert between tokenization families in general. In particular, we establish lower bounds and impossibility results showing that unfavorable tokenizations may preclude the efficient recovery of more suitable structural representations. Finally, we complement our theory with controlled experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks, validating the predicted separations and showing that different tasks favor different structural views, and combining complementary tokenizations allows the transformer to leverage distinct signals from each representation.

AI.tldr: Graph tokenization choices critically shape transformer expressivity on graphs; the same computation can require very different depths under different tokenizations. Random-walk tokenization is lossy for any walk length, spectral tokenization is lossless but ill-conditioned for local tasks, and converting between tokenizations is generally hard for limited-depth transformers. Empirical results confirm task-dependent preferences and benefit from combining tokenizations.
AI.motivation: To understand how tokenization, a core design choice in graph transformers, governs expressivity, depth requirements, and recoverability of graph structure across common tokenizations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of three building-block tokenizations (spectral, random-walk, adjacency), deriving depth-regime separations, lossiness and ill-conditioning proofs, impossibility results for cross-tokenization conversion at limited depth, plus controlled experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks to validate the theory and explore complementary benefits.
AI.result: Established depth-separation and impossibility results: random-walk is lossy for any walk length; spectral is lossless but ill-conditioned for local tasks; limited-depth transformers cannot generally convert between tokenization families; lower bounds and impossibility statements; experiments corroborate theory and show that combining tokenizations yields improved performance by leveraging distinct structural signals.
AI.conclusion: Tokenization choice is a fundamental determinant of transformer capability on graphs. Hybrid or adaptive tokenization strategies that combine multiple representations can harness complementary information and improve performance across tasks; results inform practical graph-transformer design.

title: Conceptualizing Embeddings: Sparse Disentanglement for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models learn powerful multimodal embeddings, yet their internal semantics remain opaque. While sparse autoencoders (SAEs) can extract interpretable features, they rely on expanding the representation dimension, which compromises the original geometry and introduces redundancy. We introduce CEDAR (Conceptual Embedding Disentanglement via Adaptive Rotation), a post-hoc method that reveals the compositional structure of pretrained embeddings without increasing dimensionality. By learning an invertible transformation with a top-$k$ sparsity bottleneck, CEDAR concentrates semantic information into axis-aligned disentangled coordinates. In CLIP-like architecture, individual coordinates can be interpreted with textual concepts, while for generative models such as BLIP, they can be decoded into natural language descriptions. Experiments demonstrate that CEDAR achieves a competitive reconstruction-sparsity trade-off while producing explanations that are more interpretable and better aligned with human perception. Our results suggest that the apparent entanglement in vision-language representations can be resolved through a suitable change of basis, eliminating the need for overcomplete expansions.

AI.tldr: CEDAR is a post-hoc, invertible rotation with a top-k sparsity bottleneck that yields axis-aligned, interpretable embedding coordinates for pretrained vision-language models without increasing dimensionality.
AI.motivation: Vision-language representations are entangled and hard to interpret; sparse autoencoders require dimensional expansion, which harms geometry and introduces redundancy.
AI.method: Learn an invertible transformation with a top-k sparsity bottleneck to concentrate semantic information into sparse, axis-aligned coordinates; apply post-hoc to pretrained CLIP-like encoders and generative models like BLIP; interpret coordinates via textual concepts and decode into language.
AI.result: CEDAR achieves a competitive reconstruction-sparsity trade-off and produces explanations that are more interpretable and better aligned with human perception; entanglement can be resolved via a basis change rather than expanding dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: A suitable change of basis via CEDAR removes the need for overcomplete expansions to interpret vision–language embeddings, preserving geometry while yielding disentangled, human-aligned representations.

title: Winner-Take-All bottlenecks enforce disentangled symbolic representations in multi-task learning
summary: Winner-take-all (WTA) networks constitute a central circuit motif in cortical networks of the brain. In addition, WTA-like activations are abundant in modern deep learning models in the form of the softmax activation for example in attention layers of transformers. While their role in the extraction of latent factors has been studied for relatively simple generative models, their role in the context of highly non-linearly entangled latent factors has remained elusive. In this article, we show that a WTA bottleneck within a deep neural network can enforce under certain well-defined conditions the extraction of categorical latent factors of the data in a multi-task learning setup. In particular, we prove that the representation that emerges in the WTA bottleneck is highly symbolic, where a single neuron or a population of neurons encodes the presence of a single abstract feature such as a specific object, color, or position. We furthermore show empirically on two datasets, that this also holds for architectures and setups that do not fully comply with the assumptions of our theorem and demonstrate the advantages of the acquired symbolic representation for generalization. Our proposed model provides insights into the generalization capabilities of deep neural networks with WTA-like components and may serve as an interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI systems.

AI.tldr: WTA bottlenecks in deep networks can enforce extraction of symbolic, categorical latent factors, with neurons encoding single abstract features; empirical evidence on two datasets shows symbolic representations improve generalization and may bridge symbolic/subsymbolic AI.
AI.motivation: Understand how winner-take-all (WTA) mechanisms influence latent factor extraction in deep networks, given their prominence in cortex and in deep learning, and whether they yield interpretable, symbolic representations under realistic conditions.
AI.method: Theoretically prove that a WTA bottleneck enforces extraction of categorical latent factors under defined assumptions; demonstrate that the emergent representation is highly symbolic (single neurons or neuron populations encoding specific abstract features). Empirically validate on two datasets, including architectures/ setups that partly violate the theorem's assumptions, and assess generalization benefits.
AI.result: Under certain conditions, WTA bottlenecks yield symbolic representations where individual neurons or groups signal the presence of specific features (objects, colors, positions). This structure improves generalization in multi-task learning scenarios and appears robust even when the theoretical assumptions are not fully met.
AI.conclusion: WTA-inspired bottlenecks can enhance interpretability and generalization by forming symbolic encodings of abstract features, offering a potential interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI and informing design of attention-related components in neural networks.

title: Improving Viewpoint-Invariance and Temporal Consistency for Action Detection
comment: Accepted at ICIP 2026. Code and trained models are available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/HydraView-TAD
summary: Viewpoint change invariance and action temporal consistency are critical aspects for the effective deployment of human action detection of untrimmed videos. Existing appearance-based video detection methods often struggle with limited viewpoint diversity during training, while motion-based detection approaches frequently fail to model fine-grained temporal relationships across consecutive motion windows. This paper introduces a novel two-stage action detection approach designed to improve both view-invariance and global temporal coherence properties. In the first stage, we extract motion features from augmented virtual viewpoints, solely used at training. Then, the second stage introduces a new view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder based on selective state-space sequence modelling to aggregate information across viewpoints and time scales. Experiments on PKU-MMD and BABEL benchmarks demonstrate that this approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in all considered splits. Code and trained models are available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/HydraView-TAD

AI.tldr: Two-stage action detection method HydraView-TAD improves view-invariance and temporal coherence by training with augmented virtual viewpoints and using a view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder based on selective state-space sequence modelling; achieves leading performance on PKU-MMD and BABEL.
AI.motivation: Combat limited viewpoint diversity in appearance-based detectors and coarse temporal modeling in motion-based detectors for untrimmed video action detection, aiming for robust view-invariant representations and coherent temporal aggregation.
AI.method: Stage 1: train with motion features derived from augmented virtual viewpoints. Stage 2: employ a view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder built on selective state-space sequence modelling to fuse information across viewpoints and time scales.
AI.result: Experiments on PKU-MMD and BABEL show significant improvements over state-of-the-art across all evaluation splits.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage framework enhances viewpoint robustness and global temporal coherence in untrimmed video action detection, with open-source code and trained models.

title: Structured-Sparse Attention for Entity Tracking with Subquadratic Sequence Complexity
summary: Entity tracking requires maintaining and updating latent states for entities and attributes over long sequences. Recent task-specific attention operators can compress deep Transformer stacks into a few layers by performing multi-hop state propagation within a single layer, but their dense evaluation remains expensive. We show that in this setting, learned attention is strongly structured: most mass concentrates in local block-diagonal neighborhoods with a light cross-block residue. Exploiting this, we derive a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator that keeps within-block interactions exact and routes cross-block interactions through a reduced system. The resulting evaluation is subquadratic in sequence length $O(n^{4/3}d)$ (and $O(n^{7/3})$ when $d\approx n$). On controlled tracking benchmarks, our method matches the dense operator's accuracy while reducing wall-clock time by $12-29\%$ under a standardized measurement protocol, and is up to $2.4 \times$ faster than a compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy. We further provide ablations over block size and model capacity, and identify a limitation: performance collapses when the number of simultaneously evolving properties exceeds the number of attention heads.

AI.tldr: Exploiting the observed local block-diagonal structure of learned attention, the paper develops a blockwise, resolvent-style operator that computes within-block interactions exactly and routes cross-block interactions through a reduced system, achieving subquadratic complexity with competitive accuracy and practical speedups for long-sequence entity tracking; but performance degrades when the number of evolving properties exceeds attention heads.
AI.motivation: Long-sequence entity tracking with Transformer-based architectures faces costly dense attention. The authors observe strong locality in learned attention and seek a memory- and compute-efficient evaluation that preserves accuracy.
AI.method: Derive a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator. Maintain exact within-block interactions; approximate cross-block interactions via a reduced system. Complexity scales as O(n^{4/3} d) (and O(n^{7/3}) when d≈n). Provide ablations on block size and model capacity.
AI.result: On controlled tracking benchmarks, the blockwise resolvent method matches the dense operator in accuracy while reducing wall-clock time by 12–29% under a standard protocol, and is up to 2.4× faster than a compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy. Ablations show robustness to block size and capacity, and a limitation where performance collapses when the number of simultaneously evolving properties exceeds the number of attention heads.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields subquadratic, accurate evaluation for entity-tracking tasks by exploiting structured attention; it delivers practical speedups while maintaining accuracy, but it has a limitation tied to the number of evolving properties relative to attention heads.

title: Cross-Domain Human Action Recognition from Multiview Motion and Textual Descriptions
comment: Accepted to ICPR 2026. Code and trained models available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/OrientationAware-HAR
summary: Robustness to domain changes is a key capability for effective deployment of human action recognition systems in real-world scenarios, where action categories at inference can present important domain shifts or even unseen actions from training. In this context, improving the recognition capabilities of Zero-Shot Action Recognition models (ZSAR), without requiring strong annotation efforts, remains a central challenge. Most ZSAR approaches assume that actions are observed under geometric conditions similar to those seen during training. In practice, variations in human body orientation and camera viewpoint add a significant domain gap in ZSAR, substantially limiting generalization to novel action-motion combinations. In this context, this paper presents a novel orientation-aware action recognition approach with improved cross-domain capabilities. Our approach combines motion cues of multiple camera viewpoints and text descriptions of human actions in the training phase. We present a new orientation-aware motion encoding network to learn different motion features, and adapt a specific orientation-aware text prompt to match the corresponding features at inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves ZSAR performance across different recognition benchmarks, outperforming recent state-of-the-art zero-shot approaches on NTU-RGB+D, BABEL, NW-UCLA, and on two surveillance datasets. In addition, the learned representations exhibit strong transfer learning capabilities, yielding competitive performance on both cross-domain and same-domain recognition of seen actions. Code and trained models are available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/OrientationAware-HAR

AI.tldr: An orientation-aware zero-shot action recognition framework that fuses multi-view motion cues with orientation-specific text prompts, featuring a new Motion Encoding Network, achieving improved cross-domain and unseen-action recognition across multiple benchmarks with open-source code.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot action recognition (ZSAR) faces large domain gaps when test actions appear under different viewpoints or orientations than training data. In real-world deployment, robustness to such domain shifts and unseen action-motion combinations is crucial but underexplored, motivating an orientation-aware approach.
AI.method: Introduce an orientation-aware motion encoding network to learn distinct motion features per orientation. During training, leverage motion cues from multiple camera viewpoints and text descriptions of actions. At inference, employ orientation-aware text prompts aligned with the learned features to improve matching. The approach emphasizes cross-domain generalization and is evaluated on NTU-RGB+D, BABEL, NW-UCLA, and two surveillance datasets; code and models are released.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art zero-shot methods across the reported benchmarks. Demonstrates strong transfer learning capabilities, with competitive performance on both cross-domain and same-domain recognition of seen actions. Public code/models are provided for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Orientation-aware modeling that combines multi-view motion cues with orientation-specific textual semantics enhances ZSAR generalization to domain shifts and unseen actions, supporting robust cross-domain deployment and transferable representations.

title: Implicit Regularization of Mini-Batch Training in Graph Neural Networks
summary: Mini-batch training of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is fundamentally different from training on i.i.d. data: sampling a subgraph alters the topology and introduces boundary effects, leading prior work to develop structure-aware samplers that preserve local connectivity and reduce embedding variance. Surprisingly, we demonstrate that the simplest possible scheme, Random Node Sampling (RNS), training on the induced subgraph of uniformly sampled nodes, matches or outperforms full-graph training on 8 of 10 datasets at a fraction of the wall-clock time and memory. To explain this, we apply backward error analysis to graph mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and show that it implicitly minimizes the sampled loss plus a regularizer proportional to the mini-batch gradient variance, a quantity directly shaped by the sampler. Although RNS discards local structure, it produces mini-batches whose expected loss is closer to the full-graph loss, and whose per-batch gradients have lower variance, yielding a better implicit objective. Our analysis reframes the choice of graph sampler as a form of implicit regularization, and identifies RNS as a strong, theoretically grounded method for scalable GNN training.

AI.tldr: Random Node Sampling (RNS) on induced subgraphs can match or exceed full-graph training performance in GNNs, acting as implicit regularization by reducing gradient variance, despite discarding local structure.
AI.motivation: Understand how graph mini-batch sampling influences training dynamics and generalization, and whether simple sampling can rival structure-aware methods.
AI.method: Backward error analysis of graph mini-batch SGD, combined with empirical evaluation across 8/10 datasets comparing RNS to full-graph training; analysis of per-batch gradient variance and expected loss.
AI.result: RNS matches or outperforms full-graph training on 8 of 10 datasets at lower wall-clock time and memory usage; mini-batch gradients exhibit lower variance and the expected loss is closer to the full-graph loss, revealing implicit regularization.
AI.conclusion: Graph sampler choice functions as implicit regularization; Random Node Sampling is a strong, theoretically grounded approach for scalable GNN training.

title: AnyMo: Geometry-Aware Setup-Agnostic Modeling of Human Motion in the Wild
summary: As wearable and mobile devices become increasingly embedded in daily life, they offer a practical way to continuously sense human motion in the wild. But inertial signals are highly dependent on the sensing setup, including body location, mounting position, sensor orientation, device hardware, and sampling protocol. This setup dependence makes it difficult to learn motion representations that transfer across devices and datasets, and limits the broader use of wearable IMUs beyond closed-set recognition. We introduce AnyMo, a geometry-aware framework for setup-agnostic human motion modeling. AnyMo uses physics-grounded IMU simulation over dense body-surface placements to generate diverse and plausible synthetic signals, pre-trains a graph encoder from paired synthetic placement views and masked partial observations, tokenizes multi-position IMU into full-body motion tokens, and aligns these tokens with an LLM for motion-language understanding. We evaluate AnyMo on three complementary tasks: zero-shot activity recognition across 14 unseen downstream datasets, cross-modal retrieval, and wearable IMU motion captioning, where it improves average Accuracy/F1/R@2 by 11.7\%/11.6\%/22.6\% on HAR, increases zero-shot IMU-to-text and text-to-IMU retrieval MRR by 15.9\% and 28.6\%, respectively, and improves zero-shot captioning BERT-F1 by 18.8\%. These results support AnyMo as a generalist model for wearable motion understanding in the wild. Project page: https://baiyuchen.com/project/AnyMo.

AI.tldr: AnyMo proposes a geometry-aware, setup-agnostic framework for wearable IMU-based motion understanding by simulating diverse body placements, pretraining a graph encoder, tokenizing multi-position IMUs into full-body tokens, and aligning with an LLM to enable zero-shot recognition, cross-modal retrieval, and captioning across 14 unseen datasets, achieving substantial gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Inertial signals from wearables depend heavily on sensing setup, hindering cross-device transfer and open-world deployment; a generalist, setup-agnostic model is needed for robust wearable motion understanding.
AI.method: Physics-grounded IMU simulation over dense body-surface placements; paired synthetic placement views with masked partial observations to pretrain a graph encoder; tokenization of multi-position IMU data into full-body motion tokens; alignment of tokens with a language model for motion-language tasks.
AI.result: Improved average accuracy/F1/R@2 on HAR by 11.7/11.6/22.6 percentage points; IMU-to-text and text-to-IMU retrieval MRR up by 15.9% and 28.6%; captioning BERT-F1 up by 18.8%; demonstrated zero-shot generalization across 14 unseen datasets and multiple modalities.
AI.conclusion: AnyMo demonstrates strong generalist capability for wearable motion understanding in-the-wild, enabling cross-dataset transfer and cross-modal tasks; framework shows promise for broad deployment but may depend on realism of simulated data and alignment with real sensors.

title: When Stronger Triggers Backfire: A High-Dimensional Theory of Backdoor Attacks
summary: Backdoor poisoning attacks behave counter-intuitively in high dimensions: stronger training triggers can help the defender. We study regularised generalised linear models on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime ($p/n \to κ$), varying the training trigger strength $α$ against a fixed test trigger. Three phenomena emerge: (i) clean test accuracy increases with $α$; (ii) attack success peaks at a finite $α$ and then declines; and (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of the data covariance. We prove all three results in closed form for the squared loss, and extend (i) and (ii) to general convex GLM losses via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system. We identify a finite-sample noise floor proportional to $κ$ as the mechanism behind (i), invisible to classical $n \gg p$ analysis. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Gaussian surrogates match the theory closely; ResNet-18 experiments show the same phenomena beyond the convex setting.

AI.tldr: In high-dimensional regularized GLMs with backdoor triggers, increasing trigger strength can improve clean accuracy but does not monotonically increase attack success; attack effectiveness peaks at a finite trigger strength and then drops; the most damaging trigger aligns with the data covariance's minimum eigenvector. The authors derive closed-form results for squared loss and extend to other convex GLMs, revealing a finite-sample noise floor proportional to the dimensionality ratio κ; experiments on CIFAR-10, Gaussian surrogates, and ResNet-18 confirm the phenomena.
AI.motivation: To understand how backdoor poisoning behaves in high-dimensional settings (p ~ κn) and how trigger strength interacts with training regularization, potentially informing defenses.
AI.method: Analytical study of regularised generalized linear models trained on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime; derive closed-form results for squared loss; extend to general convex GLMs using a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system; empirical validation on CIFAR-10 and Gaussian surrogates; additional ResNet-18 experiments.
AI.result: Three phenomena observed: (i) clean test accuracy increases with trigger strength α; (ii) attack success peaks at a finite α and then declines with further increases; (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of the data covariance. These are proven in closed form for squared loss and extended to general convex losses. A finite-sample noise floor proportional to κ explains (i). Experimental results closely match the theory, including deep networks showing the same behaviors beyond convex settings.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals robust, counter-intuitive dynamics of backdoor poisoning in high dimensions, highlighting the role of data geometry (minimum eigenvector) and finite-sample noise floors. The Gaussian-proxy approach yields tractable analysis for convex GLMs and the phenomena persist beyond convexity, suggesting nuanced considerations for evaluating and designing defenses in high-dimensional settings.

title: WorldKV: Efficient World Memory with World Retrieval and Compression
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models have enabled real-time, action-conditioned world generation. However, sustaining a persistent world, where revisiting a previously seen viewpoint yields consistent content, remains an open problem. Full KV-cache attention preserves this consistency but breaks real-time constraints: memory footprint and attention cost grow linearly with rollout length. Sliding window inference restores throughput but discards long-term consistency. We propose WorldKV, a training-free framework with two components: World Retrieval and World Compression. World Retrieval stores evicted KV-cache chunks in GPU/CPU memory and selectively retrieves scene-relevant chunks via camera/ action correspondence, inserting them back into the native attention window without re-encoding. World Compression prunes redundant tokens within each chunk via key-key similarity to an anchor frame, halving per-chunk storage to fit 2x more history under a fixed budget. On Matrix-Game-2.0 and LingBot- World-Fast, WorldKV matches or exceeds full-KV memory fidelity at roughly 2x the throughput, and is competitive with memory-trained baselines without any fine-tuning. Project Page: https://cvlab-kaist.github.io/WorldKV/

AI.tldr: WorldKV is a training-free framework that preserves long-term consistency in autoregressive video diffusion under sliding-window attention by retrieving evicted KV-cache chunks and compressing tokens, achieving full-KV-like fidelity with ~2x throughput on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Persistent, content-consistent world generation in autoregressive video diffusion is hindered by sliding-window attention; full KV-cache gives consistency but is not real-time feasible. A training-free approach aims to balance fidelity and throughput.
AI.method: World Retrieval stores evicted KV-cache chunks in GPU/CPU memory and selectively retrieves scene-relevant chunks via camera/action correspondences, reinserting them into the attention window without re-encoding. World Compression prunes redundant tokens within each chunk using key-key similarity to an anchor frame, halving per-chunk storage and allowing ~2x more history under a fixed budget.
AI.result: On Matrix-Game-2.0 and LingBot-World-Fast benchmarks, WorldKV matches or exceeds full-KV fidelity while achieving roughly 2x throughput; competitive with memory-trained baselines without fine-tuning; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: WorldKV offers a practical, training-free method to achieve persistent, consistent generation in autoregressive video diffusion, delivering a favorable fidelity-throughput trade-off and broad applicability potential.

title: Represented Is Not Computed: A Causal Test of Candidate Algorithmic Intermediates in a Transformer
summary: Structured prompts require integrating components according to task-relevant relations. How a network implements this integration is often hard to judge in language or vision, where those relations are rarely specified precisely enough to define a candidate internal algorithm. Arithmetic offers a cleaner setting. We study a Transformer trained on base-digit extraction: given $N$, $B$, and $D$, it must report the coefficient of $B^D$ in the base-$B$ expansion of $N$. The closed-form solution, $\lfloor N/B^D \rfloor \bmod B$, provides explicit candidate algorithmic intermediates. Across three seeds, the model reaches 99.83% exact-answer accuracy on held-out number-base intersections, establishing reliable task competence. Linear probes decode the intermediates, making staged arithmetic computation plausible. Causal tests then separate representation from use: within the localized route from the stream with $D$ as input to the output positions, behavior depends on early $D$-selective communication, independent of $N$ and $B$. Relatedly, a sparse circuit search finds mostly separate $N$, $B$, and $D$ routes that combine late rather than the staged route suggested by the probes. Thus, the model represents the intermediates that make the closed-form solution plausible, but the identified localized causal route does not transmit them to the output stream. This case shows that probe-based conclusions can diverge sharply from causal observations, even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses are available.

AI.tldr: A Transformer trained to extract a base-B digit internally represents the arithmetic intermediates needed for the closed-form solution, and probes suggest a staged computation, but causal analysis reveals a separate, late integration route—showing that probe-based inferences about internal algorithms can diverge from actual causal use.
AI.motivation: Investigate how structured prompts and explicit algorithmic tasks are realized in neural networks by using an arithmetic task with a clean, closed-form solution to compare representation and usage; assess whether linear probes reflect causal computation.
AI.method: Train a Transformer on the base-digit extraction task: given N, B, D, output the coefficient of B^D in the base-B expansion of N, i.e., floor(N/B^D) mod B. Use held-out number-base intersections to test accuracy (reported as 99.83%). Apply linear probes to decode intermediate quantities corresponding to the closed-form components. Conduct causal tests to trace information flow from the D-input stream to outputs, focusing on D-selective communication and early vs. late integration. Perform sparse circuit search to identify routing paths, typically finding mostly separate N, B, D routes that combine late rather than along the staged route suggested by probes.
AI.result: The model achieves 99.83% exact accuracy on held-out intersections. Linear probes successfully decode the intermediates needed for the closed-form solution, indicating plausible staged arithmetic computation. Causal tests show that the path from D-input to output relies on early D-selective communication, but the intermediates are not transmitted along this causal route to the output. Sparse circuit search reveals mostly independent N, B, and D routes that combine late, contradicting the staged route implied by the probes.
AI.conclusion: Probe-based conclusions about internal algorithms can diverge from causal observations, even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses exist. The model does compute intermediates relevant to the closed-form solution, but the causal path that delivers the answer does not follow the same staged route suggested by probes, underscoring the need for complementary causal analyses when interpreting neural computation.

title: Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: As generative image models evolve rapidly, the perceptual gap between generated and real images continues to narrow, making AI-generated image detection increasingly challenging. Many existing methods exploit frequency-domain cues for detection, typically described as frequency-domain artifacts or high-frequency discrepancies. However, the specific and recurring spectral regularities remain insufficiently understood and characterized. In this paper, we systematically analyze the one-dimensional radial log-power spectra of real and generated images. We find that generated images do not necessarily exhibit higher or lower energy across the entire spectrum or high-band range. Instead, their spectra deviate from the power-law decay and show an anomalous uplift in the ultra-high-frequency tail. We term this phenomenon spectral tail uplift. We further attribute this phenomenon to nonlinear harmonic accumulation in trained generative models, suggesting that it can serve as a structural cue across generative architectures. Based on this observation, we propose Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning (STAL), a frequency-domain auxiliary supervision framework for generalizable AI-generated image detection. STAL transfers spectral-tail cues from a tail-aware frequency teacher to a spatial detector during training, while all frequency-domain modules are discarded at inference time. Consequently, STAL introduces no inference overhead. Extensive experiments on 9 public datasets show that STAL achieves strong generalization and stability across generators, data distributions, and real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: STAL uses a spectral-tail cue (ultra-high-frequency uplift) found in generated images to train a tail-aware frequency teacher that provides auxiliary supervision to a spatial detector; at inference, frequency modules are discarded, yielding no extra cost while improving generalization across generators and datasets.
AI.motivation: As generative models improve, distinguishing AI-generated from real images becomes harder. Existing frequency cues are not fully understood, and a robust, architecture-agnostic detection cue is needed.
AI.method: 1) Analyze 1D radial log-power spectra of real vs. generated images to identify spectral regularities, notably spectral tail uplift. 2) Develop Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning (STAL): use a tail-aware frequency teacher to guide a spatial detector during training via frequency-domain auxiliary supervision. 3) Discard all frequency-domain modules at inference time to avoid overhead.
AI.result: STAL achieves strong generalization and stability across multiple generators, data distributions, and real-world scenarios, with no inference overhead due to removal of frequency-domain components during deployment.
AI.conclusion: Spectral tail uplift is a robust, transferable cue for AI-generated image detection. STAL effectively leverages this cue through auxiliary learning, enabling generalizable detection without additional inference cost.

title: Synthetic Data Alone is Enough? Rethinking Data Scarcity in Pediatric Rare Disease Recognition
summary: Children with rare genetic diseases often exhibit distinctive facial phenotypes, yet developing computer vision systems for early diagnosis remains challenging due to extreme data scarcity, privacy constraints, and limited data sharing in pediatric settings. These challenges not only hinder automated diagnosis but also restrict the availability of visual resources for clinical genetic counseling. While prior work has shown that synthetic data can augment real datasets and preserve phenotype-level semantics, it remains unclear whether synthetic data alone is sufficient for learning in ultra-low-resource pediatric settings. In this work, we study the synthetic-only regime for pediatric rare disease recognition. Under a controlled experimental setup, models are trained exclusively on phenotype-aware synthetic facial images at increasing scales. We find that synthetic-only training achieves performance comparable to real-data-only baselines at sufficient scale across multiple backbones, suggesting that high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions. These findings together further enable the use of synthetic pediatric facial images as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education and counseling, supporting clinician training and patient communication. Our results highlight the potential of computer vision to improve data efficiency and expand accessible visual tools in children's healthcare.

AI.tldr: Synthetic-only training with phenotype-aware pediatric facial images can match real-data baselines at scale, suggesting high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions for rare-disease recognition while enabling privacy-preserving education and counseling.
AI.motivation: Tackle extreme data scarcity and privacy constraints in pediatric genetics to enable early diagnosis and improve access to visual resources for counseling; assess whether synthetic data alone suffices in ultra-low-resource settings.
AI.method: Controlled experiments where models are trained exclusively on phenotype-aware synthetic facial images, at increasing data scales, across multiple backbone architectures, and compared to real-data-only baselines.
AI.result: When scaled sufficiently, synthetic-only training achieves performance comparable to real-data-only baselines across various backbones, indicating high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions.
AI.conclusion: High-fidelity synthetic pediatric facial data can serve as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education, counseling, and clinician training, enhancing data efficiency and expanding accessible CV tools in children’s healthcare.

title: The Signal in the Noise: OOD Detection Through Goodness-of-Fit Testing in Factorised Latent Spaces
summary: Deep generative models offer a natural foundation for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, yet prior work has shown that their assigned likelihoods are notoriously unreliable indicators for in- vs out-of-distribution data. In this paper, we address this problem by leveraging the diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties of continuous normalising flows. Our analysis shows that OOD samples are mapped to noise samples that are highly atypical under the noise prior in ways not captured by the likelihood. Based on this observation, we propose a new method -- Signal in the Noise (SITN) -- for OOD detection on the single-sample level. SITN requires no access to OOD data, incurs minimal computational overhead, and provides strict control of false positive rates. Comprehensive evaluations through standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations highlight the method's effectiveness and the absence of the complexity bias inherent to likelihood-based methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes SITN, a single-sample OOD detector based on continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) that exploit diffeomorphic and mass-preserving mappings. OOD samples map to atypical noise under the noise prior; no OOD data needed, low overhead, strict FPR control. Evaluated on standard benchmarks and perturbations, outperforming likelihood-based methods and avoiding their complexity bias.
AI.motivation: Likelihoods produced by deep generative models are unreliable for distinguishing in-distribution from out-of-distribution data. A robust, single-sample OOD detector with low computational cost and explicit false-positive control is needed, independent of access to OOD data.
AI.method: Leverages the diffeomorphic, mass-preserving properties of continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) to map data under a learned transformation. Observes that OOD inputs become atypical noise samples under the noise prior, not captured by likelihood alone. Introduces Signal in the Noise (SITN), a single-sample OOD detector that requires no OOD data and imposes minimal overhead, enabling strict false-positive rate control.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations on standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations demonstrate SITN’s effectiveness and show it avoids the complexity bias present in likelihood-based methods.
AI.conclusion: SITN provides a principled, data-efficient OOD detection approach with strong false-positive control and practical robustness, addressing a key limitation of likelihood-based detection in deep generative models.

title: DecQ: Detail-Condensing Queries for Enhanced Reconstruction and Generation in Representation Autoencoders
summary: Representation Autoencoders (RAEs) leverage frozen vision foundation models (VFMs) as tokenizer encoders, providing robust high-level representations that facilitate fast convergence and high-quality generation in latent diffusion models. However, freezing the VFM inherently constrains its spatial reconstruction capacity, limiting fine-grained generation and image editing; in contrast, incorporating reconstruction-oriented signals via fine-tuning disrupts the pretrained semantic space and degrades generative fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose DecQ, a simple yet effective framework for RAEs. Specifically, DecQ introduces lightweight detail-condensing queries that extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features through condenser modules. These queries are incorporated into the decoder to support reconstruction and are jointly generated with patch tokens during generative modeling. By aggregating information from both shallow and deep layers, DecQ effectively mitigates the reconstruction--generation trade-off, improving both reconstruction quality and generative performance. Our experiments demonstrate that: (1) with only 8 additional queries and 3.9% extra computation, DecQ improves reconstruction over the frozen DINOv2-based RAE, increasing PSNR from 19.13 dB to 22.76 dB; and (2) for generative modeling, DecQ achieves 3.3$\times$ faster convergence than RAE, attaining an FID of 1.41 without guidance and 1.05 with guidance.

AI.tldr: DecQ adds lightweight detail-condensing queries to Representation Autoencoders to balance reconstruction and generation by leveraging intermediate VFM features; yields improved reconstruction quality and faster generative convergence with minimal overhead.
