AI.motivation: Freezing vision foundation models limits spatial reconstruction; fine-tuning disrupts semantic space and fidelity. A lightweight mechanism is needed to recover fine-grained details without sacrificing pretrained semantics.
AI.method: Introduce detail-condensing queries (8 total) integrated into the decoder and produced alongside patch tokens. These queries extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features via condenser modules and aggregate information from shallow and deep layers to support both reconstruction and generation.
AI.result: Reconstruction PSNR improves from 19.13 dB to 22.76 dB with 8 queries and 3.9% extra computation. In generative modeling, DecQ achieves 3.3× faster convergence and FID 1.41 without guidance, 1.05 with guidance.
AI.conclusion: DecQ mitigates the reconstruction–generation trade-off in RAEs, delivering better reconstruction and faster, higher-quality generation with minimal additional cost.

title: The Neural Compiler: Program-to-Network Translation for Hybrid Scientific Machine Learning
comment: Use: 21 pages, 10 figures, 10 tables. Preprint; source code available at https://github.com/sheneman/neural_compiler
summary: Scientific machine learning often requires combining known physics with unknown parameters or correction terms learned from data. Existing approaches either ignore known structure, encode it as a soft penalty, or require hand-written PyTorch code for each equation. We present The Neural Compiler, a system that translates programs written in a first-order Scheme-like expression language into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules. These modules match the source program to floating-point precision and provide gradients through autograd. In hybrid models, the compiled module encodes known physics exactly while learned components model the unknown remainder. We evaluate the compiler across six experiment domains: Feynman physics equations, Lotka-Volterra dynamics, a damped pendulum, a one-dimensional heat equation, three-dimensional vector mechanics, and compositional generalization. Compiled modules match hand-coded PyTorch implementations numerically for single equations, showing no accuracy loss from compilation. With only 1 to 4 trainable parameters, compiled models recover physical constants to less than 1 percent error in most cases, while standard PINN baselines with more than 8500 parameters show 7 to 93 percent error. Compiled modules also compose with zero error, while neural approximations can accumulate large errors in deep composition chains. The main value of the compiler is not improved accuracy over hand-coded equations, but systematic composability: it generates correct, differentiable modules from symbolic specifications without rewriting each equation by hand. The system supports 51 primitive operations, including vector and matrix algebra, enabling PDE discretizations and hybrid scientific models. This string-in, module-out interface also provides a natural target for large language models that translate scientific descriptions into executable differentiable modules.

AI.tldr: Introduces The Neural Compiler that translates Scheme-like expressions into differentiable PyTorch modules, preserving known physics while enabling learning for unknown terms; achieves numerical parity with hand-coded equations and strong compositionality with small parameter counts; highlights a pathway for LLM-driven generation of differentiable physics modules.
AI.motivation: To integrate known physical structure with data-driven learning without hand-coding each equation, preserving exact physical relations where possible and enabling scalable composition across complex models.
AI.method: Translate first-order Scheme-like programs into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules (51 primitive operations, including vector/matrix algebra). The compiler preserves exact physics in the module while allowing learned components to model unknown terms; gradients flow through autograd; supports PDE discretizations and hybrid models.
AI.result: Compiled modules match hand-coded PyTorch results for single equations with no accuracy loss. With 1–4 trainable parameters, compiled models recover physical constants to <1% error in most cases, outperforming standard PINN baselines that use thousands of parameters (7–93% error). Compiled modules compose with zero error; demonstrate strong composability across six domains (Feynman equations, Lotka-Volterra, damped pendulum, 1D heat equation, 3D vector mechanics). Supports 51 primitives for broader PDE/discretization tasks.
AI.conclusion: The primary contribution is systematic composability: the tool generates correct, differentiable modules directly from symbolic specifications, avoiding manual rewriting per equation. It also provides a practical target for LLMs to translate scientific descriptions into executable differentiable modules.

title: Sensor2Sensor: Cross-Embodiment Sensor Conversion for Autonomous Driving
summary: Robust training and validation of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) require massive, diverse datasets. Proprietary data collected by Autonomous Vehicle (AV) fleets, while high-fidelity, are limited in scale, diversity of sensor configurations, as well as geographic and long-tail-behavioral coverage. In contrast, in-the-wild data from sources like dashcams offers immense scale and diversity, capturing critical long-tail scenarios and novel environments. However, this unstructured, in-the-wild video data is incompatible with ADS expecting structured, multi-modal sensor inputs for validation and training. To bridge this data gap, we propose Sensor2Sensor, a novel generative modeling paradigm that translates in-the-wild monocular dashcam videos into a high-fidelity, multi-modal sensor suite (AV logs) comprising multi-view camera images and LiDAR point clouds. A core challenge is the lack of paired training data. We address this by converting real AV logs into dashcam-style videos via 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering. Sensor2Sensor then utilizes a diffusion architecture to perform the generative conversion. We perform comprehensive quantitative evaluations on the fidelity and realism of the generated sensor data. We demonstrate Sensor2Sensor's practical utility by converting challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic, multi-modal data formats, further unlocking vast external data sources for AV development.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based Sensor2Sensor translates in-the-wild monocular dashcam video into a high-fidelity, multi-modal AV sensor set (images + LiDAR), bridging unstructured data with structured ADS data via 4D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction and diffusion-based rendering; validated with fidelity/realism metrics and demonstrated on diverse real-world footage.
AI.motivation: Robust ADS development requires large, diverse, multi-modal datasets. Proprietary AV data are high quality but limited in scale and diversity; unstructured dashcam data are abundant but lack paired multi-modal sensor formats. A bridging method is needed to convert unstructured video into realistic, structured AV sensor data.
AI.method: First, real AV logs are converted into dashcam-style videos using 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering. Then a diffusion-based architecture (Sensor2Sensor) generates high-fidelity, multi-modal sensor outputs (multi-view camera images + LiDAR point clouds) from these dashcam videos.
AI.result: Comprehensive quantitative evaluations demonstrate fidelity and realism of the generated sensor data. The method successfully converts challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic, multi-modal data formats, enabling access to vast external data sources for AV development.
AI.conclusion: Sensor2Sensor provides a practical bridge to scale-diverse, multi-modal data for ADS training/validation by translating unstructured dashcam content into realistic AV sensor streams, with potential caveats related to domain gaps and biases that warrant further study.

title: Generative Modeling by Value-Driven Transport
summary: We propose a new framework for generative modeling based on a discrete-time stochastic control formulation of measure transport. Adapting classic results from control theory, we formulate our problem as a linear program whose dual variables correspond to the \emph{optimal value function} of the control problem, which directly encodes the optimal control policy. Exploiting this LP formulation, we develop an efficient simulation-free primal-dual algorithm for computing approximately optimal value functions and the associated \emph{value-driven transport} (VDT) policies which approximate the true optimal policy. We show that well-trained VDT policies enjoy numerous favorable properties in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods based on flows, diffusions, or Schrödinger bridges: they lead to straight transport paths which can be simulated quickly and robustly, and can be enhanced in all the same ways as diffusion and flow-based models (e.g., conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, unpaired data-to-data translation are all easy to incorporate). We evaluate our methodology in a range of experiments, with results that indicate strong performance and good potential for scalability.

AI.tldr: A discrete-time stochastic control formulation of measure transport for generative modeling yields a primal-dual LP-based framework (value-driven transport, VDT). It produces straight transport paths, enables simulation-free optimization, and integrates with guidance and translation techniques, showing strong empirical performance and scalability.
AI.motivation: Develop a tractable, optimization-centric framework for transport-based generative modeling that overcomes limitations of diffusion/flow/Schrödinger-bridge methods, while providing flexible, controllable generation and easy integration of guidance and unpaired translation.
AI.method: Formulate the problem as a linear program whose duals are the optimal value function encoding the optimal control policy. Develop a simulation-free primal-dual algorithm to approximate the value function and obtain VDT policies that realize approximately optimal transport. Compare to flows, diffs, Schrödinger bridges. Provide extensions enabling conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, and unpaired data-to-data translation within the same framework.
AI.result: VDT policies yield straight transport paths that are fast to simulate and robust. The method is scalable and enhances with established guidance/translation techniques. Empirical results indicate strong performance across a range of tasks.
AI.conclusion: An LP-based, value-function–driven transport framework offers an efficient, flexible, and scalable approach to generative modeling, with favorable transport properties and broad compatibility with existing guidance and translation methodologies.

title: MotiMotion: Motion-Controlled Video Generation with Visual Reasoning
summary: Current motion-controlled image-to-video generation models rigidly follow user-provided trajectories that are often sparse, imprecise, and causally incomplete. Such reliance often yields unnatural or implausible outcomes, especially by missing secondary causal consequences. To address this, we introduce MotiMotion, a novel framework that reformulates motion control as a reasoning-then-generation problem. To encourage causally grounded and commonsense-consistent interactions, we leverage a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine image-space coordinates of primary trajectories and to hallucinate plausible secondary motions. To further improve motion naturalness, we propose a confidence-aware control scheme that modulates guidance strength, enabling the model to closely follow high-confidence plans while correcting artifacts under low-confidence inputs with its internal generative priors. To support systematic evaluation, we curate a new image-to-video benchmark, MotiBench, consisting of interaction-centric scenes where new events are triggered by motion. Both VLM-based evaluation and a human study on MotiBench demonstrate that MotiMotion produces videos with more plausible object behaviors and interaction, and is preferred over existing approaches.

AI.tldr: Introduces MotiMotion, a reasoning-then-generation framework for motion-controlled image-to-video generation that refines user trajectories with a training-free vision-language reasoner, adds a confidence-aware control scheme, and benchmarks on MotiBench, achieving more plausible object interactions than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current motion-controlled video generation struggles with sparse/inaccurate trajectories that yield implausible or causally inconsistent motions. There is a need for causally grounded, commonsense-aware motion control.
AI.method: Formulates motion control as a reasoning-then-generation process. Uses a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine primary trajectories and hallucinate plausible secondary motions. Introduces a confidence-aware control scheme to modulate guidance strength based on input confidence. Proposes MotiBench for evaluation with interaction-centric scenes. Evaluations include VLM-based metrics and human studies.
AI.result: Videos produced exhibit more plausible object behaviors and interactions; VLM-based and human evaluations prefer MotiMotion over existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: A reasoning-based, confidence-aware framework with a training-free VLM refines motion control and yields more natural, causally-consistent image-to-video generation, supported by a new benchmark for systematic evaluation.

title: Stabilising Explainability Fragility in Cybersecurity AI: The Impact and Mitigation of Multicollinearity in Public Benchmark Datasets
comment: 35 pages, 3 figures, submitted to ACM TAISAP
summary: This paper investigates a unexplored yet impactful vulnerability in AI explainability used in intrusion detection (IDS): multicollinearity-induced instability. Despite extensive reliance on post-hoc explainability tools such as SHAP or LIME, the impact of correlated features on explanation robustness is not evaluated. We introduce a formal theorem stating that multicollinearity inflates attribution variance. This demonstrates that explanations and feature importances are non-identifiable under multicollinearity. A suite of comprehensive experiments validates the theorem on a representative benchmark dataset, UNSW-NB15. Four widely used families of models are evaluated, including linear, tree-based, kernel, and neural, across full and pruned feature sets based on VIF and correlation thresholding. We propose the novel metric of Explanability Fragility Score and two novel methods to mitigate it with variable integration complexity. CAA-Filtering focuses on stabilising explanations by grouping attributions of trained models. SHARP is a novel training-time regularisation framework that penalises attribution instability, enabling controllable and monotonic improvement of explainability stability. The findings support stable predictive performance, using Kendall's τ to quantify instability across bootstrapped explanations. This work has direct implications for the trustworthiness and reproducibility of XAI in security-critical contexts, and motivates incorporating multicollinearity mitigations into the IDS pipelines, providing a set of guidelines for practitioners.

AI.tldr: Formalizes how multicollinearity inflates attribution variance, showing explainability is non-identifiable under correlated features; validates with experiments on UNSW-NB15 across model families; proposes Explanability Fragility Score and two mitigation methods (CAA-Filtering and SHARP) to stabilize explanations, with Kendall's tau used to measure stability; implications for trustworthy XAI in IDS.
AI.motivation: Explainability in intrusion detection relies on feature attributions, but multicollinearity undermines robustness; need to quantify and mitigate instability to improve trust and reproducibility.
AI.method: Proves a theoretical theorem linking multicollinearity to attribution variance. Conducts experiments on UNSW-NB15 with four model families (linear, tree-based, kernel, neural) using full and pruned feature sets (VIF/correlation thresholding). Introduces Explanability Fragility Score and two mitigation methods: CAA-Filtering (group attributions for stability) and SHARP (training-time regularization penalising attribution instability). Uses bootstrapped explanations and Kendall's tau to measure instability.
AI.result: Empirical validation of the theorem showing attribution instability under multicollinearity. CAA-Filtering and SHARP yield controllable, monotonic improvements in explainability stability while preserving predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: Underscores the need to mitigate multicollinearity in IDS XAI pipelines; provides practical guidelines and novel metrics/methods to enhance trustworthiness and reproducibility of explanations in security-critical settings.

title: Cambrian-P: Pose-Grounded Video Understanding
summary: Camera pose matters. The position and orientation of each viewpoint define a shared spatial coordinate frame that relates observations across video frames. Yet this signal is largely absent from multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) for video understanding, which process frames as isolated 2D snapshots, instead of the persistent scene humans perceive. We revisit pose as a lightweight supervisory signal and introduce Cambrian-P, a video MLLM augmented with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head. With a carefully designed sampling scheme, the model achieves substantial gains of 4.5-6.5% on spatial reasoning benchmarks such as VSI-Bench, generalizes across eight additional spatial and general video QA benchmarks, and, as a byproduct, achieves state of the art streaming pose estimation on ScanNet. Surprisingly, training on pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video further improves general video QA benchmarks, showing pose helps beyond spatial reasoning. Together, these results position camera pose as a fundamental signal for video models that reason about the physical world.

AI.tldr: Cambrian-P augments video LLMs with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head to reincorporate camera pose as a persistent spatial cue, yielding consistent gains across multiple video QA benchmarks, state-of-the-art streaming pose estimation, and improved generalization when trained on pseudo-annotated in-the-wild poses.
AI.motivation: Camera pose and the persistent spatial frame are underutilized signals in multimodal video models; current MLLMs treat frames as isolated 2D snapshots, hindering cross-frame reasoning about physical space.
AI.method: Introduce Cambrian-P, a video MLLM augmented with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head. Use a carefully designed sampling scheme and train on both ground-truth-like poses and pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild videos.
AI.result: Achieves 4.5-6.5% gains on VSI-Bench and generalizes to eight additional spatial/general video QA benchmarks. Attains state-of-the-art streaming pose estimation on ScanNet. Pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video further improve general video QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Camera pose is a fundamental signal for video models that reason about the physical world; pose supervision enhances spatial reasoning and general video QA, suggesting pose cues should be integrated in future video understanding architectures.

title: Disentanglement Beyond Generative Models with Riemannian ICA
summary: There is a gap between the theoretical foundations of disentanglement and the practice of modern representation learning. Existing theoretical frameworks, particularly Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and its nonlinear variants, assume a generative model with statistically independent latent variables underlying the data so that disentanglement amounts to identifying the latents that could have generated the data. This generative framework is interpretable and theoretically justified, but its strong assumptions make it difficult to apply to modern representation learning. Modern pretrained encoders often learn features that exhibit disentangled properties without making generative assumptions, yet there is no general theory for interpreting these features as independent factors of variation. We take a step toward such a theory by introducing Riemannian ICA (RICA), which replaces ICA's global generative model with local geometric structure. RICA is founded on the observation that in ICA, the factors of variation underlying a data point can be understood through radial curves emanating from the point that map to axis-aligned lines in the latent space. We formalize this perspective using Riemannian geometry and introduce our theory in a way that is consistent with the existing generative approach. Our main contribution is the disentanglement tensor, which encodes a second-order notion of disentanglement that we call pointwise disentanglement. This tensor depends on the Hessian of the data log likelihood as well as the Ricci curvature induced by the model. In a controlled source recovery setting with known ground-truth sources, RICA recovers sources across several manifolds, while the success of ICA baselines depends on the coordinates used to represent the observations. Our work provides a theoretical basis for studying local disentanglement without assuming a global generative model.

AI.tldr: Proposes Riemannian ICA (RICA) to define local disentanglement via a disentanglement tensor derived from the Hessian of the data log-likelihood and Ricci curvature; demonstrates recovery of sources across manifolds without a global generative model.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between ICA-based disentanglement (global generative models) and modern representation learning, by establishing a local geometric theory of disentanglement applicable to encoders that learn disentangled features without explicit generative assumptions.
AI.method: Replace the global generative model with local geometric structure using Riemannian geometry. Introduce radial curves from data points that map to axis-aligned lines in latent space and define a disentanglement tensor dependent on the Hessian of the data log-likelihood and the Ricci curvature induced by the model.
AI.result: In controlled source-recovery experiments with known ground-truth sources, RICA recovers sources across multiple manifolds; traditional ICA baselines’ success depends on the coordinate representation of observations.
AI.conclusion: Provides a theoretical basis for studying local (pointwise) disentanglement without assuming a global generative model, bridging ICA-style theory with modern representation learning.

title: Which Way Did It Move? Diagnosing and Overcoming Directional Motion Blindness in Video-LLMs
comment: Preprint. 59 pages, including appendix. Code: https://github.com/KHU-VLL/DeltaDirect
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have made rapid progress on temporal video understanding, yet many fail at a basic perceptual primitive: signed image-plane motion direction. On simple videos of a single object moving left, right, up, or down, most Video-LLMs perform near chance, with above-chance cases largely attributable to prediction biases rather than genuine direction understanding. We call this failure directional motion blindness. We localize the failure by tracing motion direction information through the Video-LLM pipeline. Motion direction remains linearly accessible from the vision encoder, projector, and LLM hidden states, but the readout fails to bind this signal to the correct verbal answer option, revealing a direction binding gap. Although synthetic motion direction instruction tuning reduces this gap on the source domain, motion direction concept vector analysis shows that visual complexity weakens the signal magnitude and limits out-of-domain generalization. We introduce MoDirect, a dataset family for motion direction instruction tuning and evaluation, and DeltaDirect, a diagnosis-driven, projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. On MoDirect-SynBench, instruction tuning with DeltaDirect improves motion direction accuracy from 25.9% to 85.4%. On MoDirect-RealBench, DeltaDirect improves real-world motion direction accuracy by 21.9 points over the vanilla baseline without real-world tuning data, while preserving standard video-understanding performance. Code: https://github.com/KHU-VLL/DeltaDirect

AI.tldr: Video-LLMs exhibit directional motion blindness: they can access motion signals but fail to bind them to the correct answer option. The authors propose MoDirect and DeltaDirect to diagnose and train alignment of 2-D motion signals with LLM outputs, yielding large gains on synthetic data and notable improvements on real data while preserving general video understanding.
AI.motivation: Current Video-LLMs struggle with a basic perceptual task—signed image-plane motion direction—showing near-chance accuracy despite perceiving motion signals. The failure is due to a gap in binding motion direction information to the correct verbal option, not a lack of signal in the vision encoder, projector, or LLM.
AI.method: 1) Diagnose the information flow through vision encoder, projector, and LLM to locate the direction-binding gap. 2) Propose DeltaDirect, a projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. 3) Create MoDirect, a dataset family for motion direction instruction tuning/evaluation. 4) Evaluate on MoDirect-SynBench and MoDirect-RealBench with and without DeltaDirect.
AI.result: DeltaDirect significantly boosts motion direction accuracy: MoDirect-SynBench accuracy improves from 25.9% to 85.4% with instruction tuning. On MoDirect-RealBench, accuracy improves by 21.9 points over a vanilla baseline without real-world tuning data, while preserving standard video-understanding performance.
AI.conclusion: The study exposes a direction binding gap in Video-LLMs and provides a targeted training objective (DeltaDirect) and benchmark suite (MoDirect) to align motion direction understanding with verbal outputs, achieving strong synthetic gains and meaningful real-world improvements without sacrificing overall video capabilities.

title: Relational Linear Properties in Language Models: An Empirical Investigation
summary: Linear properties are ubiquitous in the representations of language models; however, testing them experimentally remains a challenging task. This work focuses on relational linearity: the hypothesis that, for a fixed relation (e.g., "plays"), the unembedding of an object (e.g., "trumpet") can be predicted from the embedding of its subject (e.g.,"Miles Davis") by a linear map. We present an experimental method to test the formulation of relational linearity by Marconato et al. (2025). Specifically, we introduce a probing method, based on Kullback-Leibler divergence, to evaluate this property and examine its variation across layers and paraphrased relational queries. It is also more efficient than previous work; for example, it avoids the crude Jacobian approximations used in Linear Relational Embeddings by Hernandez et al. (2024). Our findings across four datasets show that relational linearity varies across models, exhibits layer-wise patterns consistent with prior observations about linguistic information in model representations, and is differently affected by changes in how the relation is phrased.

AI.tldr: KL-divergence–based probing reveals that relational linearity in language models is model- and layer-dependent and sensitive to relation phrasing; the method is more efficient than Jacobian-based approaches and validated across four datasets.
AI.motivation: To test the existence and behavior of relational linearity in language models efficiently, building on Marconato et al. (2025) while addressing inefficiencies in prior Jacobian-based methods.
AI.method: Introduce a KL-divergence-based probing method to evaluate relational linearity, assess across layers and paraphrased relational queries, and compare with Jacobian-approximation approaches used in earlier work (e.g., Hernandez et al. 2024).
AI.result: Relational linearity varies across models and layers, showing layer-wise patterns consistent with known linguistic information distribution; its strength is differentially affected by how the relation is phrased, as observed across four datasets.
AI.conclusion: Relational linearity is not uniform across models or layers and is sensitive to phrasing; KL-divergence probing provides an efficient, scalable tool to study this property and invites broader cross-model, cross-relation analyses.

title: F-TIS: Harnessing Diverse Models in Collaborative GRPO
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop Scalable Learning and Optimization for Efficient Multimodal AI Agents (SCALE)
summary: Reinforcement learning methods such as GRPO have seen great popularity in LLM post-training. In GRPO, models produce completions to a set of prompts, which are rewarded, and the policy is updated towards the relatively high reward completions. Due to the auto-regressive nature of models, the generation phase of such style of training can be extremely time consuming. As a solution, prior work has sought to distribute the inference step across many nodes, working parallel. These works assume primarily homogeneous models in the training in order to keep samples as close to on-policy as possible. This assumption may be impractical in decentralized systems, where parties with various computes and preferences may wish to collaborate on the same task. Thus, decentralized training requires an approach that can handle heterogeneous models - different models collaborating on the same tasks. However, this leads to highly off-policy samples presented during training, which prior work has identified that off-policy samples can hurt GRPO convergence. To enable heterogeneity, we propose Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS) - a GRPO-style training paradigm that can use off-policy samples to improve local model's learning. Our framework allows various models to collaborate in the same RL training run while being communication efficient. We extensively evaluate F-TIS in various heterogeneous setups and we show that it exhibits identical final model convergence to purely on-sample training. Furthermore, we observe in some setups better generalization on out-of-distribution tasks than on-policy training, increasing model's performance by up to 12\%.

AI.tldr: A decentralized GRPO-style RL training framework (F-TIS) enables heterogeneous models to collaborate by using filtered truncated importance sampling to leverage off-policy samples. It preserves the final convergence of on-policy training and can improve generalization on out-of-distribution tasks, with up to 12% performance gains in some setups, while improving communication efficiency.
AI.motivation: Off-policy data from heterogeneous, distributed RL training can destabilize or slow convergence in GRPO-like methods. There is a need for a method that tolerates model heterogeneity and fragmentation of data in decentralized settings, while still achieving convergence and efficient communication.
AI.method: Introduce Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS) within a GRPO-like training loop. F-TIS applies filtering and truncation to importance weights to safely incorporate off-policy samples from heterogeneous models, enabling collaboration across different architectures/computational resources in a single training run, with an emphasis on communication efficiency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in diverse heterogeneous setups show that F-TIS achieves identical final convergence to purely on-policy training. In some setups, it also improves generalization on out-of-distribution tasks, with performance gains up to 12%.
AI.conclusion: F-TIS provides a practical pathway for decentralized, heterogeneous RL training with GRPO-like objectives, maintaining convergence parity with on-policy training and offering potential generalization benefits, along with improved communication efficiency.

title: ImplicitTerrainV2: Wavelet-Guided Spatially Adaptive Neural Terrain Representation
summary: Digital elevation models (DEMs) underpin terrain analysis in Geographic Information Systems (GIS), but in their common raster form, they rely on interpolation for off-grid sampling and finite-difference operators for derivative-based analysis. Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer a continuous alternative, but prior terrain INRs lack explicit frequency control, neglect the gradient structure of terrain, and remain too large and costly to train for practical deployment. We present ImplicitTerrainV2, which advances terrain INRs toward a compact, efficient neural terrain data format by combining a spectral control mechanism with wavelet-guided spatial adaptivity, derivative-aware supervision, and post-training model compression. At its core, a wavelet complexity field (WCF) derives spatially-adaptive frequency masks from analytically computed wavelet coefficients, localizing high-frequency capacity to complex terrain regions. The same field guides complexity-aware adaptive sampling that concentrates training in high-complexity regions, while gradient matching applies extra supervision to enforce the smooth manifold structure of terrain DEMs for improved derivative fidelity. Post-training mixed-precision quantization and entropy coding reduce storage to 1.23 bpp with a 0.28 dB PSNR drop. On 50 Swiss terrain tiles, ImplicitTerrainV2 reaches 66.25 dB end-to-end PSNR, improving over the prior work by 5.70 dB while using 3.2x fewer parameters and training in 55 s per tile on a single GPU. Our compressed neural format is competitive with several established DEM codecs in rate-distortion performance, while additionally supporting off-grid point queries, closed-form derivative evaluation, and resolution-independent reconstruction, which may benefit many downstream GIS applications.

AI.tldr: A compact, derivative-aware implicit terrain representation (ImplicitTerrainV2) combines wavelet-guided spatial adaptivity with spectral control and post-training compression, enabling efficient off-grid queries and derivative evaluation with competitive rate-distortion.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of prior terrain INRs, which lack explicit frequency control, ignore gradient structure, and are costly to train/store for practical GIS deployment.
AI.method: Introduce a wavelet complexity field (WCF) to produce spatially adaptive frequency masks, guide complexity-aware adaptive sampling, apply gradient-matching supervision, and perform post-training mixed-precision quantization and entropy coding.
AI.result: On 50 Swiss terrain tiles: end-to-end PSNR 66.25 dB (+5.70 dB over prior work); 3.2x fewer parameters; ~55 s per tile training on a single GPU; compressed size 1.23 bpp with 0.28 dB PSNR degradation; competitive rate-distortion vs. established DEM codecs; supports off-grid queries, closed-form derivatives, and resolution-independent reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: ImplicitTerrainV2 delivers a compact, efficient neural terrain data format with explicit frequency control and gradient-aware supervision, achieving high fidelity and practical GIS applicability while enabling off-grid queries and derivative evaluation.

title: Neural Flow Operators can Approximate any Operator: Abstract Frameworks and Universal Approcimations
summary: We introduce an abstract neural flow framework for neural networks and neural operators. The framework contains two continuous-depth models, namely neural flows with composition and separation structures, and covers both finite-dimensional function approximation and infinite-dimensional operator approximation. We prove well-posedness and universal approximation properties for the corresponding neural flows, including, to the best of our knowledge, the first universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces. We also obtain universal approximation results for convolutional neural flow models. Through suitable time discretizations, the composition structure recovers ResNet-type architectures, while the separation structure, via a splitting-based discretization, yields plain architectures. This gives a unified flow-based route to both residual and plain architectures for neural networks and neural operators with fully connected or convolutional linear layers.

AI.tldr: Abstract proposes a continuous-depth neural flow framework for neural networks and neural operators with two structures (composition and separation). It establishes well-posedness and universal approximation, including the first universal result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces, and extends to convolutional flows. Time discretization recovers ResNet-type (composition) and plain architectures (separation), unifying residual and plain architectures for fully connected and convolutional layers.
AI.motivation: Bridge theory and practice for neural flows across finite- and infinite-dimensional settings, providing universal approximation guarantees and a unified architecture space that encompasses ResNet and plain networks via discretization.
AI.method: Define two continuous-depth neural flow models (composition and separation); prove well-posedness; prove universal approximation (finite- and infinite-dimensional); derive universal results for convolutional neural flows; analyze time discretizations that recover ResNet and splitting-based plain architectures; extend to FC and conv layers.
AI.result: First universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces; universal results for convolutional neural flows; well-posedness established; discretizations yield ResNet-type and plain architectures; unification of architectures for neural networks and neural operators.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified, flow-based view that links continuous-depth models to both residual (composition) and plain (separation) architectures for neural networks and neural operators, applicable to fully connected and convolutional layers.

title: Regret-Based $(ε,δ)$-optimal Stopping Criteria for Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a widely used iterative black-box optimization method that utilizes Gaussian process (GP) surrogate models. In practice, BO is typically terminated after a fixed evaluation budget is exhausted, which can incur unnecessary cost and provides no optimality guarantee on solution quality. Recent research in developing a practical stopping criterion has made empirical progress, yet a theoretically sound stopping criterion remains a work in progress. In this work, we present provably tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) at any given iteration. Then, we propose stopping criteria for GP-UCB based on this tighter bound that ensures an $ε$-optimal solution with high probability $1-δ$ upon termination. Numerical experiments are performed to validate and demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our stopping criteria.

AI.tldr: Proposes stopping criteria for GP-UCB based on provably tighter instantaneous regret bounds, enabling ε-optimal solutions with high probability and validated by experiments.
AI.motivation: To reduce unnecessary evaluations in Bayesian optimization by providing a theoretically sound stopping rule that guarantees near-optimality with high probability.
AI.method: Derives tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP-UCB and designs stopping criteria that ensure ε-optimality with probability 1-δ at termination; validates the criteria through numerical experiments.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates tighter bounds and shows that the proposed stopping criteria produce ε-optimal solutions with high probability and are effective and efficient in practice, as evidenced by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: A theoretically grounded and practically validated stopping rule for GP-UCB is established, enabling principled early termination in Bayesian optimization with high-probability ε-optimality.

title: GraphFlow: A Graph-Based Workflow Management for Efficient LLM-Agent Serving
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents demonstrate strong reasoning and execution capabilities on complex tasks when guided by structured instructions, commonly referred to as workflows. However, existing workflow-assisted agent serving systems typically rely on predefined templates and shallow matching mechanisms, which limit their ability to capture deep semantic relationships and generalize to previously unseen tasks. To address these limitations, we propose a new workflow management paradigm that represents workflows using a unified graph, termed wGraph, where each node corresponds to an atomic operation. wGraph serves as a shared substrate from which task-specific workflows are dynamically instantiated. Building on wGraph primitives, we introduce GraphFlow, a system that efficiently integrates workflows into agent serving through two key designs. First, adaptive workflow generation dynamically constructs workflows from wGraph based on task semantics and constraint requirements. Second, workflow state management exploits wGraph structure to efficiently manage Key-Value (KV) caches, reducing redundant computation during agent serving. Extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets show that GraphFlow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding an average performance improvement of approximately 4.95 percentage points, while achieving an approximately 4$\times$ reduction in memory footprint.

AI.tldr: Proposes wGraph, a unified graph representation of atomic operations for workflows, and GraphFlow, a system that dynamically instantiates task-specific workflows and manages KV caches to improve performance and reduce memory usage in LLM-based agents.
AI.motivation: Current workflow-assisted agent systems rely on predefined templates and shallow matching, hindering deep semantic understanding and generalization to unseen tasks; efficient KV-cache management is needed to reduce redundant computation during serving.
AI.method: Introduce wGraph where each node is an atomic operation; develop GraphFlow that (a) adaptively generates workflows from wGraph based on task semantics and constraints, and (b) manages workflow state and KV caches using the graph structure to cut redundant computation. Evaluate on five benchmark datasets against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.result: GraphFlow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with an average performance improvement of about 4.95 percentage points and roughly a 4× reduction in memory footprint across the benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A graph-based workflow representation with adaptive generation and KV-cache-aware state management enables more generalizable and efficient LLM-driven agent workflows, offering improved performance and substantial memory savings; suggests avenues for broader adoption and extensions to more complex or dynamic task settings.

title: A Tutorial on Diffusion Theory: From Differential Equations to Diffusion Models
comment: A detailed tutorial on Diffusion models and SDE
summary: This tutorial develops diffusion models from the viewpoint of differential equations. We begin with the conditional Gaussian forward process and show that this path admits both an ordinary differential equation (ODE) representation and a stochastic differential equation (SDE) representation. Averaging the conditional process over the data distribution then yields marginalized forward ODE and SDE formulations that transport the data distribution $p_0=p_{\mathrm{data}}$ to a Gaussian prior $p_1=\mathcal{N}(0,I)$. We next derive the corresponding reverse-time dynamics, namely the reverse SDE and the reverse probability-flow ODE, both of which are governed by the marginal score $\grad\log p_t(x)$. This leads to a training objective for score estimation and shows that the standard noise-prediction objective is equivalent to score matching up to an additive constant independent of the model parameters. We then discuss sampling methods for the learned reverse dynamics, including DPM-Solver, as well as guided sampling through classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance. Finally, we compare DDPM and DDIM with the reverse SDE/ODE framework and show that they share the same training objective, while DDPM sampling corresponds to discrete reverse-SDE sampling and DDIM sampling corresponds to reverse-ODE sampling.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models are reframed via forward Gaussian diffusion to yield both ODE and SDE forms; the marginal forward process transports data to a Gaussian prior; reverse dynamics are governed by the marginal score; training aligns with score matching and noise-prediction is equivalent up to a constant; sampling includes DPM-Solver and guidance methods; DDPM and DDIM are unified under this framework as discrete reverse-SDE and reverse-ODE.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, differential-equation-based understanding of diffusion models, clarifying how forward and reverse processes relate, how training objectives correspond to score estimation, and how various sampling and discrete/continuous methods fit within the same theory.
AI.method: Derive conditional Gaussian forward process with both ODE and SDE representations; marginalize over data to obtain p_t that transports p_0 to p_1 = N(0,I); derive reverse-time SDE and reverse-ODE governed by grad log p_t(x); formulate score-estimation training and show equivalence to noise-prediction; discuss sampling methods (DPM-Solver, classifier guidance, classifier-free); compare DDPM and DDIM within reverse-SDE/ODE framework, showing shared training objective and mapping of sampling to discrete/continuous reversals.
AI.result: A cohesive theoretical framework that unifies forward/marginalized and reverse dynamics under ODE/SDE, clarifies the score-based training objective, and situates common diffusion-model variants (DDPM, DDIM) and sampling techniques within this framework.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion-model literature benefits from a differential-equation perspective, enabling a consistent interpretation of objectives and sampling methods, and highlighting the equivalence of discrete and continuous formulations under a common reverse-dynamics viewpoint.

title: Do Deep Ensembles Actually Capture Uncertainty in Graph Neural Networks?
summary: While deep ensembles are widely considered to be the default method for uncertainty quantification in deep learning, their effectiveness for graph-structured data is often simply assumed based on successes in domains like computer vision. We investigate standard deep ensembles specifically for message-passing graph neural networks. Benchmarking across seven datasets representing varied tasks and complexities, we reveal that ensembles provide surprisingly little improvement over a single model. Instead, the observed marginal gains stem primarily from stabilizing optimization noise in point predictions rather than yielding meaningfully better uncertainty estimates. Through an aleatoric-epistemic decomposition, we identify epistemic collapse: independently trained networks consistently converge to overly similar predictions. Because disagreement is the fundamental mechanism through which ensembles capture epistemic uncertainty, this lack of diversity neutralizes their key advantage. Analyzing this phenomenon further, we suggest this collapse is driven by functional rather than weight-space convexity, where distinct parameter solutions induce almost identical behavior. Our results suggest that deep ensemble success does not seamlessly transfer to graph machine learning.

AI.tldr: Deep ensembles offer limited uncertainty benefits for graph neural networks; gains mainly come from stabilizing optimization noise, not better epistemic uncertainty due to epistemic collapse and lack of diversity, likely driven by functional convexity.
AI.motivation: To test whether deep ensembles that work for CV translate to graph-structured data and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Benchmark standard deep ensembles on seven graph datasets with varied tasks; perform aleatoric-epistemic decomposition; analyze predictive disagreement and weight-space vs functional diversity; diagnose convexity cause.
AI.result: Ensembles yield minimal improvement over single GNNs; gains mainly from reducing optimization noise; epistemic uncertainty not improved due to networks converging to similar predictions (epistemic collapse); likely due to functional convexity across independent training runs; diversity is limited; this undermines ensembles' core benefit.
AI.conclusion: Deep ensembles do not directly transfer to graph ML; alternative strategies needed to achieve reliable uncertainty estimation in GNNs, possibly methods that promote diversity or different uncertainty frameworks.

title: Factored Diffusion Policies:Compositionally Generalized Robot Control with a Single Score Network
summary: Robotic tasks are typically specified by a tuple of factors, such as the object to be grasped, the obstacles to be avoided, the color of the target, and so on. Collecting expert demonstrations for every combination of factor values grows combinatorially. We present factored diffusion policies: a single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, whose score decomposes additively across factors at inference. Under approximate conditional independence between factors given the action-observation pair, this composition approximates the true joint score with a bounded uniform error, reducing the training-task budget from a product of factor cardinalities to a sum. A trajectory-tube certificate chains this score-level bound through the reverse-time sampling ODE and a contracting tracking controller into a closed-loop state-trajectory tube whose radius factors into an ODE-sensitivity constant and a per-factor score-error budget. Unlike compositional-diffusion methods for control that combine separately trained networks, we use one shared network. Drone racing experiments confirm both the generalization bound and the certificate. On state-based multi-gate racing, the factored policy passes 90% of held-out gates -- matching an oracle -- while a K-network composition baseline collapses to 3%; on vision-based single-gate traversal, it transfers zero-shot to an unseen venue with +11.7pp success-rate gain and 2.4X crash-rate reduction.

AI.tldr: Single shared diffusion model with per-factor null-token dropout enables factored scores; under approximate conditional independence, achieves near joint performance with far less training data; provides a trajectory-tube certificate linking score-level bounds to closed-loop robustness; demonstrates strong generalization and transfer in drone racing tasks.
AI.motivation: Address combinatorial explosion in demonstrations when tasks are defined by multiple factors (e.g., object, obstacles, color). A scalable, generalizable policy is needed that can compose per-factor information without training on all factor combinations.
AI.method: Train one diffusion network with per-factor dropout (null-token). At inference, decompose the score additively across factors. Rely on approximate conditional independence between factors given the action-observation pair to bound the joint score error. Propagate this score bound through the reverse-time sampling ODE and a contracting tracking controller to obtain a closed-loop state-trajectory tube with radius determined by an ODE-sensitivity constant and per-factor score-error budget. Use one shared network (vs. K-network baselines of separately trained networks).
AI.result: The approach yields a uniform bound on the joint score error under the conditional-independence approximation. It reduces the training budget from a product of factor cardinalities to a sum. Empirically, in drone racing, the method generalizes well: on state-based multi-gate racing it reaches 90% of held-out gates (matching an oracle), whereas a K-network composition baseline collapses to 3%. In vision-based single-gate traversal, the method transfers zero-shot to an unseen venue with +11.7 percentage-point increase in success rate and 2.4x fewer crashes.
AI.conclusion: Factored diffusion policies with a shared network and per-factor dropout enable scalable, robust control policy learning across factor-rich tasks. Theoretical bounds (uniform score error, trajectory-tube certificate) support robustness, while empirical results demonstrate strong generalization and transfer, outperforming compositional baselines.

title: The Double Dilemma in Multi-Task Radiology Report Generation: A Gradient Dynamics Analysis and Solution
summary: While multi-task learning based automatic radiology report generation (RRG) is widely adopted to ensure clinical consistency, most focus on architectural designs yet remain limited to coarse linear scalarization strategies. These strategies cannot effectively balance the hard constraints of discriminative clinical supervision with the smoothness requirements of report generation. To address these problems, we analyze the failure mechanism of linear scalarization from the perspective of gradient dynamics, utilizing the stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework to characterize it as a "Double Dilemma" of drift term deviation and diffusion term decay. Based on this, we propose a backbone-agnostic optimizer named Conflict-Averse Magnitude-Enhanced Gradient Descent (CAME-Grad). Through conflict-averse direction rectification and magnitude-enhanced energy injection, the algorithm not only ensures geometric validity, but also avoids local optimal solutions. Then, the adaptive gradient fusion mechanism is used to establish a dynamic balance between the theoretical optimal direction and the task-specific inductive bias. Experiments show that as a universal plug-and-play optimizer, CAME-Grad brings substantial and consistent improvements across eight diverse RRG methods, elevating overall clinical efficacy performance by an average of 2.3\% on MIMIC-CXR and 1.9\% on IU X-Ray. Our code is available at https://github.com/vpsg-research/CAME-Grad.

AI.tldr: Analyzes gradient dynamics of linear scalarization in multi-task radiology report generation using SDEs, identifies a "+Double Dilemma" of drift deviation and diffusion decay, and proposes CAME-Grad, a backbone-agnostic optimizer with conflict-averse direction rectification, magnitude-enhanced energy injection, and adaptive gradient fusion, yielding consistent gains across eight RRG methods.
AI.motivation: To address the tension between discriminative clinical supervision and smooth report generation in multi-task RRG, highlighting the insufficiency of linear scalarization due to gradient dynamics leading to suboptimal convergence.
AI.method: Propose Conflict-Averse Magnitude-Enhanced Gradient Descent (CAME-Grad). Key components: conflict-averse direction rectification to ensure geometric validity, magnitude-enhanced energy injection to avoid local optima, and adaptive gradient fusion to balance theoretical optimal direction with task-specific inductive bias. Described as backbone-agnostic and plug-and-play; analyses framed via SDE to reveal the Double Dilemma.
AI.result: CAME-Grad improves performance across eight diverse RRG methods, with average gains of 2.3% on MIMIC-CXR and 1.9% on IU X-Ray; code available at the authors' GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CAME-Grad serves as a universal plug-and-play optimizer for RRG that mitigates gradient-dynamics pitfalls of linear scalarization, achieving geometric validity and better optimization trajectories, with broad applicability across architectures and datasets.

title: Benchmarking Machine Learning Architectures for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Pediatric ICUs
summary: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) is critical in pediatric intensive care units (PICUs), where diagnostic uncertainty often drives broad-spectrum antibiotic use, increasing antimicrobial resistance and potential long-term harms. Machine learning offers a promising approach for identifying patient-level opportunities for stewardship interventions from electronic health record data, yet prior work has focused largely on adult populations and static tabular representations. We present a systematic benchmarking study of AMS intervention prediction in the PICU across a public dataset and a private institutional cohort. We define four clinically relevant proxy targets for reducing antibiotic exposure: intravenous-to-oral switching, de-escalation, discontinuation, and short-course therapy. Under a unified evaluation framework, we compare tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions. We find that predictive performance is driven primarily by target prevalence and dataset characteristics rather than model complexity. Sequence models improve the precision-recall trade-off over tabular approaches at coarse (24-hour) resolution, while finer temporal modeling provides limited additional benefit. However, these gains come at the cost of poorer calibration, with simpler tabular models yielding more reliable probability estimates. Multi-task learning produces only marginal improvements, suggesting limited shared structure across stewardship targets. Our findings highlight the importance of target design, temporal representation, and calibration in clinical machine learning, and provide practical guidance for developing reliable decision support systems for pediatric AMS.

AI.tldr: Benchmark study of ML-based AMS intervention prediction in PICU across public and private cohorts. Performance is driven by target prevalence and data characteristics rather than model complexity; sequence models improve PR at 24h but calibrate poorly; tabular models offer better-calibrated probabilities; multi-task learning provides limited benefit.
AI.motivation: Reduce antibiotic exposure and resistance in pediatric ICUs by identifying patient-level opportunities for stewardship interventions using EHR data; addresses gap in pediatric-focused benchmarks and explores temporal modeling beyond static tabular representations.
AI.method: Define four proxy targets (IV-to-PO switch, de-escalation, discontinuation, short-course therapy). Compare tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions within a unified evaluation framework, using a public dataset and a private institutional cohort. Evaluate at coarse (24h) and finer time horizons; assess calibration and multi-task learning effects.
AI.result: Predictive performance is largely driven by target prevalence and dataset characteristics rather than model complexity. Sequence models improve the precision-recall trade-off over tabular methods at 24-hour resolution; finer temporal modeling offers limited additional benefit. However, sequence models exhibit poorer calibration. Tabular models provide more reliable probability estimates. Multi-task learning yields only marginal improvements, suggesting limited shared structure across stewardship targets.
AI.conclusion: Design choices—target formulation, temporal representation, and calibration—are crucial for reliable pediatric AMS ML systems. The study offers practical guidance for building decision support in PICU stewardship and highlights where model complexity yields diminishing returns.

title: Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization for LLM-Guided Program Discovery
summary: Recent LLM-guided evolutionary search methods have shown that iterative program mutation can discover strong algorithms, but they typically optimize each task independently, even when related tasks share reusable structure. We introduce Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization (EMO) for LLM-guided program discovery, and propose EMO-STA (Shared-Then-Adapt), a two-stage framework that first evolves a shared archive of executable programs across a task family and then adapts selected shared candidates to each target task. Within EMO-STA, we explore multiple adaptation strategies, including warm-starting from the shared archive, adapting the best average shared program, and adapting the shared program that performs best on each target task. Across eight task families spanning continuous optimization, geometric construction, modeling, and algorithmic optimization, EMO-STA improves over matched-compute single-task evolution in most settings, with STA Best-Local providing the strongest in-distribution adaptation and STA Best-Shared yielding robust transfer to unseen tasks. Compute-allocation experiments show that allocating a substantial fraction of the family-level budget to shared evolution is consistently beneficial, with roughly balanced shared and adaptation budgets often being optimal. Beyond compute efficiency, we show that shared evolution can mitigate overfitting in low-evidence settings (e.g. few training data), including ARC tasks and time-series feature engineering, by favoring programs that generalize across all tasks rather than exploiting task-specific brittle artifacts.

AI.tldr: EMO-STA: a two-stage Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization for LLM-guided program discovery that first evolves a shared archive across a task family and then adapts to individual tasks, yielding better cross-task generalization, compute efficiency, and reduced overfitting compared to single-task evolution.
AI.motivation: LLM-guided program mutation can discover strong algorithms but typically optimizes tasks independently, missing reusable structure across related tasks and leading to inefficiency and brittle solutions.
AI.method: Two-stage EMO-STA: Stage 1 evolve a shared archive of executable programs across a task family; Stage 2 adapt selected shared candidates to each target task using multiple strategies (warm-start from shared archive, adapt best-average shared program, adapt task-best shared program). Evaluate across eight task families including continuous optimization, geometric construction, modeling, and algorithmic optimization; assess compute-allocation between shared evolution and adaptation.
AI.result: EMO-STA generally outperforms matched-compute single-task evolution; STA Best-Local provides strongest in-distribution adaptation; STA Best-Shared offers robust transfer to unseen tasks; allocating a substantial fraction of budget to shared evolution improves performance; balanced shared/adaptation budgets often optimal; shared evolution reduces overfitting in low-evidence settings (e.g., ARC tasks, time-series feature engineering).
AI.conclusion: Shared evolutionary search with a shared archive plus task-specific adaptation is effective for cross-task generalization and compute efficiency in LLM-guided program discovery; provides practical guidance on budget allocation and adaptation strategies, and helps mitigate overfitting by favoring generic, transferable programs.

title: Two is better than one: A Collapse-free Multi-Reward RLIF Training Framework
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has substantially improved the reasoning ability of LLMs, but often depends on external supervision from human annotations or gold-standard solutions. Reinforcement learning from internal feedback (RLIF) has recently emerged as a scalable unsupervised alternative, using signals extracted from the model itself. However, existing RLIF methods typically rely on a single internal reward, which can lead to reward hacking, entropy collapse, and degraded reasoning structure. We propose a multi-reward RLIF framework that decomposes the training signal into two complementary components: an answer-level reward based on cluster voting and a completion-level reward based on token-wise self-certainty. To combine these signals robustly, we apply GDPO-based normalization to reduce reward-scale imbalance. We further introduce KL-Cov regularization, which targets low-entropy token distributions responsible for disproportionate entropy reduction, preserving exploration and preventing late-stage collapse. Across mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, our method improves stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, while achieving performance close to supervised RLVR methods. These results show that complementary internal rewards, combined with targeted regularization, can support stable long-horizon reasoning without relying on external ground-truth supervision. Code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-reward RLIF framework with two internal rewards (cluster-voting answer-level and token-wise self-certainty completion-level), GDPO normalization, and KL-Cov regularization to improve stability and robustness, achieving near-supervised RLVR performance on math reasoning and code generation without external supervision.
AI.motivation: Mitigate reward hacking, entropy collapse, and structure disruption in single-reward RLIF by leveraging complementary internal signals and preventing premature convergence.
AI.method: Decompose signals into (1) an answer-level reward based on cluster voting and (2) a token-wise self-certainty completion reward; apply GDPO-based normalization to balance reward scales; introduce KL-Cov regularization to preserve exploration by maintaining higher-entropy token distributions and mitigating low-entropy collapse; evaluate on mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, with performance approaching that of supervised RLVR methods.
AI.conclusion: Complementary internal rewards combined with targeted regularization can support stable, long-horizon reasoning without external ground-truth supervision; code will be released soon.

title: A note on convergence of Wasserstein policy optimization
summary: Wasserstein Policy Optimization (WPO) is a recently proposed reinforcement learning algorithm that leverages Wasserstein gradient flows to optimize stochastic policies in continuous action spaces. Despite its empirical success, the theoretical convergence properties of WPO in environments with continuous state and action spaces have yet to be fully established. In this note, we argue that WPO within the framework of entropy-regularised Markov Decision Processes converges linearly. This is done by leveraging recent advances in mean-field analysis for convergence of gradient flows using log-Sobole inequalities. Assuming existence of sufficiently regular solution to the gradient flow equation we demonstrate monotonic energy dissipation along the flow and establish a local log-Sobolev inequality. Ultimately, these properties allow us to argue that the value function should converge linearly to the global optimum.

AI.tldr: WPO with entropy-regularised MDPs converges linearly in continuous spaces via Wasserstein gradient flows, under log-Sobolev inequalities and a regular solution assumption, showing monotone energy dissipation and linear convergence of the value function.
AI.motivation: Provide theoretical convergence guarantees for Wasserstein Policy Optimization (WPO) in continuous state/action spaces by leveraging gradient-flow and mean-field tools. Extend entropy-regularised MDP theory to RL with Wasserstein geometry.
AI.method: Employ Wasserstein gradient flows to optimize stochastic policies, analyze energy dissipation along the flow, establish a local log-Sobolev inequality using recent mean-field gradient-flow results, and assume existence of a sufficiently regular solution to the gradient-flow equation to deduce linear convergence of the value function to the global optimum.
AI.result: Under the stated regularity and functional-analytic conditions, the policy/energy decreases monotonically along the flow, the local log-Sobolev inequality yields a linear rate, and the value function is argued to converge linearly to the global optimum.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a principled route to linear convergence guarantees for WPO in entropy-regularised MDPs via gradient-flow and log-Sobolev frameworks, contingent on regularity and inequality assumptions; it clarifies the connection between energy dissipation and policy/value convergence.

title: Why SGD is not Brownian Motion: A New Perspective on Stochastic Dynamics
summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is commonly modeled as a Langevin process, assuming that minibatch noise acts as Brownian motion. However, this approximation relies on a continuous-time limit and a sqrt(eta) noise scaling that does not match the discrete SGD update at finite learning rate. In this work, we propose an alternative formulation of SGD as deterministic dynamics in a fluctuating loss landscape induced by minibatch sampling. Starting directly from the discrete update, we derive a master equation for the parameter distribution and obtain a discrete Fokker--Planck equation that differs from the standard Langevin form at order eta^2. Using this framework, we analyze SGD dynamics near critical points of the loss. We show that the behavior decomposes along the eigenbasis of the mean Hessian into qualitatively distinct regimes. In particular, nearly-flat directions do not admit a stationary distribution: the variance grows over time, corresponding to effective diffusion along valleys with a coefficient proportional to the learning rate. We provide empirical evidence supporting these predictions on neural network models in computer vision and natural language processing, observing a clear qualitative separation between confined and diffusive modes.

AI.tldr: SGD can be viewed as deterministic dynamics in a fluctuating, discretely-sampled loss landscape; a discrete master equation yields a discrete Fokker–Planck different from Langevin at O(η^2). This reveals regime separation along Hessian eigen-directions near critical points, with near-flat directions diffusing (variance grows with time) and diffusion coefficient scaling with the learning rate; empirical NN experiments support the predictions.
AI.motivation: The standard Langevin/Brownian approximation of SGD assumes a continuous-time limit and sqrt(η) noise scaling, which misrepresents the discrete update at finite learning rate. A discrete formulation is needed to capture η^2 corrections and provide accurate insight into SGD dynamics and generalization.
AI.method: Starting from the actual discrete SGD update, derive a master equation for the distribution over parameters. From this, obtain a discrete Fokker–Planck equation that departs from the conventional Langevin form at O(η^2). Perform eigenbasis analysis with respect to the mean Hessian to identify qualitatively distinct dynamical regimes along different directions; show nearly-flat directions lack stationary distribution and exhibit diffusion with coefficient proportional to η.
AI.result: The dynamics decompose into confined versus diffusive modes in the Hessian eigenbasis. Near-flat directions do not settle into a stationary distribution; variance grows over time, indicating effective diffusion along valleys with η-dependent diffusion coefficients. These predictions are corroborated by empirical experiments on neural networks for vision and NLP tasks, revealing a clear separation between confined and diffusive modes.
AI.conclusion: A discrete, non-Langevin treatment of SGD reveals qualitative dynamics near critical points that are missed by standard Langevin approximations. Learning rate controls diffusion along valleys, and the framework provides a more accurate description of SGD behavior and its impact on generalization, consistent with observed mode-wise behavior in deep nets.

title: Posterior Collapse as Automatic Spectral Pruning
summary:
We show that posterior collapse in $β$-VAEs implements automatic spectral pruning. A latent mode collapses if its contribution to reconstruction is below the cutoff set by $β$. Equilibrium solutions with different $β$ thus reveal a cascade of collapses as latent modes decouple from least to most useful.
  We derive this as a consequence of the loss via a Landau stability analysis. We define a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter that ranks active latent modes and whose collapse thresholds identify which effective variables to inspect first.
  In the linear Gaussian case, the collapse spectrum, utility spectrum, and normalized PCA spectrum coincide, and each collapse follows a mean-field law. We test these predictions on the WorldClim dataset.

AI.tldr: Beta-VAE posterior collapse acts as automatic spectral pruning: as beta varies, latent modes collapse in a cascade from least to most useful; a Landau-stability analysis yields a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter that ranks active modes; in the linear-Gaussian case collapse, utility, and PCA spectra coincide; predictions align with WorldClim data.
AI.motivation: Explain the mechanism behind posterior collapse and its relation to latent-spectrum pruning; connect collapse dynamics to spectral decomposition (PCA) and provide a principled, testable framework.
AI.method: Perform a Landau-stability analysis of the beta-VAE loss; define a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter to rank active latent modes; analyze the linear-Gaussian limit where collapse and PCA-like spectra coincide; validate predictions on a real dataset (WorldClim).
AI.result: Identify a cascade of latent-mode collapses as beta increases; collapses follow a mean-field law; in the linear-Gaussian case the collapse spectrum, utility spectrum, and normalized PCA spectrum coincide; empirical tests on WorldClim support the predictions.
AI.conclusion: Posterior collapse implements automatic spectral pruning, providing a principled ordering of latent variables to inspect; the latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter offers a quantitative tool to assess active versus collapsed modes and generalizes to a spectrum-pruning view beyond VAE models.

title: Abstraction for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) often exhibit significant redundancy due to symmetries and shared structure across state-goal pairs in real-world Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (GCRL). While hierarchical policies have been motivated for horizon reduction via temporal abstraction in offline GCRL, we demonstrate that hierarchy also enables absolute abstraction. By introducing relativised options as well as distinct representations for different levels of the hierarchy, we demonstrate how an agent can reuse experience across similar contexts of the state-space. Based on this framework, we introduce two simple algorithms for learning relativised options and abstracting from the absolute frame of reference. Our experiments show that such inductive biases significantly improve performance in offline GCRL.

AI.tldr: Hierarchy with relativised options and distinct representations enables absolute abstraction in offline GCRL; introduces two learning algorithms; empirical results show improved performance.
AI.motivation: MDPs in real-world GCRL exhibit redundancy due to symmetries and shared structure across state-goal pairs. Hierarchical approaches can reduce horizons and enable reuse of experience; this work extends hierarchy to absolute abstraction.
AI.method: Introduce relativised options and separate representations for different hierarchy levels. Propose two simple algorithms for learning relativised options and for abstracting from the absolute frame of reference.
AI.result: Inductive biases from relativised options and frame-abstracting representations significantly improve offline GCRL performance in experiments.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical abstraction enables absolute abstraction through relativised options; the proposed algorithms effectively leverage cross-context experience, yielding improved offline GCRL performance.

title: Clipping Bottleneck: Stabilizing RLVR via Stochastic Recovery of Near-Boundary Signals
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central paradigm for scaling LLM reasoning, yet its optimization often suffers from training instability and suboptimal convergence. Through a systematic dissection of clipping-based GRPO-style objectives, we identify the rigid clipping decision induced by hard clipping as a key practical bottleneck in the studied RLVR setups. Specifically, our analysis suggests that informative signals can lie in the near-boundary region just beyond the clipping threshold, and are therefore discarded by the standard hard-clipping rule. Notably, once this bottleneck is precisely identified, even simple stochastic perturbations at the boundary can recover meaningful performance gains. Building on this finding, we propose Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR), a minimal, plug-and-play modification that stochastically retains these slightly out-of-bound tokens to recover lost signals. While NSR, via stochastic sampling, can be interpreted as inducing an implicit gradient decay in expectation, our ablations reveal that its stochastic, boundary-local rescue mechanism is consistently more effective than deterministic gradient decay. Validated by extensive experiments across model sizes from 7B to 30B and both dense and MoE architectures, as a plug-and-play solution, NSR substantially improves training stability and delivers consistent gains over strong baselines such as DAPO and GSPO.

AI.tldr: NSR is a minimal, plug-and-play method that fixes a clipping bottleneck in RLVR by stochastically retaining near-boundary tokens, improving training stability and gains across 7B–30B LLMs and both dense and MoE architectures.
AI.motivation: Rigid hard clipping in GRPO-style objectives discards informative signals near the clipping threshold, causing training instability and suboptimal convergence in RLVR for large language models.
AI.method: Introduce Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR), which stochastically retains tokens just beyond the clipping threshold to recover lost signals. Treats the stochastic retention as implicit gradient decay in expectation; ablations indicate boundary-local stochastic rescue is more effective than deterministic decay.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across model sizes 7B–30B and both dense and MoE architectures shows NSR yields substantial improvements in training stability and consistent gains over strong baselines like DAPO and GSPO.
AI.conclusion: NSR is a simple, effective plug-and-play modification that rescues near-boundary information lost due to clipping, leading to more stable RLVR training and better performance across diverse model scales and architectures.

title: The Value of Covariance Matching in Gaussian DDPMs and the Lanczos Sampler
summary: A central error measure in Gaussian DDPMs is the path-space KL divergence between the exact reverse chain and the learned Gaussian reverse process. This quantity is especially relevant for procedures such as classifier guidance, which perturb the entire reverse trajectory rather than only the terminal sample. Prior analyses show that standard isotropic reverse covariances suffer an unavoidable $Ω(1/T)$ path-KL error as the number of denoising steps $T$ grows.
