We show that matching the full posterior covariance breaks this barrier, yielding an order-wise improvement that reduces the path KL to $O(1/T^2)$. To make full covariance matching practical, we introduce the Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free method for sampling from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector products, which are available through Jacobian-vector products of the posterior mean. LGS avoids dense covariance storage and auxiliary covariance models. We prove that LGS approximation error decays exponentially in the number of Lanczos steps, where each Lanczos step requires a single Jacobian-vector product. Empirically, using only just three such steps improves sample quality over strong diagonal-covariance baselines, including OCM-DDPM, across standard image benchmarks. This identifies full covariance matching as both theoretically valuable and practically accessible for fast DDPM sampling.

AI.tldr: Full posterior covariance matching reduces path-space KL error in Gaussian DDPMs from ~1/T to ~1/T^2; the Lanczos Gaussian Sampler enables practical, training-free sampling of the optimal reverse covariance with few Jacobian-vector products, yielding improved sample quality with just a few Lanczos steps.
AI.motivation: To reduce the unavoidable path-space KL error in DDPMs that arises when using isotropic or diagonal reverse covariances, thereby improving sampling accuracy and enabling effective guidance methods that traverse the entire reverse trajectory.
AI.method: Replace diagonal reverse covariances with full posterior covariance matching. Propose Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free method that samples from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector/Jacobian-vector products. The Lanczos algorithm yields exponential decay of approximation error with the number of steps, with each step requiring one JVP.
AI.result: The path KL error reduces from Ω(1/T) to O(1/T^2) under full covariance matching. Empirically, using only three Lanczos steps improves sample quality over strong diagonal-covariance baselines (e.g., OCM-DDPM) on standard image benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Full covariance matching is both theoretically advantageous and practically feasible for fast DDPM sampling. LGS enables this without dense covariance storage or training, making high-quality reverse sampling accessible for downstream tasks such as classifier guidance.

title: Reading Task Failure Off the Activations: A Sparse-Feature Audit of GPT-2 Small on Indirect Object Identification
summary: We report a small, reproducible audit of which sparse-autoencoder (SAE) features of GPT-2 small fire differently on failed versus successful trials of the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. On 300 prompts, GPT-2 small reaches 79.7% accuracy; 146 of the 24,576 features in the layer-8 residual-stream SAE release of Bloom (2024) clear a Holm-corrected significance threshold and 105 reach a large effect size (|Cohen's d| > 0.8). The strongest single correlate of failure -- feature 17,491, d=+2.93, Neuronpedia label 'cryptographic keys' -- is essentially silent except when the prompt's transferred object is 'the keys,' on which GPT-2 small fails 93.3% of the time vs. 7.5% on the other seven objects (Fisher exact p = 8.79 x 10^-33). We put this correlate through three controls that a mechanistic claim should pass. (i) A causal ablation: zeroing feature 17,491 in the residual stream across all token positions of the 45 keys prompts does not restore accuracy (6.7% -> 4.4%); the feature is a correlate, not a sufficient cause at this layer. (ii) A representation baseline: a logistic regression on the raw 768-dimensional residual stream reaches 5-fold ROC AUC = 0.929, matching the top-100 SAE features (0.927); the SAE basis adds interpretability, not predictive power. (iii) A seed-robustness check: across five random seeds the keys-subset failure rate stays in 75.0--93.3% (the behavioural effect is real), but feature 17,491 is the top-|d| feature in only 1 of 5 runs. The methodological contribution is therefore the audit pipeline (cheap, model-agnostic, surfaces named correlates) rather than any single feature found through it. We release the code, the 300-prompt corpus, the 300x24,576 activation matrix, the ablation and baseline scripts, and the figures. The full pipeline runs on a laptop (Apple M3 Max, no discrete GPU).

AI.tldr: An audit links a subset of GPT-2 small's sparse-autoencoder features to IOI-task failures, finding one feature strongly associated with the 'keys' object but showing correlational, not causal, links; a logistic-regression baseline matches SAE interpretability gains, and the paper emphasizes a cheap, model-agnostic audit pipeline over single-feature claims.
AI.motivation: Assess whether sparse-autoencoder features in a language model correlate with specific failure modes, and evaluate whether such features are causally driving errors or are simply correlates.
AI.method: 300 prompts analyzed; layer-8 residual-stream SAE features (from Bloom 2024) evaluated for significance (Holm-corrected), effect sizes (Cohen's d); strongest feature 17,491 analyzed via ablation, representation baseline, and seed-robustness checks; compare predictive power of SAE features to a raw residual stream logistic regression; release code and data.
AI.result: GPT-2 small achieves 79.7% accuracy on IOI tasks; 146 SAE features pass Holm-corrected significance; 105 features have |d|>0.8; feature 17,491 correlates with failure on prompts where the transferred object is 'the keys' (d=+2.93); ablation shows zeroing feature 17,491 does not restore accuracy (non-causal at this layer); logistic regression on raw 768-d residual stream achieves ROC AUC 0.929, comparable to SAE features (0.927); seed-robustness: keys-related effect persists (75-93.3%) but 17,491 is top-|d| in only 1/5 seeds; overall pipeline surfaces correlates rather than causal features; full pipeline and data are released.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is an audit pipeline for surface-level correlates in model residual streams rather than a single causal feature; SAE adds interpretability without boosting predictive power; the authors provide a reproducible workflow and data release, enabling cheap, model-agnostic interrogation of failure modes.

title: Multiple Neural Operators Achieve Near-Optimal Rates for Multi-Task Learning
summary: We study the approximation and statistical complexity of learning collections of operators in a shared multi-task setting, with a focus on the Multiple Neural Operators (MNO) architecture. For broad classes of Lipschitz multiple operator maps, we derive near-optimal upper bounds for approximation and statistical generalization. On the lower-bound side, we establish a curse of parametric complexity and prove corresponding minimax rates. Together, these results show that shared representations across tasks do not increase the overall cost: multi-task operator learning follows the same scaling laws as single operator learning. We also compare MNO with a multi-task extension of DeepONet based on concatenated task inputs and show that, from a worst-case approximation-complexity perspective, both architectures satisfy essentially the same asymptotic rates.

AI.tldr: Near-optimal bounds for multi-task operator learning (MNO): shared representations do not increase cost; minimax lower bounds show a curse of parametric complexity; MNO and concatenated DeepONet share essentially the same worst-case asymptotic rates.
AI.motivation: Characterize the approximation and statistical complexity of learning multiple operators jointly, and compare two architectural approaches for multi-task operator learning.
AI.method: Derive upper bounds for approximation and statistical generalization for broad classes of Lipschitz multiple operator maps; establish minimax lower bounds revealing the parametric complexity curse; compare MNO with a multi-task DeepONet variant based on concatenated task inputs by analyzing asymptotic rates.
AI.result: Shared representations across tasks do not increase overall cost; multi-task operator learning obeys the same scaling laws as single operator learning; MNO and the concatenated-input DeepONet exhibit essentially identical asymptotic approximation-generalization rates in the worst case.
AI.conclusion: Joint multi-task operator learning can match the efficiency of single-task learning in the worst case; architectural differences (MNO vs. concatenated DeepONet) do not alter the fundamental asymptotic rates in the studied setting.

title: Proxy-Based Approximation of Shapley and Banzhaf Interactions
summary: Shapley and Banzhaf interactions capture the complex dynamics inherent in modern machine learning applications. However, current estimators for these higher-order interactions trade off between speed and accuracy. To overcome this limitation, we introduce ProxySHAP. ProxySHAP reconciles the high sample efficiency of tree-based proxy models with a principled path to consistency via residual correction. On a theoretical level, we derive a polynomial-time generalization of interventional TreeSHAP to compute exact interaction indices for tree ensembles, successfully bypassing exponential tree-depth dependencies in prior methods. Furthermore, we formally analyze the residual adjustment strategy, characterizing the specific conditions under which Maximum Sample Reuse (MSR) corrects proxy bias without its variance scaling exponentially with interaction size. Extensive benchmarking demonstrates that ProxySHAP sets a new state-of-the-art standard for approximation quality, including in large-scale applications with thousands of features. By achieving the lowest error in both small- and large-budget regimes, ProxySHAP significantly outperforms the prior best estimators ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ, while also delivering superior performance on downstream explainability tasks.

AI.tldr: ProxySHAP delivers accurate, scalable Shapley/Banzhaf interaction estimates for tree ensembles by combining proxy models with residual correction; it provides exact interaction indices in polynomial time for trees, analyzes MSR bias correction, and achieves state-of-the-art approximation on large-scale benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current estimators for higher-order interactions suffer from speed-accuracy trade-offs, especially with many features; need scalable, consistent methods for tree ensembles.
AI.method: Proposes ProxySHAP that uses tree-based proxy models plus residual correction; derives polynomial-time generalization of interventional TreeSHAP for exact interaction indices; analyzes residual adjustment (MSR) conditions to prevent exponential variance; extensive benchmarks against ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ.
AI.result: Achieves lowest approximation error in both small and large-budget regimes; outperforms existing methods on synthetic and real tasks; scales to thousands of features; improves downstream explainability tasks.
AI.conclusion: ProxySHAP provides a principled, scalable route to accurate interaction estimation in ML explainability, combining theoretical guarantees with strong empirical performance; likely to become a new standard in interaction attribution for tree ensembles.

title: Cyber-Physical Anomaly Detection in IoT-Enabled Smart Grids Using Machine Learning and Metaheuristic Feature Optimization
summary: Modern smart grids rely on dense measurement infrastructures, communication links, and intelligent field devices. Although this improves supervision and control, it also increases vulnerability to cyber-physical disruptions. Operators must distinguish physical incidents, such as faults or line disturbances, from malicious actions, such as false data injection or unauthorized command execution. This chapter investigates this problem using the well-known MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Dataset. The proposed method combines machine learning with genetic-algorithm-based feature selection. The objective is twofold: to classify attack and natural events accurately, and to determine whether a reduced set of physically informative PMU/IED measurements can support reliable detection. Several baseline models are evaluated, including logistic regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, and Extra Trees. The results show that tree-based ensemble models are the most effective for the considered dataset, with Extra Trees providing the strongest full-feature baseline. After feature selection, the GA + Extra Trees model reduces the clean PMU feature space from 112 attributes to an average of 27.4 attributes over five runs, while increasing macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837. These results indicate that many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant. A compact subset of phasor-based features can still provide accurate and interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids.

AI.tldr: GA-driven feature selection with Extra Trees reduces PMU/IED feature space and improves detection on the MSU/ORNL dataset; many measurements are redundant.
AI.motivation: Distinguish physical grid incidents from cyber-physical attacks under high-dimensional PMU/IED data and obtain a compact, physically informative feature set for reliable anomaly detection.
AI.method: Benchmark multiple classifiers (Logistic Regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, Extra Trees). Apply a genetic algorithm to select a subset of features, optimizing detection metrics. Compare full-feature vs GA-reduced models, focusing on macro-F1 and ROC-AUC.
AI.result: Tree-based ensembles are most effective; Extra Trees is the strongest full-feature baseline. GA+ET reduces features from 112 to ~27.4 on average over five runs, while raising macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837.
AI.conclusion: Many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant; a compact phasor-based feature set can still provide accurate, interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids.

title: CogAdapt: Transferring Clinical ECG Foundation Models to Wearable Cognitive Load Assessment via Lead Adaptation
comment: 7 pages, 7 figures. Submitted to IEEE-EMBS International Conference on Biomedical and Health Informatics (BHI 2026)
summary: Real-time cognitive load assessment is essential for adaptive human-computer interaction but remains challenging due to limited labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization. Recent ECG foundation models pre-trained on millions of clinical recordings offer rich representations, but cannot be directly applied to wearable devices due to sensor configuration mismatch and task differences. In this paper, we propose CogAdapt, a framework that adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment. CogAdapt introduces LeadBridge, a learnable adapter that transforms 3-lead wearable signals into anatomically consistent 12-lead representations, and ProFine, a progressive fine-tuning strategy that gradually unfreezes encoder layers while preventing catastrophic forgetting. Evaluations on two public datasets (CLARE and CL-Drive) under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation show that CogAdapt substantially outperforms baselines trained from scratch, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.626 and 0.768. These results demonstrate the promise of foundation model adaptation for subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearable sensors.

AI.tldr: CogAdapt adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment using a learnable LeadBridge adapter and ProFine progressive fine-tuning, yielding improved subject-independent performance on CLARE and CL-Drive.
AI.motivation: Real-time cognitive load estimation from ECG is hindered by scarce labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization. Pre-trained clinical ECG models are not readily transferable to wearables due to sensor and task differences.
AI.method: LeadBridge: a learnable adapter translating 3-lead wearable ECG into anatomically consistent 12-lead representations. ProFine: progressive fine-tuning that progressively unfreezes encoder layers to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: On two public datasets (CLARE, CL-Drive) with leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, CogAdapt outperformed baselines trained from scratch, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.626 and 0.768.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model adaptation is a promising approach for subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearable ECG sensors.

title: Ternary Decision Trees with Locally-Adaptive Uncertainty Zones
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 5 appendix sections. Submitted to Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery (DAMI)
summary: Decision trees partition the feature space using hard binary thresholds, assigning identical confidence to instances far from a decision boundary and to those directly on it. We introduce ternary decision trees, which augment each split node with an uncertainty zone of half-width delta centered on the optimal threshold. Instances in this zone receive predictions formed by weighted blending of both child subtrees and are flagged as boundary-uncertain, signaling that downstream applications may treat these predictions differently. Crucially, delta is computed locally at each node from statistics already available during standard CART split finding, requiring no external noise specification. We propose and evaluate five delta-estimation methods: quality-plateau (plateau width of the split criterion curve), class-overlap (empirical class-distribution overlap), gain-ratio (split quality relative to split entropy), node-bootstrap (threshold variance under node-level resampling), and margin (SVM-inspired distance to the nearest cross-class training example). Evaluated across 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets with 5-fold cross-validation, all five methods with probabilistic routing significantly outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p < 0.001). The margin method achieves the best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per unit of boundary-uncertain flagging rate), wins on 42 of 72 datasets, and requires zero additional hyperparameters. Analysis on three Breiman synthetic benchmarks reveals that margin is self-calibrating on clean data while node-bootstrap and quality-plateau best track theoretical irreducible error. Experiments on four medical and financial datasets demonstrate practical value: on mammography, node-bootstrap achieves +0.71% decided accuracy by flagging 10.8% of screening cases as boundary-uncertain.

AI.tldr: Ternary decision trees extend CART by adding an uncertainty zone around each split, enabling probabilistic routing and a boundary-uncertain flag; five locally-estimated delta rules improve decided accuracy across 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets, with the margin rule performing best, hyperparameter-free and robust; practical gains observed in medical/financial data.
AI.motivation: CART uses hard thresholds that yield equal confidence far from and exactly on the boundary. Introducing a local uncertainty zone aims to better reflect predictive ambiguity near splits and provide downstream handling via a boundary-uncertain signal, computed without external noise assumptions.
AI.method: Augment each split with an uncertainty zone of width delta around the optimal threshold. In-zone instances have predictions formed by blending the child subtrees and are flagged as boundary-uncertain. Delta is estimated locally from standard CART statistics using five methods: quality-plateau, class-overlap, gain-ratio, node-bootstrap, and margin. Evaluate with probabilistic routing on 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets (5-fold CV); compare to CART. Additional experiments include three Breiman benchmarks and four medical/financial datasets (e.g., mammography).
AI.result: All five delta-estimation methods with probabilistic routing outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (Wilcoxon p<0.001). Margin delivers the best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per boundary-uncertain rate), wins on 42/72 datasets, and requires no extra hyperparameters. Margin is self-calibrating on clean data; node-bootstrap and quality-plateau best track theoretical irreducible error. In mammography, node-bootstrap achieves +0.71% decided accuracy at a 10.8% boundary-uncertain rate.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware ternary trees improve predictive performance and provide actionable boundary-uncertainty signals. The margin delta is robust and parameter-free, while node-bootstrap and quality-plateau offer strong alignment with irreducible error. The approach demonstrates practical value in medical and financial domains, leveraging delta computed from existing split statistics for easy adoption.

title: MambaGaze: Bidirectional Mamba with Explicit Missing Data Modeling for Cognitive Load Assessment from Eye-Gaze Tracking Data
comment: Submitted to IEEE-EMBS International Conference on Biomedical and Health Informatics (BHI 2026)
summary: Real-time cognitive load assessment from eye-tracking signals could potentially enable adaptive human-centered-AI such as safety-critical applications such as driver vigilance monitoring or automated flight deck assistance, yet two challenges persist: handling frequent data missingness from blinks and tracking failures, and efficiently modeling long-range temporal dependencies. We propose MambaGaze, a framework that addresses these challenges through 1) XMD encoding, which augments raw features with observation masks and time-deltas to explicitly model data uncertainty, and 2) bidirectional Mamba-2, which captures temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity. Experiments on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets under leave-one-subject-out evaluation show that MambaGaze achieves 76.8% and 73.1% accuracy, respectively, outperforming CNN, Transformer, ResNet, and VGG baselines by 4-12 percentage points. Edge deployment benchmarks on NVIDIA Jetson platforms demonstrate real-time inference at 43-68 FPS with power consumption below 7.5W, confirming feasibility for wearable cognitive load monitoring.

AI.tldr: MambaGaze introduces XMD encoding and bidirectional Mamba-2 to enable real-time cognitive load estimation from eye-tracking, addressing data missingness and long-range temporal dependencies; it achieves competitive accuracy and real-time performance on edge devices.
AI.motivation: Handling frequent data missingness from blinks and tracking failures and efficiently modeling long-range temporal dependencies for accurate, real-time cognitive load estimation in safety-critical contexts.
AI.method: XMD encoding augments raw eye-tracking features with observation masks and time-deltas to model data uncertainty. Bidirectional Mamba-2 captures temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity. Evaluation uses leave-one-subject-out on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets; edge benchmarks on NVIDIA Jetson.
AI.result: Accuracy of 76.8% (CLARE) and 73.1% (CL-Drive), outperforming CNN, Transformer, ResNet, VGG baselines by 4–12 percentage points. Edge inference 43–68 FPS with <7.5W power consumption.
AI.conclusion: MambaGaze demonstrates feasibility of wearable cognitive load monitoring with real-time inference on resource-constrained devices and potential for safety-critical HCI applications.

title: SeqLoRA: Bilevel Orthogonal Adaptation for Continual Multi-Concept Generation
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning enables fast personalization of text-to-image diffusion models, but composing multiple custom concepts remains challenging due to representation interference. Existing modular methods either rely on expensive post-hoc fusion or freeze adaptation subspaces, which limit expressiveness and concept fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose Sequential regularized LoRA (SeqLoRA), a constrained continual learning framework that jointly optimizes both LoRA factors via bilevel optimization. Theoretically, we establish strong convergence guarantees for our algorithm and model the residual layer activations as a matrix sub-Gaussian process to derive high-probability bounds on catastrophic forgetting. We further prove that learning the LoRA basis from data minimizes residual interference energy more effectively than frozen-basis methods. Experiments on multi-concept image generation demonstrate that SeqLoRA improves identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts, while avoiding costly fusion and reducing attribute interference in composed generations.

AI.tldr: SeqLoRA provides a bilevel, sequentially regularized LoRA training approach to personalize diffusion models for many concepts, avoiding fusion while improving identity preservation and reducing interference.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, high-fidelity composition of multiple user concepts in text-to-image diffusion models without the drawbacks of either post-hoc fusion or frozen subspaces that limit expressiveness and fidelity.
AI.method: Propose Sequential Regularized LoRA (SeqLoRA), a constrained continual learning framework that jointly optimizes two LoRA factors via bilevel optimization. Theoretical analysis yields convergence guarantees; residual activations are modeled as a matrix sub-Gaussian process to bound catastrophic forgetting; learning the LoRA basis from data reduces residual interference energy compared to frozen-basis methods.
AI.result: Empirically, SeqLoRA improves identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts in multi-concept image generation, while avoiding costly fusion and reducing attribute interference in composed generations.
AI.conclusion: SeqLoRA offers a principled, scalable approach to sequential diffusion model personalization with strong theoretical guarantees and practical gains, addressing the interference and fusion trade-offs inherent in prior modular fine-tuning methods.

title: SDPM: Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model for Continuous-Time Survival Analysis
summary: Survival analysis aims to estimate a time-to-event distribution from data with censored observations. Many existing methods either impose structural assumptions on the hazard function or discretize the time axis, which may limit flexibility and introduce approximation errors. We propose the Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model (SDPM), a generative approach to continuous-time survival analysis. SDPM models the conditional distribution of the survival outcome, represented by the pair of observed time and censoring indicator, $\mathbb{P}(T,δ\mid \mathbf{x})$, using a denoising diffusion model. Under the assumption of conditionally independent censoring, conditional samples generated by the model can be transformed into survival function estimates using the Kaplan-Meier estimator. This formulation avoids parametric assumptions on the event-time distribution and does not require a discretization of the output time space. The model operates in a transformed target space, using standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation of the censoring indicator. We evaluate SDPM on ten real survival datasets and compare it with five strong baselines, including tree-based, boosting-based, and neural survival models. Results show that SDPM achieves competitive predictive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score. A study on synthetic Cox-Weibull data demonstrates that SDPM can recover the shape of an underlying continuous survival distribution more accurately than a strong nonparametric baseline when sufficiently many samples are generated. An ablation study confirms the importance of the proposed target-space transformations, which improve event-rate calibration, reduce invalid generated times, and provide consistent gains in predictive discrimination. Codes implementing the proposed model are publicly available.

AI.tldr: SDPM is a diffusion-based, nonparametric, continuous-time survival model that avoids time discretization and parametric assumptions by modeling P(T, δ | x) with a conditional denoising diffusion process; with conditional censoring independence, generated samples map to Kaplan-Meier survival estimates. It shows competitive performance on real data across key metrics and better distribution recovery on synthetic data, with ablations validating target-space design; code is released.
AI.motivation: Traditional survival methods either impose parametric/hazard structure or discretize time, limiting flexibility and introducing approximation error. A flexible, continuous-time generative approach could better capture the full time-to-event distribution without discretization or strong parametric constraints.
AI.method: Introduce Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model (SDPM) to model the conditional distribution of (T, δ) given covariates x via a denoising diffusion model. Assumes conditionally independent censoring. Use transformed target space with standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation for δ. Generated conditional samples can be transformed into survival function estimates using the Kaplan-Meier estimator. Evaluate on ten real datasets against five baselines (tree-based, boosting-based, neural models). Implementations are released.
AI.result: SDPM achieves competitive predictive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score. On synthetic Cox-Weibull data, SDPM better recovers the true continuous survival distribution than a strong nonparametric baseline given sufficient samples. Ablation confirms the importance of target-space transformations for calibration, generation validity, and discrimination gains.
AI.conclusion: SDPM provides a flexible, nonparametric framework for continuous-time survival analysis via diffusion modeling, avoiding discretization and parametric assumptions while delivering robust predictive performance. Target-space transformations are critical for calibration and valid time generation; code is publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Plug-in Losses for Evidential Deep Learning: A Simplified Framework for Uncertainty Estimation that Includes the Softmax Classifier
summary: Real-world sensor-based learning systems require uncertainty estimation that is both reliable and computationally efficient. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) provides single-pass uncertainty estimation by modeling the class probabilities via Dirichlet distributions, where the Dirichlet parameters are predicted by a learned neural network mapping. However, this approach can lead to computational challenges, as Dirichlet expected objectives are more complex than standard supervised learning losses, complicating their analysis and implementation. We address this issue by approximating the objective of the first-order empirical risk minimization problem induced by EDL with a plug-in loss evaluated at the Dirichlet mean and show that, under mild assumptions, the approximation error decays with growing evidence for a broad class of loss functions, including mean-squared error and cross-entropy loss. As a special case, our analysis provides justification for the use of softmax in the context of uncertainty estimation, since under a particular evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping, our framework includes the standard softmax classifier. We validate the proposed simplified objectives on the Google Speech Commands dataset and show that they achieve predictive accuracy and selective prediction performance comparable to classical EDL, while being simpler to implement using standard deep learning losses and training pipelines. To the best of our knowledge, this empirical analysis is the first to obtain coverage-accuracy trade-offs for speech recognition tasks through EDL.

AI.tldr: Proposes a plug-in loss to simplify Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) by evaluating a Dirichlet-mean loss instead of the full Dirichlet objective; proves the approximation error decays with increasing evidence for common losses; shows softmax can be recovered under a specific evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping; empirically validates on Google Speech Commands with performance close to classical EDL while using standard losses.
AI.motivation: Real-world sensor-based systems require reliable and efficient uncertainty estimation. EDL yields Dirichlet-based uncertainty but its objective is computationally more complex than standard losses, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: Approximate the first-order empirical risk minimization objective of EDL with a plug-in loss evaluated at the Dirichlet mean. Provide theoretical bounds showing the approximation error decays as evidence grows for a broad class of losses (e.g., mean-squared error, cross-entropy). Demonstrate that, under a particular evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping, the framework includes softmax as a special case. Implement and train using standard DL losses and pipelines.
AI.result: Empirical validation on Google Speech Commands shows predictive accuracy and selective prediction performance comparable to classical EDL, with simpler implementation. This work also provides the first coverage-accuracy trade-off analysis for speech recognition tasks using EDL.
AI.conclusion: The proposed plug-in objective is theoretically justified and practically effective, enabling simpler, softmax-compatible uncertainty estimation within EDL and facilitating its deployment in speech recognition tasks.

title: The Matching Principle: A Geometric Theory of Loss Functions for Nuisance-Robust Representation Learning
comment: 54 pages. 13 empirical task blocks. Companion software: matching-pmh (PyPI; https://github.com/vishalstark512/matching-pmh). Related arXiv note: 2604.21395 (geometric blind spot / isotropic PMH)
summary:
Robustness, domain adaptation, photometric and occlusion invariance, compositional generalisation, temporal robustness, alignment safety, and classical anisotropic regularisation are usually treated as separate problems with separate method families. This paper argues that much of their shared structure is one statistical problem: estimate the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance, then regularise the encoder Jacobian along a matrix whose range covers that covariance (the matching principle). CORAL, adversarial training, IRM, augmentation, metric learning, Jacobian penalties, and alignment-style constraints are different estimators of that object, not independent robustness tricks.
  In the linear-Gaussian model we prove closed-form optimality (Theorem A), including cube-root water-filling within the matched range; necessity of range coverage for quadratic Jacobian penalties (Theorem G); the same range dichotomy at deep global minima; and two falsification controls (Lemma C; Corollaries E), with seven conditional consistency lemmas (D1-D7) for estimation under standard identifiability assumptions.
  We introduce the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a label-free probe of embedding sensitivity when task accuracy or Jacobian Frobenius norm is insufficient.
  Thirteen pre-registered blocks from classical ML through Qwen2.5-7B test the predicted matched, then isotropic, then wrong-W ordering on geometry and deployment drift; twelve pass, and the sole exception (Office-31) is an eigengap failure named before the run. At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI where standard DPO degrades it.
  The contribution is naming the deployment nuisance covariance, stating what the regulariser must do, and supplying a closed-form falsifiable theory once that object is identified, not universality on every leaderboard.

AI.tldr: Unifies robustness, domain adaptation, and related problems as estimation of label-preserving deployment nuisance covariance, with a matched Jacobian regularizer. Provides closed-form theory in a linear-Gaussian setting, introduces a label-free Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), and empirically validates the matching principle across 13 pre-registered blocks (12 pass; Office-31 as eigengap exception). At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI; argues for naming the deployment-nuisance covariance and using a falsifiable theory over universal leaderboard claims.
AI.motivation: Despite many separate methods (CORAL, adversarial training, IRM, augmentation, etc.), there is a shared statistical task: estimate the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance and regularize the encoder Jacobian on its matched range. This provides a unifying, falsifiable framework rather than ad-hoc robustness tricks.
AI.method: Theoretical development in a linear-Gaussian setting yielding closed-form optimality (Theorem A) and a cube-root water-filling solution within the matched range; necessity of range coverage for quadratic Jacobian penalties (Theorem G); extensions to deep global minima (range dichotomy) and a set of falsification controls (Lemma C; Corollaries E) plus seven conditional consistency lemmas (D1-D7). Introduction of the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) as a label-free probe; extensive pre-registered experiments testing a ‘matched → isotropic → wrong-W’ geometry/deployment drift ordering across 13 blocks; evaluation at 7B scale focusing on Style-PMH vs DPO and preservation of Style TDI.
AI.result: Closed-form results support the matching principle; most empirical blocks align with the predicted ordering, with Office-31 as the lone eigengap-related failure. At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI where standard DPO degrades it.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is to name the deployment nuisance covariance, specify what the regulariser must do, and provide a falsifiable theory once that object is identified. It advocates a unified viewpoint rather than universal improvements across all leaderboards.

title: Vector Policy Optimization: Training for Diversity Improves Test-Time Search
summary: Language models must now generalize out of the box to novel environments and work inside inference-scaling search procedures, such as AlphaEvolve, that select rollouts with a variety of task-specific reward functions. Unfortunately, the standard paradigm of LLM post-training optimizes a pre-specified scalar reward, often leading current LLMs to produce low-entropy response distributions and thus to struggle at displaying the diversity that inference-time search will require. We propose Vector Policy Optimization (VPO), an RL algorithm that explicitly trains policies to anticipate diverse downstream reward functions and to produce diverse solutions. VPO exploits that rewards are often vector-valued in practice, like per-test-case correctness in code generation or, say, multiple different user personas or reward models. VPO is essentially a drop-in replacement for the GRPO advantage estimator, but it trains the LLM to output a set of solutions where individual solutions specialize to different trade-offs in the vector reward space. Across four tasks, VPO matches or beats the strongest scalar RL baselines on test-time search (e.g. pass@k and best@k), with the gap widening as the search budget grows. For evolutionary search, VPO models unlock problems that GRPO models cannot solve at all. As test-time search becomes more standardized, optimizing for diversity may need to become the default post-training objective.

AI.tldr: VPO introduces Vector Policy Optimization to train LLMs for diverse downstream rewards, improving performance in test-time search and evolutionary search compared to scalar RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional post-training optimizes a single scalar reward, yielding low-entropy, non-diverse outputs that hinder inference-time search. Many tasks involve vector-valued rewards (e.g., per-test-case correctness, multiple user personas), which require diverse solutions.
AI.method: VPO replaces the GRPO advantage estimator with a diversity-focused objective, training policies to output a set of solutions that specialize across different regions of the vector reward space, leveraging vector-valued rewards during training to promote diversity.
AI.result: Across four tasks, VPO matches or outperforms the strongest scalar RL baselines on test-time search metrics (pass@k, best@k); benefits grow with larger search budgets; in evolutionary search, VPO enables solutions unavailable to GRPO.
AI.conclusion: As test-time search becomes standard, optimizing for solution diversity should be the default post-training objective; VPO demonstrates that diversity-aware training yields tangible gains in both discriminative and evolutionary search settings.

title: Lumberjack: Better Differentially Private Random Forests through Heavy Hitter Detection in Trees
summary: Random forests are widely used in fields involving sensitive tabular data, but existing approaches to enforcing differential privacy (DP) typically degrade performance to the point of impracticality. In this paper, we introduce Lumberjack, a differentially private random forest algorithm that achieves substantially higher utility by constructing large random decision trees and then applying aggressive, privacy-preserving pruning to retain only sufficiently populated nodes. A key component of our approach is a novel $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP heavy hitter detection algorithm for hierarchical data, whose error is $O_{\varepsilon,δ}(\sqrt{\log h})$ for trees of height $h$ and may be of independent interest. This favorable scaling enables the use of significantly deeper trees than in prior work, leading to improved expressiveness under privacy constraints. Our empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows that Lumberjack consistently outperforms prior DP random forest methods, establishing a new state of the art. In particular, our approach yields substantial improvements in the privacy-utility trade-off for practical privacy budgets. Our findings suggest that carefully designed DP random forests can close much of the utility gap, highlighting a promising and underexplored direction for future research.

AI.tldr: Lumberjack is a differentially private random forest that builds large trees and uses privacy-preserving pruning guided by a novel DP heavy-hitter detection, enabling deeper trees and improved accuracy under DP, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Standard DP pruning in random forests severely hurts utility; there is a need for DP techniques that allow deeper, more expressive trees without excessive privacy loss.
AI.method: Train large random decision trees; apply aggressive, privacy-preserving pruning using a hierarchical DP heavy-hitter detection algorithm whose error scales as O_{ε,δ}(√log h) with tree height h; prune to retain sufficiently populated nodes, yielding a compact, private forest.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows Lumberjack outperforms prior DP random forest methods, achieving state-of-the-art privacy-utility trade-offs at practical privacy budgets.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed DP random forests with deep trees and principled pruning can close much of the DP utility gap, indicating a promising direction for future research.

title: Uniform Diffusion Models Revisited: Leave-One-Out Denoiser and Absorbing State Reformulation
summary:
Discrete diffusion models are often trained through clean-data prediction, but the prediction can be used in different ways to define the reverse dynamics. In Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) these choices largely coincide, whereas in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM) they do not. We show that the standard plug-in bridge parameterization for UDM is not optimized by the denoising posterior, but by a leave-one-out posterior that predicts each clean token without using its own noisy observation. This identifies a mismatch between the plug-in ELBO and the usual cross-entropy denoising objective. We characterize the leave-one-out target and derive exact conversions between the denoiser, the leave-one-out posterior, and the score. These conversions allow us to disentangle parameterization and training objective. Our results also lead to inference improvements without any additional training through an informed predictor-corrector sampler and improved temperature sampling based on the leave-one-out predictor.
  We further introduce an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that preserves the UDM joint law while decomposing it into masked-diffusion-like sampling operations, with simpler denoising posteriors, carry-over unmasking, and a natural remasking mechanism. On language modeling, leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation, while the absorbing construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion. These results suggest that the empirical gap between masked and uniform diffusion is driven less by the choice of marginals themselves than by parameterization and sampling design. The code and models can be found at https://github.com/samsongourevitch/rev_udm.

AI.tldr: Leave-one-out posteriors govern optimization in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM); derive exact connections between denoisers, LOO posteriors, and scores; propose absorbing-state reformulation to decouple parameterization from training; show empirical gains in language modeling and improved inference without retraining; release code.
AI.motivation: Understand why Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) and UDM differ and whether their gaps arise from marginal choices, parameterization, or training design; aim to align UDM performance with MDM through principled training targets and sampling schemes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis deriving the leave-one-out (LOO) posterior for UDM; establish exact conversions between the denoiser, LOO posterior, and the score; propose an absorbing-state reformulation that preserves the UDM joint distribution while enabling masked-diffusion-like sampling; develop an informed predictor-corrector sampler and temperature-based sampling based on the LOO predictor; empirical evaluation on language modeling; release code and models.
AI.result: LOO-parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation; absorbing-state construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion; the performance gap between MDM and UDM is largely due to parameterization and sampling design rather than the marginals themselves; provides practical inference improvements without additional training, and a simpler denoising posterior.
AI.conclusion: Parameterization and sampling design are key drivers of the observed differences between MDM and UDM; with principled LOO targets and absorbing-state reformulation, UDM can achieve parity or superiority relative to MDM, enabling improved inference and sampling in language modeling.

title: Remember to be Curious: Episodic Context and Persistent Worlds for 3D Exploration
summary: Exploration is a prerequisite for learning useful behaviors in sparse-reward, long-horizon tasks, particularly within 3D environments. Curiosity-driven reinforcement learning addresses this via intrinsic rewards derived from the mismatch between the agent's predictive model of the world and reality. However, translating this intrinsic motivation to complex, photorealistic environments remains difficult, as agents can become trapped in local loops and receive fresh rewards for revisiting forgotten states. In this work, we demonstrate that this failure stems from a lack of spatial persistence and episodic context. We show that effective curiosity requires a model of the world that is persistent and continuously updated, paired with an agent that maintains an episodic trajectory history to navigate toward novel regions. We achieve this using an online 3D reconstruction as a persistent model of the world, while the agent policy is parameterized as a sequence model over RGB observations to maintain episodic context. This design enables effective exploration during training while allowing the agent to navigate using solely RGB frames at deployment. Trained purely via curiosity on HM3D, our agent outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines and generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds. Our end-to-end policy enables efficient adaptation to downstream tasks, such as apple picking and image-goal navigation, outperforming from-scratch baselines. Please see video results at https://recuriosity.github.io/.

AI.tldr: A curiosity-driven RL approach that uses a persistent online 3D reconstruction as the world model and a sequence-model policy over RGB for episodic context, enabling effective exploration in photorealistic 3D environments and zero-shot generalization to new worlds; trained purely via intrinsic rewards on HM3D and deployable with RGB-only inputs, outperforming baselines and aiding downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Exploration in sparse-reward, long-horizon tasks in photorealistic 3D environments is hindered by local loops and loss of context. Existing curiosity methods lack spatial persistence and episodic memory, leading to inefficient exploration.
AI.method: Maintain a persistent world model via online 3D reconstruction updated online; parameterize the agent policy as a sequence model over RGB observations to preserve episodic context; train using intrinsic curiosity rewards derived from predictor-model mismatch; deploy using RGB-only inputs; evaluate on HM3D and generalize to Gibson and AI-generated worlds; demonstrate downstream task adaptation (apple picking, image-goal navigation).
AI.result: The method outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines on HM3D, generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds, and enables efficient adaptation to downstream tasks, outperforming from-scratch baselines.
AI.conclusion: Persistency of a world model plus episodic context is crucial for effective curiosity-driven exploration in complex 3D environments; the approach yields strong exploration, generalization, and downstream performance, with practical RGB-only deployment.

title: Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials
summary: We propose the Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN), a demand-first neural model for multiproduct retail demand. The model learns log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing elasticities to be derived exactly from the learned demand surface. On the Dominick's beer dataset, ICDN improves out-of-sample generalization over a directed log-log benchmark and yields more stable, economically plausible elasticity estimates, especially for weakly identified cross-price effects.

AI.tldr: Introduces Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN), a demand-first neural model for multiproduct retail demand that yields exact elasticities from a smooth log-demand surface; demonstrates improved out-of-sample generalization on Dominick's beer data and more stable, economically plausible cross-price elasticity estimates.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of traditional demand models and ad-hoc elasticity estimation by learning a smooth, context-conditioned demand surface from price data, enabling exact elasticity extraction and robust performance under weakly identified price interactions.
AI.method: Proposes ICDN, a neural model that treats log-demand as a smooth function of log-prices conditioned on context (e.g., cross-effects, promotions). Elasticities are derived analytically from the learned surface. Evaluated on the Dominick's beer dataset against a directed log–log benchmark, focusing on stability of cross-price effects.
AI.result: ICDN yields better out-of-sample generalization than the benchmark and produces more stable, economically plausible elasticity estimates, particularly for weakly identified cross-price effects.
AI.conclusion: A demand-first neural framework that provides exact elasticity derivation from a learned demand surface, improves predictive performance, and stabilizes challenging elasticity estimates, with potential applicability to broader multiproduct settings.
